Crop Nutrient Metabolism Gene Knowledge Base

A structured review of key genes extracted from scientific literature

100 articles · 216 gene entries · 5 trait categories

Table of Contents

  • Abiotic Stress Tolerance (209)
  • Flavonoid / Phenylpropanoid / Secondary Metabolism (2)
  • Yield / Growth / Development (2)

Abiotic Stress Tolerance (209 genes)

ShCOI1-4 (Saccharum spp. hybrid) Plant

Key Findings

  • ShCOI1-4 was cloned from sugarcane cultivar ROC22 as a homolog of SsCOI1-4b. [1]
  • The encoded protein carries conserved F-box, Transp_inhibit, and AMN1 (LRR) domains characteristic of COI1 F-box proteins. [1]
  • ShCOI1-4 transcripts are strongly induced by ABA and SA but are repressed by cold (4 °C) and PEG-induced drought stress. [1]
  • During Sporisorium scitamineum infection, ShCOI1-4 is inducible in several cultivars, including smut-resistant lines, linking it to hormone- and pathogen-responsive defense signaling. [1]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Hormone- and pathogen-responsive COI1-like gene whose expression is activated by ABA, SA, and smut infection and suppressed by cold and drought, suggesting involvement in sugarcane defense and abiotic stress signaling. [1]

Regulatory Mechanism

Transcriptionally up-regulated by ABA and SA and down-regulated by cold and PEG-induced drought; induction pattern under smut infection differs among cultivars, with stronger responses in some resistant lines. [1]

Pathways

Regulatory: COI1-related jasmonate signaling module with cross-talk to ABA- and SA-mediated stress-response pathways in sugarcane. [1]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCOI1 family F-box protein with F-box domain, Transp_inhibit (transport inhibitor response 1) domain, and AMN1 leucine-rich repeat (LRR) domain.
Expression PatternIn ROC22 seedlings, ShCOI1-4 expression increases ~2.9-fold at 3–6 h after ABA treatment and ~1.6-fold at 12 h after SA, then returns to control levels; transcripts are reduced under cold (4 °C) and PEG-induced drought. Under S. scitamineum infection, ShCOI1-4 is induced in some cultivars (e.g., LC05-136 at 7 dpi, GT02-467 at 3 dpi, and in resistant YZ03-258 and YT96-86) but is decreased or stable in others.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR-based expression analysis of cloned ShCOI1-4 under hormone (ABA, SA), abiotic (cold, PEG), and smut infection treatments.
Breeding Application ValueBecause ShCOI1-4 is transcriptionally responsive to ABA, SA, drought, cold, and smut infection and shows stronger induction in some resistant cultivars, it represents a candidate gene/marker for molecular breeding to improve sugarcane resistance to smut and tolerance to abiotic stresses.
Environment / TreatmentSalicylic acid (5 mM), abscisic acid (100 μM), cold (4 °C), drought simulated by 25% PEG 8000, and infection with the smut fungus Sporisorium scitamineum.

ShCOI1-5 (Saccharum spp. hybrid) Plant

Key Findings

  • ShCOI1-5 was cloned from sugarcane cultivar ROC22 as a homolog of SsCOI1-1b. [1]
  • The encoded protein contains conserved F-box, Transp_inhibit, and AMN1 (LRR) domains typical of COI1 F-box proteins. [1]
  • ShCOI1-5 is strongly induced by SA and PEG-induced drought, slightly affected or repressed by ABA, and largely unresponsive to cold. [1]
  • During smut infection, ShCOI1-5 is significantly induced in all tested smut-resistant cultivars and in most susceptible cultivars, supporting a role in pathogen-responsive signaling. [1]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Stress-responsive COI1-like gene whose expression is markedly activated by SA and drought and induced during smut infection, implicating it in hormone-mediated defense and abiotic stress responses in sugarcane. [1]

Regulatory Mechanism

Transcriptionally up-regulated by SA and PEG-induced drought, down-regulated by ABA at later time points, and not significantly changed by cold; broadly induced during smut infection in both resistant and susceptible cultivars except FN40. [1]

Pathways

Regulatory: COI1-related jasmonate signaling component participating in cross-talk with SA- and ABA-mediated stress and defense pathways. [1]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCOI1 family F-box protein with F-box domain, Transp_inhibit (transport inhibitor response 1) domain, and AMN1 leucine-rich repeat (LRR) domain.
Expression PatternIn ROC22, ShCOI1-5 transcripts are strongly induced by SA with a single peak at 12 h (~2.17-fold above control). Under ABA, expression is unchanged at 0–6 h and reduced at 12 h. Under PEG-induced drought, transcripts increase ~2.77-fold at 0.5 h and ~1.66-fold at 3–6 h, while cold treatment does not significantly alter expression. During S. scitamineum infection, ShCOI1-5 is significantly up-regulated at 7 dpi in all three smut-resistant cultivars and in susceptible GT02-467 and YZ03-103, but down-regulated in susceptible FN40.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR analysis of ShCOI1-5 expression under hormone (SA, ABA), abiotic (cold, PEG), and smut infection treatments.
Breeding Application ValueBecause ShCOI1-5 is robustly induced by SA and drought and strongly up-regulated during smut infection in resistant cultivars, it is a promising candidate gene or expression marker for breeding sugarcane with enhanced resistance to smut and improved tolerance to drought stress.
Environment / TreatmentSalicylic acid (5 mM), abscisic acid (100 μM), cold (4 °C), drought simulated by 25% PEG 8000, and Sporisorium scitamineum infection.

ShCOI1-6 (Saccharum spp. hybrid) Plant

Key Findings

  • ShCOI1-6 was cloned from sugarcane cultivar ROC22 as a homolog of SsCOI1-3b. [1]
  • The encoded protein includes conserved F-box, Transp_inhibit, and AMN1 (LRR) domains typical of COI1 F-box proteins. [1]
  • ShCOI1-6 expression is induced by SA, ABA, and PEG-induced drought but remains stable under cold stress. [1]
  • During smut infection, ShCOI1-6 expression is increased in some resistant cultivars but decreased or unchanged in most susceptible cultivars, supporting its involvement in defense-related signaling. [1]

Core Phenotypic Effect

COI1-like gene whose transcripts are activated by SA, ABA, and drought and show cultivar-dependent induction during smut infection, suggesting a role in hormone- and stress-related defense responses in sugarcane. [1]

Regulatory Mechanism

Transcriptionally induced by SA (early), by ABA from 3–12 h, and by PEG-induced drought (notably at 24 h), while remaining unchanged under cold; during smut infection, induction is evident in some resistant cultivars but not in most susceptible ones. [1]

Pathways

Regulatory: COI1-related jasmonate signaling component participating in ABA- and SA-associated stress and immune pathways in sugarcane. [1]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCOI1 family F-box protein with F-box domain, Transp_inhibit (transport inhibitor response 1) domain, and AMN1 leucine-rich repeat (LRR) domain.
Expression PatternIn ROC22 seedlings, ShCOI1-6 expression is significantly induced by SA at 3 h (~1.86-fold of control), by ABA from 3–12 h with a peak at 12 h, and by PEG-induced drought, reaching ~2.14-fold of control at 24 h; cold treatment does not alter expression. During S. scitamineum infection, ShCOI1-6 is significantly up-regulated in resistant cultivars YZ03-258 and YT96-86, whereas it is decreased or remains stable in the other four cultivars.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR expression profiling of ShCOI1-6 under hormone (SA, ABA), abiotic (cold, PEG), and smut infection treatments.
Breeding Application ValueBecause ShCOI1-6 is induced by SA, ABA, drought, and smut infection, particularly in resistant cultivars, it is a potential target for molecular breeding or marker-assisted selection to enhance smut resistance and tolerance to abiotic stresses in sugarcane.
Environment / TreatmentSalicylic acid (5 mM), abscisic acid (100 μM), cold (4 °C), drought simulated by 25% PEG 8000, and Sporisorium scitamineum infection.

AnDHN (Ammopiptanthus nanus) Plant

Key Findings

  • AnDHN (c195333_g1_i1) was isolated from a drought-induced RNA-seq library of Ammopiptanthus nanus and encodes a dehydrin protein with conserved Y- and K-segment motifs. [2]
  • AnDHN expression in A. nanus roots, stems, and leaves is strongly induced by drought (PEG 8000), and also by salt and cold stresses, consistent with the presence of multiple stress- and hormone-responsive cis-elements in its promoter. [2]
  • AnDHN protein localizes to the plasma membrane in plant cells, as shown by AnDHN-GFP colocalization with a PM marker in Nicotiana benthamiana. [2]
  • Overexpression of AnDHN in Arabidopsis enhances drought tolerance by increasing germination rates under mannitol stress, promoting deeper root system architecture, improving water retention and osmotic adjustment (higher RWC and proline, lower MDA), and enhancing ROS scavenging (higher POD, CAT, and GSH activities, lower ROS accumulation). [2]
  • AnDHN overexpression simultaneously improves plant tolerance to salt and cold stresses, with higher germination and longer roots, improved water status and antioxidant capacity, and reduced ROS levels compared with WT under NaCl and low-temperature treatments. [2]
  • In AnDHN-overexpressing Arabidopsis, ABA biosynthetic genes AtNCED3 and AtNCED5 are upregulated while catabolic genes AtCYP707A1 and AtCYP707A3 are downregulated, supporting a role for AnDHN in ABA-associated stress signaling and suggesting an AnDHN–ABA feedback loop. [2]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of AnDHN in Arabidopsis markedly enhances tolerance to drought, salt, and cold stresses by improving seed germination and seedling survival under mannitol, NaCl, and low-temperature treatments, conferring deeper root system architecture under osmotic stress, and increasing water retention, osmotic adjustment, and ROS-scavenging capacity. [2]

Regulatory Mechanism

Membrane-localized dehydrin that enhances drought, salt, and cold tolerance by improving water retention (higher RWC), osmotic adjustment (proline accumulation), and ROS detoxification (increased POD, CAT, and GSH activities and reduced ROS accumulation). Overexpression of AnDHN correlates with upregulation of ABA biosynthetic genes (AtNCED3, AtNCED5) and downregulation of ABA catabolic genes (AtCYP707A1, AtCYP707A3), suggesting modulation of ABA levels and an AnDHN–ABA positive feedback loop. [2]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in ABA-associated drought stress signaling and ROS-scavenging pathways: AnDHN is induced by ABA, and its overexpression in Arabidopsis is associated with increased expression of ABA biosynthetic genes and decreased expression of ABA catabolic genes, alongside enhanced antioxidant enzyme activities under drought. [2]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDehydrin (Group II LEA protein) harboring conserved Y-segment (DEYGNPV) and K-segment (KKGIMNKIKEKLPGY) motifs.
Expression PatternIn A. nanus, AnDHN is strongly induced by simulated drought (20% PEG 8000), with expression in roots significantly upregulated by 6 h and peaking at 24 h, and 2.8–3.1-fold induction in stems and leaves at 48 h relative to 0 h. AnDHN is also markedly induced in roots, stems, and leaves by salt (0.25 M NaCl) and cold (4°C) treatments, and responds to several hormones (ABA, IAA, NAA, MeJA, ETH, GA3) based on qRT–PCR and promoter element analysis.
Validation MethodTransgenic overexpression of AnDHN in Arabidopsis thaliana (35S:AnDHN) followed by germination and root growth assays under mannitol, NaCl, and 4°C treatments, and physiological/biochemical measurements (ion leakage, RWC, proline, MDA, antioxidant enzyme activities, ROS staining) to assess stress tolerance; subcellular localization of AnDHN-GFP to the plasma membrane.
Breeding Application ValueAnDHN is proposed as a promising candidate gene for engineering crops with enhanced tolerance to multiple abiotic stresses. Overexpression in Arabidopsis confers improved drought, salt, and cold tolerance via deeper root systems, better water retention, osmotic adjustment, and ROS scavenging, suggesting that introducing AnDHN or its orthologs into legume and other crop species could help sustain yield and quality under increasing environmental stresses.
Environment / TreatmentSimulated drought with 20% PEG 8000 in A. nanus; NaCl (0.25 M in A. nanus, 150 mM in Arabidopsis assays); osmotic stress with mannitol (200–400 mM in germination, 300 mM in seedling assays); low temperature (4°C) for A. nanus treatments and Arabidopsis cold assays; hormone treatments including ABA, IAA, NAA, MeJA, ethylene, and GA3.

UGT85E1 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • UGT85E1 is a rice UDP-dependent glycosyltransferase gene whose expression is strongly induced by mannitol and ABA, indicating involvement in drought-related signaling. [3]
  • Overexpression of UGT85E1 in rice enhances tolerance to mannitol and soil-drought stress, while ugt85e1 knockout mutants are more sensitive; overexpression also confers drought tolerance when ectopically expressed in Arabidopsis. [3]
  • UGT85E1 overexpression increases endogenous ABA levels under drought, promotes stomatal closure, reduces leaf water loss, and heightens ABA sensitivity during seedling growth, concomitant with upregulation of ABA biosynthesis and signaling genes. [3]
  • Overexpression of UGT85E1 enhances ROS-scavenging capacity, reduces H2O2 and superoxide accumulation, increases expression of ROS-scavenging enzyme genes, and leads to higher proline and soluble sugar accumulation, collectively contributing to improved drought and oxidative stress tolerance. [3]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of UGT85E1 enhances drought and oxidative stress tolerance, whereas ugt85e1 knockout mutants are hypersensitive to drought/osmotic and oxidative stresses. [3]

Regulatory Mechanism

As a putative UDP-dependent glycosyltransferase, UGT85E1 overexpression leads to increased expression of ABA biosynthesis and signaling genes and ROS-scavenging enzyme genes, resulting in higher ABA levels, enhanced stomatal closure, improved ROS scavenging, and greater accumulation of proline and soluble sugar under drought; the direct biochemical substrate(s) and precise molecular mechanism remain unresolved. [3]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in ABA-dependent drought stress response pathways by elevating ABA accumulation and the expression of ABA biosynthesis (OsABA1, OsABA2, OsABA4, OsNCED1) and ABA-responsive signaling genes (OsABI5, OsbZIP23), and in ROS homeostasis pathways by enhancing ROS-scavenging gene expression and antioxidant capacity. [3]

Biosynthetic: ABA-related metabolic and ROS-scavenging pathways associated with drought and oxidative stress responses (via altered expression of ABA biosynthesis/signaling genes and ROS-scavenging enzyme genes). [3]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainUDP-dependent glycosyltransferase of the UGT85 family
Expression PatternUGT85E1 transcript levels are strongly induced by 200 mM mannitol and 100 μM ABA from 6 to 48 h; expression is upregulated by drought stress.
Breeding Application ValueUGT85E1 is a promising candidate gene for improving drought and oxidative stress tolerance in crops. Its overexpression enhances survival and growth under mannitol and soil-drought conditions, increases ABA accumulation and stress-related gene expression, and boosts ROS-scavenging and osmoprotectant accumulation, indicating potential utility in genetic engineering or marker-assisted breeding of drought-tolerant rice and possibly other monocots and dicots.
Environment / TreatmentDrought (soil water deprivation), osmotic stress via mannitol, ABA treatment, dehydration of detached leaves, and oxidative stress induced by H2O2 and methyl viologen.

UGT85E1 (ectopic expression context) (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • Rice UGT85E1 driven by the CaMV 35S promoter is successfully overexpressed in Arabidopsis Col-0 lines OE-4 and OE-7. [3]
  • Arabidopsis plants overexpressing UGT85E1 lose water more slowly from detached leaves and show higher survival than WT after prolonged drought and recovery. [3]
  • Ectopic UGT85E1 expression reduces ROS accumulation under mannitol-induced osmotic stress, as shown by weaker DAB and NBT staining. [3]
  • UGT85E1 overexpression elevates transcript levels of abiotic stress-responsive genes AtRD29A, AtRD29B, AtDREB1A, and AtDREB1B in Arabidopsis under mannitol treatment, supporting a conserved role in enhancing drought and oxidative stress tolerance across monocots and dicots. [3]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Ectopic overexpression of rice UGT85E1 in Arabidopsis enhances drought tolerance and reduces ROS accumulation under osmotic stress. [3]

Regulatory Mechanism

Constitutive expression of rice UGT85E1 in Arabidopsis leads to lower ROS accumulation and higher expression of abiotic stress-responsive transcriptional regulators and marker genes; the direct biochemical mechanism remains unclear. [3]

Pathways

Regulatory: Rice UGT85E1 acts within Arabidopsis abiotic stress response pathways, reducing ROS accumulation and upregulating canonical stress-responsive genes AtRD29A, AtRD29B, AtDREB1A, and AtDREB1B under mannitol stress. [3]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainTransgenic expression of rice UDP-dependent glycosyltransferase UGT85E1 in Arabidopsis background; enzyme belongs to UGT85 family.
Expression PatternRice UGT85E1 is constitutively overexpressed in Arabidopsis under the CaMV 35S promoter in lines OE-4 and OE-7.
Breeding Application ValueDemonstrates that rice UGT85E1 can enhance drought and oxidative stress tolerance when expressed in a dicot model, supporting its potential use as a transferable transgene to engineer stress-tolerant dicot crops as well as monocots.
Environment / TreatmentMannitol-induced osmotic stress (250 mM), soil-drought (6 days without watering) followed by re-watering, and dehydration of detached leaves.

DnMSI1 (Dendrobium nobile) Plant

Key Findings

  • DnMSI1, an MSI1-like WD40 repeat protein with four WD40 domains, was cloned from Dendrobium nobile and shows high sequence similarity to MSI1 proteins from other plant species. [4]
  • DnMSI1 is predominantly expressed in buds, with lower expression in stems and leaves and minimal expression in roots. [4]
  • DnMSI1 transcription in buds is downregulated by abiotic stresses including drought, Cd, Ni, and NaCl, and is differentially regulated by plant growth regulators: induced by GA3 and TDZ and suppressed by ABA and paclobutrazol (PP333). [4]
  • Constitutive overexpression of DnMSI1 in Arabidopsis thaliana leads to increased sensitivity to NaCl, with stronger inhibition of primary root growth under high salinity compared with WT. [4]
  • These data support that DnMSI1 acts as a hormone-responsive, negative regulator of salinity-stress tolerance in plants, with likely functional relevance to stress responses in Dendrobium nobile. [4]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Constitutive overexpression of DnMSI1 significantly decreases tolerance to NaCl stress, leading to stronger inhibition of primary root growth under high salinity. [4]

Pathways

Regulatory: Hormone-responsive abiotic stress pathway in which DnMSI1 expression is induced by GA3 and cytokinin-like TDZ and repressed by ABA and GA-biosynthesis inhibition (PP333), contributing negatively to salinity-stress responses. [4]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainMSI1-like WD40 repeat protein containing four WD40 repeat domains.
Expression PatternIn D. nobile, DnMSI1 transcripts are detected in buds, stems, and leaves but hardly in roots, with highest expression in buds. In buds, DnMSI1 expression is downregulated by drought, Cd, Ni, and NaCl stress. DnMSI1 is induced by GA3 and TDZ, peaking around 10–15 days after treatment, and is repressed by ABA and the GA-biosynthesis inhibitor PP333. In Arabidopsis DnMSI1-overexpression lines, DnMSI1 transcript levels are much higher than in WT and decrease upon NaCl treatment but remain above WT levels.
Validation MethodTransgenic overexpression of DnMSI1 in Arabidopsis thaliana (35S::DnMSI1) combined with NaCl stress assays measuring relative root growth and RT-qPCR analysis of DnMSI1 transcript levels.
Breeding Application ValueDnMSI1 is a hormone- and stress-responsive regulator whose overexpression decreases plant tolerance to salinity stress, suggesting that down-regulation or loss-of-function of DnMSI1 could be used to enhance salt-stress resistance and improve growth performance of ornamental and medicinal Dendrobium nobile under saline or other abiotic stress conditions.
Environment / TreatmentHormone and growth regulator treatments (GA3, thidiazuron/TDZ, abscisic acid/ABA, paclobutrazol/PP333), drought, CdCl2, NiCl2, and NaCl-induced salinity stress in D. nobile; NaCl (30–300 mM, including 100 and 180 mM) in hydroponic culture of Arabidopsis DnMSI1-overexpression lines.

CsVI2 (Cucumis sativus) Plant

Key Findings

  • CsVI2 encodes an acid-type vacuolar invertase with conserved β-fructofuranosidase motifs and specifically hydrolyzes sucrose into glucose and fructose in vitro and in vivo. [5]
  • CsVI2 is expressed in multiple cucumber seedling tissues, with highest levels in the root elongation region, and its transcript abundance and vacuolar invertase activity are significantly induced by drought stress in young roots and leaves. [5]
  • Overexpression of CsVI2 in cucumber seedlings enhances vacuolar invertase activity, decreases sucrose and increases hexose (glucose and fructose) contents in leaves, and confers increased drought tolerance compared with WT plants. [5]
  • Phylogenetic and motif analyses place CsVI2 among dicot vacuolar invertases and separate it from fructosyltransferases; structural modelling suggests that the putative invertase inhibitor CsINVINH3 can bind to the CsVI2 active site region and may inhibit its activity. [5]

Core Phenotypic Effect

CsVI2 functions as a vacuolar invertase whose overexpression increases vacuolar invertase activity, shifts leaf sugars from sucrose toward hexoses, and confers enhanced drought tolerance at the seedling stage. [5]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in sucrose metabolism/carbohydrate partitioning under abiotic (drought) stress by promoting sucrose hydrolysis in the vacuole and increasing hexose availability, in coordination with drought-induced changes in SUS (CsSUS3) and SPS (CsSPS1) activities. [5]

Biosynthetic: Sucrose hydrolysis in the vacuole, converting sucrose into glucose and fructose as part of sucrose/hexose metabolism under drought stress. [5]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainVacuolar invertase (acid β-fructofuranosidase) containing conserved β-fructosidase motif NDPD/NG, cysteine-containing catalytic site WECVD and RDP motif, with multiple predicted glycosylation sites.
Expression PatternCsVI2 is expressed in leaves, roots and stems of seedlings, with higher levels in young leaves and young roots; within young roots it is expressed in all regions but peaks in the elongation region. Under drought stress, CsVI2 transcript levels increase significantly in young roots from day 3 and in young leaves from day 6, paralleling increased vacuolar invertase activity.
Validation MethodFunctional validation by heterologous expression of recombinant CsVI2 protein in Pichia pastoris (in vitro sucrose hydrolysis assays), transient overexpression in Nicotiana benthamiana leaves (compartment-specific invertase activity), and generation of stable CsVI2-overexpressing cucumber lines (OE-5, OE-12, OE-16) tested for vacuolar invertase activity, sugar contents, and drought tolerance.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of CsVI2 enhances vacuolar invertase activity, increases hexose (glucose and fructose) accumulation, and improves drought tolerance in cucumber seedlings, suggesting CsVI2 as a candidate gene for developing drought-tolerant cucumber cultivars (e.g., via transgenic or gene-editing approaches) particularly targeting seedling-stage drought resilience.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by water interruption on cucumber seedlings (sampling at 0, 3, 6 and 9 days) and two-week drought treatment used to evaluate drought tolerance of CsVI2-overexpressing lines.

SbERD4 (Salicornia brachiata) Plant

Key Findings

  • SbERD4 from the extreme halophyte Salicornia brachiata encodes a CSC1-like membrane protein with 10 transmembrane domains and localizes to the plasma membrane. [8]
  • SbERD4 transcript in S. brachiata is strongly and rapidly induced by multiple abiotic stresses (salt, osmotic/drought, cold, and heat), with up to ~20-fold up-regulation. [8]
  • Constitutive overexpression of SbERD4 in tobacco significantly enhances tolerance to salinity, osmotic/drought, cold, and heat stresses, improving seed germination, growth, relative water content, membrane stability, and reducing electrolyte leakage. [8]
  • SbERD4 overexpression promotes accumulation of osmoprotectants (proline, soluble sugars, starch, polyphenols, free amino acids) and maintains favorable K+/Na+ ratio and lower Na+ accumulation under salt stress. [8]
  • SbERD4 overexpression reduces H2O2 accumulation and is associated with strong up-regulation of antioxidant enzyme genes (NtSOD, NtAPX, NtCAT, NtGR) under stress, indicating enhanced ROS-scavenging capacity. [8]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of SbERD4 enhances plant tolerance to multiple abiotic stresses (salinity, osmotic/drought, cold, heat) by improving growth, water status, membrane stability, osmoprotectant accumulation, ion homeostasis, and antioxidant defense, resulting in reduced oxidative and cellular damage under stress. [8]

Regulatory Mechanism

SbERD4 is a plasma membrane CSC1-like, Ca-dependent channel-like protein with 10 transmembrane domains; its overexpression is associated with maintenance of ionic balance (higher K+, lower Na+, lower Na+/K+ ratio), enhanced accumulation of osmoprotectants, and transcriptional up-regulation of antioxidant enzyme genes (NtSOD, NtAPX, NtCAT, NtGR), thereby reinforcing ROS-scavenging and cell homeostasis under abiotic stress. [8]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCSC1-like ERD4 protein (708 amino acids) with a predicted Ca-dependent channel-like transmembrane domain architecture comprising 10 transmembrane domains.
Expression PatternIn S. brachiata, SbERD4 is strongly induced by abiotic stresses: up to ~20-fold under 500 mM NaCl, ~15-fold under 300 mM mannitol (osmotic/drought), and ~10-fold under 40°C heat at 24 h; ~16-fold induction at 3 h under 4°C cold; expression declines by 48 h under all stresses.
Validation MethodTransgenic overexpression of SbERD4 in Nicotiana tabacum cv. Petit Havana followed by multi-stress (salt, mannitol, cold, heat) tolerance assays (germination, growth, RWC, MSI, EL, osmoprotectants, ion content, H2O2) and qRT-PCR analysis of antioxidant genes; subcellular localization by SbERD4:RFP fusion in onion epidermal cells.
Breeding Application ValueSbERD4, derived from the extreme halophyte Salicornia brachiata, confers robust tolerance to salinity, osmotic/drought, cold, and heat stresses when overexpressed in tobacco, improving growth, water status, ion homeostasis, and ROS detoxification. These features make SbERD4 a promising candidate gene for engineering multi-abiotic-stress tolerance in crop plants, particularly for cultivation on saline or drought-prone soils, thereby supporting stable yield and resilience under climate-change-associated stress conditions.
Environment / TreatmentSalinity stress (200 mM and 500 mM NaCl); osmotic/drought stress (300 mM mannitol); cold stress (4°C); heat stress (40°C); treatments applied to S. brachiata seedlings (up to 48 h) and to transgenic tobacco plants in hydroponic and soil conditions.

LchiWRKY5 (Liriodendron chinense) Plant

Key Findings

  • LchiWRKY5 is a Group IIb WRKY transcription factor with a conserved WRKY domain and C2H2 zinc-finger motif. [9]
  • RNA-seq shows strong, sustained induction by cold, heat, and drought, especially at 1 d and 3 d for temperature stresses and 12 h–1 d for drought. [9]
  • qRT-PCR confirms high induction under all three stresses, supporting its role as a multi-abiotic-stress-responsive WRKY. [9]
  • Promoter analysis reveals multiple stress-related cis-elements, consistent with involvement in abiotic stress signaling. [9]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Stress-inducible WRKY transcription factor associated with broad-spectrum response to cold, heat, and drought in L. chinense leaves. [9]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainWRKY transcription factor, Group IIb member with a single WRKY domain and C2H2-type zinc finger motif
Expression PatternIn leaves, strongly induced by cold, heat, and drought stress. Under cold stress, RNA-seq shows upregulation from 3 h to 3 d with high expression at 1 d and 3 d; under heat stress, expression is strongly upregulated at 1 d and 3 d; under drought stress, expression peaks around 12 h to 1 d. qRT-PCR confirms strong upregulation across time points under cold and notable induction under heat and drought.
Validation MethodTime-course leaf RNA-seq under cold, heat, and drought to profile expression; TPM-based expression clustering; qRT-PCR validation of expression dynamics under the three stresses; computational subcellular localization prediction.
Breeding Application ValueMulti-abiotic-stress-inducible WRKY transcription factor that can serve as a candidate gene to improve combined cold, heat, and drought tolerance in Liriodendron chinense, supporting better survival, growth, and afforestation in climatically diverse and stress-prone regions.
Environment / TreatmentCold stress at 4 °C, heat stress at 40 °C, and drought (osmotic) stress induced by 15% PEG6000, each applied to seedlings for 1 h, 3 h, 6 h, 12 h, 1 d, and 3 d.

LchiWRKY14 (Liriodendron chinense) Plant

Key Findings

  • LchiWRKY14 is a Group IId WRKY transcription factor generated by tandem duplication. [9]
  • It is strongly and persistently induced by cold, heat, and drought, with high expression at nearly all time points under cold and drought and at 1 d–3 d under heat. [9]
  • It is identified as a key WRKY gene for cold and heat responses and as a main response gene under drought stress. [9]
  • Promoter cis-element analysis indicates enrichment in defense, low-temperature, and drought-related elements, supporting its role in abiotic stress regulation. [9]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Multi-stress-responsive WRKY transcription factor with strong and sustained activation during cold, heat, and drought stress, particularly at later response time points. [9]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainWRKY transcription factor, Group IId member with a single WRKY domain and C2H2-type zinc finger motif
Expression PatternStrongly expressed under cold, heat, and drought stress. Under cold stress, LchiWRKY14 is highly expressed at almost all time points; under heat stress, it is significantly upregulated at 1 d and 3 d; under drought stress, it shows high expression at all time points.
Validation MethodStress time-course RNA-seq under cold, heat, and drought; TPM-based clustering and identification as highly induced gene; qRT-PCR validation in selected gene set; motif and promoter cis-element analysis.
Breeding Application ValueKey multi-stress-responsive WRKY transcription factor that could be utilized to breed or engineer L. chinense trees with enhanced tolerance to cold, heat, and drought, improving forest productivity and survival under adverse climates.
Environment / TreatmentCold 4 °C, heat 40 °C, and 15% PEG6000-induced drought applied to seedlings for 1 h–3 d.

LchiWRKY18 (Liriodendron chinense) Plant

Key Findings

  • LchiWRKY18 is a Group IIe WRKY transcription factor with a conserved WRKY domain and C2H2 zinc-finger motif. [9]
  • It is mainly induced by drought stress, with nearly 4-fold upregulation and peak expression around 12 h–1 d, and is also specifically induced by heat stress at 6–12 h and 1–3 d. [9]
  • It shows little induction under cold stress, indicating stress-type-specific responsiveness. [9]
  • Subcellular localization experiments confirm nuclear localization, supporting its role as a transcription factor regulating stress-responsive genes. [9]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Drought- and heat-inducible WRKY transcription factor that is mainly associated with drought stress response and also contributes to heat stress response in L. chinense. [9]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainWRKY transcription factor, Group IIe member with a single WRKY domain and C2H2-type zinc finger motif
Expression PatternRNA-seq shows that LchiWRKY18 is mainly responsive to drought and heat. Under drought stress, it is among the genes whose expression peaks at 12 h–1 d, with nearly 4-fold induction reported; under heat stress, it peaks at 6 h–12 h and shows specific high expression from 1 d to 3 d. It is weakly or not induced by cold in RNA-seq. qRT-PCR confirms an early transient peak at 1 h under drought and low expression under cold and heat within the validation subset.
Validation MethodRNA-seq time courses under cold, heat, and drought; TPM-based expression analysis; qRT-PCR validation including early drought response; experimental subcellular localization using pJIT166-LchiWRKY18-GFP in protoplasts and confocal microscopy.
Breeding Application ValuePromising candidate to enhance drought and heat stress tolerance in Liriodendron chinense through genetic improvement, enabling better adaptation and afforestation in drought-prone and high-temperature regions.
Environment / TreatmentHeat stress at 40 °C and drought stress induced by 15% PEG6000 (1 h–3 d time course), in comparison with cold stress at 4 °C where LchiWRKY18 shows little induction.

LchiWRKY23 (Liriodendron chinense) Plant

Key Findings

  • LchiWRKY23 is a Group I WRKY transcription factor with two WRKY domains. [9]
  • It is significantly induced by cold, heat, and drought, with distinct peak times (cold: 12 h–1 d; heat: 1–3 d; drought: 12 h–1 d). [9]
  • It is one of five WRKYs showing strong cross-regulation under all three abiotic stresses. [9]
  • Despite arising from tandem duplication with LchiWRKY35, it shows divergent expression patterns, suggesting functional differentiation. [9]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Group I WRKY transcription factor with strong time-dependent induction by cold, heat, and drought, acting as a cross-regulator in multi-abiotic stress responses. [9]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainWRKY transcription factor, Group I member with two WRKY domains and C2H2-type zinc finger motifs
Expression PatternRNA-seq shows strong induction by cold, heat, and drought. Under cold stress, LchiWRKY23 peaks at 12 h–1 d and 3 d; under heat stress, it is significantly upregulated at 1 d and 3 d; under drought stress, it is among genes peaking at 12 h–1 d. qRT-PCR confirms that LchiWRKY23 is upregulated under cold (12 h peak), heat (1 d peak), and drought (12 h and 1 d peaks).
Validation MethodRNA-seq under three abiotic stresses and TPM-based clustering; qRT-PCR time-course validation under cold, heat, and drought; analysis of tandem duplication and cis-elements with its paralogue LchiWRKY35.
Breeding Application ValueMulti-stress-responsive WRKY transcription factor that can be targeted to improve L. chinense tolerance to combined cold, heat, and drought stresses, facilitating stable growth and afforestation in variable environments.
Environment / TreatmentCold 4 °C, heat 40 °C, and 15% PEG6000-induced drought, each for 1 h–3 d.

LchiWRKY27 (Liriodendron chinense) Plant

Key Findings

  • LchiWRKY27 is a Group IId WRKY transcription factor produced by tandem duplication (with LchiWRKY14, 15, 20). [9]
  • It is strongly induced by cold, heat, and drought stress, with peaks at 1–3 d under temperature stresses and 12 h–1 d under drought. [9]
  • It shares similar high heat-induced expression patterns with its paralogue LchiWRKY14, suggesting partially redundant functions. [9]
  • Its expression and duplication history indicate an important role in the expanded Group IId-based stress response module in L. chinense. [9]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Group IId WRKY transcription factor produced by tandem duplication, strongly inducible by cold, heat, and drought, contributing to cross-regulation of multi-abiotic stress responses. [9]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainWRKY transcription factor, Group IId member with a single WRKY domain and C2H2-type zinc finger motif
Expression PatternRNA-seq indicates that LchiWRKY27 responds to cold, heat, and drought. It peaks at 1 d and 3 d under cold and heat; under drought, it belongs to the high-expression group between 6 h and 1 d. qRT-PCR shows strong induction at 1 d under heat and more limited induction under cold and drought in the validation subset.
Validation MethodChromosomal mapping and MCScanX analysis to identify tandem duplication; RNA-seq under cold, heat, and drought for expression profiling; qRT-PCR validation under each stress; motif and cis-element analysis to relate structure and regulation.
Breeding Application ValueTandem-duplicated, multi-stress-responsive WRKY transcription factor that can be exploited to improve L. chinense tolerance to cold, heat, and drought, aiding afforestation and yield stability under abiotic stress.
Environment / TreatmentCold stress (4 °C), heat stress (40 °C), and 15% PEG6000-induced drought stresses over 1 h–3 d.

LchiWRKY36 (Liriodendron chinense) Plant

Key Findings

  • LchiWRKY36 is a Group IId WRKY transcription factor strongly induced by cold, heat, and drought, with peaks around 1 d (cold and heat) and 12 h–1 d (drought). [9]
  • It is one of five WRKY genes that cross-regulate multiple abiotic stresses. [9]
  • Subcellular localization experiments confirm that LchiWRKY36 is expressed in the nucleus, consistent with its role as a transcription factor. [9]
  • Its stress-responsive expression and nuclear localization make LchiWRKY36 a core candidate regulator of multi-stress responses in L. chinense. [9]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Group IId WRKY transcription factor with strong multi-stress induction and confirmed nuclear localization, acting as a key cross-stress-responsive transcription factor in L. chinense. [9]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainWRKY transcription factor, Group IId member with a single WRKY domain and C2H2-type zinc finger motif
Expression PatternRNA-seq shows that LchiWRKY36 is induced by cold, heat, and drought. Under cold stress, it is upregulated and peaks at 1 d and 3 d; under heat stress, it shows strong induction at 1 d and 3 d; under drought stress, it belongs to the high-expression group peaking at 12 h–1 d, although its drought induction is lower compared with some other WRKYs. qRT-PCR confirms strong induction at 1 d under cold and heat and relatively low expression under drought.
Validation MethodRNA-seq and TPM-based clustering under cold, heat, and drought; qRT-PCR validation; cloning of LchiWRKY36 CDS into pJIT166-GFP; PEG-mediated protoplast transformation and fluorescence microscopy to verify nuclear localization.
Breeding Application ValueImportant multi-stress-responsive WRKY with experimentally confirmed nuclear localization, representing a strong candidate gene for engineering L. chinense trees with enhanced tolerance to combined cold, heat, and drought stresses.
Environment / TreatmentCold 4 °C, heat 40 °C, and 15% PEG6000-induced drought over a 1 h–3 d time course.

LchiWRKY33 (Liriodendron chinense) Plant

Key Findings

  • LchiWRKY33 is a Group I WRKY transcription factor. [9]
  • It is mainly responsive to cold stress, showing sustained upregulation from 3 h to 1 d and strong expression at 1–3 d. [9]
  • It is also induced by heat and drought, indicating broader involvement in temperature and water-deficit responses. [9]
  • Its expression kinetics suggest a role in sustained transcriptional regulation during prolonged cold and subsequent stress adaptation. [9]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Cold-responsive WRKY transcription factor that also participates in heat and drought stress responses, with sustained induction particularly under cold stress. [9]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainWRKY transcription factor, Group I member with two WRKY domains and C2H2-type zinc finger motifs
Expression PatternRNA-seq indicates that LchiWRKY33 is mainly responsive to cold stress, with continuous upregulation from 3 h to 1 d and strong expression at 1 d and 3 d. Under heat stress, it is also upregulated at 1 d and 3 d. Under drought stress, it contributes to the high-expression cluster peaking at 12 h–1 d. qRT-PCR shows induction under heat and early drought response (1 h peak) but downregulation under cold in the tested subset.
Validation MethodCold-, heat-, and drought-stress RNA-seq time courses and TPM-based clustering; inclusion in qRT-PCR panel validates stress responsiveness, especially under heat and drought.
Breeding Application ValueTemperature-stress-responsive WRKY transcription factor that can be used as a candidate to improve L. chinense tolerance to low and high temperature, which is important for forest establishment in regions with cold snaps and heat waves.
Environment / TreatmentCold (4 °C), heat (40 °C), and 15% PEG6000-induced drought stresses over 1 h–3 d.

LchiWRKY25 (Liriodendron chinense) Plant

Key Findings

  • LchiWRKY25 is a Group I WRKY transcription factor. [9]
  • It is mainly responsive to heat stress, showing strong induction and peak expression at 1–3 d in RNA-seq and a clear 12 h peak in qRT-PCR. [9]
  • It shows weaker or repressed expression under cold and drought stresses relative to heat, indicating stress-type-specific regulation. [9]
  • Its pronounced heat-specific induction pattern identifies LchiWRKY25 as a candidate regulator of heat stress responses in L. chinense. [9]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Heat-inducible WRKY transcription factor predominantly involved in the response of L. chinense to high-temperature stress. [9]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainWRKY transcription factor, Group I member with two WRKY domains and C2H2-type zinc finger motifs
Expression PatternRNA-seq shows that LchiWRKY25 responds to all three stresses but mainly to heat: it is strongly upregulated at 1 d and 3 d under heat stress; under cold stress, it shows some induction at later time points, while under drought stress it is mostly lowly expressed. qRT-PCR indicates a clear peak at 12 h under heat and low or downregulated expression under cold and drought.
Validation MethodRNA-seq time course under heat, cold, and drought to identify heat-specific induction; qRT-PCR validation showing strong heat-responsive expression; phylogenetic and motif analyses supporting assignment to Group I.
Breeding Application ValueHeat-responsive WRKY transcription factor that may be utilized to improve high-temperature tolerance of L. chinense, supporting tree growth and afforestation in warming and heat-stress-prone environments.
Environment / TreatmentHeat stress at 40 °C, with comparison to cold (4 °C) and 15% PEG6000-induced drought.

NtLTPI.38 (Nicotiana tabacum) Plant

Key Findings

  • NtLTPI.38 is a type I non-specific lipid transfer protein gene in Nicotiana tabacum whose expression is strongly induced by drought and other abiotic stresses and shows high expression in stems and leaves. [10]
  • Transgenic overexpression of NtLTPI.38 in tobacco (cv. K326) increases plant height and stem circumference under field conditions. [10]
  • Under mannitol-simulated drought stress, NtLTPI.38 overexpression lines exhibit enhanced drought tolerance, with maintained growth, increased lateral root number, and reduced wilting compared with wild type. [10]
  • NtLTPI.38 overexpression reduces accumulation of O2- and H2O2 and lowers MDA content under drought, indicating enhanced antioxidant defense as a mechanism for improved drought tolerance. [10]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Transgenic overexpression of NtLTPI.38 in tobacco increases drought tolerance by enhancing antioxidant defense (lower O2- and H2O2 accumulation) and increasing lateral root number, leading to reduced wilting under mannitol-simulated drought. [10]

Regulatory Mechanism

Overexpression of NtLTPI.38 enhances antioxidant defense under drought, as evidenced by reduced accumulation of superoxide (O2-) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), lower MDA content, and increased lateral root formation, thereby mitigating drought-induced oxidative damage. [10]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainNon-specific lipid transfer protein (nsLTP), type I; contains the conserved eight-cysteine motif (ECM) and two conserved pentapeptide domains T/S-X-X-D-R/K and P-Y-X-I-S characteristic of type I LTPs.
Expression PatternNtLTPI.38 is strongly induced by drought in RNA-seq and qRT-PCR analyses; its expression is also induced by salt and high-temperature stresses but shows little response to low temperature; it is induced by IAA and slightly by GA and is inhibited by MeJA; tissue profiling shows high expression in stems, leaves and flowers but low expression in roots.
Validation MethodStable transgenic overexpression in tobacco (Agrobacterium-mediated transformation), followed by phenotypic analysis under mannitol-induced drought stress, NBT staining for O2- accumulation, and biochemical measurements of MDA, O2- and H2O2 contents.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of NtLTPI.38 enhances drought tolerance and promotes growth in tobacco by improving antioxidant capacity and lateral root formation, indicating its potential utility as a candidate gene for breeding tobacco cultivars with improved drought resistance and vigor under water-limited conditions.
Environment / TreatmentMannitol-induced drought stress (100 and 200 mM D-mannitol) applied to seedlings on MS medium and to 50-day-old seedlings in a floating water culture system; comparison with normally watered controls under the same growth conditions.

NtbHLH86 (Nicotiana tabacum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • NtbHLH86, a tobacco bHLH transcription factor and NtMYC2 homologue, was selected from a genome-wide NtbHLH family analysis. [11]
  • NtbHLH86 transcript levels are induced by drought treatment in tobacco seedlings. [11]
  • Multiple independent NtbHLH86 overexpression lines show significantly higher NtbHLH86 expression confirmed by qRT-PCR. [11]
  • Under drought stress, NtbHLH86 overexpression plants display reduced wilting and a significantly higher survival ratio than wild-type plants, whereas no obvious growth difference is observed under normal watering. [11]
  • These results demonstrate that NtbHLH86 positively regulates drought stress tolerance in cultivated tobacco. [11]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of NtbHLH86 in tobacco increases resistance to drought stress, leading to less wilting and higher survival after prolonged water deficit, while showing no obvious growth differences from wild type under normal conditions. [11]

Pathways

Regulatory: Abiotic stress response pathway, acting as a positive regulator of drought stress tolerance in tobacco. [11]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainBasic helix–loop–helix (bHLH) transcription factor family member.
Expression PatternNtbHLH86 is among NtbHLH genes with relatively high expression across tested tissues in microarray data and its transcript level is further induced by drought stress in whole seedlings.
Validation MethodStable overexpression of NtbHLH86 in tobacco (transgenic lines generated via Agrobacterium-mediated transformation) combined with drought-stress assays and qRT-PCR analysis of NtbHLH86 induction by drought and transgene expression levels.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of NtbHLH86 significantly enhances drought tolerance in cultivated tobacco without penalizing growth under normal watering, suggesting NtbHLH86 is a promising candidate gene for breeding or engineering drought-tolerant tobacco cultivars to improve performance under water-limited conditions.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by carefully removing plants from pots, washing off surface soil, and air-drying whole seedlings on the bench, and by withholding water from potted plants for 14–20 days.

VvGH3-9 (Vitis vinifera) Plant

Key Findings

  • VvGH3-9 encodes a GH3 family protein with conserved GH3 domain(s) and an N-terminal leucine zipper motif and is localized to the nucleus and cytoplasm in plant cells. [12]
  • In grape, VvGH3-9 shows tissue- and stage-specific expression, with higher transcript levels in vigorous tissues (e.g., seed fruit set, seed post fruit set, latent buds) and responsiveness to ABA, MeJA, IAA, and abiotic stresses including NaCl, PEG, and cold. [12]
  • Overexpression of VvGH3-9 in Arabidopsis results in significantly increased VvGH3-9 transcript levels under 20% PEG and leads to higher IAA and ABA contents compared with WT after 72 h PEG treatment. [12]
  • Under PEG-induced drought stress, VvGH3-9 overexpression lines exhibit higher H2O2 and MDA contents and relative electrolyte conductivity, lower activities of POD, SOD, and CAT and reduced proline accumulation, and more severe leaf wilting than WT. [12]
  • The expression of six drought-responsive Arabidopsis genes (AtPOD, AtSOD, AtCAT, AtWRKY40, AtbZIP62, AtPYD1) is significantly lower in VvGH3-9 overexpression lines than in WT after 72 h of 20% PEG treatment. [12]
  • Collectively, these data demonstrate that VvGH3-9 functions as a negative regulator of drought tolerance, decreasing drought resistance in the Arabidopsis model and indicating a similar negative role in grape drought resistance. [12]

Core Phenotypic Effect

VvGH3-9 acts as a negative regulator of drought tolerance: its overexpression in Arabidopsis reduces tolerance to PEG-induced drought stress by disrupting hormone balance and antioxidant defenses, leading to greater oxidative and membrane damage. [12]

Regulatory Mechanism

Overexpression of VvGH3-9 in Arabidopsis under 20% PEG increases endogenous IAA and ABA levels, elevates H2O2 and MDA contents and relative electrolyte conductivity, reduces proline accumulation and POD, SOD, and CAT activities, and is associated with reduced expression of drought-responsive genes such as AtPOD, AtSOD, AtCAT, AtWRKY40, AtbZIP62, and AtPYD1, thereby weakening antioxidant defense and enhancing membrane damage under drought stress. [12]

Pathways

Regulatory: Auxin (IAA) and ABA-mediated drought/osmotic stress response pathway. [12]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGH3 family protein containing conserved GH3 domain(s) and an N-terminal leucine zipper motif (motif 5).
Expression PatternIn grape, VvGH3-9 shows relatively high expression in vigorous tissues such as seed fruit set, seed post fruit set, and latent buds, and is induced in roots and leaves by ABA (strongly in roots, ~13-fold) and slightly by MeJA, while being down-regulated in leaves under NaCl, PEG, and 5 °C treatments for 24 h. Its transcript level decreases in berry skin as fruits ripen.
Breeding Application ValueBecause VvGH3-9 overexpression decreases drought tolerance, VvGH3-9 functions as a negative regulator of drought resistance in grape and represents a potential target for genetic improvement; reducing its expression or activity (e.g., by gene editing or marker-assisted selection) could enhance drought tolerance in grape cultivars.
Environment / Treatment20% PEG 6000 treatment to impose drought/osmotic stress for up to 72 h in transgenic and WT Arabidopsis; additional assays of VvGH3-9 expression under NaCl (200 mmol L^-1), 10% PEG, 5 °C cold stress, and ABA, MeJA, and IAA treatments in grape tissues.

OsCyp2-P (Oryza sativa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of OsCyp2-P enhances tolerance to multiple abiotic stresses (salinity, drought, osmotic, heat, heavy metal, oxidative) by maintaining higher K+/Na+ ratios, reduced electrolyte leakage, lower MDA, and higher antioxidant enzyme activities, leading to better growth and yield (higher harvest index and percentage of filled grains) under stress; knockdown plants show the opposite, stress-sensitive phenotype. [13]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsCyp2-P encodes an active cyclophilin (PPIase) whose overexpression increases total PPIase activity in rice, correlating with improved stress tolerance. OsCyp2-P physically interacts with calmodulin (OsCaM1) in a Ca2+-dependent manner, suggesting regulation through a Ca2+-CaM module. Overexpression elevates activities of antioxidant enzymes (catalase, APX, SOD) and reduces MDA, indicating enhanced ROS scavenging and membrane protection. OE and KD alter expression of auxin-responsive Aux/IAA genes (OsIAA11, OsIAA16, OsIAA20, OsIAA9) and endogenous auxin levels, linking OsCyp2-P PPIase activity to modulation of auxin signaling and transport, likely via cis–trans isomerization of auxin-related proteins as supported by prior work on OsCYP2. [13]

Pathways

Regulatory: Ca2+-calmodulin signaling pathway; auxin signaling pathway (via Aux/IAA genes); ROS detoxification/antioxidant defense pathway underlying abiotic stress responses. [13]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCyclophilin; peptidyl-prolyl cis/trans isomerase (PPIase)
Expression PatternTranscript levels strongly induced (up to ~600–800-fold) in 14-day-old shoots of both IR64 and Pokkali under salinity (NaCl), osmotic (mannitol), high-temperature, and CdCl2 stress, with generally higher induction in tolerant Pokkali than sensitive IR64.
Validation MethodFunctional validation via overexpression and RNAi knockdown of OsCyp2-P in Oryza sativa cv IR64 followed by multi-stress tolerance assays (seed germination, leaf-disc, whole-plant salinity and drought tests), physiological and biochemical measurements (K+/Na+, chlorophyll, electrolyte leakage, MDA, antioxidant enzymes, photosynthesis), auxin quantification and auxin/NPA response assays for root development, and demonstration of Ca2+-dependent interaction with OsCaM1 by CaM gel overlay and BiFC in rice protoplasts.
Breeding Application ValueOsCyp2-P is a strong candidate gene for improving salinity and drought tolerance and maintaining yield in rice. Introgression or transgenic overexpression of the Pokkali-derived OsCyp2-P allele in high-yielding but stress-sensitive cultivars like IR64 increases stress resilience at both seedling and reproductive stages, sustaining higher harvest index and grain filling under stress without evident penalties under control conditions. Its role in integrating Ca2+-CaM signaling, ROS detoxification, ion homeostasis, and auxin-dependent root and seed traits suggests high utility for breeding climate-resilient, high-yielding rice lines.
Environment / TreatmentMultiple abiotic and hormonal treatments: salinity (200–250 mM NaCl), drought (5% PEG 6000 at seedling stage and 15-day water withdrawal at reproductive stage), osmotic stress (0.5 M mannitol), high temperature (42°C), heavy metal stress (10 μM CdCl2), oxidative stress (5 mM H2O2), auxin (IAA 0.1 μM) and auxin transport inhibitor NPA (0.1 μM).

SlHK2 (Solanum lycopersicon) Plant

Key Findings

  • SlHK2 is a tomato cytokinin receptor histidine kinase whose expression is suppressed by abiotic stress and whose knockdown via RNAi (SlHK2 RNAi lines L1 and L2) significantly reduces SlHK2 transcript levels. [14]
  • SlHK2 RNAi plants show enhanced tolerance to drought, heat, and combined drought + heat stresses, with less wilting, higher relative water content, and lower electrolyte leakage than wild type. [14]
  • Under stress, SlHK2 RNAi plants maintain higher photosynthetic rate, stomatal conductance, PSII efficiency (Fq'/Fm', ETR, qL, Fv/Fm), and higher expression of PSII oxygen-evolving complex genes PsbP and PsbQ compared with wild type. [14]
  • SlHK2 RNAi plants accumulate less MDA and H2O2 and display weaker DAB staining under stress, concomitant with higher activities and transcript levels of antioxidant enzymes (GR, APX, SOD, POD, DHAR, CAT). [14]
  • SlHK2 RNAi plants accumulate more osmoprotectants (proline, glycine betaine, trehalose, soluble sugars) and show up-regulation of osmoprotectant synthesis genes (SlSPS, SlP5CS, SlT6PS) under drought, heat, and combined stresses. [14]
  • Cuticular wax biosynthesis genes (SlCER3, SlLTP1, SlTTS1, SlKCS1) are up-regulated and total leaf cuticular wax loads are higher in SlHK2 RNAi plants, even under control conditions, correlating with reduced electrolyte leakage. [14]
  • Stress- and ABA-related genes SIDREB1 and SINCED, as well as heat shock genes SlHSP17.4 and SlHSP21, are more strongly induced in SlHK2 RNAi plants than in wild type under relevant stress conditions. [14]
  • Overall, SlHK2 functions as a negative regulator of drought, heat, and combined stress tolerance in tomato, and its down-regulation improves multi-stress resilience. [14]

Core Phenotypic Effect

RNAi-mediated knockdown of the cytokinin receptor SlHK2 acts as a loss-of-function that increases tomato tolerance to drought, heat, and combined drought + heat stresses, characterized by reduced leaf wilting, higher relative water content, lower electrolyte leakage and oxidative damage, better maintenance of photosynthetic performance, stronger antioxidant defense, greater osmoprotectant accumulation, and increased cuticular wax deposition compared with wild type. [14]

Regulatory Mechanism

SlHK2 encodes a cytokinin receptor histidine kinase that functions as a negative regulator of drought, heat, and combined stress tolerance. Its down-regulation suppresses cytokinin signaling, likely increases ABA sensitivity, and is associated with up-regulation of drought-responsive (SIDREB1, SINCED), heat shock, antioxidant, osmoprotectant synthesis, and cuticular wax biosynthesis genes, collectively enhancing water retention, membrane stability, ROS scavenging, osmotic adjustment, and cuticular barrier formation under stress. [14]

Pathways

Regulatory: Cytokinin signaling pathway in tomato, where SlHK2 (a cytokinin receptor) mediates cytokinin responses and negatively modulates abiotic stress adaptation, interacting with ABA-related drought signaling, antioxidant defense pathways, osmoprotectant synthesis pathways, and cuticular wax biosynthesis under drought, heat, and combined stresses. [14]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainHistidine kinase-type cytokinin receptor (tomato histidine kinase 2)
Expression PatternSlHK2 expression is reduced in SlHK2 RNAi lines L1 and L2 relative to wild type, and abiotic stress treatments (drought, heat, combined) suppress SlHK2 expression; a GUS reporter driven by the 35S promoter in the SlHK2 RNAi construct is active in multiple tissues (leaf, fruit, sepal), with activity patterns depending on developmental stage.
Validation MethodStable transgenic RNAi knockdown lines (SlHK2 RNAi, lines L1 and L2) generated in tomato cv. Micro-Tom using a 35S-driven SlHK2 RNAi construct were phenotypically and physiologically analyzed under controlled drought, heat, and combined stress treatments, together with qRT-PCR confirmation of SlHK2 silencing and downstream gene-expression changes.
Breeding Application ValueDown-regulation of the cytokinin receptor gene SlHK2 via RNAi enhances tolerance of tomato to drought, heat, and combined drought + heat stresses by improving water status, maintaining membrane integrity, protecting photosynthetic machinery, increasing antioxidant enzyme capacity, promoting osmoprotectant accumulation, and elevating cuticular wax loads. These results suggest that SlHK2 is a promising negative regulator target for breeding or engineering tomato and possibly other crops with improved resilience to increasingly frequent combined abiotic stresses under climate change.
Environment / TreatmentFour treatments applied to 35-day-old plants for 5 consecutive days: (1) well-watered control, 26/20 °C day/night with daily watering; (2) drought stress, 26/20 °C day/night with no watering; (3) heat stress, 32/26 °C day/night with daily watering; and (4) combined drought + heat stress, 32/26 °C day/night with no watering.

TCP13 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Key positive regulator of dehydration stress tolerance that modulates leaf and root growth: overexpression causes narrow, downward-rolled leaves, reduced leaf and primary root growth, ABA-hypersensitive root growth inhibition, hypersensitivity of leaf growth to osmotic stress, and enhanced survival with reduced water loss under severe dehydration; loss-of-function (tcp13) leads to ABA-insensitive root growth and reduced induction of dehydration- and ABA-inducible genes. [15]

Regulatory Mechanism

Nuclear CIN-like TCP transcription factor whose dehydration-inducible expression is associated with ABRE motifs in its promoter. By binding TCP motifs in target promoters, it acts mainly as a transcriptional activator: SRDX-fused TCP13 represses genes containing TCP binding motifs, and many targets also contain ABREs. TCP13 is required for full activation of ABA-regulated genes (AHG3, Gols2, RD20, NCED3) in response to ABA/dehydration and for auxin-regulated genes (IAA5, LBD1, IAA19, PIN4) in response to IAA/dehydration, thereby modulating leaf and root growth under stress. [15]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in ABA- and dehydration-responsive transcriptional networks and auxin signaling under dehydration and osmotic stress. Acts downstream of ABA signaling to promote inhibition of primary root growth and positively regulates dehydration- and ABA-inducible genes (e.g., AHG3, RD20, NCED3, Gols2) and auxin-responsive genes (e.g., IAA5, IAA19, LBD1, PIN4), integrating ABA and auxin pathways in the dehydration stress response. [15]

Biosynthetic: Regulates ABA and auxin signaling pathways and downstream genes involved in raffinose, proline, ABA metabolism, jasmonic acid metabolism, and flavonoid biosynthesis. [15]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCIN-like class II TCP transcription factor of the TEOSINTE BRANCHED1/CYCLOIDEA/PCF (TCP) family, containing a conserved noncanonical bHLH domain.
Expression PatternStrongly expressed in leaves (cotyledons and true leaves), especially in expanding cells at the upper region of young leaves; low expression in roots. Induced early and strongly by dehydration stress and slightly by ABA and high salinity. Public expression data indicate expression in leaf mesophyll cells and in root endodermis and cortex under salt stress.
Breeding Application ValueTCP13 is a dehydration-inducible regulator whose overexpression restricts leaf expansion, promotes leaf rolling, and inhibits root growth while enhancing dehydration tolerance and reducing water loss, suggesting that targeted modulation of TCP13 activity could be used to improve drought/dehydration tolerance through controlled adjustment of shoot and root growth. However, strong constitutive activation incurs growth penalties, so practical applications would likely require stress- or tissue-specific tuning of TCP13 expression in crops.
Environment / TreatmentDehydration stress (leaf desiccation on parafilm and withholding water from soil-grown plants), osmotic stress with 25 mM mannitol, ABA treatment (5–100 μM), and salt stress with 175 mM NaCl.

OsSGT1 (Oryza sativa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsSGT1 expression is induced by drought stress and further enhanced by melatonin in rice seedlings. [16]
  • CRISPR knockout of OsSGT1 (sgt1 mutants) abolishes melatonin-mediated improvements in growth, survival, water status, stomatal conductance, photosynthesis, and osmotic adjustment under severe drought. [16]
  • OsSGT1 is required for melatonin-induced suppression of ROS and oxidative damage and for restoration of antioxidant enzyme activities and ASA/GSH levels under drought. [16]
  • OsSGT1 mutation prevents melatonin-dependent restoration of SA and JA contents and associated signaling gene expression and strongly reduces OsABI5 expression under drought. [16]
  • Double mutants of OsSGT1 and OsABI5 completely lose melatonin-induced activation of antioxidant genes (OsSODA1, OsCAT2, OsAPX4, OsGR2), supporting a model in which melatonin promotes SGT1-involved signals to activate ABI5 and antioxidant defenses against drought. [16]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Loss of OsSGT1 abolishes the beneficial effects of melatonin on drought-stressed rice seedlings, leading to reduced growth and survival, impaired stomatal conductance and water status, sustained osmolyte accumulation, elevated ROS and oxidative damage, weakened antioxidant enzyme activities, and failure to restore ABA, SA, and JA levels under drought. [16]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsSGT1 acts upstream of OsABI5 to positively regulate its expression and the expression of antioxidant-responsive genes under drought, mediating melatonin-induced activation of the antioxidant system in an ABA-independent but SA/JA-associated manner. [16]

Pathways

Regulatory: Melatonin–SGT1–ABI5–antioxidant signaling pathway under drought, involving crosstalk with ABA (ABA accumulation and ABI5 induction), and SA/JA pathways that modulate SGT1 expression and downstream antioxidant responses. [16]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSGT1 cochaperone protein
Expression PatternOsSGT1 transcript levels in leaves are induced by drought stress and further increased by melatonin treatment; OsSGT1 expression is higher under drought than under control in WT, abi5, and sgt1-1abi5 backgrounds, whereas exogenous ABA feeding under non-stress does not significantly alter OsSGT1 expression.
Validation MethodCRISPR/Cas9 knockout of OsSGT1 (sgt1-1, sgt1-2) targeting the first exon, generation of OsSGT1–OsABI5 double mutants, followed by drought and melatonin treatments and assessment of growth, survival, stomatal behavior, osmolytes, ROS, antioxidant enzyme activities, phytohormones, and expression of OsABI5 and antioxidant genes by qRT-PCR.
Breeding Application ValueOsSGT1 is a key positive regulator required for melatonin-mediated drought tolerance in rice. Enhancing OsSGT1 function, together with foliar melatonin application, improves seedling survival, photosynthesis, antioxidant capacity, and hormone balance (ABA, SA, JA) under severe drought, making OsSGT1 a promising target for developing drought-resilient rice cultivars and for melatonin-based agronomic management.
Environment / TreatmentSevere drought stress imposed as 25% field capacity; foliar spraying with 100 µM melatonin every two days during drought; exogenous 10 µM ABA treatments under 100% or 25% field capacity.

OsABI5 (Oryza sativa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsABI5 transcripts are strongly induced by drought and further upregulated by melatonin and ABA in WT rice seedlings. [16]
  • In sgt1 mutants and double mutants, OsABI5 expression is not properly induced by drought, melatonin, or ABA, indicating that OsABI5 acts downstream of OsSGT1 in this context. [16]
  • abi5 and sgt1-1abi5 mutants show reduced growth and survival and elevated ROS accumulation under drought, and melatonin fails to confer protection, demonstrating that OsABI5 is required for melatonin-mediated drought tolerance. [16]
  • Mutation of OsABI5 (alone or with OsSGT1) abolishes melatonin-induced activation of antioxidant genes (OsSODA1, OsCAT2, OsAPX4, OsGR2) and the associated increases in antioxidant enzyme activities. [16]
  • The data support a model in which melatonin promotes OsSGT1-dependent induction of OsABI5, which in turn activates antioxidant defenses to restore redox homeostasis under drought. [16]

Core Phenotypic Effect

OsABI5 is required for melatonin-mediated alleviation of drought stress in rice; abi5 and sgt1-1abi5 mutants exhibit weak growth, low survival, high ROS accumulation, and impaired antioxidant systems under drought, and they fail to benefit from melatonin or respond normally to ABA accumulation and feeding. [16]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsABI5 acts downstream of OsSGT1 and ABA to activate antioxidant genes under drought; melatonin promotes OsABI5 expression largely through OsSGT1 in an ABA-independent manner, thereby enhancing antioxidant capacity and ROS scavenging. [16]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA–ABI5 signaling pathway and melatonin–SGT1–ABI5–antioxidant pathway under drought, integrating ABA accumulation with melatonin-induced modulation of ABA, SA, and JA to regulate antioxidant gene expression. [16]

More Details
Expression PatternIn WT, OsABI5 expression in leaves is upregulated by drought stress and further induced by melatonin under drought; exogenous ABA feeding also strongly induces OsABI5 expression. In sgt1 mutants and sgt1-1abi5 double mutants, OsABI5 expression is markedly reduced or shows no induction under drought, melatonin, or ABA treatments.
Validation MethodUse of abi5-1 and abi5-2 loss-of-function mutants (previously generated) and OsSGT1–OsABI5 double mutants, combined with drought and melatonin treatments, ABA feeding, and measurements of growth, survival, ABA content, ROS, antioxidant enzyme activities, and expression of OsABI5 and antioxidant genes by qRT-PCR.
Breeding Application ValueOsABI5 is a central positive regulator of drought tolerance downstream of ABA and OsSGT1, required for melatonin-induced activation of antioxidant defenses. Manipulating OsABI5 activity, together with exogenous melatonin application, could improve drought tolerance, although its classical role in ABA-mediated growth restraint suggests that fine-tuning its expression or activity will be critical for breeding drought-resilient yet productive rice cultivars.
Environment / TreatmentSevere drought stress (25% field capacity), foliar 100 µM melatonin treatment, and 10 µM ABA feeding under well-watered and drought conditions.

OsBIERF3 (Oryza sativa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsBIERF3 is an ERF transcription factor from rice that acts as a transcriptional activator, with its C-terminal region required for transactivation activity in yeast. [17]
  • OsBIERF3 expression is induced by Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae infection, defense-related hormones (ACC, SA, MeJA, ABA), and abiotic stresses (drought, salt, cold), with stronger induction during incompatible X. oryzae pv. oryzae interactions and under cold stress. [17]
  • Overexpression of OsBIERF3 in rice enhances resistance to Magnaporthe oryzae and Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae, whereas RNAi-mediated suppression reduces resistance, indicating that OsBIERF3 is a positive regulator of rice immunity. [17]
  • Transcriptome and qRT-PCR analyses show that OsBIERF3 up-regulates numerous defense-related genes, including PR genes and MAPK cascade components (OsMPK3, OsMPK6, OsMEK3), and cell wall biosynthetic genes; OsBIERF3-OE plants have thicker cell walls and OsBIERF3-Ri plants thinner walls, implicating OsBIERF3 in cell wall biosynthesis. [17]
  • OsBIERF3 negatively regulates cold tolerance: OsBIERF3-OE plants are more sensitive and OsBIERF3-Ri plants more tolerant to cold, accompanied by reduced proline accumulation and down-regulation of cold-responsive genes in OE plants and the opposite trends in Ri plants, while drought and salt tolerance are unaffected. [17]
  • ABA and ethylene signaling are required for OsBIERF3-mediated cold response, as ACC and ABA treatments rescue the cold-sensitive phenotype of OsBIERF3-OE plants, and AgNO3 treatment diminishes the enhanced cold tolerance of OsBIERF3-Ri plants. [17]
  • Manipulating OsBIERF3 expression does not impair major agronomic traits and may improve some yield-related traits, suggesting its potential utility for breeding disease-resistant rice in environments where cold stress is not limiting. [17]

Core Phenotypic Effect

OsBIERF3 is a transcriptional activator that positively regulates resistance to Magnaporthe oryzae and Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae and negatively regulates cold tolerance, while having no detectable effect on drought and salt tolerance; it also enhances cell wall thickness in rice. [17]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsBIERF3 functions as a transcriptional activator whose C-terminal region is required for transactivation in yeast. Overexpression of OsBIERF3 up-regulates numerous defense-related genes, including PR proteins and MAPK cascade components, and cell wall biosynthetic genes, while its suppression down-regulates these genes. OsBIERF3 also modulates the expression of cold-responsive genes and alters proline accumulation under cold stress, likely by activating upstream negative regulators of cold response. Its function in cold tolerance depends on intact ABA and ethylene signaling, as ACC and ABA pretreatments restore, and AgNO3 pretreatment diminishes, cold tolerance phenotypes associated with altered OsBIERF3 expression. [17]

Pathways

Regulatory: OsBIERF3 participates in rice immune signaling by transcriptionally activating components of MAPK cascades (OsMPK3, OsMPK6, OsMEK3) and defense-related genes, and contributes to cell wall biosynthesis pathways by up-regulating cellulose synthase and glucan endo-1,3-beta-glucosidase genes. In abiotic stress response, OsBIERF3 functions within ABA- and ethylene-dependent cold stress signaling pathways, where its activity leads to repression of cold-responsive genes and reduced cold tolerance. [17]

Biosynthetic: Cell wall biosynthesis, including cellulose and β-1,3-glucan synthesis. [17]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainERF (AP2/ERF) transcription factor, member of group IX of the rice ERF subfamily, containing an AP2 DNA-binding domain.
Expression PatternOsBIERF3 transcript levels are induced by Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae infection, with stronger and earlier induction in the incompatible BB10–PXO86 interaction than in the compatible IR24–PXO86 interaction. OsBIERF3 expression is also induced by the defense-related hormones ACC (ethylene precursor), salicylic acid, methyl jasmonate and abscisic acid, and by abiotic stresses including rapid dehydration, high salinity (150 mM NaCl) and cold (4°C), with cold treatment causing up to ~11-fold induction at 24 h.
Validation MethodStable overexpression and RNAi-mediated suppression of OsBIERF3 in rice (cv. Xiushui 11) generated OsBIERF3-OE and OsBIERF3-Ri lines; disease resistance was assayed by M. oryzae spray inoculation and X. oryzae pv. oryzae leaf-clipping inoculation; cold tolerance was evaluated in growth chambers at 4°C with or without pretreatment by ACC, ABA or AgNO3; drought and salt tolerance assays were performed; defense-, MAPK cascade-, cell wall- and cold-responsive gene expression was analyzed by qRT-PCR and microarray; cell wall thickness was examined by TEM; transcriptional activation activity was tested in yeast using GAL4 DNA-binding domain fusions.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of OsBIERF3 in rice enhances resistance to the major diseases blast (Magnaporthe oryzae) and bacterial leaf blight (Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae) without detectable penalty on key agronomic traits (grain yield, panicle weight, grain number) or on drought and salt tolerance, although it reduces cold tolerance. The authors propose OsBIERF3 as a promising target to develop disease-resistant rice varieties for regions where cold stress is not a major constraint.
Environment / TreatmentBiotic treatments: inoculation with Magnaporthe oryzae race ZE3 strain 97-220 and Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae strain PXO86. Hormone treatments: foliar spraying with ACC (ethylene precursor), salicylic acid, methyl jasmonate and abscisic acid. Abiotic treatments: drought (withholding water or leaf dehydration), salt stress (150 mM NaCl), and cold stress at 4°C, including cold assays combined with ACC, ABA or AgNO3 (ethylene action inhibitor) pretreatments.

ItfWRKY70 (Ipomoea trifida) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of ItfWRKY70 in sweet potato markedly enhances drought tolerance by improving plant growth and biomass under PEG-induced and soil-based drought, increasing ABA and proline accumulation, elevating SOD and POD activities, reducing H2O2 and MDA levels, and decreasing stomatal aperture and water loss under drought stress [18]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a nuclear WRKY transcription factor whose C-terminal region functions as a transcriptional activation domain in yeast; overexpression elevates transcription of ABA biosynthesis genes (NCED, AAO/AAO3), osmolyte biosynthesis and LEA genes (P5CS, LEA/LEA5), ROS-scavenging genes (SOD, POD, CAT), and stomatal-movement regulators (SLAC1, OST1/SnRK2.6) in transgenic sweet potato under drought stress [18]

Pathways

Regulatory: Participates in ABA-dependent drought stress responses by promoting ABA biosynthesis and ABA-responsive gene expression, activates ROS-scavenging pathways, and modulates stomatal movement pathways involved in water-loss control [18]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainWRKY transcription factor, group III WRKY containing a conserved WRKYGQK domain and a C2HC-type zinc-finger motif
Expression PatternIn Ipomoea trifida, ItfWRKY70 is most highly expressed in leaves compared with stems and roots, and its transcript level is strongly induced by 20% PEG6000 and 100 μM ABA, peaking at 0.5 h (~2.9-fold) after PEG and 3 h (~3.5-fold) after ABA treatment
Validation MethodTransgenic overexpression of ItfWRKY70 in sweet potato followed by PEG-induced in vitro and soil-based drought tolerance assays with physiological measurements (ABA, proline, ROS, antioxidant enzyme activities, MDA); stomatal aperture and water-loss assays; qRT-PCR of stress-responsive genes; subcellular localization and yeast transcriptional activation assays confirming its function as a nuclear transcriptional activator
Breeding Application ValueItfWRKY70, derived from the drought-resistant wild diploid I. trifida, is a promising genetic resource for engineering or breeding drought-tolerant sweet potato cultivars, as its overexpression enhances drought resistance without obvious growth penalties under normal conditions and improves ABA accumulation, ROS detoxification and water-use efficiency; it may also be useful for improving drought tolerance in other crops via transgenic approaches.
Environment / Treatment20% PEG6000 applied to I. trifida and to in vitro sweet potato plants to mimic drought; 100 μM ABA treatment of I. trifida plants; soil-based drought stress imposed by withholding water from potted/transplanting-box sweet potato plants for 4–6 weeks; detached-leaf dehydration at 37 °C to assay water loss.

SiMYB19 (Setaria italica) Plant

Key Findings

  • SiMYB19 (Seita.1G250600.1) is an R2R3-MYB transcription factor from foxtail millet, located on chromosome 1, with three exons and two introns and two SANT conserved domains. [19]
  • SiMYB19 is predominantly expressed in roots and is strongly induced by salt (NaCl), osmotic stress (PEG), low nitrogen, and ABA, and weakly by IAA. [19]
  • SiMYB19 protein localizes to the nucleus and functions as a transcriptional activator in yeast, consistent with a role as a transcription factor. [19]
  • Heterologous overexpression of SiMYB19 in rice (OE-6, OE-8, OE-9) significantly enhances seedling salt tolerance in the greenhouse, with higher survival, growth, and lower MDA under 100 mM NaCl. [19]
  • In field and salt-pond trials, SiMYB19-overexpressing line OE-6 shows greater plant height, grain yield per plant, and overall salt tolerance under 0.3% and 0.5% NaCl than WT. [19]
  • SiMYB19 overexpression increases ABA accumulation and ABA sensitivity in rice; ABA-related phenotypes are abolished by tungstate, indicating involvement of ABA biosynthesis. [19]
  • Expression of ABA biosynthesis gene OsNCED3, ABA signaling genes OsPK1 and OsABF2, and stress-related gene OsLEA7 is upregulated in SiMYB19 transgenic rice under control and salt conditions. [19]
  • OE-6 exhibits improved drought tolerance in soil-drought experiments, suggesting that SiMYB19 mediates broader abiotic stress tolerance through an ABA-dependent pathway. [19]

Core Phenotypic Effect
