SiMYB19 overexpression in rice enhances tolerance to high salinity at germination and seedling stages, reduces oxidative damage, and increases plant height and grain yield of field-grown rice under saline conditions. [19]

Regulatory Mechanism

Nuclear R2R3-MYB transcriptional activator that promotes ABA accumulation and ABA-dependent stress responses by increasing expression of ABA biosynthesis gene OsNCED3 and ABA signaling components OsPK1 and OsABF2, leading to upregulation of downstream stress-protective gene OsLEA7. [19]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA-dependent stress signaling pathway regulating salt and drought tolerance via increased ABA biosynthesis (NCED3) and ABA signal transduction (PK1, ABF2), leading to induction of stress-protective genes (e.g., LEA). [19]

Biosynthetic: Regulation of ABA biosynthesis (via upregulation of OsNCED3) under salt stress [19]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainR2R3-MYB transcription factor containing two SANT conserved domains
Expression PatternIn foxtail millet, SiMYB19 is mainly expressed in roots and is strongly induced by NaCl, osmotic stress (10% PEG6000), low nitrogen (0.2 mM NO3-), and ABA, and weakly induced by IAA; in transgenic rice, SiMYB19 is highly expressed in OE lines (OE-6, OE-8, OE-9) and remains inducible by salt.
Validation MethodFunctional validation by Agrobacterium-mediated overexpression in rice (Kitaake) and assessment of salt tolerance in greenhouse, field, and salt ponds; germination assays under graded NaCl concentrations; ABA sensitivity and ABA content measurements with and without tungstate; soil drought-tolerance assay; subcellular localization via GFP fusion in foxtail millet protoplasts; transcription activation assay in yeast; qRT-PCR analysis of ABA-pathway and stress-related genes.
Breeding Application ValueSiMYB19 provides a practical candidate gene for improving salt tolerance and yield in cereal crops cultivated on saline-alkali soils. Its overexpression in rice confers robust salt tolerance from germination through field growth and increases grain yield under 0.3% NaCl, demonstrating stable performance outside controlled environments. Integration of SiMYB19 into breeding programs could generate salt-tolerant, high-yielding germplasm suitable for saline-alkali land utilization, thereby helping to expand arable land and support food security; multi-year field trials are suggested to validate long-term agronomic stability.
Environment / TreatmentSalt stress (100 mM NaCl in hydroponics; 0.3% and 0.5% NaCl in field and 0.3% NaCl in salt ponds; 100–250 mM NaCl during seed germination), osmotic stress (10% PEG6000), low nitrogen (0.2 mM NO3-), exogenous ABA (100 μM), auxin (100 μM IAA), ABA synthesis inhibitor tungstate (5 mM), and soil drought (15 d water withholding followed by rewatering).

TaVAMP727 (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of TaVAMP727 enhances tolerance to cold, drought and salt stresses and promotes earlier heading in wheat, and improves germination and stress tolerance in transgenic Arabidopsis by altering the expression of multiple ATG genes. [20]

Regulatory Mechanism

Overexpression of TaVAMP727 is proposed to promote SNARE-mediated fusion of autophagosomes with the tonoplast in the late stage of autophagy, accelerating intracellular material and energy recycling; the resulting enhancement of autophagic flux alters the expression levels of multiple ATGs to maintain autophagic homeostasis, especially under cold, drought and salt stresses. [20]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts in the autophagy pathway downstream of ATG complexes, likely facilitating SNARE-mediated autophagosome–vacuole fusion and thereby contributing to abiotic stress responses and developmental regulation such as heading. [20]

Biosynthetic: Autophagy pathway, particularly SNARE-mediated fusion of autophagosomes with the tonoplast/vacuole during vesicular trafficking. [20]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainVesicle-associated membrane protein 727 (VAMP727), a seed plant-specific R-SNARE involved in vacuolar transport, plant growth and seed maturation.
Expression PatternIn non-transgenic wheat (Fielder), TaVAMP727 expression remained stable under normal, cold, drought and salt stress conditions. In TaVAMP727-overexpressing wheat, TaVAMP727 transcripts were strongly elevated under normal conditions and significantly down-regulated under cold, drought and salt stresses, but still remained much higher than in Fielder. In TaVAMP727-overexpressing Arabidopsis, TaVAMP727 expression was significantly up-regulated under salt stress and down-regulated under drought (D-mannitol) stress compared with control conditions.
Validation MethodConstitutive overexpression in transgenic Arabidopsis thaliana (Col-0) and wheat (cv. Fielder) using Agrobacterium-mediated transformation, followed by stress-germination assays, seedling drought survival assays, heading date measurement, and RT-qPCR analysis of TaVAMP727 and ATG gene expression under control and abiotic stress conditions.
Breeding Application ValueTaVAMP727 is a promising transgenic and possibly marker-assisted breeding target to develop wheat cultivars with enhanced tolerance to cold, drought and salt stresses and earlier heading, enabling stable yield and potential expansion of wheat cultivation into colder, drier and saltier environments.
Environment / TreatmentCold stress (4 °C for 12 h), drought stress (air-dry for 6 h in wheat; 200–415 mM D-mannitol in Arabidopsis media; soil water withholding until wilting followed by rewatering), and salt stress (150 mM NaCl for 24 h in wheat; 120–180 mM NaCl in Arabidopsis germination and expression assays).

Ta2A_ATG8a (Triticum turgidum) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Natural variation in cis-acting element–changing SNPs in the promoter of Ta2A_ATG8a defines three haplotypes whose distribution in tetraploid wheat populations is associated with differences in seed size: Hap-TAA confers the highest thousand kernel weight and grain width but shortest grain length, Hap-CGA is intermediate, and Hap-CGC is associated with lower thousand kernel weight. [20]

Regulatory Mechanism

Promoter SNPs alter the presence of cis-elements such as GAGAC (S-deficiency-responsive), TTATTT (linked to glutamine synthetase gene regulation important for seed germination and yield structure), and CAAT and other motifs, suggesting that selection on these cis-elements modulates Ta2A_ATG8a regulation and thereby contributes to differences in grain size between wild emmer and durum. [20]

Pathways

Regulatory: Autophagy-related processes influencing nutrient remobilization and stress responses, with promoter variation linked to seed development and grain size during tetraploid wheat domestication. [20]

Biosynthetic: Autophagy pathway, with subfamily-level evidence for involvement in nitrogen starvation and abiotic stress responses. [20]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainATG8 family protein (autophagy-related protein 8 subfamily).
Validation MethodHaplotype-based association analysis of promoter SNPs in Ta2A_ATG8a using resequencing-derived SNP data, definition of three promoter haplotypes, cis-acting element prediction, and statistical association of haplotypes with grain traits (TKW, grain width, grain length) in wild emmer and durum tetraploid wheat populations.
Breeding Application ValueTa2A_ATG8a promoter haplotypes, particularly Hap-TAA, provide useful molecular markers for selecting tetraploid wheat lines with higher thousand kernel weight and favorable grain width, and the seed size–related cis-elements GAGAC and TTATTT in its promoter are prime candidates for marker-assisted selection and functional improvement during domestication and breeding.

MeGRXC3 (Manihot esculenta) Plant

Key Findings

  • MeGRXC3 is a cassava CC-type glutaredoxin with autonomous transcriptional activation activity in yeast. [21]
  • Overexpression of MeGRXC3 in Arabidopsis (Col-0) causes hypersensitivity to mannitol-induced osmotic stress, strongly inhibiting seed germination and seedling growth. [21]
  • MeGRXC3 overexpression markedly up-regulates multiple stress-related transcription factor genes (PDF1.2, ERF1, ERF6, ORA59, DREB2A, WRKY33, WRKY40, WRKY53, GA2OX6) in transgenic Arabidopsis under non-stress conditions. [21]
  • MeGRXC3 physically interacts with Arabidopsis TGA2 and TGA5 in the nucleus (yeast two-hybrid and BiFC), and nuclear localization of MeGRXC3 is required and sufficient for conferring mannitol hypersensitivity. [21]
  • Conserved motifs of MeGRXC3 (CCMC redox motif, GSH-binding motif, C-terminal L**LL and ALWL motifs) are essential for its transcriptional activation in yeast and for its nuclear function in regulating mannitol-induced osmotic stress and downstream gene expression in Arabidopsis. [21]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of cassava MeGRXC3 in Arabidopsis negatively regulates tolerance to mannitol-induced osmotic stress by enhancing inhibition of seed germination and seedling growth in a manner dependent on its nuclear activity and conserved redox/GSH-binding and C-terminal motifs. [21]

Regulatory Mechanism

MeGRXC3 is a nuclear CC-type glutaredoxin whose redox (CCMC) and GSH-binding motifs, together with C-terminal L**LL and ALWL motifs, are required for autonomous transcriptional activation in yeast and for modulation of target gene expression in planta. MeGRXC3 interacts with bZIP TGA transcription factors TGA2 and TGA5 in the nucleus and, via its redox activity and conserved C-terminal motifs, positively regulates JA/ET- and stress-related transcription factor genes such as ERF6 and ORA59, thereby altering osmotic stress sensitivity. [21]

Pathways

Regulatory: Osmotic stress response and JA/ethylene-associated stress signaling, through modulation of ERF6, ORA59, PDF1.2 and other stress-related transcription factor genes in Arabidopsis; integration with redox-dependent regulation via TGA2/TGA5 in the nucleus. [21]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCC-type glutaredoxin (CC-type GRX), a land plant–specific GRX subgroup related to ROXY family proteins.
Validation MethodHeterologous overexpression of MeGRXC3 and nuclear/cytoplasmic/motif-mutant versions in Arabidopsis combined with mannitol stress phenotyping (seed germination and seedling biomass), qPCR of downstream genes, yeast transactivation assays, and demonstration of nuclear interaction with TGA2/TGA5 by yeast two-hybrid and BiFC.
Breeding Application ValueMeGRXC3 acts as a negative regulator of mannitol-induced osmotic stress tolerance via nuclear redox-dependent modulation of TGA transcription factors and downstream stress-related transcription factor genes. These results suggest that downregulation or functional attenuation of MeGRXC3 in cassava could be a potential strategy to enhance drought/osmotic stress tolerance and to modulate ROS-associated responses such as drought-induced leaf abscission. MeGRXC3 and its conserved motifs also provide molecular markers or engineering targets for fine-tuning ABA/JA/ET-related stress signalling networks in cassava improvement programs.
Environment / TreatmentD-mannitol-induced osmotic stress (100–200 mM D-mannitol in 1/2 MS medium) applied during seed germination and seedling growth of Arabidopsis overexpressing MeGRXC3.

ShPLR (Sinopodophyllum hexandrum) Plant

Key Findings

  • ShPLR encodes PINORESINOL-LARICIRESINOL REDUCTASE, a key enzyme converting pinoresinol to secoisolariciresinol in the dedicated branch of podophyllotoxin biosynthesis. [22]
  • A 1,034-bp 5′-upstream promoter of ShPLR was cloned and shown in silico to harbor numerous cis-elements responsive to light, low temperature, dehydration, ABA, and other hormones, particularly multiple MYB/MYC and drought-responsive motifs. [22]
  • ShPLR transcript levels in roots are upregulated under progressive water deficit and remain higher than irrigated controls even after re-watering, in parallel with increased podophyllotoxin accumulation. [22]
  • These data indicate that ShPLR participates in stress-responsive reinforcement of podophyllotoxin biosynthesis, and that its promoter has potential utility as a stress-inducible regulatory element for engineering water-deficit tolerance and lignan/podophyllotoxin production in plants. [22]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Under progressive water deficit, ShPLR is transcriptionally upregulated in roots in parallel with increased podophyllotoxin accumulation, indicating that ShPLR contributes to stress-responsive enhancement of podophyllotoxin biosynthesis in S. hexandrum. [22]

Regulatory Mechanism

A 1,034-bp promoter region contains multiple MYB/MYC-binding and ABA/drought-responsive motifs (e.g., MYBPLANT, MYB1AT, MYBCORE, MYCCONSENSUSAT, ACGTATERD1, STRE) as well as light- and temperature-associated cis-elements; ShPLR transcripts are induced by water deficit and decrease upon re-watering, consistent with transcriptional activation via these stress-responsive promoter elements. [22]

Pathways

Regulatory: Drought/water-deficit and ABA-related stress signaling pathways acting through MYB and MYC family transcription factors and other cis-acting elements to activate the phenylpropanoid and podophyllotoxin biosynthetic pathway. [22]

Biosynthetic: Phenylpropanoid-derived lignan and podophyllotoxin biosynthetic pathway; ShPLR catalyzes the two-step reduction of pinoresinol to secoisolariciresinol en route to matairesinol and deoxypodophyllotoxin/podophyllotoxin. [22]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainPINORESINOL-LARICIRESINOL REDUCTASE (PLR) enzyme involved in lignan biosynthesis.
Expression PatternIn roots, ShPLR transcripts increase rapidly during the initial phase of water deprivation and remain upregulated through 10 and 15 days of stress compared with irrigated controls; expression declines after 15 days of re-watering but remains higher than irrigated plants, and shows an age-dependent gradual increase in both irrigated and stressed plants.
Validation MethodqRT-PCR expression profiling of ShPLR in roots across 5, 10, and 15 days of water deficit and after 15 days of re-watering, using ShACTIN as internal control, combined with cloning of a 1,034-bp 5′-upstream promoter by GenomeWalker PCR and in silico cis-element analysis (PLACE, PlantCARE).
Breeding Application ValuePromoter of ShPLR contains multiple drought-, temperature-, and light-responsive cis-elements and drives water deficit-inducible expression of a key podophyllotoxin biosynthetic gene, suggesting use of this promoter to engineer stress-inducible expression of transgenes for improved water-deficit tolerance and/or enhanced podophyllotoxin or other lignan production in S. hexandrum or heterologous plant systems.
Environment / TreatmentProgressive water deficit induced by withholding irrigation for 5, 10, and 15 days (non-irrigated plants) followed by 15 days of re-watering, compared with plants irrigated every other day with 150 ml water.

ShSLD (Sinopodophyllum hexandrum) Plant

Key Findings

  • ShSLD encodes SECOISOLARICIRESINOL DEHYDROGENASE, which converts secoisolariciresinol to matairesinol in the dedicated branch of podophyllotoxin biosynthesis. [22]
  • A 785-bp 5′-upstream promoter region of ShSLD was cloned and shown in silico to contain numerous cis-acting elements responsive to drought/dehydration (MYB, MYC, ACGTATERD1, STRE), ABA (DPBFCOREDCDC3), cytokinin (ARR1, ARR10), and light (GT1CONSENSUS, SORLIP2AT). [22]
  • ShSLD is transcriptionally upregulated in roots during progressive water deficit, and its expression remains higher than in irrigated controls after re-watering, accompanying elevated podophyllotoxin levels. [22]
  • These findings support a role for ShSLD in stress-responsive podophyllotoxin biosynthesis and indicate that its promoter can function as a stress-inducible regulatory element with potential application in engineering drought tolerance and secondary metabolism in plants. [22]

Core Phenotypic Effect

ShSLD transcript abundance in roots increases under water deficit and remains elevated relative to irrigated controls after re-watering, coinciding with enhanced podophyllotoxin accumulation, indicating that ShSLD participates in stress-responsive control of podophyllotoxin biosynthesis in S. hexandrum. [22]

Regulatory Mechanism

The 785-bp ShSLD promoter contains multiple abiotic stress-related cis-elements (e.g., MYB, MYC, ACGTATERD1, STRE), ABA-responsive (DPBFCOREDCDC3), cytokinin-related (ARR1, ARR10), and light-responsive (SORLIP2AT, GT1CONSENSUS, RBCSCONSENSUS) motifs; ShSLD is transcriptionally induced by water deficit and partially downregulated upon re-watering, consistent with stress- and hormone-regulated activation via these cis-elements. [22]

Pathways

Regulatory: Drought/water-deficit, ABA, cytokinin, and light signaling pathways converging on MYB/MYC and other transcription factors that bind the ShSLD promoter to regulate the phenylpropanoid and podophyllotoxin biosynthetic pathway under stress. [22]

Biosynthetic: Phenylpropanoid-derived lignan and podophyllotoxin biosynthetic pathway; ShSLD encodes SECOISOLARICIRESINOL DEHYDROGENASE that converts secoisolariciresinol to matairesinol, a direct precursor of deoxypodophyllotoxin and podophyllotoxin. [22]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSECOISOLARICIRESINOL DEHYDROGENASE (SLD/SDH) enzyme involved in lignan and podophyllotoxin biosynthesis.
Expression PatternIn roots, ShSLD is upregulated during water deprivation, with transcript levels increasing over 5–15 days of stress in non-irrigated plants relative to irrigated controls; expression decreases after re-watering but remains above control levels, and also shows an age-dependent gradual increase even in the absence of stress.
Validation MethodqRT-PCR expression profiling of ShSLD in roots across progressive water-deficit (5, 10, 15 days) and recovery, using ShACTIN as reference, together with cloning of a 785-bp 5′-upstream promoter fragment by GenomeWalker PCR and in silico identification of cis-regulatory elements (PLACE, PlantCARE).
Breeding Application ValueThe ShSLD promoter carries multiple drought-, low-temperature-, light-, and hormone-responsive cis-elements and drives water deficit-inducible expression of a downstream podophyllotoxin biosynthetic gene, supporting its use as a stress-inducible promoter to control transgene expression for improving water-deficit tolerance and modulating podophyllotoxin or related lignan accumulation in S. hexandrum or other crops.
Environment / TreatmentProgressive water deficit by withholding irrigation for 5, 10, and 15 days, followed by 15 days of re-watering, compared with plants irrigated every second day with 150 ml water.

MiCOL1A (Mangifera indica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MiCOL1A is a CONSTANS-LIKE1 homolog from mango with four exons, three introns, and a single CCT domain, grouping into CO/COL family group IV. [23]
  • MiCOL1A is predominantly expressed in juvenile and adult leaves, shows developmentally regulated and circadian expression correlated with floral induction in mango. [23]
  • MiCOL1A protein localizes to the nucleus and exhibits transcriptional activation activity via its front-region domain in yeast. [23]
  • Overexpression of MiCOL1A in Arabidopsis delays flowering under long-day conditions by repressing AtFT and AtSOC1 and increasing AtTFL expression. [23]
  • Overexpression of MiCOL1A enhances drought tolerance in Arabidopsis, promoting root elongation under severe mannitol stress and increasing survival after soil drought, accompanied by upregulation of AtCOR15A, AtNCED3 and AtDREB2. [23]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Heterologous overexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana delays flowering under long-day conditions and enhances drought tolerance, including increased root elongation under severe mannitol-induced osmotic stress and higher survival after soil drought. [23]

Regulatory Mechanism

Nuclear-localized transcription factor with intrinsic transcriptional activation activity mediated by its N-terminal front region; in transgenic Arabidopsis, overexpression represses AtFT and AtSOC1 and enhances AtTFL expression, and under drought conditions upregulates stress-responsive genes AtCOR15A, AtNCED3 and AtDREB2. [23]

Pathways

Regulatory: Photoperiodic flowering pathway, acting as a transcriptional regulator that represses flowering integrator genes FT and SOC1 and upregulates TFL1 homolog expression, and an abiotic stress-response pathway influencing drought-responsive genes. [23]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCONSTANS-LIKE (CO/COL) family group IV protein containing a single CCT domain and lacking a B-box domain.
Expression PatternExpressed in all tested mango tissues, with highest levels in juvenile and adult leaves, lower levels in adult stems, and lowest in flowers. Seasonally, expression in adult leaves is lowest during the vegetative stage, increases markedly during floral induction, decreases during floral differentiation, then rises again in late floral differentiation before declining in floral organs. Shows a clear circadian rhythm in leaves, peaking around 3:00 at night and then decreasing.
Validation MethodGene cloning and sequence analysis; qRT-PCR for tissue, seasonal and circadian expression in mango; subcellular localization using GFP fusion in onion epidermal cells; yeast-based transcriptional activation assay; constitutive overexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana Col-0 under the CaMV35S promoter; phenotyping for flowering time, plant height, root length under mannitol stress, and survival after drought; qRT-PCR of endogenous flowering (AtFT, AtSOC1, AtTFL) and stress-responsive (AtCOR15A, AtNCED3, AtDREB2) genes in transgenic Arabidopsis.
Breeding Application ValueAs a mango CONSTANS-LIKE1 transcription factor whose overexpression delays flowering and enhances drought tolerance in Arabidopsis, MiCOL1A represents a candidate target for manipulating flowering time and improving drought resistance in mango through marker-assisted selection or genetic engineering once effective strategies for modulating its activity in mango are established.
Environment / TreatmentLong-day photoperiod (16 h light/8 h dark) at approximately 22 °C for Arabidopsis; osmotic stress imposed by 300 and 400 mmol/L mannitol in 1/2 MS medium; soil drought by withholding water for ~12 days followed by 3 days rewatering.

MiCOL1B (Mangifera indica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MiCOL1B is a CONSTANS-LIKE1 homolog from mango with four exons, three introns, and a single CCT domain, grouping into CO/COL family group IV. [23]
  • MiCOL1B is expressed in all examined mango tissues, with highest expression in flowers and seasonally elevated expression in leaves during floral induction and late floral differentiation, and shows an unstable but circadian-associated diurnal pattern. [23]
  • MiCOL1B protein localizes to the nucleus and displays transcriptional activation activity through its front-region domain in yeast. [23]
  • Overexpression of MiCOL1B in Arabidopsis delays flowering under long-day conditions by repressing AtFT and AtSOC1 and increasing AtTFL expression. [23]
  • Overexpression of MiCOL1B enhances drought tolerance in Arabidopsis, increasing root elongation under severe mannitol stress and survival after soil drought, accompanied by upregulation of AtCOR15A and AtNCED3, while AtDREB2 expression is not consistently significantly elevated. [23]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Heterologous overexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana delays flowering under long-day conditions and enhances drought tolerance, including increased root elongation under severe mannitol-induced osmotic stress and higher survival after soil drought. [23]

Regulatory Mechanism

Nuclear-localized transcription factor with intrinsic transcriptional activation activity via its front-region domain; in transgenic Arabidopsis, overexpression represses AtFT and AtSOC1 and enhances AtTFL expression, and under drought conditions upregulates AtCOR15A and AtNCED3, with AtDREB2 induction not consistently significant in MiCOL1B lines. [23]

Pathways

Regulatory: Photoperiodic flowering pathway, acting as a transcriptional regulator that represses flowering integrator genes FT and SOC1 and upregulates TFL1 homolog expression, and an abiotic stress-response pathway influencing drought-responsive genes. [23]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCONSTANS-LIKE (CO/COL) family group IV protein containing a single CCT domain and lacking a B-box domain.
Expression PatternExpressed in all tested mango tissues, with highest levels in flowers and lower but similar levels in juvenile leaves, adult leaves and adult stems. Seasonally, expression in adult leaves is lowest during the vegetative stage, increases during floral induction, decreases during floral differentiation, then peaks again in late floral differentiation before declining in floral organs. Circadian analysis shows an unstable diurnal pattern in leaves, with peaks around 0:00 on the first two days and around 18:00 on the third day.
Validation MethodGene cloning and sequence analysis; qRT-PCR for tissue, seasonal and circadian expression in mango; subcellular localization using GFP fusion in onion epidermal cells; yeast-based transcriptional activation assay; constitutive overexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana Col-0 under the CaMV35S promoter; phenotyping for flowering time, plant height, root length under mannitol stress, and survival after drought; qRT-PCR of endogenous flowering (AtFT, AtSOC1, AtTFL) and stress-responsive (AtCOR15A, AtNCED3, AtDREB2) genes in transgenic Arabidopsis.
Breeding Application ValueAs a mango CONSTANS-LIKE1 transcription factor whose overexpression delays flowering and enhances drought tolerance in Arabidopsis, MiCOL1B represents a candidate target for modifying flowering time and improving drought resistance in mango breeding or transgenic approaches, pending direct functional validation in mango.
Environment / TreatmentLong-day photoperiod (16 h light/8 h dark) at approximately 22 °C for Arabidopsis; osmotic stress imposed by 300 and 400 mmol/L mannitol in 1/2 MS medium; soil drought by withholding water for ~12 days followed by 3 days rewatering.

PHS1 (Chlamydomonas reinhardtii) Microbial

Key Findings

  • Chlamydomonas reinhardtii possesses a single-copy PHS1 gene (Cre08.g375000) encoding a protein with an N-terminal tubulin kinase domain and a C-terminal MAP kinase phosphatase-like domain. [24]
  • Mild hyperosmotic stress (e.g., 0.2–0.3 M sorbitol or ~0.15 M NaCl, 20–40% artificial seawater) rapidly and transiently induces PHS1-dependent phosphorylation of α-tubulin at Thr349, with more than 60% of cellular tubulin phosphorylated at peak. [24]
  • In wild-type and PHS1-complemented cells, PHS1-mediated tubulin phosphorylation causes rapid, transient depolymerization of cytoplasmic microtubules in both G1 and mitotic cells, with microtubule arrays recovering within about 1 h in G1 and within several hours in mitosis. [24]
  • The phs1 insertion mutant completely lacks stress-induced α-tubulin Thr349 phosphorylation and fails to undergo rapid microtubule depolymerization under hyperosmotic stress, confirming that PHS1 is solely responsible for this modification. [24]
  • Transient microtubule destabilization mediated by PHS1 does not measurably affect G1 phase cell growth (cell volume increase) under sorbitol stress but contributes to delaying mitotic progression and daughter cell cluster formation under 0.3 M sorbitol. [24]
  • Hyperosmotic stress strongly inhibits daughter cell hatching, causing persistent daughter cell clusters (palmelloid-like aggregates); this mitotic delay is less severe in phs1 cells than in wild type and complemented lines, indicating that PHS1-mediated microtubule destabilization is partly responsible for stress-responsive cell cycle delay. [24]
  • PHS1 homologues with conserved tubulin kinase and phosphatase domain architecture are widely present in freshwater Chlorophyta but absent in some marine green algae, supporting a role for PHS1-mediated cytoskeletal remodeling in adaptation to freshwater or moderately saline environments. [24]

Core Phenotypic Effect

PHS1 mediates hyperosmotic stress-induced phosphorylation of α-tubulin at Thr349, causing transient depolymerization of cytoplasmic microtubules and contributing to a delay in mitotic cell cycle progression under mild hyperosmotic stress, while having little effect on G1 phase cell growth [24]

Regulatory Mechanism

Upon mild hyperosmotic stress, PHS1 tubulin kinase activity is rapidly activated and phosphorylates α-tubulin at Thr349, reducing tubulin polymerization competence and causing transient depolymerization of cytoplasmic microtubules; phosphorylated tubulin is then dephosphorylated within 1 h in G1 cells and within ~3 h in mitotic cells, restoring microtubule organization. This transient microtubule destabilization contributes to delaying mitotic progression under stress. [24]

Pathways

Regulatory: Hyperosmotic stress response pathway leading to rapid cytoskeletal remodeling (microtubule destabilization) and stress-responsive regulation of mitotic cell cycle progression in freshwater green algae [24]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainPHS1 encodes a large protein with an N-terminal atypical tubulin kinase domain (related to actin-fragmin kinases) and a C-terminal MAP kinase phosphatase-like domain within a single polypeptide
Validation MethodLoss-of-function analysis using a PHS1 insertion mutant (phs1-1) and genomic complementation, combined with stress-induced tubulin phosphorylation assays (phospho-Thr349-α-tubulin immunoblotting and phos-tag SDS-PAGE) and microtubule immunofluorescence to assess microtubule depolymerization and mitotic progression under hyperosmotic stress
Environment / TreatmentMild hyperosmotic stress imposed by 0.2–0.3 M sorbitol, 0.1–0.2 M NaCl, or 20–40% artificial seawater, applied to synchronized cells in G1 phase or just before/during mitosis

TaDHN12-A1 (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaDHN12-A1 encodes a YSK2-type dehydrin protein and is part of a tandemly duplicated TaDHN12 cluster in bread wheat. [25]
  • RNA-seq–based expression profiling shows that tandem TaDHN genes (including TaDHN12s) are predominantly up-regulated under cold, drought, and high-salinity stresses, whereas non-tandem DHN1/2/3/6 genes remain low-expressed. [25]
  • TaDHN12-A1 protein is localized to the cytoplasm, as confirmed by TaDHN12-A1–GFP fusion transiently expressed in Nicotiana benthamiana leaf epidermal cells. [25]
  • The strong abiotic-stress inducibility and tandem expansion of TaDHN12 genes suggest that TaDHN12-A1 contributes to wheat adaptation to dehydration-related stresses and is a promising candidate for stress-resistance breeding. [25]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Cytoplasmic dehydrin whose gene is strongly induced (as part of the TaDHN12 tandem cluster) by cold, drought, and high-salinity stresses, indicating an important role in abiotic stress responses in bread wheat. [25]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDehydrin (DHN) family protein; YSK2-type (YnSKn-type dehydrin with one Y-, one S-, and two K-segments).
Expression PatternAs a member of the TaDHN12 tandem cluster, TaDHN12-A1 shows RNA-seq evidence of induction under cold, drought, and high-salinity (300 mM NaCl) stress, whereas TaDHN family members TaDHN1/2/3/6 are largely insensitive. Tandem TaDHN4/5/12 genes are mostly up-regulated under abiotic stress, suggesting stress-responsive expression of TaDHN12-A1.
Validation MethodTaDHN12-A1–GFP fusion transient expression in N. benthamiana leaf epidermal cells followed by confocal microscopy to validate cytoplasmic localization; RNA-seq–based expression profiling under multiple abiotic and biotic stresses.
Breeding Application ValueTaDHN12-A1, as a tandem-duplicated, strongly abiotic-stress-inducible cytoplasmic dehydrin, is a promising candidate locus for enhancing wheat tolerance to drought, cold, and high salinity. Its clear genomic position and associated gene-specific SSRs enable potential use in marker-assisted selection and in transgenic or genome-editing strategies to fine-tune stress responses in bread wheat.
Environment / TreatmentAbiotic stresses: cold, drought, heat, and salt (100, 200, 300 mM NaCl); biotic stresses: infection with Fusarium graminearum (FHB), Blumeria graminis (powdery mildew), and Puccinia striiformis (stripe rust); developmental tissues: roots, leaves, stems, spikes, seeds. Subcellular localization tested in N. benthamiana leaves.

TaDHN7-A (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaDHN7-A encodes an SK3-type dehydrin protein. [25]
  • TaDHN7-A is constitutively expressed in all tested tissues and generally shows high expression under diverse abiotic and biotic stress conditions, except reduced responsiveness under cold. [25]
  • Subcellular localization of TaDHN7-A–GFP demonstrates that TaDHN7-A protein localizes to the nucleus in N. benthamiana leaf epidermal cells. [25]
  • Together with evidence from SK3-type dehydrins in other plants, the strong and broad stress-responsive expression pattern of TaDHN7-A indicates that it is an important component of wheat’s general abiotic stress response. [25]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Nuclear SK3-type dehydrin with constitutive and generally high expression across tissues and under multiple abiotic stresses, suggesting a central role in broad-spectrum stress adaptation in wheat. [25]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDehydrin (DHN) family protein; SK3-type (three K-segments plus S-segment; SKn-type dehydrin).
Expression PatternTaDHN7 genes (including TaDHN7-A) are constitutively expressed in all examined tissues (roots, leaves, stems, spikes, seeds) and show generally high TPM values under cold, drought, heat, and salt stress, with TaDHN7-A being less responsive under cold. They are also expressed under F. graminearum, B. graminis, and P. striiformis infection, reflecting broad stress responsiveness.
Validation MethodTaDHN7-A–GFP fusion transiently expressed in N. benthamiana leaf epidermal cells and observed by confocal microscopy to validate nuclear localization; RNA-seq–based expression profiling under diverse abiotic and biotic stresses.
Breeding Application ValueBecause TaDHN7-A is a nuclear SK3-type dehydrin with constitutively high expression and strong responsiveness to multiple abiotic stresses, it is a strong candidate gene for engineering or selecting wheat varieties with broad-spectrum stress tolerance. Its clear genomic definition within the DHN7 homoeologous group and linkage with gene-specific SSRs facilitate its potential use in marker-assisted selection.
Environment / TreatmentCold, drought, heat, and salt (NaCl) stresses; infections with Fusarium graminearum, Blumeria graminis, and Puccinia striiformis; multiple tissues (roots, leaves, stems, spikes, seeds). Subcellular localization assayed in N. benthamiana leaves.

TaDHN4-D1 (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaDHN4-D1 encodes a YSK2-type dehydrin and is part of a tandem TaDHN4 cluster. [25]
  • TaDHN4 genes, including TaDHN4-D1, show root-specific expression and are mainly induced by drought and salt stress in RNA-seq datasets. [25]
  • RT-qPCR shows that TaDHN4-D1 is strongly responsive to hormone treatments: it is significantly down-regulated by ABA, but strongly induced by MeJA (peak at 6 h), while GA and SA have weaker or suppressive effects. [25]
  • These expression characteristics suggest that TaDHN4-D1 participates in drought- and salt-related stress responses in roots and is modulated by ABA and jasmonate signaling. [25]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Root-specific dehydrin that is induced by drought and salt stress and shows strong MeJA inducibility but ABA-mediated repression, indicating a role in hormone-regulated root responses to osmotic and salinity stress. [25]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling (negative regulation of TaDHN4-D1 expression) and jasmonate (MeJA) signaling (strong positive regulation of expression), in the context of abiotic stress responses. [25]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDehydrin (DHN) family protein; YSK2-type dehydrin (YnSKn-type with one Y-, one S-, and two K-segments).
Expression PatternTaDHN4 genes exhibit root-specific expression; TaDHN4-D1 is mainly induced by drought and salt stresses in RNA-seq datasets. RT-qPCR shows that TaDHN4-D1 responds strongly to hormone treatments: ABA significantly down-regulates its expression (p < 0.01), MeJA markedly induces expression with a peak at 6 h, whereas GA and SA treatments result in weaker or suppressive effects.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR time-course of TaDHN4-D1 under ABA, GA, SA, and MeJA treatments, combined with RNA-seq expression profiles under drought and salt stress, to validate its hormone and stress responsiveness.
Breeding Application ValueGiven its root-specific expression, strong induction by drought and salt, and pronounced MeJA responsiveness, TaDHN4-D1 is a promising candidate gene for improving root-based tolerance to water deficit and salinity in wheat. Its tandem location and clear expression signature make it suitable as a target for functional validation and for marker-assisted selection in stress-resilient cultivars.
Environment / TreatmentDrought and heat (from SRP045409), cold (SRP043554), salt (SRP300360; NaCl 100–300 mM), and pathogen infections (F. graminearum, B. graminis, P. striiformis). Hormone treatments: ABA 100 μM, GA 50 μM, SA 100 μM, MeJA 100 μM applied to 2-week-old Chinese Spring seedlings.

TaDHN7-B (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaDHN7-B encodes an SK3-type dehydrin. [25]
  • TaDHN7 genes (including TaDHN7-B) are constitutively expressed in all tissues and generally show high expression across diverse abiotic stresses. [25]
  • RT-qPCR shows that TaDHN7-B is strongly induced by ABA and MeJA and significantly up-regulated by SA, whereas GA has only weak effects. [25]
  • The combination of broad stress expression and strong responsiveness to ABA, SA, and MeJA suggests that TaDHN7-B is an important integrator of hormone and stress signaling in wheat. [25]

Core Phenotypic Effect

SK3-type dehydrin with constitutive and high expression under multiple abiotic stresses and strong induction by ABA, SA, and MeJA, indicating a key role in hormone-mediated and general abiotic stress responses in wheat. [25]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling (strong positive regulation of TaDHN7-B expression), salicylic acid signaling (significant up-regulation), and jasmonate (MeJA) signaling (sustained induction throughout treatment), in the context of abiotic and biotic stress responses. [25]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDehydrin (DHN) family protein; SK3-type dehydrin.
Expression PatternTaDHN7 genes are constitutively expressed in roots, leaves, stems, spikes, and seeds and are generally highly expressed under cold, drought, heat, and salt stress (with TaDHN7-A less responsive to cold). RT-qPCR shows that TaDHN7-B is strongly induced by ABA and MeJA and significantly up-regulated by SA (p < 0.01), while GA treatment causes only weak induction or inhibition.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR time-course of TaDHN7-B under ABA, GA, SA, and MeJA treatments combined with RNA-seq profiles under abiotic and biotic stresses to validate its strong hormone and stress responsiveness.
Breeding Application ValueTaDHN7-B’s broad stress responsiveness and strong induction by ABA, SA, and MeJA make it an attractive candidate for improving multi-stress tolerance and for fine-tuning hormone-regulated defense and adaptation pathways in wheat breeding programs.
Environment / TreatmentCold, drought, heat, and salt stresses; infections with F. graminearum, B. graminis, and P. striiformis; hormone treatments with ABA 100 μM, GA 50 μM, SA 100 μM, MeJA 100 μM applied to Chinese Spring seedlings.

TaDHN8-D (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaDHN8-D encodes a Kn-type dehydrin and is part of the Group I Kn-type DHNs (DHN8/9/10/13). [25]
  • Group I DHNs are mainly up-regulated under cold and drought stress and show sensitivity to high salinity, while they are largely down-regulated during B. graminis and P. striiformis infections. [25]
  • RT-qPCR reveals that TaDHN8-D is strongly induced by ABA and MeJA and remains up-regulated throughout the MeJA treatment period, whereas GA and SA exert weaker effects. [25]
  • These features support a role for TaDHN8-D in ABA- and jasmonate-mediated responses to dehydration-related stresses. [25]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Kn-type dehydrin that is induced under cold and drought and is highly responsive to ABA and especially MeJA, remaining up-regulated throughout MeJA treatment, indicative of a role in hormone-modulated abiotic stress responses. [25]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling (positive regulation of TaDHN8-D expression) and jasmonate (MeJA) signaling (sustained strong induction), within the context of cold, drought, and high-salinity stress responses; reduced expression under certain fungal infections (B. graminis, P. striiformis). [25]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDehydrin (DHN) family protein; K3-type Kn dehydrin (three K-segments, lacking Y- and S-segments).
Expression PatternAs part of Group I Kn-type DHNs (DHN8/9/10/13), TaDHN8-D is mainly up-regulated under cold and drought stress and shows sensitivity to high salinity (notably at 300 mM NaCl), while it is mostly down-regulated during B. graminis and P. striiformis infections. RT-qPCR shows strong ABA- and MeJA-induced expression, with TaDHN8-D remaining up-regulated throughout the entire MeJA treatment period.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR time-course of TaDHN8-D expression under ABA, GA, SA, and MeJA treatments combined with RNA-seq profiles under cold, drought, salt, and pathogen stresses to validate its hormone and stress responsiveness.
Breeding Application ValueBecause TaDHN8-D is a Kn-type dehydrin strongly induced by cold and drought and highly responsive to ABA and MeJA, it represents a useful candidate for breeding or engineering wheat with enhanced tolerance to dehydration-related stresses, particularly in environments where hormone-mediated stress signaling is critical.
Environment / TreatmentCold, drought, heat, and salt (100–300 mM NaCl) stresses; infections with Fusarium graminearum, Blumeria graminis, and Puccinia striiformis; hormone treatments with ABA, GA, SA, and MeJA in Chinese Spring seedlings.

TaDHN9-A (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaDHN9-A encodes a K2-type Kn dehydrin and is part of the Group I DHN8/9/10/13 cluster. [25]
  • Group I DHNs (including TaDHN9-A) are mainly up-regulated under cold and drought and show sensitivity to high salinity, but are mostly down-regulated during B. graminis and P. striiformis infection. [25]
  • RT-qPCR reveals that TaDHN9-A is strongly induced by ABA and MeJA (peaking at 12 h under MeJA) and significantly up-regulated by SA, with GA causing only weak changes. [25]
  • The combination of strong hormone responsiveness and abiotic stress induction indicates that TaDHN9-A is a key hormone-responsive dehydrin in wheat stress adaptation. [25]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Strongly hormone-responsive Kn-type dehydrin, with particularly high induction by ABA and MeJA and SA-responsive expression, and up-regulation under cold, drought, and high-salinity stress, pointing to a central role in hormone-modulated abiotic stress responses. [25]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling (marked positive regulation of TaDHN9-A expression), salicylic acid signaling (significant up-regulation), and jasmonate (MeJA) signaling (strong induction with peak at 12 h), associated with abiotic stress responses to cold, drought, and high salinity. [25]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDehydrin (DHN) family protein; K2-type Kn dehydrin (two K-segments).
Expression PatternAs part of Group I DHNs, TaDHN9-A is mainly up-regulated under cold and drought and shows sensitivity to high salinity, while being mostly down-regulated during B. graminis and P. striiformis infection. RT-qPCR shows that ABA induces strong expression, SA significantly up-regulates TaDHN9-A (p < 0.01), and MeJA markedly induces expression with a peak at 12 h; GA causes only weak induction or inhibition.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR time-course of TaDHN9-A under ABA, GA, SA, and MeJA treatments combined with RNA-seq stress-expression data to validate its strong hormone and abiotic-stress responsiveness.
Breeding Application ValueBecause TaDHN9-A is a strongly ABA-, SA-, and MeJA-responsive Kn-type dehydrin that is induced by cold, drought, and high salinity, it is a particularly attractive target for breeding or engineering wheat with enhanced, hormone-integrated tolerance to multiple abiotic stresses.
Environment / TreatmentCold, drought, heat, and salt stresses; infections with F. graminearum, B. graminis, and P. striiformis; hormone treatments with ABA, GA, SA, and MeJA (100/50/100/100 μM) in Chinese Spring seedlings.

TaDHN12-B5 (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaDHN12-B5 encodes a YSK2-type dehydrin and belongs to the TaDHN12 tandem gene cluster. [25]
  • Tandem TaDHN12 genes are mostly up-regulated under cold, drought, and high-salinity stress in RNA-seq datasets. [25]
  • Under F. graminearum infection, TaDHN12-B5 is up-regulated at 72 h, whereas the DHN family as a whole responds weakly to most biotic stresses. [25]
  • RT-qPCR shows that TaDHN12-B5 is strongly induced by ABA and MeJA (with expression peaking at 12 h under MeJA), while GA and SA have relatively minor or negative effects. [25]
  • These characteristics identify TaDHN12-B5 as a hormone-responsive, tandem-duplicated dehydrin implicated in abiotic and specific biotic (Fusarium) stress responses. [25]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Tandem YSK2-type dehydrin that is up-regulated under drought, cold, high salinity, and Fusarium graminearum infection and shows strikingly high ABA- and strong MeJA-induced expression, indicating a role at the intersection of hormone signaling and stress responses. [25]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling (very strong positive regulation of TaDHN12-B5 expression), jasmonate (MeJA) signaling (induction peaking at 12 h), and possible involvement in pathogen-triggered responses to Fusarium graminearum; GA and SA have weaker or inhibitory effects. [25]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDehydrin (DHN) family protein; YSK2-type dehydrin.
Expression PatternAs a member of the TaDHN12 tandem cluster, TaDHN12-B5 is among the DHN genes up-regulated under cold, drought, and high-salinity stress, while many non-tandem DHNs show low responsiveness. Under F. graminearum infection, TaDHN12-B5 is up-regulated at 72 h. RT-qPCR shows strong and sensitive ABA-induced expression (one of the two genes with strikingly high ABA responsiveness) and MeJA-induced expression peaking at 12 h, with GA and SA causing weaker or negative effects.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR time-course of TaDHN12-B5 under hormone treatments combined with RNA-seq analysis under abiotic stresses and Fusarium infection to validate its stress and hormone responsiveness.
Breeding Application ValueTaDHN12-B5, with strong ABA and MeJA inducibility and responsiveness to both abiotic stresses and Fusarium infection, is a promising candidate for breeding wheat with combined abiotic and disease stress tolerance and for developing stress- and hormone-responsive markers based on DHN-specific SSRs.
Environment / TreatmentCold, drought, heat, and salt (100–300 mM NaCl) stresses; infection with Fusarium graminearum (FHB) and other pathogens; hormone treatments with ABA, GA, SA, and MeJA applied to Chinese Spring seedlings.

TaDHN13-A (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaDHN13-A encodes a K3-type Kn dehydrin and clusters with Group I DHNs (DHN8/9/10/13). [25]
  • Group I DHNs are mainly up-regulated by cold and drought and sensitive to high salinity, but are frequently down-regulated during B. graminis and P. striiformis infection. [25]
  • RT-qPCR shows that TaDHN13-A is strongly induced by ABA and MeJA, with MeJA-induced expression peaking at 6 h; SA and GA have weaker or no positive effects. [25]
  • This pattern suggests that TaDHN13-A contributes to ABA- and MeJA-mediated abiotic stress responses in wheat. [25]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Kn-type dehydrin that is part of the cold- and drought-inducible Group I DHNs and shows strong ABA- and MeJA-induced expression, indicating a role in hormone-regulated abiotic stress responses. [25]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling (strong positive regulation of TaDHN13-A expression) and jasmonate (MeJA) signaling (induction peaking at 6 h), associated with cold, drought, and high-salinity stress responses. [25]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDehydrin (DHN) family protein; K3-type Kn dehydrin.
Expression PatternTaDHN13-A, as a Group I Kn-type DHN, is mainly up-regulated under cold and drought and shows sensitivity to high salinity, while being mostly down-regulated during B. graminis and P. striiformis infections. RT-qPCR shows strong ABA-induced expression and MeJA-induced expression peaking at 6 h; SA and GA exert weak or non-inductive effects.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR time-course of TaDHN13-A expression under ABA, GA, SA, and MeJA treatments combined with RNA-seq stress-expression profiles to validate its hormone and abiotic-stress responsiveness.
Breeding Application ValueTaDHN13-A, as a cold- and drought-inducible, strongly ABA- and MeJA-responsive Kn-type dehydrin, is a candidate for breeding or engineering wheat with enhanced tolerance to dehydration-related stresses and for dissecting hormone–stress crosstalk in dehydrin-mediated protection.
Environment / TreatmentCold, drought, heat, and salt stresses; pathogen infections (F. graminearum, B. graminis, P. striiformis); hormone treatments with ABA, GA, SA, and MeJA on Chinese Spring seedlings.

BnaCPK5 (Brassica napus) Plant

Key Findings

  • BnaCPK5 encodes a Group I calcium-dependent protein kinase with an N-terminal kinase domain and C-terminal CaM-like domain containing four EF-hand motifs, and is predicted to be myristoylated and palmitoylated at its N-terminus. [26]
  • BnaCPK5 localizes to both the plasma membrane and nucleus in plant cells, with N-terminal lipidation contributing to membrane association, and is expressed in roots, stems and leaves with higher transcript abundance in leaves. [26]
  • Constitutive overexpression of BnaCPK5 in oilseed rape significantly enhances drought tolerance, as shown by higher survival rate, relative water content, proline accumulation, and reduced MDA under water deficit compared to GFP controls. [26]
  • BnaCPK5 physically interacts with ABA-responsive transcription factors BnaABF3 and BnaABF4 in nuclei and phosphorylates them in vitro, increasing their transcriptional activity toward the RD29B promoter and stabilizing BnaABF3/4 proteins by reducing their 26S proteasome-dependent degradation. [26]
  • BnaCPK5 also interacts with clade A PP2Cs BnaAHG3 and BnaHAB1, suggesting that its kinase activity is integrated into the canonical ABA signaling module of kinases and phosphatases that fine-tune ABA and drought responses. [26]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of BnaCPK5 in oilseed rape enhances drought tolerance, associated with improved water status, increased proline accumulation, reduced lipid peroxidation, and modulation of ABA-responsive gene regulation via phosphorylation of BnaABF3 and BnaABF4. [26]

Regulatory Mechanism

Ca2+-dependent Ser/Thr protein kinase that autophosphorylates and phosphorylates BnaABF3 and BnaABF4, enhancing their transcriptional activity on the RD29B promoter and increasing their protein stability by reducing 26S proteasome-dependent degradation; physically interacts with clade A PP2Cs BnaAHG3 and BnaHAB1, which are expected to antagonize its kinase activity as negative regulators of ABA signaling. [26]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA-dependent drought stress signaling pathway, where BnaCPK5 acts as a positive regulator that decodes Ca2+ signals and modulates core ABA-signaling transcription factors BnaABF3/4 and potentially interacts with clade A PP2Cs. [26]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCalcium-dependent protein kinase (CPK) family; N-terminal Ser/Thr kinase domain, junction region, and C-terminal calmodulin-like domain with four EF-hand motifs; predicted N-terminal myristoylation and palmitoylation sites.
Expression PatternConstitutively expressed in roots, stems and leaves of one-month-old plants, with higher transcript levels in leaves than in stems or roots.
Breeding Application ValueBnaCPK5 is a validated positive regulator of drought tolerance in oilseed rape, improving survival, water retention, osmoprotection, and reducing oxidative damage under water deficit. Its ability to phosphorylate and stabilize core ABA-signaling transcription factors (BnaABF3/4) and to integrate Ca2+ and ABA signaling makes it a promising target for genetic engineering or marker-assisted improvement of drought-resilient Brassica napus cultivars.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by withholding water from potted plants for 12 d followed by rewatering; osmotic stress during assays; treatment of N. benthamiana leaves with the 26S proteasome inhibitor MG132 (50 μM) in protein stability assays; mannitol-induced plasmolysis (500 mM) for subcellular localization.

BnaABF3 (Brassica napus) Plant

Key Findings

  • BnaABF3 is an ABA-responsive bZIP transcription factor (ABF/AREB) from oilseed rape that interacts strongly with BnaCPK5 in the nucleus, as shown by BiFC and Co-IP assays. [26]
  • BnaCPK5 directly phosphorylates BnaABF3 in vitro, as shown by fluorometric kinase assays using recombinant proteins. [26]
  • In a dual-luciferase reporter system with the RD29B promoter, co-expression of BnaABF3 with BnaCPK5 significantly enhances RD29B-driven LUC activity compared with BnaABF3 alone, indicating that BnaCPK5-mediated phosphorylation increases BnaABF3 transcriptional activity. [26]
  • In modified dual-luciferase assays, co-expression of BnaCPK5 increases the protein level and LUC activity of BnaABF3-LUC fusion proteins, and this stabilization effect disappears upon MG132 treatment, indicating that BnaABF3 is subject to 26S proteasome-dependent degradation and that phosphorylation by BnaCPK5 enhances its stability. [26]

Core Phenotypic Effect

BnaABF3 functions as a nuclear ABA-responsive transcription factor whose phosphorylation by BnaCPK5 enhances its transcriptional activation of the RD29B promoter and increases its protein stability, supporting a positive role in ABA-dependent drought-responsive gene expression. [26]

Regulatory Mechanism

As a bZIP ABF/AREB transcription factor, BnaABF3 binds ABREs and activates transcription of RD29B; phosphorylation by BnaCPK5 enhances its transcriptional activity and increases its protein stability, reducing its degradation by the 26S proteasome, as indicated by MG132 treatments. [26]

Pathways

Regulatory: Part of the ABA-dependent signaling pathway mediating drought-responsive gene expression via binding to ABRE elements and activating RD29B; positioned downstream of Ca2+-dependent kinase BnaCPK5. [26]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainABA-responsive element binding factor (ABF/AREB), a basic leucine zipper (bZIP) family transcription factor that binds ABRE motifs in promoters of ABA- and drought-inducible genes.
Breeding Application ValueAs a core ABA-signaling transcription factor whose activity and stability are enhanced by BnaCPK5, BnaABF3 represents a downstream node through which BnaCPK5 can upregulate LEA-like RD29B and other stress-responsive genes; coordinated manipulation of BnaABF3 and BnaCPK5 could be exploited to fine-tune ABA-responsive transcriptional networks and improve drought tolerance in oilseed rape.
Environment / TreatmentProteasome inhibitor MG132 (50 μM) used to block 26S proteasome-dependent degradation in protein stability assays; luciferin applied to visualize LUC activity; transient expression performed under standard growth conditions in N. benthamiana leaves.

BnaABF4 (Brassica napus) Plant

Key Findings

  • BnaABF4 is an ABA-responsive bZIP transcription factor (ABF/AREB) from oilseed rape that interacts strongly with BnaCPK5 in the nucleus as demonstrated by BiFC and Co-IP. [26]
  • BnaCPK5 directly phosphorylates BnaABF4 in vitro, as shown by kinase assays using recombinant proteins. [26]
  • Co-expression of BnaABF4 with BnaCPK5 in a ProRD29B::LUC dual-luciferase assay leads to significantly increased RD29B-driven luciferase activity compared with BnaABF4 alone or GFP control, indicating that phosphorylation by BnaCPK5 enhances BnaABF4 transcriptional activity. [26]
  • In protein stability assays, co-expression with BnaCPK5 increases the accumulation and LUC activity of BnaABF4-LUC fusion proteins, and this stabilization is lost when the proteasome inhibitor MG132 is applied, indicating that BnaABF4 is subject to 26S proteasome-dependent degradation and that phosphorylation by BnaCPK5 promotes its stability. [26]

Core Phenotypic Effect

BnaABF4 is a nuclear ABA-responsive transcription factor whose phosphorylation by BnaCPK5 increases its transcriptional activity toward the RD29B promoter and enhances its protein stability, supporting a role in promoting ABA-dependent drought-responsive gene expression. [26]

Regulatory Mechanism

As an ABF/AREB bZIP transcription factor, BnaABF4 binds ABRE motifs to activate RD29B transcription; BnaCPK5-mediated phosphorylation enhances BnaABF4 transcriptional activity and stabilizes the protein, counteracting 26S proteasome-dependent degradation revealed by MG132 treatment. [26]

Pathways

Regulatory: Component of the ABA-dependent signaling pathway controlling drought-responsive gene expression via ABREs, acting downstream of the Ca2+-dependent kinase BnaCPK5. [26]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainABA-responsive element binding factor (ABF/AREB), basic leucine zipper (bZIP) transcription factor binding ABRE elements in ABA- and drought-inducible promoters.
Breeding Application ValueBnaABF4, together with BnaABF3, constitutes a core ABA-signaling transcription factor targeted by BnaCPK5; manipulating BnaABF4 activity or its phosphorylation status offers a means to modulate ABA-responsive gene expression (including LEA-like RD29B) and may contribute to engineering improved drought tolerance in oilseed rape when combined with modulation of BnaCPK5.
Environment / TreatmentProteasome inhibitor MG132 (50 μM) applied to test 26S proteasome-dependent degradation of BnaABF4-LUC; luciferin application to leaves to visualize LUC activity during transient expression assays.

PtrBES1-7 (Populus trichocarpa) Plant

Key Findings

  • PtrBES1-7 is one of 14 BES1 transcription factors identified in Populus trichocarpa and shows strong induction in leaves and roots by ABA, PEG6000-simulated drought, and NaCl treatments, with the highest expression under drought. [27]
  • Promoter analysis indicates that PtrBES1 family members, including PtrBES1-7, harbor multiple ABA- and stress-responsive cis-elements such as ABRE and MBS, consistent with their stress-inducible expression. [27]
  • Transient overexpression of PtrBES1-7 in P. trichocarpa reduces PEG6000-induced ROS (O_2^- and H_2O_2) accumulation, decreases MDA and EL levels, and increases SOD and POD activities compared with control plants. [27]
  • RNAi silencing of PtrBES1-7 leads to higher ROS, MDA, and EL and lower antioxidant enzyme activities under drought, indicating increased oxidative damage and reduced drought tolerance. [27]
  • Collectively, PtrBES1-7 acts as a positive regulator of drought tolerance in P. trichocarpa by enhancing ROS scavenging and protecting cell membranes from oxidative damage. [27]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of PtrBES1-7 in Populus trichocarpa enhances tolerance to PEG6000-simulated drought stress by reducing ROS (O_2^- and H_2O_2) accumulation and membrane lipid peroxidation, while RNAi silencing of PtrBES1-7 increases ROS levels, oxidative damage, and drought sensitivity. [27]

Regulatory Mechanism

PtrBES1-7 positively regulates drought stress tolerance by enhancing antioxidant defense: its overexpression increases SOD and POD activities under drought, leading to reduced accumulation of O_2^- and H_2O_2, lower MDA levels, and decreased EL, thereby limiting membrane lipid peroxidation and preserving cell membrane integrity. [27]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling-mediated drought response and ROS/oxidative stress pathway; PtrBES1 family members, including PtrBES1-7, are suggested to function in brassinosteroid and ABA signaling and to act as key transcription factors regulating oxidative stress responses under abiotic stress. [27]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainBES1_N domain (PF05687) transcription factor of the BES1/BZR1 family.
Expression PatternPtrBES1-7 is strongly induced in both leaves and roots by ABA (200 μM), PEG6000 (7%) drought, and 200 mM NaCl; under PEG6000 drought, PtrBES1-7 shows the highest induction among PtrBES1 genes, with leaf expression increasing to about 10-fold above control at 24 h.
Validation MethodTransient overexpression and RNAi knockdown in P. trichocarpa followed by PEG6000-induced drought stress, RT-qPCR verification of transgene expression, physiological assays (MDA, EL, ROS quantification, SOD and POD activities), and histochemical DAB and NBT staining.
Breeding Application ValuePtrBES1-7 is a drought stress-responsive transcription factor whose overexpression enhances ROS scavenging and drought tolerance in Populus trichocarpa, providing a promising genetic target and theoretical basis for breeding drought-resistant woody plants and improving drought resilience in poplar cultivation.
Environment / TreatmentPEG6000 (7%) to simulate drought stress; 200 mM NaCl for salt stress; 200 μM ABA for hormone treatment; assays typically performed up to 24–48 h after treatment.

AtTRE1 (AtTRE1L / AtTRE1S) (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • The single AtTRE1 transcript in Arabidopsis produces two trehalase isoforms, AtTRE1L and AtTRE1S, via alternative translation initiation at two in-frame AUG codons; AtTRE1S lacks the N-terminal transmembrane domain. [28]
  • AtTRE1L is an N-glycosylated plasma-membrane protein with its catalytic domain in the apoplast, whereas AtTRE1S localizes to the cytoplasm and nucleus via a functional KRP112–114 nuclear localization signal. [28]
  • AtTRE1L and AtTRE1S physically interact, and the interaction with AtTRE1L prevents nuclear localization of AtTRE1S by sequestering it in the cytoplasm. [28]
  • Both AtTRE1 isoforms are phosphorylated and activated by the calcium-dependent protein kinase AtCPK10; phosphorylation of Ser71 and Thr128 is essential for trehalase activity. [28]
  • In tre1-2 mutant plants, expression of either AtTRE1L or AtTRE1S restores trehalase activity and growth on trehalose and normalizes drought-related stomatal phenotypes, but AtTRE1S-only lines exhibit significantly higher drought tolerance, reduced leaf water loss, and smaller stomatal aperture than AtTRE1L or WT AtTRE1 lines. [28]
  • Drought stress shifts the AtTRE1L/AtTRE1S protein ratio toward the short isoform by decreasing AtTRE1L levels, suggesting stress-dependent regulation of isoform abundance to favor AtTRE1S-mediated responses. [28]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Trehalase AtTRE1, through its two isoforms, promotes ABA-induced stomatal closure, reduces stomatal aperture and leaf water loss, and enhances plant survival under drought stress, with the short cytoplasmic/nuclear isoform AtTRE1S being significantly more effective than the full-length plasma-membrane AtTRE1L in mediating drought tolerance. [28]

Regulatory Mechanism

Alternative translation initiation at two in-frame AUG codons in a single AtTRE1 transcript generates AtTRE1L and AtTRE1S isoforms. AtTRE1L is N-glycosylated at multiple asparagine residues, consistent with a secretory/membrane protein. Both isoforms are phosphorylated by AtCPK10; phosphorylation of Ser71 and Thr128 is required for full trehalase activity, as S71A T128A mutations abolish activity while phosphomimic S71E T128E restores it. Physical interaction between AtTRE1L and AtTRE1S through the N-terminal cytosolic region of AtTRE1L sequesters the short isoform in the cytoplasm and prevents its nuclear localization. Under drought stress, the relative protein level of AtTRE1S increases due to a decrease in AtTRE1L, favoring nuclear/cytosolic AtTRE1S function. [28]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling and Ca2+-dependent stomatal closure pathway in guard cells, where drought/ABA-induced signaling activates AtCPK10, which phosphorylates and activates AtTRE1, leading to trehalose hydrolysis and promotion of stomatal closure. [28]

Biosynthetic: Trehalose catabolic pathway / trehalose hydrolysis as part of trehalose metabolism. [28]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainTrehalose-hydrolyzing enzyme trehalase with an N-terminal transmembrane segment (aa 46–63) and a nuclear localization signal motif KRP112–114; multiple N-glycosylation motifs (N193, N292, N391, N427) in the luminal/apoplastic domain.
Expression PatternTrehalase protein is strongly expressed in stomatal guard cells of 4-week-old rosette leaves; in WT AtTRE1-complemented plants both isoforms are abundant in guard cells, with the short nuclear/cytosolic isoform relatively enriched under drought stress due to reduced AtTRE1L levels.
Breeding Application ValueAtTRE1 is a promising target to improve drought stress tolerance via manipulation of trehalose metabolism in guard cells. Complementation of the tre1-2 mutant with AtTRE1, and especially with the short AtTRE1S isoform, enhances survival after severe drought and reduces water loss by promoting ABA-induced stomatal closure without altering stomatal density. Increasing AtTRE1S abundance or activity in guard cells, or modulating the AtTRE1L/AtTRE1S isoform ratio, could be used in breeding or engineering strategies to enhance drought resilience in Arabidopsis and potentially in crops by fine-tuning stomatal behavior.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress induced by withholding water for 20 days until soil relative water content ~22%; dehydration of 10-day-old seedlings by removal from liquid medium for 5 h; ABA treatment (0 or 20 μM) during stomatal aperture assays; detached rosette leaves exposed to ambient conditions for up to 3 h for water loss measurements; growth on trehalose-containing medium (25 mM trehalose).

AtCPK10 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • AtCPK10 directly interacts with both AtTRE1 isoforms (AtTRE1L and AtTRE1S) as shown by BiFC assays, indicating that trehalase is a direct kinase target. [28]
  • Phos-tag mobility shift assays in Atcpk10 protoplasts demonstrate that co-expression of constitutively active AtCPK10ac induces AtTRE1 phosphorylation, while mutation of Ser71 and Thr128 reduces but does not entirely abolish the shift, indicating multiple phosphorylation sites. [28]
  • Trehalase activity is significantly increased in AtCPK10 overexpression plants and decreased in Atcpk10 knockout plants compared with wild-type, confirming that AtCPK10 positively regulates trehalase activity in vivo. [28]
  • In the context of drought and ABA signaling, AtCPK10 is proposed to act downstream of ABA/Ca2+ elevation and upstream of AtTRE1, providing a mechanistic link between Ca2+-dependent kinase signaling and trehalose metabolism in guard cells. [28]

Core Phenotypic Effect

AtCPK10 phosphorylates and activates AtTRE1; loss of AtCPK10 reduces trehalase activity, whereas AtCPK10 overexpression increases trehalase activity in flowers and siliques, indicating that AtCPK10 positively regulates trehalase function linked to drought and ABA-induced stomatal responses. [28]

Regulatory Mechanism

AtCPK10, a Ca2+-dependent kinase, directly phosphorylates AtTRE1 at multiple sites including Ser71 and Thr128, as shown by Phos-tag mobility shift assays; these phosphorylation events are required for full trehalase activity. Phosphorylation is stimulated by an activated AtCPK10ac variant in Atcpk10 protoplasts, and trehalase activity is positively correlated with AtCPK10 expression level in planta. [28]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA and Ca2+-dependent guard cell signaling pathway, in which drought/ABA-induced Ca2+ influx activates AtCPK10, which in turn phosphorylates and activates AtTRE1 to promote stomatal closure. [28]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCalcium-dependent protein kinase (CDPK/CPK) with an N-terminal myristoylation site (Gly2) important for membrane association.
Breeding Application ValueBecause AtCPK10 directly phosphorylates and activates AtTRE1, modulating AtCPK10 activity provides an upstream handle to tune trehalase activity and thereby influence ABA-dependent stomatal closure and drought responses. Enhancing AtCPK10 activity (e.g., through overexpression or favorable alleles) could be used to strengthen AtTRE1-mediated control of trehalose metabolism in guard cells, complementing direct manipulation of AtTRE1 isoforms in breeding or engineering strategies for drought-tolerant crops.
Environment / TreatmentConditions that activate AtCPK10 in the proposed pathway include drought stress and ABA-induced Ca2+ influx into guard cell cytosol; in this study, activation was mimicked by using a constitutively active AtCPK10ac variant in protoplasts.

CfAPX (Cryptomeria fortunei) Plant

Key Findings

  • CfAPX cDNA from Cryptomeria fortunei encodes a 249-aa, 27.22 kDa hydrophilic ascorbate peroxidase belonging to the cytosolic APX (cAPX) subgroup and plant peroxidase family, with conserved heme-, substrate- and K+-binding sites. [29]
  • Subcellular localization shows CfAPX is cytoplasmic and lacks transcriptional self-activation activity in yeast. [29]
  • CfAPX is constitutively expressed in C. fortunei with highest levels in cones and is strongly induced by salt, drought, heat and cold stresses in both C. fortunei and CfAPX-overexpressing Arabidopsis. [29]
  • Heterologous expression of CfAPX in yeast and overexpression in Arabidopsis significantly enhance tolerance to salt, drought, heat and freezing by increasing antioxidant enzyme activities and AsA content, reducing H2O2 and MDA accumulation, and maintaining chlorophyll content, PSII efficiency and survival under stress. [29]

Core Phenotypic Effect

CfAPX, a cytosolic ascorbate peroxidase from C. fortunei, confers enhanced tolerance to salt, drought, heat and freezing stress by increasing antioxidant capacity, reducing ROS and membrane lipid peroxidation, and maintaining chlorophyll content and PSII photochemical efficiency. [29]

Regulatory Mechanism

CfAPX overexpression increases APX activity and AsA content, enhancing the AsA-dependent reduction of H2O2 and lowering H2O2 and MDA accumulation; this is accompanied by higher activities of other antioxidant enzymes (SOD, POD, CAT) and better preservation of chlorophyll and PSII photochemistry, thereby mitigating oxidative damage under abiotic stress. [29]

Pathways

Regulatory: Antioxidant defense and ROS-scavenging pathway via the ascorbate–glutathione cycle during abiotic stress responses. [29]

Biosynthetic: Ascorbate–glutathione (AsA–GSH–NADPH) cycle for H2O2 scavenging in ROS metabolism [29]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAscorbate peroxidase (APX), member of the plant peroxidase (POD) family, containing conserved heme-binding, substrate-binding and K+-binding sites.
Expression PatternIn C. fortunei, CfAPX is constitutively expressed with highest transcript levels in cones, followed by young needles, and much lower levels (about 20–25% of cones) in stems, seeds and roots. Under salt (200 mM NaCl), drought (10% PEG-6000), heat (42°C) and cold (4°C) treatments, CfAPX expression in C. fortunei is induced, typically increasing to 3.4–3.6-fold of control at 12 h for salt and drought, peaking early (about 306% of control at 2 h) under heat then decreasing, and reaching about 1.6-fold at 24 h under cold. In Arabidopsis, CfAPX expression in 35S::CfAPX plants is much higher than in WT and further increases transiently under salt, drought, heat and cold, peaking at 2.5–4.7-fold the WT level at specific time points.
Validation MethodHeterologous overexpression in yeast under NaCl and mannitol stress; stable overexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana (35S::CfAPX) followed by salt, drought, heat and freezing stress assays; qRT-PCR analysis of CfAPX stress-inducible expression in C. fortunei and transgenic Arabidopsis; physiological and biochemical measurements (antioxidant enzyme activities, AsA, H2O2, MDA, chlorophyll and chlorophyll fluorescence).
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of CfAPX enhances tolerance to salt, drought, heat and freezing by improving ROS scavenging and protecting photosystem II, indicating that CfAPX is a promising candidate gene for molecular breeding or genetic engineering of C. fortunei and other trees to improve multi-abiotic-stress tolerance and expand planting in harsh environments.
Environment / TreatmentSalt stress (up to 200 mM NaCl in media or solution), osmotic/drought stress (mannitol up to 400 mM; 10% PEG-6000), heat stress (40°C for Arabidopsis, 42°C for C. fortunei), cold stress (4°C) and freezing stress (-6°C) applied to seedlings and cuttings for defined durations.

AtALDH7B4 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • Endogenous AtALDH7B4 transcripts are strongly induced in Arabidopsis by 300 mM NaCl for 5 days, whereas Eutrema requires 600 mM NaCl to induce its ortholog, indicating different salinity thresholds between the glycophyte and halophyte. [30]
  • The AtALDH7B4 promoter fused to GUS recapitulates endogenous salt-induced expression and is also induced by drought, dehydration and wounding. [30]
  • When proAtALDH7B4::GUS is integrated into the Eutrema genome, induction requires 600 mM NaCl, showing that the genetic background, rather than the promoter sequence alone, determines the NaCl threshold for ALDH7B4 activation. [30]
  • Comparative promoter analyses indicate that conserved ACGT-containing motifs, including a G-box, are essential cis-elements shared with EsALDH7B4 for stress-inducible expression. [30]

Core Phenotypic Effect

AtALDH7B4 expression is strongly induced by salinity and other abiotic stresses, with a low salt threshold (300 mM NaCl) in Arabidopsis; its promoter confers robust, stress-inducible expression that is modulated by the host genetic background. [30]

Regulatory Mechanism

Promoter cis-elements, including ACGT-containing motifs and a conserved G-box (CACGTG), are essential for basal and stress-induced expression and confer similar stress responsiveness to that of the Eutrema ortholog when assayed in the same genetic background. [30]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAldehyde dehydrogenase family 7B (ALDH7B4) enzyme.
Expression PatternEndogenous AtALDH7B4 transcripts show strong induction after 5 days of 300 mM NaCl treatment in Arabidopsis; promoter-driven GUS activity is detected in multiple vegetative and reproductive organs and is induced by salinity, drought, dehydration and wounding.
Validation MethodRNA blot analysis of endogenous ALDH7B4 transcripts under graded NaCl treatments; stable transgenic Arabidopsis and Eutrema lines carrying proAtALDH7B4::GUS; GUS histochemical and fluorometric assays under salinity, drought, dehydration and wounding.
Breeding Application ValueDissection of AtALDH7B4 promoter cis-elements and the strong, stress-inducible expression pattern provides a template for engineering stress-responsive promoters in crops. Because the same AtALDH7B4 promoter shows different NaCl-induction thresholds in Arabidopsis versus Eutrema genetic backgrounds, it can be used to fine-tune expression levels of stress-tolerance genes depending on host species, supporting breeding or engineering for tailored salinity and drought tolerance.
Environment / TreatmentSalinity stress (100, 300 and 600 mM NaCl for 5 days; 250 mM NaCl for 10 days), drought stress (14 days without watering), dehydration and mechanical wounding of leaves.

EsALDH7B4 (Eutrema salsugineum) Plant

Key Findings

  • EsALDH7B4 is the ortholog of AtALDH7B4 in Eutrema salsugineum and its expression is induced by salinity, drought and wounding, but in the halophyte induction requires higher salinity (600 mM NaCl) than in Arabidopsis. [30]
  • EsALDH7B4 promoter::GUS fusions show activity in multiple tissues, with increasing activity during seed maturation, indicating developmental regulation similar to AtALDH7B4. [30]
  • Both AtALDH7B4 and EsALDH7B4 promoters respond to the same salt thresholds as their endogenous genes when integrated into the respective genetic backgrounds, demonstrating that the host background largely determines the NaCl induction threshold. [30]
  • Promoter deletion analysis in Arabidopsis reveals that a 95 bp region containing ACGT motifs and a G-box is essential for expression and stress responsiveness, while deletion of a 38 bp TC-rich motif (present only in EsALDH7B4) increases promoter activity. [30]
  • The TC-rich motif negatively regulates EsALDH7B4 promoter activity by recruiting MYB-like transcription factors that act as transcriptional repressors. [30]

Core Phenotypic Effect

EsALDH7B4 is a stress-inducible gene whose expression in Eutrema requires higher salinity (600 mM NaCl) than in Arabidopsis; its promoter activity depends on conserved ACGT motifs and a G-box for activation, while a unique TC-rich motif mediates repression and lowers expression under stress. [30]

Regulatory Mechanism

ACGT-containing motifs, including a G-box (CACGTG), are essential for EsALDH7B4 promoter activity and stress responsiveness; deletion of this region nearly abolishes GUS expression. A species-specific 38 bp TC-rich motif recruits MYB-like transcriptional repressors, leading to reduced promoter activity and likely contributing to the higher salt threshold required for induction in Eutrema. [30]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAldehyde dehydrogenase family 7B (ALDH7B4) enzyme.
Expression PatternEndogenous EsALDH7B4 shows clear induction only at 600 mM NaCl in E. salsugineum, whereas lower concentrations are ineffective; EsALDH7B4 promoter-driven GUS is active in rosette leaves, cauline leaves, flowers, green and mature siliques, and seeds, with promoter activity increasing during seed maturation; in Arabidopsis and Eutrema backgrounds, the EsALDH7B4 promoter is induced by salinity, drought, dehydration and wounding, but induction requires 600 mM NaCl in Eutrema versus 300 mM in Arabidopsis.
Validation MethodRNA blot analysis of endogenous EsALDH7B4 under graded NaCl treatments; stable transgenic A. thaliana and E. salsugineum plants harboring EsALDH7B4 promoter::GUS; promoter 5′-deletion series and TC-motif deletion (noTC) constructs; GUS staining and fluorometric assays under salinity and drought; yeast one-hybrid, EMSA, and transient dual-luciferase assays to test cis-element function and MYB-like-mediated repression.
Breeding Application ValueThe EsALDH7B4 promoter contains a short core region with essential ACGT motifs and a G-box that confer strong, stress-inducible expression and could be exploited as an inducible promoter module for engineering abiotic stress tolerance. The species-specific 38 bp TC-rich motif, which recruits MYB-like repressors to attenuate expression, represents a tunable cis-element whose removal or modification could enhance EsALDH7B4 or transgene expression at lower salinity, potentially contributing to improved stress tolerance when transferred to crops.
