Environment / TreatmentHigh salinity (300 and 600 mM NaCl for 5 days; 250 mM NaCl for 10 days), drought (14 days water withholding), dehydration, mechanical wounding; stress treatments applied to leaves and whole plants.

AtMYB-like (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • Yeast one-hybrid screening with the 38 bp TC-rich motif from the EsALDH7B4 promoter identifies AtMYB-like (AT5G56840) as the sole positive binding transcription factor. [30]
  • EMSA demonstrates that recombinant AtMYB-like protein specifically binds the TC-rich motif of the EsALDH7B4 promoter. [30]
  • GAL4BD-VP16-AtMYB-like fusion significantly represses VP16-mediated activation of a GAL4-LUC reporter, confirming that AtMYB-like functions as a transcriptional repressor. [30]
  • Transient dual-luciferase assays show that AtMYB-like slightly but significantly represses EsALDH7B4 promoter activity, but does not repress AtALDH7B4 promoter activity, indicating promoter specificity mediated by the TC-rich motif. [30]

Core Phenotypic Effect

AtMYB-like functions as a transcriptional repressor that binds the TC-rich motif of the EsALDH7B4 promoter and represses its activity, while not repressing the AtALDH7B4 promoter. [30]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a transcriptional repressor: when fused to GAL4BD-VP16, it suppresses the activation capability of VP16. Directly binds the TC-rich motif in the EsALDH7B4 promoter, leading to reduced promoter activity in transient assays. [30]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainMYB-like transcription factor.
Validation MethodIdentified by yeast one-hybrid screening using the 38 bp TC-rich motif as bait; binding to the TC-rich motif confirmed by EMSA with recombinant MBP–AtMYB-like protein; transcriptional repression demonstrated by transient dual-luciferase assays in Arabidopsis protoplasts using GAL4BD-VP16-AtMYB-like fusion and EsALDH7B4::LUC reporters.
Breeding Application ValueAtMYB-like is a transcriptional repressor that specifically binds the TC-rich motif in the EsALDH7B4 promoter and represses its activity. Modulating AtMYB-like-like activity or disrupting its binding site could be used to de-repress stress-inducible promoters derived from EsALDH7B4, providing a regulatory handle to enhance or fine-tune expression of stress-tolerance genes in Arabidopsis or other crops.

EsMYB-like (Eutrema salsugineum) Plant

Key Findings

  • EsMYB-like is identified as the Eutrema ortholog of the Arabidopsis MYB-like transcriptional repressor (AT5G56840). [30]
  • EMSA assays show that EsMYB-like binds specifically to the TC-rich motif present in the EsALDH7B4 promoter. [30]
  • Transient dual-luciferase assays demonstrate that EsMYB-like represses EsALDH7B4 promoter activity but does not repress the AtALDH7B4 promoter, indicating dependence on the TC-rich motif. [30]
  • Together with AtMYB-like, EsMYB-like confirms that MYB-like repressors act through the TC-rich motif to negatively regulate EsALDH7B4 expression. [30]

Core Phenotypic Effect

EsMYB-like is an Eutrema ortholog of AtMYB-like that binds the TC-rich motif in the EsALDH7B4 promoter and represses its activity. [30]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a transcriptional repressor by binding the TC-rich motif in the EsALDH7B4 promoter and reducing its transcriptional activity in transient reporter assays. [30]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainMYB-like transcription factor (ortholog of Arabidopsis AtMYB-like).
Validation MethodEMSA using recombinant MBP–EsMYB-like protein to demonstrate binding to the TC-rich motif; transient dual-luciferase assays in Arabidopsis protoplasts co-expressing EsMYB-like with EsALDH7B4::LUC reporter to show promoter repression.
Breeding Application ValueEsMYB-like, as a MYB-like repressor that binds the TC-rich motif in the EsALDH7B4 promoter, provides a species-specific negative regulatory factor that could be targeted to modulate EsALDH7B4 expression and potentially adjust salinity thresholds for gene induction in the halophyte or in heterologous crops using EsALDH7B4-based promoters.

OsSOS2 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • Stable OsSOS2 overexpression (OESOS2) and knockdown (KDSOS2) lines were generated in the high-yielding but stress-sensitive rice cultivar IR64 and molecularly confirmed by PCR, Southern blot, and siRNA detection. [31]
  • Under salinity, OsSOS2-OE plants maintain higher root and shoot growth, higher relative water content, lower electrolyte leakage and MDA, and a higher K+/Na+ ratio than WT and KD plants, indicating improved ionic and osmotic stress tolerance. [31]
  • OsSOS2-OE plants retain more chlorophyll, accumulate more proline, and maintain favorable macro- and micro-element profiles (including K+, P+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and Si) in roots and shoots under salinity compared with WT and KD plants. [31]
  • Microscopy shows OsSOS2 overexpression reduces Na+ accumulation and cell damage in roots, preserves root cortical and vascular bundle cell integrity, and maintains stomatal opening under salinity, whereas KD plants show severe cell damage and stomatal closure. [31]
  • OsSOS2-OE plants exhibit lower H2O2 accumulation and higher SOD, APX, and CAT activities under salinity, linking OsSOS2 to enhanced antioxidant-mediated ROS detoxification and redox homeostasis. [31]
  • OsSOS2 overexpression increases transcript abundance of stress-responsive genes OsHBP1b, OsMYB, OsRR21, OsSOS3, and OsSOS1 under salinity, suggesting OsSOS2 acts within a broader regulatory network for salinity tolerance. [31]
  • At the reproductive stage, OsSOS2-OE lines sustain panicle length, spikelet number, grain number per panicle, and thousand-grain weight much better than WT and KD lines under both salinity and drought, demonstrating improved yield stability under osmotic stresses. [31]
  • The study concludes that OsSOS2 has multifaceted regulatory roles integrating ion, osmotic, and redox homeostasis, making it a promising target for biotechnological improvement of salinity and drought tolerance in rice. [31]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of OsSOS2 enhances salinity and drought tolerance in rice by maintaining growth, ion homeostasis, redox homeostasis, stomatal function, and yield under stress, whereas OsSOS2 knockdown increases sensitivity. [31]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsSOS2 overexpression enhances activities of antioxidant enzymes (SOD, APX, CAT) leading to improved ROS scavenging and redox homeostasis; it promotes proline accumulation for osmotic adjustment and maintains higher cytosolic Ca2+ levels that are proposed to activate SOS3 and SOS1 to restore ion homeostasis under salinity. [31]

Pathways

Regulatory: SOS (Salt Overly Sensitive) signaling pathway controlling Na+ efflux and ion homeostasis, integrated with antioxidant-mediated redox homeostasis and osmotic stress responses under salinity and drought. [31]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSOS2 family serine/threonine protein kinase
Expression PatternOsSOS2 is constitutively expressed in WT and overexpression plants under control and salinity, with higher transcript abundance in OESOS2 lines under salt stress; OsSOS2 transcript is undetectable in KDSOS2 lines under both control and salinity conditions.
Validation MethodFunctional validation via constitutive overexpression of OsSOS2 and RNAi-mediated knockdown in rice cv. IR64 followed by comprehensive physiological, biochemical, anatomical, microscopic, molecular, and agronomic analyses under salinity and drought stresses.
Breeding Application ValueOsSOS2 is a strong candidate gene for engineering and marker-assisted introgression to develop high-yielding rice cultivars with enhanced tolerance to combined salinity and drought stress, maintaining grain yield and quality under adverse conditions and potentially expanding cultivation to salt- and drought-prone areas.
Environment / TreatmentHigh salinity (NaCl-induced and electrical conductivity-based salinization) and cyclic drought stress imposed at seedling, vegetative, and reproductive stages.

MsSPL8 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • A single efficient gRNA (gRNA1) enabled high-frequency, specific CRISPR/Cas9-induced indels in up to three of four MsSPL8 alleles in tetraploid alfalfa, with no detectable off-target mutations. [32]
  • Edited MsSPL8 genotypes displayed distinct morphological changes, including reduced internode length, shorter plants, smaller leaves and early flowering, with phenotypic severity increasing with the number of mutated MsSPL8 alleles (allelic dosage effect). [32]
  • MsSPL8-edited plants exhibited improved drought resilience, wilting at lower soil moisture levels, maintaining higher leaf relative water content and photosynthetic rate under drought, and achieving 100% survival after severe water deficit compared to 50% survival in controls. [32]
  • MsSPL8 edits were stably inherited, and transgene-free F1 progeny carrying a range of edited allelic combinations (0–4 of 4 alleles edited) were generated by crossing with an elite cultivar, providing a practical route to deploy MsSPL8 alleles in breeding. [32]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Reduced MsSPL8 function via CRISPR/Cas9-induced indels causes smaller leaves, shorter internodes and reduced plant height, decreased shoot and root biomass and root length at high allelic mutation dosage, accelerated flowering, and enhanced drought resilience (delayed wilting, higher leaf relative water content, maintenance of photosynthetic rate and full survival under severe water deficit) [32]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSQUAMOSA PROMOTER-BINDING PROTEIN-LIKE (SPL) transcription factor with a single SBP (SQUAMOSA promoter-binding protein) DNA-binding domain
Expression PatternMsSPL8 alleles (MSAD_231217 and MSAD_231229) are highly expressed in flowers, moderately in elongated stems, and at low levels in roots, nodules and leaves
Validation MethodCRISPR/Cas9-mediated editing of MsSPL8 alleles followed by molecular confirmation (ddPCR, T7E1 assay, Sanger sequencing) and phenotypic evaluation of growth and drought stress responses in edited versus empty vector control plants
Breeding Application ValueCRISPR/Cas9 editing of MsSPL8 generated alfalfa genotypes with heritable, allele-dosage-dependent mutations that confer enhanced drought resilience (delayed wilting, improved leaf water status, maintenance of photosynthesis and full survival after severe water deficit), along with earlier flowering. Non-transgenic F1 plants carrying MsSPL8 edits in one or more alleles were obtained after crossing with cultivar AC Blue J, demonstrating the feasibility of producing edited, transgene-free germplasm. These MsSPL8 loss-of-function or partial-loss alleles provide breeding material to improve drought tolerance in alfalfa, with the possibility to tune mutation dosage to balance stress resilience with acceptable biomass and plant stature for forage production.
Environment / TreatmentGreenhouse-grown plants under well-watered conditions versus progressive water-deficit imposed by withholding irrigation until soil volumetric moisture reached ~1%, followed by re-watering

Ac1-SST (Allium cepa) Plant

Key Findings

  • Ac1-SST from onion was cloned and stably integrated as a single-copy transgene into cotton, with confirmed expression in leaves across multiple generations. [33]
  • Ac1-SST-overexpressing cotton plants accumulated 1-kestose (absent in wild-type) and showed higher levels of total soluble sugars, glucose, fructose, and other soluble carbohydrates, particularly under drought stress, accompanied by reduced sucrose levels. [33]
  • Under drought, transgenic plants exhibited higher proline and relative water content and lower MDA than wild-type, indicating improved osmotic adjustment and reduced membrane lipid peroxidation. [33]
  • Transgenic lines maintained higher photosynthetic rates, PSII performance (Fv/Fm, ΦPSII, PI_total), and water use efficiency under drought compared with wild-type. [33]
  • In multi-year field trials under drought, Ac1-SST transgenic lines had more fruiting branches and bolls and significantly higher seed yield per plant and per plot, with reduced yield loss relative to wild-type. [33]
  • These results demonstrate that Ac1-SST functions as an effective fructan biosynthesis gene in cotton, enhancing drought tolerance and yield, and represents a promising gene for breeding drought-resistant cotton and other crops. [33]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of Ac1-SST in cotton enhances drought tolerance and maintains or increases yield under drought by increasing 1-kestose and other soluble sugars, elevating proline and relative water content, reducing malondialdehyde accumulation, and sustaining photosynthetic and PSII performance under water-limiting conditions. [33]

Regulatory Mechanism

Ac1-SST catalyzes transfer of a fructosyl moiety from one sucrose molecule to another to form 1-kestose and glucose, reducing sucrose and increasing low-DP fructans and other soluble carbohydrates, which enhances osmotic adjustment and water retention, reduces membrane lipid peroxidation (lower MDA), and contributes to maintaining photosynthetic capacity and growth under drought. [33]

Pathways

Regulatory: Abiotic stress (drought) response mediated by fructan-driven osmotic adjustment, membrane protection, and maintenance of photosynthetic performance in transgenic cotton. [33]

Biosynthetic: Fructan biosynthesis pathway; sucrose:sucrose 1-fructosyltransferase catalyzing formation of 1-kestose from sucrose (low-degree polymerization fructan). [33]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSucrose:sucrose 1-fructosyltransferase (1-SST), a fructosyltransferase enzyme involved in fructan synthesis.
Expression PatternAc1-SST is constitutively expressed from the CaMV 35S promoter in cotton leaves, with detectable transcripts in all three transgenic lines under both normal irrigation and drought; transcript levels and Ac1-SST enzyme activity are higher under drought stress than under well-watered conditions.
Validation MethodTransgenic overexpression of Ac1-SST driven by CaMV 35S promoter in cotton (Gossypium hirsutum R15) via Agrobacterium-mediated transformation; confirmation of transgene presence and copy number by genomic PCR and Southern blot; verification of transcript accumulation by RT-PCR and qRT-PCR; measurement of Ac1-SST enzyme activity in leaves; quantification of 1-kestose and other soluble sugars by HPLC and colorimetric assays; physiological assays for proline, MDA, and relative water content; greenhouse and multi-year field drought trials with agronomic trait and photosynthetic/fluorescence measurements.
Breeding Application ValueEctopic expression of Ac1-SST in cotton significantly improves drought tolerance and maintains seed yield under field drought, reducing yield loss per square meter by about 20.9% over two years and increasing seed yield per plant by ~28.2% and 20.6% in 2019 and 2020. The gene confers higher boll and fruiting branch numbers and better photosynthetic performance under water deficit, indicating that Ac1-SST is a promising candidate gene for breeding drought-tolerant, high-yield cotton and potentially other crops grown in arid environments.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by withholding irrigation: in the field, plants received only natural precipitation after germination (soil water content can drop to ~11%) compared with normally irrigated controls; in the greenhouse, watering was stopped for up to 32 days (16 days for some enzyme assays) for 45-day-old plants; well-watered plants served as controls.

OsRF1 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsRF1 encodes a small ER-anchored RING-H2 E3 ubiquitin ligase whose transcript is strongly induced by ABA, drought and salt stress. [34]
  • Overexpression of OsRF1 in rice elevates expression of ABA biosynthetic genes (ZEP, ABA4, NCED3/ABA3), increases endogenous ABA levels, and causes ABA-hypersensitive inhibition of shoot growth, reduced GA sensitivity, semi-dwarf growth and enhanced seed dormancy. [34]
  • OsRF1-OE plants show improved drought and salinity tolerance and reduced water loss at leaf and whole-plant levels, correlating with higher ABA accumulation. [34]
  • OsRF1 directly interacts with clade A PP2Cs (especially OsPP2C09) and mediates their polyubiquitination and 26S proteasome-dependent degradation, thereby relieving PP2C inhibition of ABA signaling and indirectly stabilizing the DELLA protein SLR1 to dampen GA-induced growth under stress. [34]

Core Phenotypic Effect

OsRF1 overexpression enhances drought and salt tolerance and reduces leaf and whole-plant water loss by elevating ABA biosynthesis/signaling, while simultaneously restraining GA-dependent shoot elongation through stabilization of the DELLA protein SLR1, resulting in ABA-hypersensitive, semi-dwarf plants with improved stress tolerance. [34]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a RING-H2 E3 ubiquitin ligase that self-ubiquitinates and polyubiquitinates clade A PP2Cs (especially OsPP2C09), targeting them for 26S proteasome-mediated degradation in an ABA- and stress-dependent manner, thereby relieving PP2C inhibition of ABA signaling and indirectly stabilizing SLR1 to attenuate GA signaling. [34]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling pathway and ABA–GA signaling crosstalk in the regulation of drought and salinity responses and growth restraint via SLR1. [34]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSmall membrane-anchored RING-H2 type RING finger protein containing a canonical RING-H2 domain and an N-terminal transmembrane segment.
Expression PatternOsRF1 expression in 10-day-old shoots is induced ~3.25-fold by 10 μM ABA (6 h), up to ~7-fold by drought (2–4 h) and ~2.25-fold by 200 mM NaCl (6 h). Database analysis (RiceXPro) indicates expression in multiple tissues with relatively higher levels in reproductive organs.
Validation MethodFunctional validation primarily by OsRF1 overexpression in rice combined with ABA/GA growth assays and drought/salt tolerance tests, supported by ABA quantification, qRT-PCR of ABA biosynthetic genes, subcellular localization, Y2H/BiFC/co-IP with OsPP2C09, in vitro OsPP2C09 ubiquitination, and cell-free degradation assays.
Breeding Application ValueOsRF1 is a promising transgenic or gene-editing target to improve rice tolerance to drought and salinity by boosting ABA-mediated stress responses and water conservation. However, constitutive overexpression causes semi-dwarfism and enhanced seed dormancy, indicating that breeding applications will likely require controlled expression (e.g., stress- or tissue-specific promoters) or fine-tuning OsRF1 activity to balance improved stress tolerance with acceptable growth and yield.
Environment / TreatmentAbscisic acid (ABA) treatment, 200 mM NaCl salt stress, drought (air-drying and water withholding), and gibberellin (GA3) treatment; proteasome inhibitor MG132 used in some interaction/localization assays.

OsPP2C09 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsPP2C09 is a clade A PP2C from rice that directly interacts with the RING-H2 E3 ligase OsRF1 in yeast and rice protoplasts. [34]
  • OsPP2C09 is polyubiquitinated in vitro by OsRF1 and undergoes 26S proteasome-dependent degradation, which is enhanced by ABA and is faster in OsRF1-OE extracts than in WT. [34]
  • Drought stress and ABA treatment further decrease OsPP2C09 protein stability, linking its degradation to stress-induced ABA signaling. [34]
  • In OsPP2C09 overexpression lines, the DELLA protein SLR1 accumulates to lower levels under GA3 or ABA treatment than in WT, indicating that OsPP2C09 promotes GA-induced SLR1 degradation and participates in ABA–GA cross-regulation of growth. [34]

Core Phenotypic Effect

OsPP2C09 acts as a direct OsRF1 substrate whose ABA- and drought-enhanced, proteasome-dependent degradation relieves PP2C inhibition of ABA signaling, while increased OsPP2C09 expression promotes GA-induced SLR1 degradation, thereby modulating the balance between ABA-mediated stress responses and GA-driven growth. [34]

Regulatory Mechanism

Functions as a clade A PP2C phosphatase that negatively regulates ABA signaling; its protein stability is controlled by OsRF1-dependent polyubiquitination and 26S proteasome degradation, which is stimulated by ABA and drought. Elevated OsPP2C09 promotes GA-induced SLR1 degradation, thereby antagonizing the OsRF1/ABA-mediated stabilization of SLR1 and growth restraint. [34]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling pathway (as a clade A PP2C negative regulator) and GA signaling via modulation of DELLA protein SLR1 stability, forming part of an ABA–GA cross-regulatory network together with OsRF1. [34]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainClade A protein phosphatase type 2C (PP2C; PP2CA).
Validation MethodBiFC and yeast two-hybrid interaction assays with OsRF1, co-immunoprecipitation in rice protoplasts, in vitro ubiquitination and proteasome-dependent degradation assays, and analysis of SLR1 protein stability in OsPP2C09-OE rice seedlings under GA3 and ABA treatments.
Breeding Application ValueBecause OsPP2C09 is a clade A PP2C substrate of OsRF1 that negatively regulates ABA signaling and promotes GA-induced SLR1 degradation, reducing OsPP2C09 activity or enhancing its OsRF1-mediated degradation could be used to strengthen ABA-dependent drought and salinity tolerance and increase SLR1-mediated growth restraint in stress-prone environments. Conversely, OsPP2C09 overexpression favors GA responses and growth at the likely cost of stress tolerance, suggesting it as a tunable node for balancing yield and resilience in rice breeding.
Environment / TreatmentABA treatment, drought stress, and GA3 treatment; MG132 used to block proteasome-mediated degradation in some assays.

PeJRL04 (Phyllostachys edulis) Plant

Key Findings

  • PeJRL04 was identified as a jacalin-related lectin gene in Phyllostachys edulis carrying a single jacalin domain and located on scaffold S3. [35]
  • qRT-PCR showed that PeJRL04 expression is downregulated by abscisic acid and shows a delayed response to salicylic acid, while being upregulated under drought stress. [35]
  • D structural prediction indicates that PeJRL04 forms a typical jacalin β-prism monomer. [35]
  • Yeast two-hybrid assays demonstrated that PeJRL04 does not self-interact, in contrast to PeJRL13. [35]
  • Subcellular localization assays revealed that PeJRL04 accumulates mainly in the nucleus and cell membrane, with a small amount in the nucleoplasm. [35]

Core Phenotypic Effect

PeJRL04 encodes a jacalin-related lectin localized mainly to the nucleus and cell membrane that is transcriptionally responsive to drought and hormone treatments but does not form a self-interacting homodimer in yeast, indicating a monomeric JRL involved in stress and hormone response signaling. [35]

Regulatory Mechanism

No PeJRL04–PeJRL04 interaction detected in yeast two-hybrid assays, indicating that PeJRL04 does not form homodimers under the tested conditions and likely acts as a monomeric jacalin lectin. [35]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainJacalin-related lectin (JRL) with a single jacalin domain (type I JRL)
Expression PatternqRT-PCR showed that PeJRL04 expression responds to hormone and abiotic treatments in moso bamboo leaves: it displays a delayed response to salicylic acid (SA) treatment, with changes evident after 24 h; its transcript level is downregulated at almost all time points after abscisic acid (ABA) treatment; under drought stress simulated by 30% PEG6000, PeJRL04 expression is upregulated compared with 0 h controls.
Validation MethodqRT-PCR expression analysis under hormone (SA, ABA) and drought stress treatments; yeast two-hybrid assay testing PeJRL04–PeJRL04 self-interaction (no homodimerization detected); subcellular localization of 35S:PeJRL04-GFP fusion in tobacco epidermal cells; 3D structural prediction of jacalin domain
Breeding Application ValueAs a drought- and hormone-responsive nuclear and membrane-localized jacalin-related lectin with defined molecular properties, PeJRL04 represents a potential candidate marker or engineering target for modulating drought stress and hormone signaling responses in moso bamboo, providing a reference for selecting stress-related lectin genes for future breeding or biotechnological improvement.
Environment / TreatmentSalicylic acid (100 μM foliar spray), abscisic acid (100 μM foliar spray), drought stress induced by 30% PEG6000 irrigation

PeJRL13 (Phyllostachys edulis) Plant

Key Findings

  • PeJRL13 was identified as a dirigent–jacalin chimeric JRL gene in Phyllostachys edulis, carrying both jacalin and dirigent domains and located on scaffold S12. [35]
  • Structural modeling predicted that PeJRL13 forms a dimer composed of jacalin and dirigent monomers, each containing two sugar-binding sites. [35]
  • qRT-PCR analysis showed that PeJRL13 expression is highest in stems and is induced by drought and salt stress, while being repressed by salicylic acid and methyl jasmonate treatments. [35]
  • Yeast two-hybrid assays demonstrated that PeJRL13 can self-interact to form a homodimer in yeast strain AH109. [35]
  • Subcellular localization assays revealed that PeJRL13 is distributed in the cell membrane, nucleus, and cytoplasm when expressed as a GFP fusion in tobacco epidermal cells. [35]
  • Combined evidence suggests that PeJRL13 participates in abiotic stress responses in moso bamboo in the form of a homodimeric dirigent–jacalin lectin. [35]

Core Phenotypic Effect

PeJRL13 encodes a dirigent–jacalin chimeric lectin that localizes to the cell membrane, nucleus, and cytoplasm and forms a self-interacting homodimer; its transcript is induced by drought and salt stress and repressed by salicylic acid and methyl jasmonate, supporting a role in abiotic stress and hormone response pathways in moso bamboo. [35]

Regulatory Mechanism

PeJRL13 forms a self-interacting homodimer via protein–protein interaction demonstrated by yeast two-hybrid assays, consistent with its predicted dirigent–jacalin dimeric structure and suggesting that homodimerization is important for its role in abiotic stress responses. [35]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDirigent–jacalin chimeric jacalin-related lectin (DJRL), containing both jacalin and dirigent domains and forming a predicted dimer with two glycan-binding sites per monomer
Expression PatternqRT-PCR showed that PeJRL13 expression is highest in stems among the tested tissues (roots, stems, young leaves, mature leaves). Under hormone treatments, PeJRL13 is downregulated or not expressed at early time points after salicylic acid treatment and its expression is inhibited by methyl jasmonate. Under abiotic stress, PeJRL13 is upregulated by drought stress (30% PEG6000) and is reported to be induced by salt stress, consistent with its classification as a stress-responsive dirigent–jacalin lectin.
Validation MethodqRT-PCR expression profiling under hormone (SA, ABA, MeJA) and abiotic (drought, salt) treatments; yeast two-hybrid assays demonstrating PeJRL13–PeJRL13 self-interaction; subcellular localization of 35S:PeJRL13-GFP fusion in tobacco epidermal cells; AlphaFold2-based 3D structural modeling of the dirigent–jacalin dimer.
Breeding Application ValueBecause PeJRL13 is strongly induced by drought and salt stress, repressed by defense-related hormones, localized to key cellular compartments, and forms a homodimer, it represents a promising stress-related candidate gene for future molecular breeding or genetic engineering aimed at improving drought and salt tolerance in moso bamboo or for developing markers associated with stress-resistance pathways.
Environment / TreatmentSalicylic acid (100 μM foliar spray), methyl jasmonate (100 μM foliar spray), abscisic acid (100 μM, tested at the family level), drought stress induced by 30% PEG6000 irrigation, and salt stress induced by 200 mM NaCl

SLOST1 (Solanum lycopersicum) Plant

Key Findings

  • SLOST1 is a tomato SnRK2/OST1 kinase highly similar to Arabidopsis OST1 and is expressed in multiple tissues, including leaves, buds, and fruits. [36]
  • CRISPR/Cas9 loss-of-function slost1 mutants are hypersensitive to drought and osmotic stress, show higher water loss rates and lower leaf temperature, and are delayed in flowering under both normal and drought conditions. [36]
  • SLOST1 directly interacts with and phosphorylates the transcription factor SIVOZ1 at Ser67, enhancing SIVOZ1 protein stability and nuclear accumulation in an ABA-dependent manner. [36]
  • Through SIVOZ1, SLOST1 positively regulates expression of the flowering integrator SFT and is essential for full drought-accelerated flowering in tomato. [36]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Loss-of-function slost1 mutants are hypersensitive to drought and exhibit delayed flowering under both normal and drought conditions, indicating that SLOST1 positively regulates drought tolerance and flowering transition, including drought-accelerated flowering. [36]

Regulatory Mechanism

Upon ABA and drought, SLOST1 kinase activity is activated and it directly phosphorylates SIVOZ1 at Ser67, which enhances SIVOZ1 protein stability, reduces proteasomal degradation, and promotes its nuclear accumulation, thereby enabling SIVOZ1-dependent transcriptional activation of downstream targets such as SFT. [36]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA-activated SnRK2/OST1 signaling pathway that integrates drought stress perception with flowering control via phosphorylation-dependent activation of SIVOZ1 and subsequent induction of the flowering integrator SFT. [36]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSnRK2 family serine/threonine protein kinase OST1 ortholog
Expression PatternHighly expressed in leaves, buds, and fruits at different developmental stages; kinase activity activated by ABA
Validation MethodCRISPR/Cas9-generated knockout alleles in two genetic backgrounds (Micro Tom and Ailsa Craig) combined with drought survival assays, water loss rate and leaf temperature measurements, ABA and mannitol treatments, and flowering time quantification under long- and short-day conditions; kinase activity assays, phosphoproteomics, protein–protein interaction (LCI, Co-IP, BiFC) and Phos-Tag phosphorylation assays to establish molecular function.
Breeding Application ValueBecause SLOST1 simultaneously enhances drought tolerance and promotes flowering, including drought-accelerated flowering, manipulating SLOST1 activity or expression could help develop tomato varieties with improved performance and yield stability under water-limited conditions by optimizing both stress resistance and flowering time.
Environment / TreatmentSoil drought stress (water withholding and controlled low soil water content), osmotic stress induced by 200 mM mannitol, and ABA treatment, all of which modulate SLOST1 activity and associated phenotypes.

SIVOZ1 (Solanum lycopersicum) Plant

Key Findings

  • SIVOZ1 is a VOZ1-type NAC transcription factor highly expressed in multiple tomato tissues and identified as a direct substrate of the kinase SLOST1. [36]
  • SLOST1 phosphorylates SIVOZ1 at Ser67, which is essential for SIVOZ1 protein stability and nuclear localization and is enhanced by ABA treatment. [36]
  • CRISPR/Cas9 slvoz1 mutants are late flowering, hypersensitive to drought/osmotic stress, and less responsive to ABA, indicating that SIVOZ1 positively regulates both drought tolerance and flowering transition. [36]
  • DAP-seq, EMSA, transactivation, and ChIP-qPCR show that SIVOZ1 binds specific promoter motifs and directly activates the flowering integrator SFT, making SIVOZ1 a central regulator of drought-accelerated flowering in tomato. [36]

Core Phenotypic Effect

CRISPR slvoz1 mutants flower later than wild type under both long-day and short-day conditions and are hypersensitive to drought/osmotic stress and less responsive to drought-accelerated flowering, demonstrating that SIVOZ1 positively regulates drought tolerance and flowering transition in tomato. [36]

Regulatory Mechanism

Phosphorylation of SIVOZ1 at Ser67 by SLOST1 increases SIVOZ1 protein stability by reducing proteasome-dependent degradation and promotes its nuclear translocation in an ABA-dependent manner; in the nucleus, SIVOZ1 binds specific DNA motifs (including GC(G/T)T(N)7A(A/C)GC, GATGGCATGCCACGC, CGACCGTGTGTGC, TACGCNAC/T, and ABRE-like ACGTA/G motifs) in target promoters such as SFT to activate transcription. [36]

Pathways

Regulatory: Central transcription factor in an ABA-activated SLOST1–SIVOZ1–SFT module that links drought stress signaling to activation of flowering integrator genes and drought-accelerated flowering. [36]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainVOZ (VASCULAR PLANT ONE-ZINC FINGER) NAC-type transcription factor
Expression PatternHighly expressed in tomato leaves, roots, flower tissues, and fruits; SIVOZ1 protein levels are lower in slost1 mutants than in wild type in leaves, buds, and flowers, whereas SIVOZ1 transcript levels are similar, indicating posttranscriptional regulation.
Validation MethodGeneration of independent CRISPR/Cas9 slvoz1 mutant alleles in two tomato backgrounds followed by flowering time analysis under long- and short-day conditions and drought treatment; drought and osmotic tolerance assays (mannitol, water loss, leaf temperature, electrolyte leakage, ABA sensitivity); in vitro kinase assays and Phos-Tag assays to confirm SLOST1-dependent phosphorylation at Ser67; cell-free degradation and protoplast stability assays; subcellular localization and nuclear translocation assays; DAP-seq, EMSA, transactivation (SFTpro:LUC), and ChIP-qPCR to identify and validate direct DNA targets.
Breeding Application ValueAs a key transcription factor that promotes both drought tolerance and flowering, and directly activates SFT under drought, SIVOZ1 represents a promising target to engineer or select alleles that optimize drought-escape flowering and stress resilience in tomato, potentially improving yield under water-deficit conditions.
Environment / TreatmentABA and drought/osmotic stress (mannitol) treatments, which activate SLOST1 kinase activity and thereby enhance SIVOZ1 phosphorylation, protein stability, and nuclear accumulation.

SFT (Solanum lycopersicum) Plant

Key Findings

  • SFT is an FT ortholog and major flowering-integrator gene in tomato. [36]
  • DAP-seq and motif analysis reveal multiple SIVOZ1 binding motifs in the SFT promoter, and EMSA confirms direct binding of SIVOZ1 but not SLOST1 to these promoter fragments. [36]
  • Transient SFTpro:LUC assays show that SIVOZ1 activates SFT transcription, with SLOST1 slightly enhancing this activation, and ChIP-qPCR confirms in vivo binding of SIVOZ1 to SFT promoter regions. [36]
  • SFT transcript levels are induced by mannitol (drought-mimic) treatment in wild-type seedlings but remain low in slost1 and slvoz1 mutants, correlating reduced SFT expression with delayed flowering and attenuated drought-accelerated flowering. [36]

Core Phenotypic Effect

SFT functions as a major flowering integrator whose expression is induced by drought/ osmotic stress and is directly activated by SIVOZ1; reduced SFT expression in slost1 and slvoz1 mutants correlates with their delayed flowering and attenuated drought-accelerated flowering. [36]

Regulatory Mechanism

SIVOZ1 binds directly to multiple motifs (including ABRE/G-box-related sites) in the SFT promoter and activates SFT transcription; SLOST1 slightly enhances SIVOZ1-mediated SFT transactivation by stabilizing and promoting nuclear accumulation of SIVOZ1. [36]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts as the flowering-integrator component of the SLOST1–SIVOZ1–SFT pathway that mediates drought-accelerated flowering in tomato. [36]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainFT ortholog flowering integrator (florigen-like protein)
Expression PatternActs as a major flowering-integrator gene; SFT transcript levels are reduced in slost1 and slvoz1 mutants compared with wild type under normal conditions and are induced by mannitol (drought-mimicking) treatment in wild-type seedlings but remain relatively low in the mutants.
Validation MethodDAP-seq to identify SFT as a SIVOZ1-bound gene; motif analysis showing multiple SIVOZ1 binding motifs in the SFT promoter; EMSA demonstrating direct binding of SIVOZ1 (but not SLOST1) to SFT promoter fragments; transient SFTpro:LUC assays in N. benthamiana leaves showing SIVOZ1-dependent transcriptional activation; ChIP-qPCR using anti-SIVOZ1 antibody on tomato plants confirming in vivo binding of SIVOZ1 to SFT promoter regions; RT-qPCR showing SFT induction by mannitol and reduced SFT expression in slost1 and slvoz1 mutants.
Breeding Application ValueAs a major flowering-integrator gene directly activated by SIVOZ1 under drought-related conditions, SFT is a key downstream node through which breeding or engineering of the SLOST1–SIVOZ1 module could fine-tune flowering time and drought-escape responses in tomato.
Environment / TreatmentOsmotic/drought-mimicking stress imposed by mannitol, which induces SFT expression in wild-type seedlings and is used to probe drought-associated flowering responses.

TaKNOX11-A (Triticum aestivum) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaKNOX11-A is a wheat KNOXII-type TALE transcription factor with strong expression across developmental stages and high transcriptional induction by drought, salt, MeJA, and ABA treatments. [37]
  • qRT-PCR shows that TaKNOX11-A expression peaks at 6 h of drought, 9 h of salt, and 1 h of MeJA and ABA treatment in wheat seedlings, consistent with promoter enrichment for stress- and hormone-responsive cis-elements. [37]
  • Overexpression of TaKNOX11-A in Arabidopsis increases seed germination rates, root length, and fresh weight under PEG6000- and NaCl-induced stress compared with WT and knat3 plants. [37]
  • Under soil drought and salt treatments, TaKNOX11-A-overexpressing Arabidopsis lines display higher survival rates, higher proline content, and lower malondialdehyde levels than WT and knat3, indicating reduced cellular damage. [37]
  • These results support that TaKNOX11-A functions as a positive regulator of plant drought and salt tolerance and represents a useful genetic resource for improving wheat stress resistance. [37]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of wheat TaKNOX11-A in Arabidopsis enhances drought and salt tolerance, leading to higher survival, stress-resistant growth, and altered stress-related physiological indices under drought (PEG6000 or water withholding) and salt (NaCl) stress. [37]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainTALE superfamily transcription factor; KNOX (KNOTTED-like homeodomain) protein of the KNOXII subclass
Expression PatternAmong KNOXII members, TaKNOX11-A shows one of the highest transcription levels across wheat developmental stages and tissues. qRT-PCR in wheat seedlings shows that TaKNOX11-A is strongly induced by drought, salt, MeJA, and ABA treatments, with peak expression at 6 h under drought, 9 h under salt, and 1 h under MeJA and ABA.
Validation MethodHeterologous overexpression of TaKNOX11-A in Arabidopsis Col-0 and phenotyping under drought and salt stress (seed germination assays on PEG6000 and NaCl media; root length and fresh weight measurements; soil drought and salt tolerance assays with survival scoring), combined with qRT-PCR analysis of TaKNOX11-A expression in wheat under abiotic and hormone treatments and measurement of proline and malondialdehyde contents.
Breeding Application ValueTaKNOX11-A acts as a positive regulator of drought and salt tolerance when expressed in plants and is strongly inducible by multiple abiotic and hormonal signals in wheat, indicating that it is a promising genetic resource for improving wheat stress resistance. The gene could be used in transgenic or genome-editing approaches to enhance drought and salt tolerance and as a candidate locus for developing stress-resilient wheat varieties.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress (PEG6000 in Arabidopsis; dehydration of wheat seedlings on dry filter paper; soil dry-down), salt stress (NaCl), heat stress (42 °C for wheat seedlings), and hormone treatments with ABA, MeJA, ETH, and GA.

CBP60g (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • CBP60g is strongly induced in Arabidopsis leaves by Pst DC3000 infection but this induction is drastically attenuated under combined moderate drought+Pst stress. [38]
  • The reduction in CBP60g expression under combined stress is ABA-dependent, as CBP60g transcripts are higher in the ABA biosynthesis mutant aao3 than in WT under pathogen and combined stress. [38]
  • Promoter activity measured using pCBP60g::GUS reporter lines is reduced by about 50% under combined stress compared with Pst alone, indicating transcriptional repression by drought/ABA. [38]
  • cbp60g and cbp60g sard1 mutants show markedly higher bacterial loads and disease-associated chlorosis under both pathogen-alone and combined stress, with the double mutant being most susceptible. [38]
  • Overexpression of CBP60g in the cbp60g background leads to ~40-fold higher CBP60g transcript levels and significantly reduces disease severity and bacterial CFU under combined stress relative to WT. [38]
  • In npr3 and npr4 mutants, CBP60g expression under combined stress is higher than under pathogen-alone treatment and plants are more resistant, indicating that NPR3/4 mediate drought/ABA-induced repression of CBP60g. [38]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Positive regulator of SA-dependent immunity; CBP60g is required for robust resistance to Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato DC3000, and its suppression under combined drought+pathogen stress leads to markedly higher disease severity and in planta bacterial growth. cbp60g and cbp60g sard1 mutants are highly susceptible, whereas CBP60g overexpression confers enhanced resistance under combined stress. [38]

Regulatory Mechanism

CBP60g functions as a transcriptional activator of SA biosynthesis and signalling genes; under combined drought+Pst stress, ABA signalling suppresses CBP60g transcription, at least partly via NPR3/4-mediated repression of CBP60g promoter activity, leading to reduced SA accumulation and attenuated defence gene induction. [38]

Pathways

Regulatory: SA-mediated immune signalling including PTI, ETI, systemic acquired resistance and SA-independent defence branches, and ABA–SA crosstalk under combined drought and bacterial stress. [38]

Biosynthetic: Upstream transcriptional regulation of salicylic acid biosynthesis and signalling pathways. [38]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCalmodulin-binding Protein 60 family transcription factor; calmodulin-binding, plant-specific transcription factor implicated in SA-pathway regulation.
Expression PatternIn rosette leaves, CBP60g transcripts are strongly induced by Pst DC3000 infection but this induction is drastically attenuated under combined drought+Pst stress; CBP60g shows a modest induction under drought or ABA alone and its induction under pathogen and combined stress is enhanced in the ABA-deficient aao3 mutant. pCBP60g::GUS reporter activity in leaves is reduced by about 50% under combined stress relative to Pst alone, indicating transcriptional repression by drought/ABA.
Validation MethodFunctional analysis using cbp60g-1 loss-of-function mutant, cbp60g-1 sard1-2 double mutant, CBP60g-overexpressing transgenic lines, and pCBP60g::GUS reporter lines; disease scoring, chlorotic leaf area quantification, and in planta bacterial CFU assays under pathogen-alone and combined drought+Pst stress; RT-qPCR of CBP60g and defence marker genes; analysis in ABA-deficient aao3 mutant background.
Breeding Application ValueCBP60g is a master positive regulator of SA-mediated defence whose expression is specifically suppressed by moderate drought via ABA and NPR3/4 during bacterial infection. Loss of CBP60g (alone or together with SARD1) causes strongly enhanced disease and bacterial proliferation under pathogen and combined drought+Pst stress, whereas CBP60g overexpression restores resistance and lowers bacterial load under combined stress. Therefore, maintaining or enhancing CBP60g expression under drought (for example by drought-insensitive promoters or by weakening ABA/NPR3/4-mediated repression) could be used to breed or engineer crops with improved resistance to hemibiotrophic bacterial pathogens during moderate drought, while carefully balancing effects on drought responses.
Environment / TreatmentModerate drought (soil 60% field capacity decreasing to ~35% by 3 dpi), Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato DC3000 infection, combined drought+Pst stress, and ABA/fluridone treatments that modulate CBP60g expression via ABA levels.

SARD1 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • SARD1, like CBP60g, is induced by Pst DC3000 infection but its induction is attenuated under combined moderate drought+Pst stress. [38]
  • SARD1 expression under pathogen and combined stress is higher in the ABA biosynthesis mutant aao3 than in WT, indicating ABA-dependent repression under combined stress. [38]
  • In the npr4 mutant, SARD1 expression is higher under combined stress than under pathogen-alone treatment, and npr4 plants are more resistant, implicating NPR4 in SARD1 repression. [38]
  • cbp60g sard1 double mutants show more severe disease symptoms and higher diseased leaf area than cbp60g single mutants under both pathogen and combined stress, demonstrating additive roles of SARD1 and CBP60g in defence. [38]
  • Suppression of SARD1, together with CBP60g, under combined stress contributes to reduced SA levels and compromised SA-mediated defences. [38]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Positive regulator of SA-dependent immunity that acts additively with CBP60g; reduced SARD1 function, especially together with CBP60g loss, leads to strongly increased disease severity and bacterial proliferation under Pst and combined drought+Pst stress, indicating that SARD1 is indispensable for robust defence under combined stress. [38]

Regulatory Mechanism

SARD1 acts as a transcriptional activator of SA biosynthesis and signalling genes together with CBP60g. Under combined drought+Pst stress, ABA signalling suppresses SARD1 transcription, and NPR4 in particular contributes to repression of SARD1 expression, leading to reduced SA accumulation and weakened defence. [38]

Pathways

Regulatory: SA-mediated defence signalling (PTI, ETI, SAR and SA-independent defence branches) and ABA–SA crosstalk during combined drought and bacterial stress. [38]

Biosynthetic: Upstream transcriptional regulation of salicylic acid biosynthesis and signalling pathways. [38]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCBP60-related plant-specific transcription factor; homolog of calmodulin-binding protein CBP60g but functions independently of calmodulin binding.
Expression PatternIn rosette leaves, SARD1 transcripts are induced by Pst infection and this induction is attenuated under combined drought+Pst stress, though the reduction is less steep than for CBP60g. SARD1 is not notably induced by drought alone but its expression under pathogen and combined stress is enhanced in the ABA-deficient aao3 mutant and in the npr4 mutant under combined stress.
Validation MethodFunctional analysis using sard1-2 single mutant and cbp60g sard1 double mutant in Col-0 background; disease severity and in planta bacterial CFU assays under pathogen-alone and combined drought+Pst stress; RT-qPCR analysis of SARD1 expression under different stress and genetic (aao3, npr3, npr4) backgrounds.
Breeding Application ValueSARD1 is a master transcription factor that acts partly redundantly with CBP60g to promote SA production and defence. Under combined drought+pathogen stress its induction is dampened, and cbp60g sard1 double mutants show greatly enhanced susceptibility compared with single mutants. Enhancing or stabilizing SARD1 (together with CBP60g) under drought could support SA accumulation and defence gene expression during bacterial attack, offering a breeding or engineering strategy to improve bacterial disease resistance in crops exposed to intermittent drought.
Environment / TreatmentModerate drought (60% field capacity), Pst DC3000 infection, combined drought+Pst stress, and altered ABA levels in the aao3 mutant background.

AAO3 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • AAO3 encodes the aldehyde oxidase that catalyses the last step of ABA biosynthesis and is induced by drought stress. [38]
  • Pharmacological ABA application enhances susceptibility to Pst, whereas the ABA biosynthesis inhibitor fluridone and the aao3 mutant reduce disease severity and bacterial load under both pathogen-only and combined drought+Pst stress. [38]
  • Initial bacterial entry (0 dpi CFU) is similar in WT and aao3 plants, but at 3 dpi aao3 plants have significantly lower bacterial loads, indicating that AAO3-mediated ABA affects post-invasion defence. [38]
  • Expression of CBP60g and SARD1 under pathogen and combined stress is higher in aao3 than in WT, showing that AAO3-dependent ABA negatively regulates these master immune regulators under combined stress. [38]
  • AAO3 therefore links drought-induced ABA biosynthesis to suppression of SA-mediated defence via the CBP60g–SARD1 module. [38]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Key ABA biosynthesis enzyme that promotes susceptibility to Pst; aao3 loss-of-function plants are more resistant and show lower in planta bacterial loads than WT under both pathogen-alone and combined drought+Pst stress, indicating that AAO3-mediated ABA production contributes to suppression of defence in these conditions. [38]

Regulatory Mechanism

AAO3 catalyses the final oxidation step in ABA biosynthesis, increasing ABA levels that in turn repress CBP60g/SARD1 expression and SA-mediated defences under combined drought+Pst stress, thereby promoting susceptibility. [38]

Pathways

Regulatory: Abscisic acid biosynthesis and signalling pathway, involved in drought responses and antagonistic cross-talk with SA-mediated defence under combined drought and bacterial stress. [38]

Biosynthetic: Abscisic acid biosynthesis pathway (final oxidation of abscisic aldehyde to ABA). [38]

More Details
Expression PatternAAO3 is induced under drought stress but its transcript level under pathogen-alone and combined drought+Pst stress is similar to control; nonetheless, ABA levels are elevated under drought and pathogen conditions, and defence phenotypes indicate AAO3-dependent ABA contributes to susceptibility. In the aao3 mutant CBP60g and SARD1 induction under pathogen and combined stress is higher than in WT, consistent with reduced ABA-mediated repression.
Validation MethodUse of aao3-4 ABA biosynthesis mutant (AT2G27150; SALK_072361) in Col-0 background; comparison of disease severity and in planta bacterial CFU at 0 and 3 dpi under pathogen-alone and combined drought+Pst stress between WT and aao3; RT-qPCR of AAO3 and other ABA biosynthesis genes; hormone quantification and genetic interaction with CBP60g/SARD1 expression.
Breeding Application ValueAAO3 catalyses the last step of ABA biosynthesis and promotes susceptibility to Pst both under pathogen-alone and combined drought+Pst stress. The aao3 mutant, with reduced ABA production, shows significantly lower bacterial loads and disease severity. Modulating AAO3 activity or expression could therefore be used to reduce drought-induced suppression of defence and improve bacterial disease resistance during moderate drought, though potential trade-offs with drought tolerance must be considered.
Environment / TreatmentModerate drought (60% field capacity), Pst DC3000 infection, combined drought+Pst stress, and exogenous ABA and fluridone treatments that modulate ABA levels.

NPR3 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • NPR3 is a transcriptional repressor of CBP60g/SARD1 identified as an upstream regulator of these master immune genes. [38]
  • CBP60g expression under combined drought+Pst stress is dramatically higher in npr3 mutants than under pathogen-alone treatment, indicating that NPR3 mediates repression of CBP60g under combined stress. [38]
  • npr3 mutants show markedly reduced disease severity and in planta bacterial loads at 3 dpi under both pathogen-only and combined stress compared with WT, while initial bacterial entry is unaffected. [38]
  • NPR3 transcript levels do not differ between pathogen and combined stress, nor between WT and aao3, suggesting that ABA does not regulate NPR3 at the transcriptional level in these conditions. [38]
  • NPR3 thus functions as a key negative regulator that links SA signalling to suppression of the CBP60g–SARD1 module under combined drought and bacterial stress. [38]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Negative regulator of SA-dependent immunity under combined stress; npr3 mutant plants display enhanced resistance and dramatically lower Pst CFU at 3 dpi compared with WT under both pathogen-alone and combined drought+Pst stress, associated with increased CBP60g expression under combined stress. [38]

Regulatory Mechanism

NPR3 acts as an SA-responsive transcriptional corepressor; under combined drought+Pst stress it mediates repression of CBP60g (and to a lesser extent SARD1), thereby suppressing SA biosynthesis and defence signalling. NPR3 is not transcriptionally controlled by ABA under combined stress. [38]

Pathways

Regulatory: Salicylic acid signalling pathway and ABA–SA crosstalk in the context of combined drought and bacterial stress. [38]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainNPR1-like SA receptor/corepressor protein involved in SA-responsive transcriptional regulation.
Expression PatternNPR3 transcript levels do not differ significantly between pathogen-alone and combined drought+Pst treatments or between WT and aao3 mutants, indicating that its role in combined stress is mediated at the level of its regulatory activity rather than transcriptional induction.
Validation MethodUse of npr3-2 T-DNA insertion mutant (AT5G45110; SALK_043055) in Col-0 background; RT-qPCR analysis of CBP60g and SARD1 expression under pathogen-alone and combined drought+Pst stress; phenotypic assessment and in planta bacterial CFU measurements comparing Col-0 and npr3 under these treatments.
Breeding Application ValueNPR3 functions as a transcriptional corepressor that suppresses CBP60g (and thereby SA-mediated immunity) under combined drought+Pst stress. npr3 mutants show elevated CBP60g expression and strong resistance with reduced bacterial loads under pathogen and combined stress. Thus, reducing NPR3 activity could be a strategy to maintain SA-dependent defence under drought and improve resistance to bacterial pathogens, while monitoring possible impacts on SA signalling homeostasis.
Environment / TreatmentModerate drought (60% field capacity), Pst DC3000 infection, combined drought+Pst stress, and altered ABA levels via the aao3 mutant used to test whether ABA regulates NPR3 transcription.

NPR4 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • NPR4 is a transcriptional repressor of CBP60g and SARD1 implicated as an upstream regulator in the suppression of these immune regulators under combined drought+Pst stress. [38]
  • CBP60g and SARD1 expression under combined stress is higher in npr4 mutants than under pathogen-alone treatment, indicating that NPR4 mediates repression of both genes in the combined stress context. [38]
  • npr4 mutants show markedly reduced disease severity and in planta bacterial loads at 3 dpi under both pathogen-only and combined stress relative to WT, while initial bacterial entry is unchanged. [38]
  • NPR4 transcript levels are similar between pathogen-alone and combined stress and between WT and aao3, suggesting that its regulation by ABA is not at the mRNA level. [38]
  • NPR4, together with NPR3, constitutes a key repressor module that attenuates the CBP60g–SARD1 branch of SA-mediated immunity under combined drought and bacterial stress. [38]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Negative regulator of SA-dependent immunity under combined stress; npr4 mutant plants exhibit higher CBP60g and SARD1 expression under combined stress and show enhanced resistance and markedly reduced Pst bacterial loads compared with WT under both pathogen-alone and combined drought+Pst treatments. [38]

Regulatory Mechanism

NPR4 acts as an SA-responsive transcriptional corepressor; under combined drought+Pst stress it represses CBP60g and SARD1, thereby weakening SA biosynthesis and signalling. NPR4 transcription is not regulated by ABA under combined stress, suggesting ABA may modulate its activity at a post-transcriptional level. [38]

Pathways

Regulatory: Salicylic acid signalling network and ABA–SA crosstalk controlling plant immunity under combined drought and bacterial stress. [38]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainNPR1-like SA receptor/corepressor protein involved in negative regulation of SA-responsive transcription.
Expression PatternNPR4 transcript levels are not significantly different between pathogen-alone and combined drought+Pst treatments or between WT and aao3 mutants, indicating that changes in its regulatory activity, rather than its mRNA level, underlie its role in combined stress.
Validation MethodUse of npr4-2 T-DNA insertion mutant (AT4G19660; SALK_098460) in Col-0 background; RT-qPCR analysis of CBP60g and SARD1 expression under pathogen and combined drought+Pst stress; assessment of disease symptoms and in planta bacterial CFU at 0 and 3 dpi in Col-0 versus npr4 plants.
Breeding Application ValueNPR4, together with NPR3, represses CBP60g and SARD1 under combined drought+Pst stress. npr4 mutants show increased expression of both CBP60g and SARD1 and strong resistance with lower bacterial loads under pathogen and combined stress. Targeting NPR4 (alone or with NPR3) could help maintain SA-mediated defences and reduce drought-induced disease susceptibility in crops, while carefully managing potential impacts on SA signalling balance.
Environment / TreatmentModerate drought (60% field capacity), Pst DC3000 infection, combined drought+Pst stress, and altered ABA levels via the aao3 mutant used to test for ABA-dependent transcriptional control of NPR4.

Ghd2 (Oryza sativa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • Transcriptome (Ghd2-CRISPR) and microarray (Ghd2-OE) analyses identified the Rubisco activase gene RCA as a Ghd2-regulated target. [39]
  • Ghd2 overexpression upregulates total RCA transcripts (mainly RCAS under normal conditions and both RCAS and RCAL under drought), while Ghd2-CRISPR reduces RCA expression. [39]
  • Ghd2 directly binds the CACA motif within the RCA promoter via its CCT domain and activates RCA transcription, as shown by dual-luciferase, ChIP-qPCR, and EMSA. [39]
  • Suppression of RCA in a Ghd2-overexpressing background restores drought tolerance to the level of RCA-RNAi plants, demonstrating that Ghd2 reduces drought resistance largely through RCA activation. [39]
  • Ghd2 thereby accelerates drought-induced leaf senescence in rice by upregulating the drought-responsive RCAL transcript of RCA. [39]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Negatively regulates drought tolerance by directly activating RCA (especially the RCAL transcript), which accelerates leaf senescence under drought stress; suppression of RCA largely abolishes the drought-sensitive phenotype of Ghd2-overexpressing plants. [39]

Regulatory Mechanism

Ghd2 directly binds a CACA-repeat motif within the RCA promoter region (−303 to −254 bp relative to ATG) via its CCT domain and activates RCA transcription, as shown by dual-luciferase assays, ChIP-qPCR enrichment between −368 and −50 bp, and EMSA competition assays. [39]

Pathways

Regulatory: Drought-response regulatory pathway in which Ghd2, a CCT-domain transcription factor, promotes drought-induced leaf senescence and decreases drought tolerance by transcriptionally activating RCA/RCAL; this pathway connects CCT-mediated photoperiod flowering networks with abiotic stress responses. [39]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCONSTANS-like (COL) transcription factor containing BBOX and CCT domains; the CCT domain functions as the DNA-binding domain for the RCA promoter.
Validation MethodOverexpression (Ghd2-OE) and CRISPR knockout lines; dual-luciferase reporter assays with RCA promoter fragments in rice protoplasts; ChIP-qPCR using Ghd2-flag plants; EMSA with recombinant Ghd2 and RCA promoter probes; domain deletion analysis (Ghd2-CCT, Ghd2-BBOX) in protoplast assays; genetic interaction with RCA-RNAi (double mutant Ghd2-OE x RCA-RNAi) under drought stress.
Breeding Application ValueGhd2 is a CONSTANS-like transcription factor that directly activates RCA/RCAL transcription and thereby accelerates drought-induced leaf senescence and reduces drought tolerance. Downregulating or knocking out Ghd2, or blocking its ability to activate RCA, could be used to improve drought tolerance and delay senescence in rice, but potential trade-offs with flowering time and yield (regulated by Ghd2 in other studies) must be carefully evaluated in breeding programs.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress applied at seedling (four-leaf stage) and reproductive stages in pots and field; water withholding until severe leaf rolling followed by rewatering.

RCA (Oryza sativa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • RCA was identified as a Ghd2-regulated gene, downregulated in Ghd2-CRISPR plants and upregulated in Ghd2-overexpressing plants. [39]
  • RCA produces two alternative transcripts, RCAS and RCAL; RCAS is predominant under normal conditions, whereas RCAL is strongly induced under drought. [39]
  • Overexpression of RCAL (but only weakly RCAS) increases drought sensitivity and mimics the drought-sensitive phenotype of Ghd2-overexpressing plants at both seedling and reproductive stages. [39]
  • RCA-RNAi plants (suppressing both RCAS and RCAL) and RCAL-only knockdown/knockout plants exhibit enhanced drought tolerance, with less leaf rolling, higher chlorophyll content, lower free amino acid accumulation, and reduced SAG expression under drought. [39]
  • RCAL knockout plants show no significant differences in major agronomic traits compared with wild-type under normal conditions, indicating that RCAL is dispensable for normal growth but critical for drought-induced senescence. [39]
  • RCA-mediated drought responses occur in an ABA-independent manner despite the presence of ABA-related cis-elements in its promoter, as ABA pathway marker genes do not differ between RCA mutants and wild-type under drought. [39]

Core Phenotypic Effect

RCA, particularly its long transcript RCAL, negatively regulates drought tolerance by promoting drought-induced leaf senescence: RCAL overexpression increases drought sensitivity and SAG expression, whereas knockdown or knockout of RCAL (or both RCA transcripts) enhances drought resistance, maintains higher chlorophyll, lowers free amino acid accumulation, and reduces SAG expression under drought without affecting growth under normal conditions. [39]

Regulatory Mechanism

RCA transcription is directly activated by Ghd2 binding to a CACA-repeat motif in the promoter region (−303 to −254 bp), with maximal activation mapped between −672 and −270 bp and ChIP-qPCR enrichment between −368 and −50 bp. The RCA promoter contains ABA-responsive (ABRE) and MYB-binding (MBS) drought-related cis-elements, as well as gibberellin- and auxin-responsive elements, yet RCA-mediated drought responses occur without significant changes in key ABA biosynthesis or catabolism genes, indicating an ABA-independent regulatory mechanism. [39]

Pathways

Regulatory: Component of a drought-response and leaf-senescence pathway downstream of Ghd2: Ghd2 upregulates RCA (especially RCAL) under drought, which promotes chlorophyll loss, free amino acid accumulation, and SAG expression, thereby accelerating leaf senescence and reducing drought tolerance in an ABA-independent manner. [39]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAAA-type ATPase family protein functioning as Rubisco activase, the central enzyme activating Rubisco in photosynthesis.
Expression PatternRCA produces two alternative transcripts, RCAS and RCAL. Under normal growth conditions in rice, RCAS is predominantly expressed and RCAL is low. In Ghd2-overexpressing plants under normal conditions, total RCA transcripts increase mainly through upregulation of RCAS, while RCAL changes little. Under drought stress, expression of both RCAS and RCAL is significantly increased by Ghd2 overexpression and decreased by Ghd2-CRISPR, with RCAL showing a strong drought-induced increase.
Validation MethodTransgenic overexpression lines for RCAL (RCAL-OE) and RCAS (RCAS-OE); RNAi knockdown lines targeting both RCA transcripts (RCA-RNAi) and specifically RCAL (RCAL-RNAi); CRISPR/Cas9 knockout lines for RCAL (RCAL-CR); drought stress assays at seedling and reproductive stages in pots and field; measurements of survival, leaf rolling, chlorophyll content, free amino acid content; qRT-PCR analysis of SAG expression; genetic interaction with Ghd2-OE via double mutant (Ghd2-OE x RCA-RNAi).
Breeding Application ValueKnockdown or knockout of the long RCA transcript RCAL, or simultaneous suppression of both RCA transcripts, confers enhanced drought tolerance and delayed leaf senescence without significant yield loss or major agronomic penalties under normal conditions. RCAL mutants thus represent valuable genetic resources and breeding targets for developing drought-tolerant rice varieties with maintained productivity.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by withholding water at the four-leaf seedling stage and at the reproductive stage (panicle differentiation to grain filling) in pots, PVC tubes, and field; plants were rewatered after severe leaf rolling to assess survival and senescence-related traits.

ZmPIP2;5 (Zea mays) Plant

Key Findings

  • PIP2;5 is a plasma membrane aquaporin of maize expressed in roots and leaves, with detectable protein in epidermal and subsidiary cells of stomatal complexes and lower abundance in guard cells; overexpressing lines show about twofold higher epidermal PIP2;5 signal, while the knockdown line shows reduced signal (~36% reduction) compared with WT. [40]
  • PIP2;5 overexpression increases whole-plant transpiration under well-watered and severe water deficit conditions, whereas under mild water deficit (ψ_soil = -0.1 to -0.2 MPa) and high VPD (~2.2–2.5 kPa) it promotes a stronger decrease in transpiration compared with WT; conversely, pip2;5 knockdown plants show higher transpiration than WT under mild water deficit. [40]
  • In excised leaves, low ABA concentration (0.1 μM) induces a faster and larger decrease in stomatal conductance in PIP2;5 OE lines than in WT, whereas pip2;5 KD leaves show an attenuated decrease; these differences disappear at higher ABA concentrations (≥0.5 μM). [40]
  • In epidermal peels treated with 10 μM ABA, stomata of PIP2;5 OE lines close significantly within 30 min, while WT stomata require 60 min to show a significant reduction in aperture; in contrast, pip2;5 KD stomata are insensitive or even show slightly increased aperture in response to ABA. [40]
  • ABA-induced H2O2 accumulation in guard cells is accelerated and enhanced in PIP2;5 OE lines (significant increase after 2 min of 10 μM ABA), delayed in WT (significant only after 15 min), and abolished in pip2;5 KD plants, indicating that PIP2;5 is required for rapid ABA-dependent ROS build-up in guard cells. [40]
  • These results support a model in which PIP2;5 acts as an H2O2 and water channel in guard-cell and subsidiary-cell plasma membranes, enhancing ABA signalling sensitivity and hydraulic responses, thereby increasing the sensitivity and speed of stomatal closure and transpiration decrease under mild water deficit and high VPD, which can be beneficial for water conservation and growth under moderate drought. [40]

Core Phenotypic Effect

PIP2;5 increases the sensitivity and speed of ABA- and mild water deficit-induced stomatal closure and enhances the decrease in transpiration under mild drought and high vapour pressure deficit; its downregulation reduces ABA sensitivity and slows or abolishes stomatal closure and ROS accumulation in guard cells. [40]

Regulatory Mechanism

Upon mild water deficit or low ABA, PIP2;5 in guard-cell and subsidiary-cell plasma membranes facilitates rapid H2O2 influx into guard cells, leading to earlier and stronger ROS accumulation, which promotes activation of guard-cell ion transport systems, outward water flow, turgor loss and stomatal closure; under high VPD, active PIP2;5 water channels accelerate guard-cell water efflux, causing faster turgor decrease and enhanced sensitivity of transpiration decrease. [40]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in ABA-dependent stomatal closure and hydraulic responses to water deficit and high vapour pressure deficit, in which ABA signalling and apoplastic H2O2 production in guard cells drive ROS accumulation, activation of membrane transport systems, loss of turgor and stomatal closure. [40]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainPlasma membrane intrinsic protein aquaporin, PIP2 subfamily
Expression PatternExpressed in maize root tissues at higher levels than in leaves, and detectable in mature leaf zone, epidermis and stomatal complexes, with strong signal in subsidiary and epidermal cells and weak signal in guard cells.
Breeding Application ValuePIP2;5 is a promising target to improve maize drought resilience and water use efficiency by modifying stomatal closure dynamics. Overexpression increases the sensitivity and speed of stomatal closure to mild soil water deficit and high VPD, enhancing early transpiration decrease, water conservation and maintenance of leaf growth under moderate stress. However, because overexpression also increases transpiration under well-watered and severe drought conditions, optimal manipulation (e.g., tissue- or condition-specific expression) would be needed to exploit PIP2;5 for breeding water-efficient, drought-resilient maize cultivars.
Environment / TreatmentSoil water status: well-watered soil; mild water deficit with ψ_soil = -0.1 to -0.2 MPa; severe water deficit with ψ_soil ≈ -0.3 to -0.6 MPa, imposed by withholding irrigation. Vapour pressure deficit: low VPD ~1 kPa vs high VPD ~2.2–2.5 kPa in growth chambers. ABA treatments: 0.1 μM ABA applied via the transpiration stream to excised leaves for gs measurements; 10 μM ABA applied to epidermal peels for stomatal aperture and ROS measurements; higher ABA concentrations (≥0.5 μM) tested for gs but did not show PIP2;5-dependent differences.

GmEF8 (Glycine max) Plant

Key Findings

  • GmEF8 is a soybean EF-Tu family protein that physically interacts with the Ca2+ sensor GmCBL1, as shown by yeast two-hybrid, BiFC, and GST pull-down assays. 2. GmEF8 localizes mainly to the cytoplasm and nucleus and its transcript is rapidly induced by drought, heat, and ABA but not strongly by salt. 3. GmEF8 expression rescues the drought- and heat-sensitive phenotype of an Arabidopsis EF-Tu T-DNA knockout mutant. 4. Overexpression of GmEF8 in Arabidopsis enhances drought and heat tolerance, increasing root growth and survival under stress. 5. Overexpression of GmEF8 in soybean hairy roots improves drought and heat tolerance, with higher RWC and proline and lower MDA, whereas RNAi knockdown causes hypersensitivity. 6. GmEF8 overexpression upregulates drought-responsive genes GmP5CS and GmRD22, indicating a positive regulatory role in abiotic stress responses. [41]

Core Phenotypic Effect

GmEF8 positively regulates abiotic stress tolerance. Overexpression in Arabidopsis and soybean hairy roots increases drought and heat tolerance, with longer roots, greater root surface area, higher survival under drought, higher RWC and proline, and lower MDA, whereas RNAi knockdown in soybean hairy roots causes hypersensitivity with severe growth inhibition and leaf wilting under stress. [41]

Regulatory Mechanism

GmEF8 physically interacts with the Ca2+ sensor GmCBL1 at the plasma membrane and localizes to the cytoplasm and nucleus. Overexpression of GmEF8 leads to upregulation of drought-responsive genes GmP5CS and GmRD22 and enhanced osmotic adjustment (increased proline, higher RWC, reduced MDA) under drought and heat stress. [41]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts in a Ca2+-sensor (GmCBL1)-associated abiotic stress signaling pathway that positively regulates drought and heat stress responses in soybean. [41]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainElongation factor Tu (EF-Tu) family protein, cytosolic counterpart EF1-alpha-like GTPase.
Expression PatternGmEF8 transcript levels are rapidly and strongly induced by drought, heat, and ABA treatments in soybean seedlings, peaking around 1–2 h, whereas expression changes little in response to salt treatment.
Validation Method35S-driven overexpression of GmEF8 in Arabidopsis; complementation of an Arabidopsis EF-Tu T-DNA mutant with GmEF8; overexpression and RNAi-mediated knockdown of GmEF8 in soybean hairy root composite plants; phenotypic and physiological assays under PEG-induced drought and heat stress; yeast two-hybrid, BiFC, and GST pull-down to validate interaction with GmCBL1; GFP fusion subcellular localization in Arabidopsis protoplasts.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of GmEF8 in soybean hairy root composite plants enhances drought and heat tolerance by increasing relative water content and proline levels and reducing MDA accumulation; GmEF8 also induces drought-responsive genes (e.g., GmRD22, GmP5CS). These results support using GmEF8 as a candidate gene for engineering or breeding soybean cultivars with improved drought and heat tolerance.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress simulated with PEG6000; heat stress (Arabidopsis at 45 °C, soybean at 42 °C); ABA spray (100 μM); salt stress (200 mmol/L NaCl) used for expression analysis.

AT5G60390 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • AT5G60390 is the closest Arabidopsis homolog of soybean GmEF8, an EF-Tu cytosolic counterpart EF1-alpha-like protein. 2. A T-DNA insertion mutant (SALK_035228C) lacking functional AT5G60390 displays normal growth under control conditions but shows increased sensitivity to drought and heat stress, with reduced root length and surface area compared to WT. 3. Overexpression of soybean GmEF8 in this mutant partially rescues the drought- and heat-sensitive phenotype, supporting a positive role of AT5G60390 in abiotic stress tolerance. [41]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Loss of AT5G60390 function in the T-DNA insertion mutant (SALK_035228C) causes increased sensitivity to drought and heat stress, with reduced root length and root surface area under stress, while growth is similar to WT under normal conditions, indicating that AT5G60390 positively regulates drought and heat tolerance. [41]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainEF-Tu cytosolic counterpart EF1-alpha-like GTPase.
Validation MethodAnalysis of a homozygous T-DNA insertion knockout mutant SALK_035228C (AT5G60390) under PEG-induced drought and heat stress, comparing root length and root surface area to WT Col-0 plants.
Breeding Application ValueAT5G60390 encodes an EF-Tu/EF1-alpha-like protein that positively regulates drought and heat tolerance in Arabidopsis; its loss-of-function mutant shows hypersensitivity to these stresses. Functional characterization of AT5G60390 provides a model for EF-Tu-mediated abiotic stress tolerance and supports targeting its crop homologs, such as soybean GmEF8, for improving drought and heat tolerance in crops.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress simulated by growing seedlings on MS medium containing 6% PEG6000 and soil-grown plants subjected to water withholding; heat stress imposed by incubating seedlings at 45 °C for 2 h followed by recovery.

LpNAC6 (Lilium pumilum) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of LpNAC6 in tobacco enhances alkali tolerance but reduces drought tolerance at seed, seedling, and adult stages, as evidenced by better growth, higher survival, and improved physiological status under alkali stress, and conversely poorer performance and survival under drought. [42]

Regulatory Mechanism

LpNAC6 alters antioxidant defense (upregulating SOD, POD, CAT activities and lowering H2O2, O2^- and MDA under alkali stress but doing the opposite under drought), modulates osmotic adjustment (higher proline under alkali, lower under drought), maintains chlorophyll content and photosynthetic capacity under alkali but accelerates their decline under drought, and changes expression of K+ transporter NtHAK1, lipid transporter NtLTP1, and LEA genes NtLEA5 and NtERD10C, thereby exerting opposite effects on alkali versus drought tolerance. [42]

Pathways

Regulatory: Abiotic stress-responsive transcriptional regulatory pathway involving modulation of ROS-scavenging, ion homeostasis, osmotic stress responses, and LEA-mediated dehydration tolerance under alkali and drought stress. [42]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainNAC transcription factor
Expression PatternLpNAC6 is highly expressed in three independent transgenic tobacco lines (Tr6-1, Tr6-2, Tr6-3) compared with WT, as confirmed by qRT-PCR; lines with relatively high LpNAC6 expression in T1 were selected to generate T2 seeds used for stress assays.
Validation MethodFunctional validation by overexpression of LpNAC6 in tobacco followed by controlled alkali and drought stress assays, combined with physiological/biochemical measurements and qRT-PCR analysis of stress-related genes.
Breeding Application ValueLpNAC6 is a candidate gene for improving alkali tolerance of Lilium pumilum and potentially other crops grown on arid saline-alkali soils, as its overexpression markedly enhances alkali tolerance in a heterologous system. However, because it simultaneously decreases drought tolerance and post-drought recovery, its use in molecular breeding requires balancing alkali and drought environments and careful deployment in breeding programs for resistant new varieties of Lilium pumilum in arid saline-alkali regions.
Environment / TreatmentAlkali stress imposed with NaHCO3: 5–15 mM in 1/2 MS medium for seeds and seedlings and 300 mM NaHCO3 irrigation (100 mL per plant every other day) in soil; drought stress imposed by mannitol (100–300 mM) in 1/2 MS medium and by withholding water for 30 days in soil followed by 7 days of rewatering; all at 23 ± 2 °C, 16 h light/8 h dark.

Bra035235 (Brassica rapa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • Bra035235 is significantly upregulated in growth cones of the cold-tolerant variety “Longyou-7” at 3 h of cold (4°C) stress and remains higher than in the weakly cold-resistant “Lenox”, indicating early and strong cold responsiveness. [43]
  • Under cold stress, Bra035235 expression in “Longyou-7” growth cones is 4.1-, 6.6-, and 5.4-fold higher than in “Longyou-7” leaves, “Lenox” leaves, and roots, respectively, at 3 h, and 7.1-fold higher than in “Lenox” growth cones at 24 h. [43]
  • Bra035235 is strongly induced in “Longyou-7” growth cones under freezing (-4°C), drought (18% PEG6000), and heat (40°C) stresses, with transcript levels several- to >10-fold higher than in “Lenox”. [43]
  • The multi-stress inducibility and higher expression in the cold-tolerant cultivar suggest Bra035235 is closely associated with abiotic stress responses and ROS-scavenging capacity in winter B. rapa. [43]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Stress-inducible APX gene whose transcript level is strongly elevated in the cold-tolerant cultivar under cold, freezing, drought, and heat stresses, indicating a close association with enhanced abiotic stress tolerance in winter rapeseed. [43]

Regulatory Mechanism

Functions as an ascorbate peroxidase family member involved in H2O2 detoxification within the ascorbate–glutathione antioxidant cycle, contributing to ROS scavenging under abiotic stresses. [43]

Pathways
