Regulatory: Antioxidant/ROS-scavenging pathway (ascorbate–glutathione cycle) and plant peroxidase-related stress-response pathway. [43]

Biosynthetic: Ascorbate–glutathione (Halliwell–Asada) cycle for hydrogen peroxide detoxification; associated with phenylpropanoid biosynthesis pathway based on STRING functional annotation. [43]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAscorbate peroxidase (APX), plant peroxidase family (PF00141) containing conserved APX motifs identified by MEME.
Expression Pattern- Cold (4°C): Strongly upregulated in growth cones of “Longyou-7” at 3 h; expression much higher in “Longyou-7” than in “Lenox” and higher in growth cones than in leaves and roots; in leaves, continuously upregulated with higher levels at 24 h, especially in the cold-tolerant variety. - Freezing (-4°C): Significantly induced in “Longyou-7” growth cones; expression ~2-fold higher than in “Lenox” at 3 h. - Drought (18% PEG6000): Strong induction in “Longyou-7” growth cones after 24 h, reaching ~13.4-fold higher expression than in “Lenox” growth cones. - Heat (40°C): Markedly upregulated in growth cones and leaves of both cultivars, with higher expression in “Longyou-7” than in “Lenox”.
Validation MethodDifferential expression analysis from RNA-seq combined with qRT-PCR validation in contrasting cold-resistant cultivars under cold, freezing, drought, and heat stress treatments.
Breeding Application ValueBra035235 is a strong multi-abiotic-stress-inducible APX gene with markedly higher expression in the ultra-cold-resistant cultivar “Longyou-7” than in the weakly cold-resistant “Lenox”, especially in growth cones under cold, freezing, drought, and heat. This makes Bra035235 a promising candidate marker or target gene for breeding winter B. rapa cultivars with enhanced cold and broad abiotic stress tolerance through marker-assisted selection or transgenic approaches.
Environment / TreatmentCold stress at 4°C (3 h and 24 h), freezing stress at -4°C, drought stress via 18% PEG6000 solution, and heat stress at 40°C applied to six-leaf-stage seedlings; tissues: growth cones and leaves of cultivars “Longyou-7” (ultra-cold-resistant) and “Lenox” (weakly cold-resistant).

Bra003918 (Brassica rapa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • Bra003918 is highly expressed in the cold-tolerant variety “Longyou-7” under cold (4°C) stress, with much higher transcript levels than in the weakly cold-resistant “Lenox”; it is one of the most strongly cold-induced BrAPX genes. [43]
  • Under cold stress, Bra003918 expression in “Longyou-7” growth cones is significantly higher than in “Lenox” at both 3 h and 24 h, and is also induced in leaves, with expression at 24 h higher than at 3 h. [43]
  • Bra003918 is significantly upregulated in “Longyou-7” growth cones under freezing, drought, and heat stresses and is consistently more highly expressed than in “Lenox”, indicating broad abiotic stress responsiveness. [43]
  • The combination of strong, sustained induction and cultivar-specific upregulation suggests Bra003918 plays an important role in the abiotic stress response and ROS homeostasis in winter B. rapa. [43]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Cold- and multi-abiotic-stress-inducible APX gene with markedly higher expression in the cold-tolerant cultivar, closely associated with enhanced abiotic stress response and ROS-scavenging capacity. [43]

Regulatory Mechanism

As an APX family member, contributes to enzymatic scavenging of H2O2 in the ascorbate–glutathione antioxidant cycle during abiotic stress. [43]

Pathways

Regulatory: Antioxidant/ROS-scavenging (peroxisome-related) pathway and plant peroxidase-associated stress-response pathway. [43]

Biosynthetic: Ascorbate–glutathione (Halliwell–Asada) cycle for detoxification of reactive oxygen species. [43]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAscorbate peroxidase (APX), member of the plant peroxidase (PF00141) gene family with conserved APX motifs.
Expression Pattern- Cold (4°C): Strongly upregulated in growth cones of “Longyou-7” at both 3 h and 24 h, with expression 2.9- and 3-fold higher than in “Lenox” growth cones, respectively; in leaves, continuously upregulated with higher expression in “Longyou-7”. - Freezing (-4°C): Significantly induced in “Longyou-7” growth cones, with expression ~4.3- and 2.2-fold higher than in “Lenox” growth cones at 3 h and 24 h, respectively; expression higher in growth cones than in leaves. - Drought (18% PEG6000): Upregulated under drought stress, with higher expression in “Longyou-7” than in “Lenox”; in drought, Bra003918 shows relatively higher expression in leaves than in growth cones. - Heat (40°C): Significantly upregulated in growth cones and leaves of both cultivars, with higher expression in “Longyou-7” than in “Lenox”.
Validation MethodGene expression validated by RNA-seq and qRT-PCR in contrasting cold-resistant cultivars under multiple abiotic stress treatments.
Breeding Application ValueBecause Bra003918 is strongly and consistently induced by cold, freezing, drought, and heat stresses and is more highly expressed in the ultra-cold-resistant cultivar, it represents a promising candidate for improving multi-abiotic stress tolerance in winter B. rapa through marker-assisted selection or targeted genetic modification.
Environment / TreatmentCold stress at 4°C (3 h and 24 h), freezing at -4°C, drought via 18% PEG6000, and heat at 40°C applied to growth cones and leaves of cultivars “Longyou-7” and “Lenox”.

Bra033040 (Brassica rapa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • Bra033040 is significantly upregulated in “Longyou-7” growth cones under cold (4°C) stress and exhibits much higher expression than in “Lenox”, especially at 24 h. [43]
  • Under cold stress, Bra033040 expression in “Longyou-7” growth cones is 10.8- and 34.5-fold higher than in “Lenox” growth cones at 3 h and 24 h, respectively; in leaves, it is continuously upregulated with higher levels in “Longyou-7”. [43]
  • Bra033040 is strongly induced in “Longyou-7” growth cones under freezing (-4°C), drought, and heat stresses, with expression exceeding that in “Lenox” by several- to many-fold, indicating broad abiotic stress responsiveness. [43]
  • Its strong growth-cone-biased induction and cultivar-specific upregulation indicate Bra033040 is an important APX gene associated with abiotic stress responses in winter B. rapa. [43]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Strongly stress-inducible APX gene with large-fold induction in the cold-tolerant cultivar, particularly in growth cones, closely associated with cold, freezing, drought, and heat stress responses. [43]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as an APX family peroxidase involved in scavenging H2O2 via the ascorbate–glutathione antioxidant system during abiotic stress. [43]

Pathways

Regulatory: Antioxidant/ROS-scavenging pathway linked to peroxisome-related stress responses; categorized within plant peroxidase-related pathways. [43]

Biosynthetic: Participates in the ascorbate–glutathione (Halliwell–Asada) cycle for ROS detoxification. [43]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAscorbate peroxidase (APX) in the plant peroxidase (PF00141) family, containing conserved APX motifs identified by MEME.
Expression Pattern- Cold (4°C): In growth cones of “Longyou-7”, expression is 10.8-fold higher than in “Lenox” at 3 h and 34.5-fold higher at 24 h; in leaves, continuously upregulated during cold stress with higher levels in “Longyou-7”. - Freezing (-4°C): In “Longyou-7” growth cones, expression is about 3.4-fold higher than in “Lenox” at 3 h and 6-fold higher at 24 h; expression higher in growth cones than in leaves. - Drought (18% PEG6000): After 24 h, significantly upregulated in “Longyou-7” growth cones, ~3.4-fold higher than in “Lenox”. - Heat (40°C): Strong induction in growth cones and leaves of both cultivars, with consistently higher levels in “Longyou-7”.
Validation MethodExpression changes validated by RNA-seq and qRT-PCR in contrasting cold-resistance cultivars under multiple abiotic stresses.
Breeding Application ValueThe very strong induction of Bra033040 in growth cones and leaves of the ultra-cold-resistant cultivar under cold, freezing, drought, and heat makes it an attractive candidate gene for breeding winter B. rapa varieties with improved multi-stress tolerance using marker-assisted selection or functional engineering.
Environment / TreatmentCold (4°C), freezing (-4°C), drought (18% PEG6000), and heat (40°C) stress applied at the six-leaf stage to growth cones and leaves of “Longyou-7” and “Lenox”.

Bra017120 (Brassica rapa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • Bra017120 is significantly upregulated in “Longyou-7” growth cones under cold (4°C) stress, with higher expression than in “Lenox” at both 3 h and 24 h. [43]
  • Under cold stress, Bra017120 expression in “Longyou-7” growth cones is 2.4- and 4.4-fold higher than in “Lenox” at 3 h and 24 h, respectively; its expression is also induced in leaves and is higher in “Longyou-7”. [43]
  • Bra017120 is markedly induced in “Longyou-7” growth cones under freezing (-4°C), drought, and heat stresses; under freezing at 3 h, expression is ~2.9-fold higher than in “Lenox”, and under drought at 24 h, ~32.9-fold higher; under heat, expression in “Longyou-7” growth cones is 12.2- and 2.7-fold higher than in “Lenox” at 3 h and 24 h, respectively. [43]
  • These patterns indicate Bra017120 is a highly responsive APX gene associated with cold and broad abiotic stress responses in winter B. rapa. [43]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Strong and sustained stress-inducible APX gene whose expression is consistently higher in the cold-tolerant cultivar across cold, freezing, drought, and heat stresses, implicating it in enhanced abiotic stress tolerance. [43]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as part of the APX-mediated ROS-scavenging system, reducing H2O2 via the ascorbate–glutathione cycle during abiotic stress. [43]

Pathways

Regulatory: Antioxidant/ROS-scavenging pathway associated with peroxidase activity under abiotic stress. [43]

Biosynthetic: Ascorbate–glutathione (Halliwell–Asada) cycle for ROS detoxification. [43]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAscorbate peroxidase (APX), member of the plant peroxidase (PF00141) family with conserved APX motifs.
Expression Pattern- Cold (4°C): In “Longyou-7” growth cones, expression is 2.4- and 4.4-fold higher than in “Lenox” at 3 h and 24 h, respectively; in leaves, continuously upregulated with higher levels in “Longyou-7”. - Freezing (-4°C): Significantly induced in “Longyou-7” growth cones, with ~2.9-fold higher expression than in “Lenox” at 3 h. - Drought (18% PEG6000): Strongly upregulated in “Longyou-7” growth cones after 24 h, ~32.9-fold higher than in “Lenox”. - Heat (40°C): Markedly induced in “Longyou-7” growth cones, with 12.2- and 2.7-fold higher expression than in “Lenox” at 3 h and 24 h, respectively; also upregulated in leaves with higher levels in “Longyou-7”.
Validation MethodRNA-seq–based differential expression coupled with qRT-PCR validation in contrasting cold-resistant cultivars under multiple abiotic stress treatments.
Breeding Application ValueBra017120’s strong induction across cold, freezing, drought, and heat in the ultra-cold-resistant cultivar suggests it is a valuable candidate gene for breeding winter B. rapa with improved multi-abiotic stress tolerance, potentially useful as a marker or engineering target.
Environment / TreatmentCold (4°C), freezing (-4°C), drought (18% PEG6000), and heat (40°C) stresses applied to growth cones and leaves of “Longyou-7” and “Lenox”.

Bra031934 (Brassica rapa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • Bra031934 is significantly upregulated in “Longyou-7” growth cones at 3 h of cold (4°C) stress and remains induced in leaves, with higher expression in “Longyou-7” than in “Lenox”. [43]
  • In leaves under cold stress, Bra031934 is among the most highly expressed BrAPX genes in “Longyou-7”, with 2.3-fold higher expression than in “Lenox” leaves at 24 h. [43]
  • Bra031934 is strongly induced in “Longyou-7” growth cones under freezing and drought stresses, with expression ~10-fold higher than in “Lenox” growth cones after 24 h of drought; it is also induced by heat stress, with higher expression in “Longyou-7”. [43]
  • These patterns indicate Bra031934 contributes to the abiotic stress-responsive APX network, particularly in leaves, and is associated with stress tolerance in winter B. rapa. [43]

Core Phenotypic Effect

APX gene strongly induced by cold, freezing, drought, and heat, particularly in leaves of the cold-tolerant cultivar, supporting a role in abiotic stress response and ROS management. [43]

Regulatory Mechanism

Functions as part of the APX-mediated H2O2 scavenging system (ascorbate–glutathione cycle) under abiotic stress conditions. [43]

Pathways

Regulatory: Antioxidant/ROS-scavenging pathway related to peroxidase activity in response to abiotic stress. [43]

Biosynthetic: Ascorbate–glutathione (Halliwell–Asada) cycle for ROS detoxification. [43]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAscorbate peroxidase (APX), plant peroxidase (PF00141) family member with conserved APX motifs.
Expression Pattern- Cold (4°C): Significantly upregulated in “Longyou-7” growth cones at 3 h; in leaves, Bra031934 shows strong induction and is 2.3-fold more highly expressed in “Longyou-7” than in “Lenox” at 24 h. - Freezing (-4°C): One of five APX genes significantly expressed in “Longyou-7” under freezing, with higher transcript levels than in “Lenox”. - Drought (18% PEG6000): After 24 h, markedly upregulated in “Longyou-7” growth cones, ~10-fold higher than in “Lenox” growth cones. - Heat (40°C): Significantly upregulated in growth cones and leaves of both cultivars, with higher expression in “Longyou-7” than in “Lenox”.
Validation MethodExpression validation by RNA-seq and qRT-PCR in contrasting cold-resistance cultivars under multiple abiotic stress treatments.
Breeding Application ValueGiven its strong induction in leaves and growth cones of the ultra-cold-resistant cultivar under cold, freezing, drought, and heat, Bra031934 is a promising candidate gene for developing winter B. rapa lines with improved abiotic stress tolerance via marker-assisted breeding or functional manipulation.
Environment / TreatmentCold (4°C), freezing (-4°C), drought (18% PEG6000), and heat (40°C) stress at the six-leaf stage applied to growth cones and leaves of “Longyou-7” and “Lenox”.

HrTCP20 (Hippophae rhamnoides L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • HrTCP20 is a TCP transcription factor from sea buckthorn encoding a 299-amino-acid protein with a conserved bHLH TCP domain and is strongly up-regulated (~18-fold) by PEG-induced drought stress. [44]
  • Overexpression of HrTCP20 in Arabidopsis thaliana enhances drought tolerance, increasing SOD and POD activities and chlorophyll content while reducing MDA levels under drought. [44]
  • VIGS-mediated silencing of HrTCP20 in sea buckthorn reduces drought tolerance, with decreased defense-related enzymes and RWC, increased REC, MDA, and H2O2, and more severe wilting and ROS accumulation under drought. [44]
  • Silencing HrTCP20 significantly decreases JA content and specifically down-regulates the JA biosynthetic gene HrLOX2; yeast two-hybrid assays confirm a direct HrTCP20–HrLOX2 interaction, indicating that HrTCP20 improves drought resistance by modulating JA biosynthesis via HrLOX2. [44]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Positive regulator of drought tolerance that enhances antioxidant enzyme activities and chlorophyll content, maintains higher water status, and reduces membrane damage and ROS accumulation under drought stress. [44]

Regulatory Mechanism

Silencing HrTCP20 decreases expression of the JA biosynthetic gene HrLOX2 and reduces JA accumulation under drought, while yeast two-hybrid assays show a direct HrTCP20–HrLOX2 protein interaction, indicating HrTCP20 positively regulates JA biosynthesis through HrLOX2 to modulate antioxidant defenses and drought tolerance. [44]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in the jasmonic acid (JA) signaling and biosynthetic pathway that mediates sea buckthorn responses to drought stress. [44]

Biosynthetic: Regulates the jasmonic acid (JA) biosynthetic/signaling pathway indirectly via modulation of downstream JA biosynthetic gene HrLOX2. [44]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainTCP transcription factor of the PCF (class I) subfamily containing a conserved basic helix-loop-helix TCP domain.
Expression PatternStrongly induced by PEG-induced drought stress in sea buckthorn leaves, with transcript levels increasing continuously and reaching approximately 18-fold at 12 h of treatment.
Validation MethodOverexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana (35S:HrTCP20) followed by PEG-induced drought assays and physiological measurements; VIGS-mediated silencing in sea buckthorn with drought treatment; qRT-PCR expression analysis; hormone quantification; yeast two-hybrid interaction assay with HrLOX2.
Breeding Application ValueProvides a validated drought-resistance transcription factor in sea buckthorn that enhances antioxidant defenses, maintains water status, and acts through JA signaling, making HrTCP20 a promising target gene for molecular breeding and genetic engineering of drought-tolerant forest trees.
Environment / TreatmentPEG-6000-induced drought stress and water depletion treatments applied to Arabidopsis and sea buckthorn; VIGS treatment in field-like conditions (~25 °C, 60–70% humidity).

HrLOX2 (Hippophae rhamnoides L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • HrLOX2 was identified as a lipoxygenase gene involved in the JA biosynthetic pathway in sea buckthorn. [44]
  • Among several JA biosynthesis-related genes tested, HrLOX2 expression is the most strongly and specifically reduced (~0.36-fold of control) in HrTCP20-silenced plants under drought stress. [44]
  • Reduced HrLOX2 expression in HrTCP20-silenced plants coincides with a significant decrease in JA content, linking HrLOX2 to HrTCP20-dependent JA biosynthesis under drought. [44]
  • Yeast two-hybrid assays demonstrate a direct physical interaction between HrLOX2 and HrTCP20 proteins, supporting a model in which HrTCP20 modulates JA biosynthesis and drought response through HrLOX2. [44]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Functions as a JA biosynthetic gene whose expression is specifically and strongly down-regulated when HrTCP20 is silenced, contributing to reduced JA accumulation under drought stress. [44]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a JA biosynthetic enzyme whose transcript abundance is positively regulated by the transcription factor HrTCP20; reduced HrLOX2 expression in HrTCP20-silenced plants is associated with decreased JA content, and direct physical interaction between HrLOX2 and HrTCP20 proteins is detected by yeast two-hybrid. [44]

Pathways

Regulatory: Participates in the jasmonic acid biosynthetic pathway that contributes to drought-responsive hormone signaling in sea buckthorn. [44]

Biosynthetic: Jasmonic acid biosynthetic pathway, at the 13-lipoxygenase (LOX2) step converting α-linolenic acid to 13-hydroperoxyoctadecatrienoic acid (13-HPOT). [44]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainLipoxygenase (LOX2) class enzyme implicated in 13-lipoxygenase activity within the JA biosynthetic pathway.
Expression PatternAmong JA biosynthesis-related genes, HrLOX2 shows the strongest response to HrTCP20 silencing, with its expression significantly reduced (~0.36-fold relative to control) in VIGS-treated plants under drought stress.
Validation MethodqRT-PCR analysis of HrLOX2 expression in HrTCP20-silenced versus control sea buckthorn under drought; yeast two-hybrid assay demonstrating physical interaction between HrLOX2 protein and HrTCP20.
Breeding Application ValueAs a key JA biosynthetic lipoxygenase whose expression is positively regulated by HrTCP20 and correlates with JA content under drought, HrLOX2 represents a downstream target whose manipulation, together with HrTCP20, could be used to fine-tune JA levels and improve drought response in sea buckthorn breeding and genetic engineering programs.
Environment / TreatmentPEG-6000-induced drought stress applied to VIGS-treated and control sea buckthorn plants.

SlCER1-1 (Solanum lycopersicum) Plant

Key Findings

  • SlCER1-1 encodes a FAH superfamily/WAX2 C-terminal domain protein localized to the ER and is highly expressed in wax-producing tomato tissues (leaves, fruits, stems), with strong induction by drought and ABA. [45]
  • Overexpression of SlCER1-1 in Arabidopsis markedly increases total cuticular wax load, specifically elevating C25–C35 n-alkanes and branched alkanes, confirming its catalytic role in VLC alkane formation. [45]
  • RNAi knockdown of SlCER1-1 in tomato (slcer1-1) drastically reduces VLC alkanes in leaves, fruits, stems, and flowers, increases aldehydes and primary alcohols, and greatly decreases total wax loads. [45]
  • Under drought and postharvest storage, SlCER1-1-deficient plants show impaired drought tolerance, higher cuticular water loss, lower relative water content, and severely crinkled fruits, demonstrating that SlCER1-1-mediated VLC alkane biosynthesis is critical for cuticular barrier function, drought tolerance, and fruit storability. [45]

Core Phenotypic Effect

SlCER1-1 is a key positive regulator of VLC alkane accumulation in tomato cuticular wax; its activity increases n-alkane and branched alkane levels in leaves, fruits, stems, and flowers, thereby reducing cuticular permeability, enhancing drought tolerance, and improving fruit storability. Loss of SlCER1-1 function greatly reduces VLC alkanes, increases cuticular water loss, and sensitizes plants and fruits to drought and desiccation. [45]

Regulatory Mechanism

ER-localized aldehyde decarbonylase activity converting very-long-chain aldehydes to VLC alkanes without clear chain-length preference between C25 and C35 and using both straight-chain and branched substrates; reduced SlCER1-1 activity diverts substrates toward aldehydes and primary alcohols. [45]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in the alkane-forming branch of cuticular wax biosynthesis and is transcriptionally induced during drought and ABA treatment as part of the drought stress response pathway. [45]

Biosynthetic: Cuticular wax very-long-chain (C25–C35) n-alkane and branched alkane biosynthesis via the alkane-forming pathway, catalyzing conversion of aldehydes to VLC alkanes in tomato cuticles. [45]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainFAH superfamily domain (PF04116) and WAX2 C-terminal domain (PF12076) containing three His-rich motifs
Expression PatternHighly expressed in leaves, fruits, and stems but not roots; transcript levels in leaves higher at 80 days than at 30 days; higher expression in orange-stage fruits than immature green fruits; strongly induced in leaves by drought (34-fold at 12 h) and ABA (4-fold at 12 h), moderately affected by cold and dark treatments.
Validation MethodFunctional validation by generating SlCER1-1 RNAi knockdown tomato lines (slcer1-1) and analyzing wax composition and drought-related traits; heterologous overexpression of SlCER1-1 in Arabidopsis leaves followed by wax profiling; subcellular localization of SlCER1-1–eGFP fusion; drought and postharvest storage phenotyping of RNAi lines versus wild type.
Breeding Application ValueBecause SlCER1-1 promotes VLC alkane accumulation, reduces cuticular water loss, and enhances drought tolerance and fruit storability, increasing SlCER1-1 activity or selecting alleles that boost VLC alkanes via conventional breeding or gene editing is a promising strategy to improve drought resilience and extend shelf life of tomato fruits. The slcer1-1 mutant provides a useful model for dissecting CER1 homolog function and designing breeding targets.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by withholding water; ABA treatment (100 μM) applied by spraying; cold treatment at 4°C; continuous dark treatment; postharvest fruit storage at room temperature to assess water loss.

BnPIP1 (Brassica napus) Plant

Key Findings

  • BnPIP1 protein is present in leaves of well-watered oilseed rape plants and is detectable as a specific band in immunoblots. [46]
  • In control and silicon-complex-treated plants, BnPIP1 accumulation is significantly higher in the oldest plants compared with younger ones, with the maximum after three silicon-complex applications. [46]
  • Plants watered only with silicon do not show this age-dependent increase in BnPIP1, and overall no simple correlation between BnPIP1 accumulation and silicon presence was established. [46]
  • These patterns indicate that BnPIP1 is part of the silicon-responsive aquaporin network regulating leaf water management, particularly under optimal watering. [46]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Leaf plasma-membrane aquaporin whose accumulation is enhanced by repeated silicon-complex supplementation in older, well-watered plants, indicating a role in silicon-modulated regulation of leaf water transport under non-stress conditions, although no simple correlation with silicon alone was observed. [46]

Regulatory Mechanism

BnPIP1 protein accumulation is modulated by plant age and the presence of silicon complex, suggesting post-transcriptional or stability-based regulation rather than a simple dependence on external silicon concentration. [46]

Pathways

Regulatory: Silicon-responsive regulation of aquaporin-mediated leaf water transport under non-stress conditions. [46]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAquaporin, plasma membrane intrinsic protein (PIP1) water channel family.
Expression PatternBnPIP1 protein is detectable in leaves under well-watered conditions; its accumulation increases with plant age in control and silicon-complex-treated plants, with the highest level after three applications of the silicon complex, whereas plants watered only with silicon do not show this age-dependent increase.
Validation MethodProtein-level detection and semi-quantitative analysis by immunoblotting with PIP1-specific antibody and densitometry in silicon-treated versus control plants.
Breeding Application ValueBnPIP1 responsiveness to silicon+Fe supplementation under well-watered conditions indicates that agronomic use of silicon complexes could be used to fine-tune leaf aquaporin abundance and water transport capacity in oilseed rape, potentially improving hydration and vigor in high-input systems, though its direct contribution to drought mitigation is less clear than for other aquaporins.
Environment / TreatmentSilicon supplementation as orthosilicic acid or Optysil silicon+Fe complex under well-watered conditions, with repeated applications at weekly intervals.

BnPIP2-1-7 (Brassica napus) Plant

Key Findings

  • BnPIP2-1-7 protein (monomers and dimers) is present in leaves of oilseed rape and its accumulation increases with plant age under well-watered conditions in control and silicon-complex-treated plants, with the highest level after three applications of silicon complex. [46]
  • In silicon-only plants under well-watered conditions, the age-dependent pattern is reversed, with highest BnPIP2-1-7 accumulation in the youngest plants. [46]
  • Under drought, silicon supplementation (both sources) significantly reduces BnPIP2-1-7 protein accumulation compared to control plants, while silicon complex increases BnPIP2-1-7 transcript levels. [46]
  • These contrasting protein and transcript responses support a role for BnPIP2-1-7 as a key silicon-responsive aquaporin regulating leaf water transport, with downregulation of protein abundance under drought interpreted as a mechanism to minimize water loss and maintain turgor. [46]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Leaf PIP2 aquaporin considered a highly efficient water transporter whose protein accumulation is strongly modulated by silicon and drought: silicon complex enhances BnPIP2-1-7 in well-watered, older plants, while under drought silicon reduces its protein level relative to control, consistent with silicon-mediated restriction of plasma-membrane water permeability to maintain leaf turgor during water deficit. [46]

Regulatory Mechanism

Under drought, silicon treatments decrease BnPIP2-1-7 protein abundance while silicon complex simultaneously elevates its transcript level, indicating silicon-dependent post-transcriptional and/or post-translational downregulation of this aquaporin to limit plasma-membrane water permeability during water deficit. [46]

Pathways

Regulatory: Silicon-mediated drought response pathway influencing aquaporin-dependent leaf water transport and water conservation. [46]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAquaporin, plasma membrane intrinsic protein (PIP2) water channel family.
Expression PatternUnder well-watered conditions, BnPIP2-1-7 protein (monomers and dimers) accumulates to higher levels in leaves of older plants, with the highest accumulation after three applications of silicon complex; in silicon-only plants, the highest accumulation occurs in the youngest plants. Under drought, both monomeric and dimeric BnPIP2-1-7 are present, but silicon supplementation significantly reduces total protein accumulation relative to control plants, while silicon complex concurrently increases BnPIP2-1-7 transcript abundance.
Validation MethodFunctional involvement inferred from protein and transcript responses to silicon and drought treatments demonstrated by immunoblotting of BnPIP2-1-7 monomers and dimers and qRT-PCR of BnPIP2-1-7 transcripts in leaves under well-watered and drought conditions.
Breeding Application ValueBecause BnPIP2-1-7 is a highly efficient water channel whose protein level is downregulated by silicon under drought, management or breeding strategies that favor silicon responsiveness of this gene could help reduce transpirational water loss and maintain leaf turgor in oilseed rape during water deficit, while silicon complex-induced upregulation under non-stress conditions may support efficient water uptake and growth.
Environment / TreatmentDrought versus well-watered soil moisture (30% vs 65–70% field water capacity) combined with silicon supplementation as orthosilicic acid or Optysil silicon+Fe complex.

BnTIP1;1 (Brassica napus) Plant

Key Findings

  • BnTIP1;1 tonoplast aquaporin is present as monomeric and dimeric forms in leaves under both well-watered and drought conditions. [46]
  • Under well-watered conditions, silicon complex significantly increases BnTIP1;1 protein accumulation compared with control and silicon-only treatments, and transcript levels show a similar trend. [46]
  • Under drought, silicon supplementation from both sources significantly elevates BnTIP1;1 protein accumulation relative to control plants, whereas BnTIP1;1 transcripts are highest in control and reduced by silicon, especially silicon complex. [46]
  • These results support BnTIP1;1 as a key silicon-responsive aquaporin involved in maintaining water balance and contributing to the mitigation of drought-induced stress in oilseed rape, particularly in response to silicon complex supplementation. [46]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Tonoplast aquaporin whose accumulation is enhanced by silicon complex in well-watered leaves and by silicon from both sources under drought; under drought, higher BnTIP1;1 protein abundance in silicon-treated plants despite reduced transcripts suggests a silicon-mediated stabilization or increased translation of this TIP, contributing to maintenance of vacuolar water status and improved drought tolerance. [46]

Regulatory Mechanism

Under drought, silicon treatments increase BnTIP1;1 protein accumulation while reducing transcript levels relative to control, suggesting silicon-dependent post-transcriptional regulation (enhanced protein stability or translation) that maintains tonoplast aquaporin abundance during water deficit. [46]

Pathways

Regulatory: Silicon-mediated drought response pathway affecting tonoplast aquaporin-dependent water balance and associated antioxidant defense. [46]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAquaporin, tonoplast intrinsic protein (TIP1) water channel family.
Expression PatternBnTIP1;1 monomers and dimers are clearly detectable in leaves under both well-watered and drought conditions. Under well-watered conditions, BnTIP1;1 protein accumulation is significantly higher in silicon-complex-treated plants than in control or silicon-only plants. Under drought, silicon supplementation from either source markedly increases BnTIP1;1 protein accumulation compared to control. Transcript levels under well-watered conditions follow the protein pattern (highest in silicon-complex plants), whereas under drought the highest BnTIP1;1 transcript level is in control plants and is reduced by silicon, particularly by silicon complex.
Validation MethodProtein and transcript responses to silicon and drought demonstrated by immunoblotting of TIP1 monomers and dimers and qRT-PCR of BnTIP1;1 transcripts in leaves under well-watered and drought conditions.
Breeding Application ValueBecause BnTIP1;1 protein is consistently enhanced by silicon supplementation, especially silicon complex, under drought and is proposed to support water balance and oxidative stress mitigation, it represents a promising molecular target or marker for breeding oilseed rape with improved silicon-responsive drought tolerance; agronomic silicon fertilization that upregulates or stabilizes BnTIP1;1 could improve survival and performance under water deficit.
Environment / TreatmentDrought versus well-watered conditions combined with silicon supplementation as orthosilicic acid or Optysil silicon+Fe complex.

ATHB-6 (Zea mays L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • ATHB-6 encodes an HD-Zip I transcription factor with a conserved HD and leucine zipper motif, and its protein is efficiently expressed and accumulates as a ~26 kDa band. [47]
  • ATHB-6 is nuclear-localized and specifically binds the CAATAATTG inverted repeat motif, indicating a direct role in transcriptional regulation. [47]
  • ATHB-6 expression and promoter activity are strongly induced by drought in maize roots, with promoter::GUS lines showing root-specific and time-dependent induction under 12% PEG6000. [47]
  • Overexpression of ATHB-6 in maize increases seed germination, root elongation and adventitious/supporting root formation, and improves survival and recovery under PEG6000 and soil/field drought, whereas CRISPR knockout lines are more sensitive to drought. [47]
  • ATHB-6 overexpression reduces ROS (H2O2 and O2−) accumulation and MDA content under drought by enhancing SOD and CAT activities, thereby alleviating oxidative damage. [47]
  • ATHB-6 positively regulates the expression of multiple ROS-related and drought-responsive genes (ZmCAT3, ZmSOD1, ZmSOS1, ZmLTP3, ZmRD29B, ZmRD22, ZmCBF4, ZmABI4) and ABA pathway genes (NCED3, SnRK2.6, COR15, DREB2A) under drought. [47]
  • Transgenic ATHB-6-overexpressing maize lines display improved seed setting rate, yield, and seed vigor under field drought, supporting ATHB-6 as a promising target gene for improving drought tolerance in maize. [47]

Core Phenotypic Effect

ATHB-6 acts as a positive regulator of drought tolerance in maize, enhancing seed germination, root growth (including adventitious and supporting roots), plant survival and recovery under drought, while reducing oxidative damage and improving yield-related traits under water deficit. [47]

Regulatory Mechanism

ATHB-6 is a nuclear HD-Zip I transcription factor that specifically binds the CAATAATTG inverted repeat motif in yeast one-hybrid assays and, when overexpressed in maize, enhances expression of ROS-scavenging and ABA-responsive genes, increases SOD and CAT enzyme activities, and thereby reduces ROS accumulation and oxidative damage under drought. [47]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in ROS signaling and ABA-dependent and ABA-independent stress-response pathways under drought, as indicated by activation of ROS-scavenging genes and ABA-responsive marker genes. [47]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainHomeodomain-leucine zipper I (HD-Zip I) transcription factor containing a highly conserved homeodomain (HD) and adjacent leucine zipper (LZ) motif.
Expression PatternATHB-6 is strongly induced in maize roots by drought: among 17 HD-Zip I genes in roots of drought-treated M8186 plants, ATHB-6 showed a marked change in expression; promoter::GUS analysis revealed root-specific GUS expression that increased progressively with longer 12% PEG6000 treatment times.
Validation MethodFunctional validation was achieved by generating maize lines overexpressing ATHB-6 under the CaMV35S promoter and CRISPR/Cas9 ATHB-6 knockout lines, then comparing seed germination, root growth, plant wilting and recovery, and yield-related traits under PEG6000-induced and soil/field drought; subcellular localization of ATHB-6-GFP in tobacco nuclei; yeast one-hybrid assays for DNA-binding specificity to CAATAATTG; DAB staining and biochemical assays for ROS (H2O2, O2−), MDA, proline, SOD and CAT activities; and qRT-PCR analyses of ROS-related, drought-responsive, and ABA pathway marker genes in WT, OE6, and KO23 plants.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of ATHB-6 in maize enhances drought tolerance in greenhouse and field, improving root system architecture, supporting root development, seed setting rate, yield, and seed vigor under drought; thus ATHB-6 is proposed as a crucial target gene for breeding or engineering drought-tolerant maize cultivars.
Environment / TreatmentOsmotic drought stress induced by 12% (w/v) PEG6000 solution in seedlings, air-drying dehydration, and soil/field drought stress; analyses focused on responses of ATHB-6 transgenic and WT plants under these water-deficit conditions.

SlMYB55 (Solanum lycopersicum) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

SlMYB55 acts as a negative regulator of drought and salt tolerance and a regulator of flowering and floral organ development; silencing SlMYB55 enhances salt and drought tolerance, advances flowering, increases flower number, and produces smaller flowers and sepals. [48]

Regulatory Mechanism

SlMYB55 is an R2R3-MYB transcription factor whose expression is induced by ABA, salt and drought. It directly binds and regulates the promoters of WUS and 4CL, and physically interacts with the MADS-box protein MBP21. Silencing SlMYB55 leads to altered expression of multiple stress-related genes (Cat1, Cat2, LEA/TAS14, P5CS, GME2, ER5) and inflorescence/flowering genes (S, TMF, LS, FA, SP, AP2a, SOC1, LFY, WUS, MBP21), indicating it functions as a transcriptional regulator integrating ABA signaling, stress responses and floral development. [48]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA-dependent osmotic stress signaling pathway; stress-responsive antioxidant and osmoprotectant pathways; inflorescence and flower meristem development pathway; flavonoid biosynthesis pathway. [48]

Biosynthetic: Flavonoid biosynthesis via regulation of 4CL (4-coumaroyl CoA ligase); influences ABA biosynthesis/signaling. [48]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainR2R3-MYB transcription factor family
Expression PatternSlMYB55 is expressed in all tested tomato organs with highest levels in stems, moderate levels in immature and mature green fruits and flowers, and low levels in young leaves and sepals. Its transcript level is transiently induced by ACC, ABA, IAA, MeJA and GA3, peaking at 8 h (ACC, GA3), 12 h (ABA, MeJA) and 24 h (IAA). Under 300 mM NaCl and dehydration, SlMYB55 expression is induced, with NaCl causing a 1.34- to 6.35-fold increase by 8 h and drought induction evident by 12 h.
Validation MethodStable RNA interference (SlMYB55-RNAi) in tomato followed by salt and drought tolerance assays and flowering/flower phenotyping; qRT-PCR analysis of stress-related and flower development genes; tobacco transient expression to test direct regulation of WUS and 4CL promoters; yeast two-hybrid analysis of SlMYB55–MBP21 protein interaction.
Breeding Application ValueSilencing SlMYB55 improves tomato tolerance to drought and high salinity while advancing flowering and increasing flower number, making SlMYB55 a promising molecular target to develop stress-resilient tomato cultivars with modified flowering behavior and floral organ size. The gene’s direct regulation of WUS and 4CL and its integration of ABA-dependent stress responses suggest it can be manipulated to enhance stress tolerance through improved antioxidant capacity and ABA signaling without compromising pollen viability, providing a basis for tomato improvement via transgenic or genome-editing approaches.
Environment / TreatmentSalt stress (NaCl 40–100 mM on MS medium, 300 mM NaCl irrigation in soil); drought stress (withholding water up to 21 days; dehydration of detached leaves); hormone treatments including ABA (3–100 μM), ACC, MeJA, GA3 and IAA; ABA-containing media for sensitivity assays.

FvZFP1 (Fragaria vesca) Plant

Key Findings

  • FvZFP1 encodes a RING finger and CHY zinc finger domain-containing protein with CHY zinc finger, zinc-ribbon, and RING-finger domains, closely related to ZFPs from other Rosaceae species. [49]
  • Stable overexpression of FvZFP1 in Nicotiana benthamiana reduces TMV accumulation and delays systemic spread, enhancing antiviral resistance. [49]
  • FvZFP1 overexpression enhances resistance to Pst DC3000, restricting lesion development and reducing bacterial populations by up to ~20-fold relative to WT plants. [49]
  • During pathogen infection, FvZFP1 increases ROS accumulation and activates SA signaling (upregulating NbNPR1 and NbPR1, increasing SA levels) while modulating JA signaling (altering NbJAZ and NbPDF1.2 expression and JA content). [49]
  • FvZFP1 improves seed germination and whole-plant tolerance under salinity and drought, associated with increased SOD activity and reduced MDA content, limiting oxidative damage. [49]
  • Under salt and drought stress, FvZFP1-overexpressing plants show upregulated ABA-related genes NbP5CS1 and NbNCED1, indicating activation of the ABA pathway in abiotic stress tolerance. [49]
  • Overall, FvZFP1 functions as an inducer of SA and ABA pathways and ROS homeostasis, conferring broad-spectrum resistance to both biotic and abiotic stresses in transgenic plants. [49]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of FvZFP1 enhances resistance to TMV and Pst DC3000 and improves salinity and drought tolerance in transgenic N. benthamiana by increasing ROS accumulation during pathogen infection, elevating SOD activity and reducing MDA under salt/drought, and activating SA- and ABA-related defense gene expression. [49]

Regulatory Mechanism

Overexpression of FvZFP1 elevates ROS (H2O2) accumulation during early stages of TMV and Pst DC3000 infection, increases SOD activity and decreases MDA under salt and drought stress, and alters the expression of hormone-responsive genes in SA (NbNPR1, NbPR1), JA (NbJAZ, NbPDF1.2), and ABA (NbP5CS1, NbNCED1) pathways, indicating regulation of ROS homeostasis and hormone signaling to enhance stress tolerance. [49]

Pathways

Regulatory: Positively regulates the salicylic acid (SA) signaling pathway during TMV and Pst DC3000 infection (upregulation of NbNPR1 and NbPR1, increased SA accumulation); negatively modulates the jasmonic acid (JA) pathway during TMV infection (upregulation of negative regulator NbJAZ, downregulation of NbPDF1.2, decreased JA content), with no significant JA changes during Pst DC3000 infection; activates the abscisic acid (ABA) pathway under salinity and drought by upregulating ABA-related genes NbP5CS1 and NbNCED1. [49]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainRING finger and CHY zinc finger domain-containing protein 1 with CHY zinc finger, zinc-ribbon, and RING-finger domains; zinc finger protein (ZFP) family.
Expression PatternConstitutively overexpressed in transgenic Nicotiana benthamiana (35S::FvZFP1), with FvZFP1 transcript and 29 kDa protein detected in 4-week-old leaves of T3 homozygous lines but not in WT plants.
Validation MethodStable overexpression of FvZFP1 in transgenic N. benthamiana (35S::FvZFP1) combined with pathogen inoculation (TMV-GFP and Pst DC3000), abiotic stress assays (salt and drought during germination and at whole-plant stage), physiological measurements (ROS staining, SOD activity, MDA content), hormone quantification (SA and JA), and qRT-PCR analysis of hormone-responsive genes.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of FvZFP1 confers broad-spectrum resistance to biotic (TMV and Pst DC3000) and abiotic (salt and drought) stresses in transgenic Nicotiana benthamiana by activating SA- and ABA-related defense pathways and modulating ROS/SOD balance, indicating that FvZFP1 is a promising candidate gene to engineer multi-stress resistance in strawberry and other Rosaceae crops.
Environment / TreatmentTMV-GFP infection; Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato DC3000 infection; salinity stress (125 mM NaCl for germination and 200 mM NaCl irrigation for seedlings); drought stress (5% PEG8000 during germination and limited watering at 5 mL/day for 14 d).

PtrbHLH186 (Populus trichocarpa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression in Populus trichocarpa leads to retarded plant growth (reduced height, internode number and stem basal diameter), early and enhanced lignification with ~20% higher total lignin and ~20% higher G-lignin, reduced S- and H-lignin and polysaccharides, increased number of vessel elements with smaller lumen and thicker walls and more fibres with thicker secondary walls, resulting in a larger total vessel lumen area and markedly improved drought tolerance. [50]

Regulatory Mechanism

Nuclear bHLH TF that, when overexpressed, transregulates multiple monolignol biosynthetic genes, increasing G-lignin and total lignin and decreasing S and H lignin as well as carbohydrate content, while altering vessel and fibre development; regulation is driven by upstream activation from the PtrMYB074–PtrWRKY19 heterodimer. [50]

Pathways

Regulatory: Core downstream effector of the PtrMYB074–PtrWRKY19 dimer; its promoter is bound at a W-box by PtrWRKY19, with PtrMYB074 recruited via dimerization, and co-expression of PtrMYB074 and PtrWRKY19 is required to strongly activate PtrbHLH186 transcription in SDX. Activated PtrbHLH186 then modulates a network of monolignol pathway genes and xylem cell-type development. [50]

Biosynthetic: Monolignol and lignin biosynthesis pathway: overexpression upregulates monolignol biosynthetic genes (including PtrPAL2, PtrPAL4, PtrCCoAOMT2, PtrCCoAOMT3, PtrCCR2, PtrCAD1) leading to increased total lignin and guaiacyl (G) lignin with reduced syringyl (S) and p-hydroxyphenyl (H) lignin. [50]

More Details
Protein Family / Domainbasic helix-loop-helix (bHLH) transcription factor family protein.
Expression PatternXylem-abundant and xylem-specific among the common TF targets of PtrMYB074 and PtrWRKY19; overexpressed lines show 8–10-fold higher PtrbHLH186 transcript levels in stem-differentiating xylem than wild-type.
Validation MethodStable overexpression of PtrbHLH186 under the CaMV 35S promoter in P. trichocarpa (OE-PtrbHLH186 lines) with RT-qPCR confirmation; phenotypic analysis of plant growth; histological staining of stem cross and longitudinal sections; scanning electron microscopy of xylem cells; quantitative wood chemistry (lignin and carbohydrate content and lignin composition); drought treatment and survival assay; multi-omics integrative modeling of monolignol biosynthesis using RT-qPCR-based expression profiles of 22 pathway genes.
Breeding Application ValuePtrbHLH186 overexpression produces wood with higher lignin (especially G-lignin) and lower carbohydrate content together with more, smaller vessel elements and enhanced drought tolerance. These traits suggest that targeted manipulation of PtrbHLH186 or its upstream regulators could be used to generate Populus trees with improved drought resilience and tailored lignin composition for specific industrial uses, while carefully managing the trade-off with reduced growth.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed on 4-month-old plants by withholding water until soil relative water content decreased from 60% to 13%, followed by rehydration to assess survival.

GhHB8-5D (Gossypium hirsutum) Plant

Key Findings

  • GhHB8-5D is an HD-ZIP III transcription factor from Gossypium hirsutum with HD, LZ, START, SAD, and MEKHLA domains, localizes to the nucleus, and has a MEKHLA-dependent transcriptional activation activity. [51]
  • GhHB8-5D shows fiber-preferential, developmentally increasing expression in cotton and contains drought-related cis-elements in its promoter, suggesting a role in fiber secondary wall thickening and stress responses. [51]
  • Ectopic overexpression of GhHB8-5D in Arabidopsis causes dwarfism, curly leaves, twisted stems, ectopic secondary wall deposition, irregular xylem vessels, and altered vascular patterning, accompanied by upregulation of secondary wall-related transcription factors, cellulose synthase, and lignin biosynthetic genes. [51]
  • Overexpression of GhHB8-5D or a miR166-resistant synonymous mutant GhHB8-5Dm enhances green seedling rate but reduces root length under mannitol-induced drought stress, and perturbs Arabidopsis HD-ZIP III gene expression, indicating that GhHB8-5D positively regulates secondary wall biosynthesis and contributes to drought resistance by affecting xylem morphology and cell wall thickening. [51]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Ectopic overexpression of GhHB8-5D in Arabidopsis promotes xylem differentiation and ectopic secondary wall (lignin and cellulose) deposition and enhances tolerance to mannitol-simulated drought stress, indicating that GhHB8-5D acts as a positive regulator of secondary wall thickening associated with drought response. [51]

Regulatory Mechanism

GhHB8-5D encodes a nuclear transcriptional activator; yeast assays with full-length and C-terminal truncations demonstrate that the transcriptional activation domain resides in the MEKHLA domain. A conserved miR166-binding site in the START domain mediates post-transcriptional regulation; synonymous mutation of this site (GhHB8-5Dm) abolishes miR166 targeting without changing the protein, altering downstream gene expression patterns. Overexpression of GhHB8-5D in Arabidopsis upregulates master regulators of secondary wall formation (AtNST1, AtVND7, AtMYB46), cellulose synthase genes (AtCESA4, AtCESA8), and lignin biosynthetic genes (At4CL1, AtCCoAOMT1, AtCCR1), whereas GhHB8-5Dm overexpression generally represses these genes. GhHB8-5D overexpression also downregulates AtHB8 and AtPHV and upregulates AtREV, while GhHB8-5Dm induces AtPHB and AtPHV and represses AtHB8, AtCNA, and AtREV, indicating that GhHB8-5D perturbs the HD-ZIP III regulatory network controlling xylem differentiation and secondary wall deposition. [51]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions within the secondary cell wall transcriptional network that governs xylem differentiation and lignin/cellulose biosynthesis, influencing downstream NAC/MYB-type master switches, cellulose synthase, and lignin biosynthetic genes; contributes to drought stress response pathways by modifying xylem morphology and secondary wall thickening, which are linked to improved drought resistance. [51]

Biosynthetic: Secondary cell wall biosynthesis involving cellulose and lignin deposition in vascular tissues and fiber [51]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainHD-ZIP III transcription factor containing a homeodomain (HD) and leucine zipper (LZ), a START lipid-binding domain, an adjacent SAD region, and a C-terminal MEKHLA domain; the MEKHLA domain harbors the transcriptional activation region.
Expression PatternGhHB8-5D is predominantly expressed in cotton fibers, with transcript levels increasing as fiber development progresses (20–25 DPA); its homoeolog GhHB8-5A shows broader expression in root, flower, and 20–25 DPA fiber. The GhHB8-5D promoter contains MBS cis-elements associated with drought inducibility.
Validation Method35S-driven overexpression of GhHB8-5D and its miR166-binding-site synonymous mutant GhHB8-5Dm in Arabidopsis thaliana Col-0; histological and histochemical staining (toluidine blue, phloroglucinol, S4B, lignin autofluorescence) of stem cross sections; qRT-PCR analysis of secondary wall-related genes and Arabidopsis HD-ZIP III genes; mannitol-based drought tolerance and root growth assays; transient GhHB8-5D–eGFP localization in Nicotiana benthamiana; yeast GAL4 DNA-binding domain fusion transcriptional activation assay in Saccharomyces cerevisiae AH109.
Breeding Application ValueGhHB8-5D is a promising target for improving cotton fiber quality and drought resistance; manipulating GhHB8-5D expression or miR166-insensitive variants (GhHB8-5Dm-like) could enhance secondary wall thickening and lignification and thereby increase mechanical strength and drought adaptation in cotton.
Environment / TreatmentMannitol-induced osmotic stress (50–200 mM) applied on MS medium to mimic drought during Arabidopsis seed germination and seedling growth; cotton fiber developmental stages (0–25 DPA); promoter contains an MBS cis-element related to drought inducibility.

GhHB8-5D (via GhHB8-5Dm miRNA-resistant variant) (Gossypium hirsutum) Plant

Key Findings

  • GhHB8-5Dm is a synonymous, miR166-resistant variant of GhHB8-5D that abolishes miRNA targeting without altering the protein. [51]
  • Overexpression of GhHB8-5Dm in Arabidopsis leads to severe alterations in vascular organization, including amphivasal vascular bundles and loss of normal interfascicular secondary wall deposition. [51]
  • GhHB8-5Dm overexpression generally represses secondary wall-related TFs, cellulose synthase, and lignin biosynthetic genes and strongly perturbs the expression of Arabidopsis HD-ZIP III genes (inducing AtPHB/AtPHV and repressing AtHB8/AtCNA/AtREV). [51]
  • GhHB8-5Dm enhances green seedling rate but causes shorter roots under mannitol-induced drought stress compared with wild type, highlighting the functional importance of miR166 regulation of GhHB8-5D in balancing secondary wall formation and drought responses. [51]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of the miR166-resistant GhHB8-5Dm in Arabidopsis alters vascular patterning (amphivasal bundles, disrupted interfascicular secondary walls) and generally represses secondary wall-related genes while still enhancing green seedling rate and increasing sensitivity of root growth to mannitol, showing that miRNA-regulated GhHB8-5D activity modulates secondary wall deposition and drought response. [51]

Regulatory Mechanism

By removing miR166-mediated repression while preserving GhHB8-5D protein sequence, GhHB8-5Dm shifts the balance of HD-ZIP III activity: overexpression broadly represses secondary wall regulators and biosynthetic genes and alters HD-ZIP III expression (upregulating AtPHB and AtPHV, downregulating AtHB8, AtCNA, AtREV), resulting in disrupted vascular patterning and secondary wall deposition; this demonstrates the importance of miRNA control in tuning HD-ZIP III-dependent secondary wall and drought responses. [51]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts within the HD-ZIP III-centered secondary wall and vascular development network, where altered miR166 sensitivity changes the regulatory balance among HD-ZIP III members and their downstream secondary wall genes, thereby impacting xylem patterning and drought-related traits. [51]

Biosynthetic: Secondary cell wall biosynthesis and regulation of lignin and cellulose deposition via altered HD-ZIP III network activity [51]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainEncodes the same HD-ZIP III protein as GhHB8-5D, containing HD, LZ, START, SAD, and MEKHLA domains; only the miR166-binding site at the nucleotide level is altered by synonymous mutations.
Expression PatternGhHB8-5Dm uses the same GhHB8-5D coding sequence at the protein level and therefore reflects the fiber-preferential, developmentally increasing expression pattern of GhHB8-5D in cotton, but the synonymous changes abolish miR166 targeting, effectively altering post-transcriptional regulation rather than spatial expression domains.
Validation Method35S-driven overexpression of GhHB8-5Dm in Arabidopsis thaliana; histological and histochemical staining of stem cross sections; qRT-PCR of secondary wall-related and HD-ZIP III genes; mannitol-based drought tolerance and root length assays; comparison with GhHB8-5D overexpression lines.
Breeding Application ValueThe miRNA-resistant GhHB8-5Dm variant illustrates that modulating miR166 sensitivity of GhHB8-5D can strongly influence secondary wall-related gene expression, vascular patterning, and drought-associated traits, suggesting that engineered alleles with altered miRNA binding could be exploited to fine-tune fiber secondary wall formation and drought adaptation in cotton breeding or biotechnology.
Environment / TreatmentMannitol (50–200 mM) applied to transgenic and wild-type Arabidopsis seedlings to simulate drought stress during germination and early root growth.

IbRAP2.4 (Ipomoea batatas) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as a nuclear AP2/ERF transcriptional activator that promotes lateral root formation and enhances drought tolerance when overexpressed in Arabidopsis, but in sweetpotato overexpression inhibits storage root formation and increases lignified pencil roots, associated with activation of lignin biosynthesis genes [52]

Regulatory Mechanism

Nuclear-localized AP2/ERF transcription factor that specifically binds DRE (TACCGACAT) and GCC-box (AGCCGCC) cis-elements and functions as a transcriptional activator, increasing expression of lignin biosynthesis genes (especially COMT and CCR) in sweetpotato roots, which leads to enhanced lignification and altered root type differentiation [52]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts within AP2/ERF-DREB-mediated abiotic stress and hormone-responsive signaling pathways to regulate root development and lignin biosynthesis, by binding DRE and GCC-box cis-elements and activating downstream gene expression [52]

Biosynthetic: Lignin biosynthesis pathway (upregulation of CCR, COMT, CCoAOMT in sweetpotato roots) [52]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAP2/ERF transcription factor, DREB subfamily, containing a typical AP2 DNA-binding domain
Expression PatternConstitutively expressed in leaves, stems, fibrous roots, pencil roots, and storage roots with higher expression in pigment roots, storage roots, and fibrous roots; induced by NaCl (upregulated peaking at 6 h), slightly and transiently affected by PEG8000-induced dehydration, induced by ethylene (maximum at 24 h), and strongly induced by IAA after 6–15 h in Sushu 16 seedlings
Validation MethodGeneration of CaMV35S:IbRAP2.4 overexpression lines in Arabidopsis thaliana (Col-0) and sweetpotato (Xushu 29) with phenotypic analysis; EMSA showing specific binding to DRE and GCC-box elements; dual-luciferase reporter assays in Arabidopsis protoplasts demonstrating transcriptional activation; yeast transcriptional activation assay; subcellular localization of IbRAP2.4-GFP fusion by confocal microscopy
Breeding Application ValueIbRAP2.4 is proposed as a molecular target for sweetpotato improvement: functional analyses show that it strongly influences root system architecture by promoting lateral root formation (in heterologous Arabidopsis) and shifting sweetpotato roots from storage roots to more lignified pencil roots through activation of lignin biosynthetic genes. Understanding and modulating IbRAP2.4 activity could aid molecular breeding strategies aimed at optimizing storage root development, root architecture, and stress responsiveness for high-yield sweetpotato, for example by fine-tuning its expression or activity to balance lignification and storage root swelling.
Environment / TreatmentNaCl (150 mM) salt stress, 20% PEG8000 dehydration stress, 100 μM ethylene, 100 μM IAA treatments in sweetpotato seedlings; soil drought (withholding water for 7 days followed by re-watering) in Arabidopsis; field cultivation in Nanjing for sweetpotato root phenotype evaluation

FLS2 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • fls2 mutants are less sensitive to drought and salt stress than WT, showing reduced growth inhibition and leaf damage under these stresses. [53]
  • FLS2 contributes to regulation of ROS and proline accumulation under drought and salt stress, with fls2 mutants displaying reduced H2O2 accumulation and altered proline levels compared with WT. [53]
  • RNA-seq reveals that FLS2-mediated signaling broadly affects the transcriptome under drought and salt, with fls2 effects under control conditions resembling WT under drought. [53]
  • FLS2-mediated signals regulate PIF4 expression under drought and NaCl, placing FLS2 upstream of PIF4 in the FLS2–RBOHD–PIF4 stress-response module. [53]

Core Phenotypic Effect

FLS2 positively regulates plant sensitivity to drought and salt stress; fls2 mutants exhibit reduced growth inhibition and leaf damage under drought and NaCl compared with WT, and show altered induction of stress-responsive genes and PIF4 under these conditions. [53]

Regulatory Mechanism

Upon stress perception, FLS2-mediated signaling contributes to ROS and Ca2+ signaling and modulates PIF4 expression, thereby influencing transcriptome reprogramming and physiological responses (proline accumulation, ROS balance) under drought and salt stress. [53]

Pathways

Regulatory: Component of pattern-triggered immunity and abiotic stress signaling; functions upstream in the FLS2–RBOHD–PIF4 module that integrates immune ROS signaling with transcriptional control of drought and salt stress responses. [53]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainPattern-recognition receptor kinase for the bacterial flagellin peptide flg22, functioning as an immune signal receptor at the cell surface.
Validation MethodFunctional analysis of T-DNA insertion fls2 mutants (SALK_099606, SALK_141277) under drought and NaCl soil treatments; phenotyping of growth and leaf area; ROS (O2− and H2O2) and cell-death staining; proline quantification; RNA-seq-based differential expression and GO analysis; comparison of PIF4 expression in WT versus fls2 under drought and NaCl.
Breeding Application ValueLoss-of-function fls2 mutants show reduced sensitivity and increased tolerance to drought and salt stress, indicating that down-regulation of FLS2-mediated signaling could be used to enhance plant tolerance to combined abiotic stresses, although potential trade-offs with pathogen immunity must be considered.
Environment / TreatmentSoil drought (water withheld until soil water content ~10%), 100 mM NaCl watering, and growth in standard 1/2 MS medium; immune-related flg22 is discussed for FLS2 signaling but abiotic phenotypes were assayed mainly under drought and NaCl.

RBOHD (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • rbohd rbohf double mutants are strongly hypersensitive to drought and salt stress, with severely inhibited growth and leaf yellowing compared with WT. [53]
  • RBOHD-dependent H2O2 production is impaired in rbohd rbohf mutants under both control and stress conditions, correlating with increased cell death and altered proline accumulation. [53]
  • Under normal and stress conditions, rbohd rbohf mutants exhibit extensive transcriptome reprogramming, including constitutive activation of genes associated with salt and drought responses, JA signaling, oxidative stress, hypoxia, and innate immune responses. [53]
  • GO and heatmap analyses indicate that RBOHD-mediated signals are essential for proper regulation of genes involved in plant water-deprivation response and salt stress, supporting a central role for RBOHD in abiotic stress adaptation within the FLS2–RBOHD–PIF4 module. [53]

Core Phenotypic Effect

RBOHD is essential for appropriate ROS (H2O2) production and transcriptome reprogramming under drought and salt stress; rbohd rbohf double mutants show reduced growth, impaired H2O2 accumulation, increased cell death, and strong hypersensitivity to drought and NaCl, with constitutive and exaggerated activation of abiotic and oxidative stress-related genes. [53]

Regulatory Mechanism

RBOHD-dependent H2O2 production modulates ROS homeostasis and is required for appropriate activation and repression of genes responsive to drought, salt, hypoxia, oxidative stress, JA, and immune signals; loss of RBOHD results in defective H2O2 accumulation and exaggerated activation of stress-response pathways. [53]

Pathways

Regulatory: Key ROS-generating component of the FLS2–RBOHD–PIF4 module; functions downstream of FLS2 in immune and abiotic stress signaling to generate H2O2, which in turn contributes to transcriptome reprogramming and regulation of PIF4 and other stress-related genes under drought and salt stress. [53]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainRespiratory burst oxidase homolog D, a member of the NADPH oxidase (NOX) family that regulates ROS production.
Validation MethodPhenotypic analysis of rbohd rbohf T-DNA double mutants (CS9558 and CS68522) under drought and 100 mM NaCl soil treatments; growth and leaf-area measurements; DAB and NBT staining for ROS; trypan blue staining for cell death; proline quantification; RNA-seq and GO enrichment to characterize transcriptome reprogramming under control, drought, and salt conditions.
Breeding Application ValueRBOHD-mediated H2O2 production is required to maintain ROS homeostasis and prevent hypersensitivity to drought and salt stress; enhancing or fine-tuning RBOHD activity could help breed plants that better tolerate drought and salinity without constitutive stress activation or growth retardation.
Environment / TreatmentSoil drought (progressive drying to ~10% soil water content) and 100 mM NaCl watering under controlled conditions, which strongly inhibit rbohd rbohf growth compared with WT, and under which ROS and transcriptome responses were evaluated.

PIF4 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • TF network analysis under drought identifies PIF4 as a primary transcription factor in WT, regulated by multiple other TFs. [53]
  • PIF4pro::GUS reporter plants show strong induction of GUS activity under mannitol and NaCl treatments, indicating stress-inducible promoter activity. [53]
  • PIF4 transcript levels are regulated by FLS2- and RBOHD-mediated signals; under drought PIF4 induction is weaker in fls2 and rbohd rbohf mutants than in WT, and under NaCl PIF4 expression decreases in these mutants. [53]
  • pif4 mutants display increased tolerance to both drought and salt stress compared with WT and exhibit reduced flg22-triggered root growth inhibition, supporting a role for PIF4 as a positive regulator of stress sensitivity and immune responses downstream of FLS2 and RBOHD. [53]

Core Phenotypic Effect

PIF4 acts as a central transcription factor downstream of FLS2 and RBOHD; pif4 mutants are more tolerant to drought and salt stress, and show reduced inhibition of root elongation by flg22, indicating that PIF4 positively contributes to stress sensitivity and FLS2-dependent immune responses. [53]

Regulatory Mechanism

As a transcription factor, PIF4 integrates signals downstream of FLS2- and RBOHD-mediated pathways, modulating ROS homeostasis and the expression of drought- and salt-responsive genes as well as components of the immune response; reduced PIF4 function shifts plants toward increased tolerance to drought and salt and diminished flg22 sensitivity. [53]

Pathways

Regulatory: Central TF in the FLS2–RBOHD–PIF4 module; acts downstream of immune receptor FLS2 and ROS producer RBOHD to regulate transcriptional networks controlling plant responses to drought, salt stress, and pathogen-associated molecular patterns. [53]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainPHYTOCHROME INTERACTING FACTOR 4, a phytochrome-interacting transcription factor.
Expression PatternPIF4pro::GUS expression in seedling cotyledons is strongly induced by mannitol and NaCl treatments compared with untreated control; PIF4 transcript levels are induced by drought in WT and to a lesser extent in fls2 and rbohd rbohf, while under NaCl treatment PIF4 expression decreases in fls2 and rbohd rbohf compared with WT.
Validation MethodPhenotypic analysis of the pif4 T-DNA mutant (SAIL_1288_E07) under drought and 100 mM NaCl soil treatments; root-growth assay in response to 1 μM flg22; expression analysis of PIF4 in WT, fls2, and rbohd rbohf mutants under drought and NaCl; GUS staining of PIF4pro::GUS transgenic seedlings grown on mannitol and NaCl to monitor promoter activity.
Breeding Application ValueLoss-of-function pif4 mutants display increased tolerance to both drought and salt stress and reduced sensitivity to flg22-triggered root growth inhibition, indicating that down-regulating PIF4 activity could be a promising strategy to improve multi-stress tolerance by modulating ROS homeostasis and stress-responsive transcription networks.
Environment / TreatmentMannitol (150 mM) and NaCl (100 mM) in 1/2 MS medium to induce PIF4pro::GUS activity; soil drought and 100 mM NaCl watering to test pif4 stress tolerance; 1 μM flg22 treatment to test FLS2-dependent immune signaling.

GhTIP1;1-like (Gossypium hirsutum) Plant

Key Findings

  • GhTIP1;1-like encodes a tonoplast aquaporin of the TIP1;1 subfamily with conserved NPA motifs and six transmembrane helices and localizes to the vacuolar membrane. [54]
  • GhTIP1;1-like is strongly induced by cold and also by heat, drought and salt stresses, with distinct tissue- and stage-specific expression patterns in cotton, and its promoter contains an MYC motif essential for cold-inducible activity. [54]
  • Virus-induced silencing of GhTIP1;1-like in cotton seedlings reduces cold tolerance, increases O2− accumulation and MDA, decreases SS and SOD, and suppresses expression of cold-responsive genes GhCBF1/2/3 and GhKIN1. [54]
  • Overexpression of GhTIP1;1-like in Arabidopsis accelerates bolting, increases seed number per pod, delays reproductive-stage senescence, and significantly enhances cold tolerance by improving physiological indices (higher SP and POD, lower MDA) and strongly up-regulating multiple cold-responsive genes (AtCBF1/2/3, AtCOR15a, AtKIN1/2, AtCOR47, AtRD29A). [54]
  • Yeast two-hybrid assays show that GhTIP1;1-like physically interacts with GhBK1 and protochlorophyllide reductases GhPORA and GhPORC, and these interactors are differentially regulated by cold stress, implicating GhTIP1;1-like in a broader cold-response network. [54]

Core Phenotypic Effect

GhTIP1;1-like acts as a positive regulator of plant cold tolerance: silencing in cotton reduces cold tolerance, whereas overexpression (tested in Arabidopsis) enhances tolerance to chilling/freezing stress. [54]

Regulatory Mechanism

Tonoplast aquaporin that likely modulates cellular water status and ROS homeostasis under cold stress: GhTIP1;1-like influences O2− accumulation, antioxidant enzyme activities (SOD, POD), soluble osmolytes (SS, SP) and lipid peroxidation (MDA) in cotton and Arabidopsis during chilling; its promoter contains an MYC cis-element that is critical for cold-inducible transcription; GhTIP1;1-like physically interacts with protochlorophyllide reductases (GhPORA, GhPORC) and GhBK1, which may integrate membrane transport, chloroplast function and BR signaling in cold responses. [54]

Pathways

Regulatory: Cold stress response pathway in cotton and Arabidopsis, involving activation of CBF and downstream COR/KIN/RD genes; GhTIP1;1-like protein also interacts with GhBK1, a protein associated with brassinosteroid (BR) signaling, suggesting a potential connection between aquaporin-mediated cold responses and BR-related pathways. [54]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainTonoplast intrinsic protein (TIP) aquaporin of the major intrinsic protein (MIP) family; contains two NPA motifs and six hydrophobic α-helical transmembrane regions typical of tonoplast aquaporins
Expression PatternIn cotton, GhTIP1;1-like is differentially expressed among organs: highest in sepals, followed by 15 dpa fibers, petals, leaves, stems and roots, with low expression in 10 dpa fibers and stamens and the lowest in 5 dpa fibers. In 7-day-old seedlings (cotyledon stage), GhTIP1;1-like is induced by cold (4°C), heat (42°C), salt (300 mM NaCl) and dehydration (10% PEG6000), with expression peaking around 3 h in cotyledons and roots and stronger induction in cotyledons than roots. In 28-day-old seedlings (true leaf stage), basal expression is higher in roots than leaves; under cold, heat, salt and PEG treatments, expression in both roots and leaves increases then decreases, with stronger induction generally in roots; in leaves, heat and salt induce peaks at 3 h, whereas PEG-induced expression peaks at 24 h. ProGhTIP1;1-like::GUS transgenic Arabidopsis show promoter activity in leaves and roots at 22°C that is enhanced and extended to stems and anthers after cold (4°C), and the −33 to −1033 bp promoter fragment containing an MYC motif confers strong cold-responsive GUS expression, especially in roots, stems and anthers.
Breeding Application ValueGhTIP1;1-like is a promising target for improving cold tolerance in upland cotton: its silencing decreases, while its overexpression (tested in Arabidopsis) markedly enhances cold tolerance, accompanied by favorable ROS and antioxidant profiles and strong induction of cold-responsive genes. The gene also responds to heat, drought and salt, suggesting potential for broad-spectrum abiotic stress improvement. The MYC-containing cold-inducible promoter fragment could be exploited to drive stress-responsive expression of beneficial genes. Manipulating GhTIP1;1-like and its promoter via transgenic or genome-editing approaches could enhance seedling survival, maintain photosynthetic tissues under low temperature, and indirectly support yield stability in cotton grown in cold-prone regions.
Environment / TreatmentCold stress at 4°C (for cotton seedlings, VIGS cotton, and Arabidopsis acclimation) and 0°C (prolonged cold treatment of Arabidopsis); heat stress at 42°C; salt stress with 300 mM NaCl; dehydration with 10% PEG6000; normal growth at 22–25°C; assays performed after 0–24 h (and up to 120 h for some cold treatments).

MbMYB4 (Malus baccata) Plant

Key Findings

  • MbMYB4 was cloned from Malus baccata as a 762 bp ORF encoding a 253–amino acid hydrophilic R2R3-MYB transcription factor with two SANT-MYB DNA-binding domains and nuclear localization. [55]
  • MbMYB4 is expressed in multiple organs of M. baccata, with higher expression in roots and young leaves, and is strongly induced by low temperature, dehydration, and ABA, and to a lesser extent by high salt and high temperature. [55]
  • Overexpression of MbMYB4 in Arabidopsis thaliana significantly improves cold tolerance, increasing survival after -6 °C treatment and enhancing chlorophyll and proline contents, POD and CAT activities, while reducing MDA accumulation and membrane damage. [55]
  • Overexpression of MbMYB4 also improves drought tolerance in Arabidopsis, increasing survival after 10 d of water deprivation and improving physiological indices associated with osmotic adjustment and ROS scavenging. [55]
  • Under cold stress, MbMYB4-overexpressing plants show elevated expression of AtCBF1, AtCBF3, AtCOR15a, and AtRD29a, indicating that MbMYB4 activates a CBF-dependent cold response pathway. [55]
  • Under drought stress, MbMYB4-overexpressing plants show elevated expression of ABA-related genes AtNCED3 and AtSnRK2.4 and drought/ROS-related genes AtCAT1 and AtP5CS, suggesting that MbMYB4 enhances drought tolerance through ABA biosynthesis/signaling and improved ROS detoxification and proline accumulation. [55]

Core Phenotypic Effect

MbMYB4 is a nuclear R2R3-MYB transcription factor whose overexpression confers enhanced cold and drought tolerance, as evidenced by higher survival rates, better maintenance of chlorophyll, increased proline, POD and CAT activities, and reduced MDA content and membrane damage under stress. [55]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a nuclear transcriptional regulator that, when overexpressed, positively regulates expression of cold-responsive genes AtCBF1 and AtCBF3 and their targets AtCOR15a and AtRD29a under cold stress, and upregulates ABA biosynthesis and signaling genes AtNCED3 and AtSnRK2.4 as well as drought/ROS-related genes AtCAT1 and AtP5CS under drought, thereby enhancing ROS scavenging and osmotic adjustment. [55]

Pathways

Regulatory: Participates in a CBF-dependent cold response pathway by acting upstream of AtCBF1/AtCBF3 and their downstream cold-responsive genes AtCOR15a and AtRD29a, and in ABA-mediated drought response by promoting ABA biosynthesis and signaling via AtNCED3 and AtSnRK2.4 and enhancing ROS detoxification and proline accumulation via AtCAT1 and AtP5CS. [55]

Biosynthetic: Positively influences ABA biosynthesis via upregulation of AtNCED3 and promotes proline biosynthesis via activation of AtP5CS under drought stress. [55]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainR2R3-MYB transcription factor containing two SANT-MYB DNA-binding domains with helix-turn-helix structure.
Expression PatternMbMYB4 is expressed in young leaf, stem, root, and mature leaf of Malus baccata, with higher basal expression in root and young leaf than in mature leaf and stem. Its transcript level in young leaf and root is strongly induced by low temperature, high-salt, dehydration, high temperature, and ABA treatments, with the highest induction under cold and dehydration stresses (up to about 7.8–8.7-fold over control at peak times).
Validation MethodFunctional validation by constitutive overexpression of MbMYB4 in Arabidopsis thaliana (pCAMBIA2300-MbMYB4, T3 homozygous lines S1, S3, S5) followed by cold stress (-6 °C for 14 h) and drought stress (10 d without watering) assays, physiological index measurements (proline, chlorophyll, POD, CAT, MDA, relative conductivity), and qPCR analysis of stress-responsive genes.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of MbMYB4 significantly enhances cold and drought tolerance in transgenic Arabidopsis thaliana, indicating that MbMYB4 is a promising candidate gene for engineering or breeding Malus and other crops with improved tolerance to low temperature and water deficit conditions, particularly in cold and arid regions.
Environment / TreatmentLow-temperature stress at 4 °C, high-salt stress with 200 mM NaCl, dehydration stress with 20% PEG6000, high-temperature stress at 37 °C, and ABA treatment at 50 μM for Malus baccata seedlings; cold stress of -6 °C for 14 h and drought stress by withholding water for 10 days for Arabidopsis thaliana overexpressing MbMYB4.

RcNAC091 (Rosa chinensis) Plant

Key Findings

  • RcNAC091 is an ATAF subfamily NAC transcription factor in Rosa chinensis with a promoter enriched in ABA- and stress-related cis-regulatory elements. [56]
  • Transcriptome analysis and RT-qPCR show that RcNAC091 is highly expressed in several organs and floral stages and is strongly induced in leaves and roots by drought, salt, ABA, PEG, and NaCl treatments. [56]
  • Virus-induced gene silencing reduces RcNAC091 expression by ~40% and causes decreased dehydration tolerance, with greater wilting, smaller leaf-disc area, lower chlorophyll content, and higher ion leakage after dehydration and rehydration. [56]
  • Silenced discs accumulate more ROS and exhibit more cell death, demonstrating that RcNAC091 positively regulates the dehydration stress response and is a promising target for enhancing drought resistance in rose. [56]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Positive regulator of dehydration stress tolerance in rose; silencing RcNAC091 decreases tolerance, causing stronger wilting, reduced leaf-disc area, lower chlorophyll content, higher ion leakage, increased cell death, and elevated ROS accumulation after dehydration and rehydration. [56]

Pathways
