Regulatory: Involved in ABA-dependent abiotic stress response pathways, particularly dehydration/drought and salt stress responses, based on enrichment of ABRE and other stress-related cis-elements in its promoter and strong induction by ABA and abiotic stresses. [56]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainNAC (NAM, ATAF, and CUC) transcription factor, member of the ATAF subfamily containing a conserved NAC (NAM) DNA-binding domain.
Expression PatternRcNAC091, an ATAF subfamily NAC, shows relatively high expression across multiple organs and floral transition stages and is strongly induced in leaves and roots under drought and salt stress in transcriptome datasets; RT-qPCR confirmed that RcNAC091 is upregulated in leaves and roots by ABA, PEG, and NaCl treatments, indicating broad responsiveness to abiotic stress and ABA.
Validation MethodVirus-induced gene silencing (VIGS) of RcNAC091 in rose leaf discs using TRV-RcNAC091, followed by controlled dehydration (0, 4, 8 h) and 3 h rehydration assays with measurements of disc area, chlorophyll content, ion leakage, cell death (trypan blue), and ROS (DAB, NBT); RT-qPCR used to verify RcNAC091 silencing efficiency and its induction by ABA, PEG, and NaCl.
Breeding Application ValueRcNAC091 is a drought/dehydration stress–inducible NAC transcription factor whose silencing reduces dehydration tolerance, indicating it is a promising candidate gene for improving abiotic stress (especially drought/dehydration) resistance in cultivated roses through molecular breeding or transgenic approaches.
Environment / TreatmentAbiotic stress conditions including drought and salt stress in whole plants (transcriptome data), dehydration of detached leaf discs for 0, 4, and 8 h followed by 3 h rehydration, and chemical treatments with ABA (50 μM), PEG 6000 (10%), and NaCl (200 mM).

PAPH1 (Os01g0800500) (Oryza sativa f. spontanea) Plant

Key Findings

  • Selection and GWAS analyses using whole-genome resequencing of 501 accessions and drought traits in 274 japonica-type accessions identified Os01g0800500 (PAPH1) within a chromosome 1 region under strong selection and associated with leaf withering degree under drought. [57]
  • PAPH1 encodes a purple acid phosphatase-family protein; knockout via CRISPR/Cas9 in weedy rice accession WR16 greatly reduces drought tolerance (higher leaf withering degree), while overexpression markedly enhances drought tolerance (almost no leaf withering) under severe drought. [57]
  • Under severe drought, PAPH1 expression is strongly decreased in edited lines and increased in overexpression lines; in parallel, loss of PAPH1 causes Ca2+ efflux and reduced K+ influx in mesophyll cells, whereas overexpression increases both Ca2+ and K+ influx, indicating a role in regulating ion flux homeostasis. [57]
  • Two japonica-type PAPH1 haplotypes (Hap1 and Hap2) are widely distributed among weedy, landrace, and improved rice and are each associated with high drought-resistance index D, suggesting that PAPH1 haplotypes and alleles selected during de-domestication can be valuable genetic resources for breeding drought-resistant cultivated rice. [57]

Core Phenotypic Effect

PAPH1 positively regulates drought resistance; loss of function increases leaf withering and reduces drought tolerance, whereas overexpression markedly enhances drought tolerance in weedy rice. [57]

Regulatory Mechanism

By encoding a PAP-family phosphatase, PAPH1 modulates Ca2+ and K+ fluxes across mesophyll cell membranes under drought; loss of PAPH1 causes Ca2+ efflux and reduced K+ influx, whereas overexpression leads to increased Ca2+ and K+ influx, indicating a role in maintaining cellular ion homeostasis during drought stress. [57]

More Details
Protein Family / Domainpurple acid phosphatase (PAP) family protein
Expression PatternUnder severe drought at the meiosis stage, PAPH1 transcript levels are strongly reduced in CRISPR-edited knockout lines and strongly elevated in overexpression lines compared with their respective wild-type controls.
Validation MethodCRISPR/Cas9 knockout of PAPH1 and construction of PAPH1 overexpression lines in WR16, followed by drought-stress phenotyping (leaf withering degree), qRT-PCR expression analysis, and Ca2+ and K+ ion flux assays using NMT in flag leaf mesophyll cells under severe drought.
Breeding Application ValuePAPH1 is a drought-resistance gene from weedy rice that enhances tolerance by improving leaf survival and maintaining Ca2+ and K+ influx under severe drought; its favorable haplotypes and functional alleles can be exploited as donors or markers in breeding programs to improve drought resistance in cultivated rice, and PAPH1 overexpression or precise editing could be used to develop varieties with reduced leaf withering and better performance under water deficit.
Environment / TreatmentSevere drought stress imposed by stopping irrigation in field trials and by withholding water from potted WR16 plants at the meiosis stage until soil water content reached ~4.1%, when flag leaves were strongly drought-stressed and withering.

BpERF017 (Betula platyphylla) Plant

Key Findings

  • BpERF017 is a first-layer ERF transcription factor in the birch drought-response GRN and regulates more TFs and structural genes than any other upstream TF. [58]
  • It controls 16 second-layer TFs whose downstream targets (131 structural genes) span all 10 drought-related biological processes, including JA biosynthesis/metabolism, response to JA, response to water deprivation, osmotic and oxidative stress responses, and water transport. [58]
  • GRN construction from drought time-course RNA-seq combined with extensive ChIP-PCR and qRT-PCR validation supports BpERF017 as a central upstream regulator in the drought-response network. [58]
  • The breadth of biological processes regulated by BpERF017 suggests that modulating its activity could strongly influence drought tolerance in birch. [58]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as a key upstream transcription factor that coordinates drought response by regulating a large number of downstream TFs and structural genes involved in JA signaling, water deprivation, osmotic and oxidative stress responses, and water transport. [58]

Regulatory Mechanism

Upstream ERF transcription factor that, based on GRN inference and TF–DNA binding assays, regulates multiple second-layer TFs whose downstream targets include structural genes in diverse drought-related biological processes. [58]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in a drought-responsive transcriptional regulatory network integrating jasmonic acid signaling (JA biosynthesis, metabolism, and response), response to water deprivation, response to osmotic and oxidative stress, and water transport. [58]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainERF (ethylene response factor) transcription factor, member of the AP2/ERF family.
Expression PatternFirst-layer TF whose expression is triggered by PEG6000-induced drought stress and that is associated with regulation of genes across all 10 drought-related GO processes.
Validation MethodFunctional role as an upstream regulator was supported by its position in the partial correlation-based GRN derived from drought time-course RNA-seq, together with experimental validation of many first-layer TF–target relationships using ChIP-PCR, ChIP-qPCR, and qRT-PCR in transiently transformed birch.
Breeding Application ValueAs a first-layer hub transcription factor that regulates many downstream TFs and structural genes across all 10 drought-related biological processes (including JA biosynthetic and metabolic processes, response to JA, response to water deprivation, osmotic and oxidative stress, and water transport), BpERF017 is a strong candidate target for molecular breeding or genetic engineering of birch with broad-spectrum drought tolerance by modulating multiple stress-response pathways simultaneously.
Environment / TreatmentPEG6000-simulated drought stress (typically 20% w/v PEG6000 applied to roots for 2, 4, 6, and 9 hours) used to induce transcriptional responses.

BpAGL61 (Betula platyphylla) Plant

Key Findings

  • BpAGL61 is a first-layer agamous-like MADS-box transcription factor in the birch drought-response GRN. [58]
  • It regulates nine second-layer TFs, which together control 118 structural genes through 289 regulatory relationships. [58]
  • BpAGL61 regulates most genes in JA biosynthetic/metabolic processes and response to JA, as well as seven of nine genes in response to ethylene, linking it to JA and ethylene signaling under drought. [58]
  • It also regulates most genes in regulation of stomatal movement and response to osmotic stress, and a large proportion of genes in response to water deprivation and oxidative stress, indicating a central role in multiple drought-response pathways. [58]
  • The breadth of its regulatory influence suggests BpAGL61 is an important upstream regulator contributing to drought tolerance in birch. [58]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as an important upstream MADS-box regulator that modulates drought tolerance by controlling genes involved in JA and ethylene signaling, stomatal movement, osmotic stress, water deprivation, and oxidative stress. [58]

Regulatory Mechanism

First-layer agamous-like MADS-box transcription factor that regulates nine second-layer TFs, which collectively have 289 regulatory relationships with 118 structural genes spread across multiple drought-related biological processes. [58]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in a drought-responsive regulatory network that integrates JA biosynthetic and metabolic pathways, JA response, ethylene signaling, regulation of stomatal movement, and responses to osmotic and oxidative stress. [58]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAgamous-like MADS-box transcription factor (AGL-type).
Expression PatternTop-layer TF whose expression is induced by drought in the PEG6000 treatment regime and that is associated with regulation of large subsets of genes in JA and ethylene signaling, stomatal movement, and osmotic/oxidative stress responses.
Validation MethodRole inferred from its top-layer position and extensive downstream edges in the partial correlation-based GRN, with many first- and second-layer TF–target edges experimentally verified by ChIP-PCR, ChIP-qPCR, and qRT-PCR.
Breeding Application ValueAs a major first-layer MADS-box regulator that controls large fractions of genes in response to osmotic stress, water deprivation, oxidative stress, JA and ethylene signaling, and stomatal movement, BpAGL61 is a promising target for breeding or engineering birch trees with enhanced drought tolerance via coordinated modulation of multiple hormone and stress-response pathways.
Environment / TreatmentPEG6000-induced drought stress (20% w/v for 2–9 hours) used to trigger transcriptomic changes incorporated into the GRN.

BpNAC090 (Betula platyphylla) Plant

Key Findings

  • BpNAC090 is a first-layer NAC transcription factor in the birch drought-response GRN and regulates eight second-layer TFs that control 97 structural genes. [58]
  • Compared with BpERF017 and BpAGL61, BpNAC090 mainly regulates genes in response to water deprivation, oxidative stress, regulation of stomatal movement, and response to osmotic stress, with limited involvement in JA biosynthesis/metabolism and water transport. [58]
  • Stable and transient overexpression of BpNAC090 in birch leads to enhanced drought tolerance, manifested as higher fresh weight, increased lateral root number, higher relative water content, and significantly reduced MDA content, electrolyte leakage, and ROS levels under PEG-induced drought. [58]
  • ChIP-PCR/ChIP-qPCR and qRT-PCR confirm that BpNAC090 directly binds to promoters of predicted targets and alters their expression, validating its role as an upstream transcriptional regulator in the drought-response network. [58]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of BpNAC090 confers enhanced drought tolerance, with reduced ROS, MDA content, and electrolyte leakage and improved fresh weight, lateral root formation, and relative water content under PEG-induced drought. [58]

Regulatory Mechanism

First-layer NAC transcription factor that regulates eight second-layer TFs, which collectively have 159 regulatory relationships with 97 structural genes; ChIP-based assays show that BpNAC090 binds to promoters of predicted target genes and modulates their expression, thereby influencing drought-related processes. [58]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in a drought-responsive transcriptional network mainly through regulation of genes involved in response to water deprivation, oxidative stress, regulation of stomatal movement, and response to osmotic stress, rather than JA or water transport pathways. [58]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainNAC transcription factor.
Expression PatternFirst-layer TF whose expression is induced during PEG6000 drought treatment and whose regulatory connections are enriched for genes in response to water deprivation, oxidative stress, stomatal movement, and osmotic stress.
Validation MethodFunctional validation by stable and transient overexpression in birch under PEG-induced drought combined with physiological assays (MDA, electrolyte leakage, ROS, growth traits) and ChIP-PCR/ChIP-qPCR plus qRT-PCR to confirm direct regulation of downstream target genes.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of BpNAC090 enhances drought tolerance in birch by reducing ROS, MDA content, and electrolyte leakage and by increasing lateral root number, fresh weight, and relative water content under PEG-induced drought, making it a practical candidate gene for developing birch lines with improved drought tolerance and root system architecture through transgenic approaches or targeted genome editing.
Environment / TreatmentPEG6000-induced drought: 20% (w/v) PEG6000 time-course treatment used for GRN construction and 9–10% (w/v) PEG6000 applied for 15 days to assess drought tolerance of BpNAC090-overexpressing lines.

BpMADS11 (Betula platyphylla) Plant

Key Findings

  • BpMADS11 is a first-layer MADS-box transcription factor in the birch drought-response GRN. [58]
  • Stable and transient overexpression of BpMADS11 in birch plants results in higher fresh weight and significantly longer roots under both normal and PEG6000-induced drought conditions. [58]
  • BpMADS11-overexpressing plants exhibit enhanced drought tolerance, with significantly reduced MDA content, electrolyte leakage, and ROS levels compared with wild type under drought. [58]
  • ChIP-PCR/ChIP-qPCR and qRT-PCR confirm that BpMADS11 binds to promoters of predicted target genes and regulates their expression, supporting its role as an upstream regulator of drought-response genes. [58]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of BpMADS11 confers enhanced drought tolerance, characterized by reduced ROS, MDA content, and electrolyte leakage and improved fresh weight and root length under PEG-induced drought. [58]

Regulatory Mechanism

MADS-box transcription factor that binds to promoters of predicted target genes in birch, as shown by ChIP-PCR and ChIP-qPCR, and alters their expression according to qRT-PCR, thereby modulating drought-related physiological traits. [58]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts within the drought-response regulatory network as a first-layer MADS-box TF, influencing processes related to drought tolerance; specific downstream biological processes are not detailed in the text but overlap with stress-response pathways validated for first-layer TFs. [58]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainMADS-box transcription factor.
Expression PatternFirst-layer TF whose expression is induced by drought in the GRN context and whose overexpression in birch leads to drought-tolerant phenotypes.
Validation MethodFunctional validation via stable and transient overexpression combined with drought physiological assays, plus ChIP-PCR/ChIP-qPCR and qRT-PCR to confirm direct regulation of target genes.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of the MADS-box transcription factor BpMADS11 increases drought tolerance and promotes root growth (especially root length) in birch, suggesting its utility for breeding or engineering trees with improved drought resilience and root architecture.
Environment / TreatmentPEG6000-induced drought: 20% (w/v) PEG6000 used for transcriptomic GRN construction and 9–10% (w/v) PEG6000 applied for 15 days to evaluate drought tolerance of BpMADS11-overexpressing lines.

ZjVND7 (Ziziphus jujuba var. spinosa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

NAC transcription factor whose direct DNA-binding targets are enriched in genes for xylem development, secondary wall synthesis, cell death, cell division, DNA replication and stress/hormone responses; shows stronger binding and a larger repertoire of promoter targets in autotetraploid sour jujube under drought, consistent with increased xylem vessel and parenchyma cell numbers and enhanced drought response in autotetraploids. [59]

Regulatory Mechanism

ZjVND7 is a NAC transcription factor that binds preferentially to proximal promoter regions (within 2.0 kb upstream of TSS) with conserved CTTNAAG-containing motifs; binds a 19-nt motif including TTGCTT/AAGCAA; directly binds the ZjSMR1 promoter in yeast one-hybrid; in autotetraploids, shows increased number and strength of binding peaks, including to an ABRE (ACGTG) motif in the ZjSMR1 promoter, indicating transcriptional regulation of cell-cycle and stress-related genes. [59]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts in a xylem vessel differentiation and secondary cell wall regulatory network; controls downstream genes enriched in metabolic process, developmental process, response to stimulus, response to hormone, ABA signaling, cell death, cell division, and DNA replication; in autotetraploids targets additional pathways, including defense response, hydrogen peroxide metabolism, stress-related signal transduction, triterpenoid metabolism, flavonoid biosynthesis, photosynthesis, and glutathione metabolism under drought. [59]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainNAC transcription factor; secondary wall NAC (SWN) / VND subfamily
Expression PatternExpressed in leaves; under drought (soil water content 4% for 7 days), ZjVND7 shows a larger fold-change in autotetraploid leaves than in diploid leaves; expression is downregulated shortly after ABA spray (2 h) and then increases by 12 h, indicating ABA responsiveness.
Validation MethodGene cloning and phylogenetic analysis; qRT-PCR expression analysis in diploid and autotetraploid leaves under drought and ABA treatments; DAP-seq to map genome-wide binding peaks and motifs in diploid and autotetraploid genomes; GO/KEGG enrichment of bound targets; subcellular localization of ZjVND7-eGFP in tobacco leaves; yeast one-hybrid showing binding of ZjVND7 to the ZjSMR1 promoter.
Breeding Application ValueZjVND7 represents a key regulatory gene whose target network in autotetraploid sour jujube is enriched for xylem development and stress-response genes, and its altered binding and expression under drought provide a mechanistic entry point to modulate vessel number, parenchyma cell proliferation, and drought tolerance; understanding and manipulating ZjVND7 or its promoter binding sites may aid in breeding sour jujube rootstocks or polyploid lines with improved hydraulic efficiency and drought resilience.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by withholding water until soil water content reached 4% field capacity for 7 days; foliar ABA treatment at 10 mg/L with sampling at 2 h and 12 h; comparison between diploid and colchicine-derived autotetraploid sour jujube.

ZjSMR1 (Ziziphus jujuba var. spinosa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Downstream cell division–related gene directly targeted by ZjVND7; carries DAP-seq peaks in both diploid and autotetraploid genomes with stronger binding signal in autotetraploids; expression is downregulated under drought and shows ABA-responsive dynamics similar to ZjVND7, supporting a role in cell division and DNA endoreduplication adjustments associated with increased xylem parenchyma cells and vessel differentiation, particularly in autotetraploids. [59]

Regulatory Mechanism

Promoter carries a ZjVND7-targeted ABRE (ACGTG) motif and is directly bound by ZjVND7 as shown by DAP-seq and yeast one-hybrid; stronger DAP-seq peak scores in autotetraploids (53 and 63) than in diploids (20) indicate enhanced binding; literature and discussion indicate SMR genes suppress cell cycle progression and promote endoreduplication, suggesting ZjSMR1 acts as a cell-cycle checkpoint regulator downstream of ZjVND7 in drought/ABA responses. [59]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in a ZjVND7-centered regulatory pathway controlling cell division and DNA endoreduplication in xylem and leaf tissues under drought and ABA; implicated in the balance between cell division and differentiation of xylem parenchyma cells that precede vessel formation, and in stress-induced adjustments of the cell cycle/endoreduplication program. [59]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainPlant-specific SMR (SIAMESE-RELATED) cell cycle inhibitor family
Expression PatternZjSMR1 expression is downregulated under drought stress in both diploid and autotetraploid sour jujube, with expression changes paralleling those of ZjVND7; after ABA spray on diploid plants, ZjSMR1 expression decreases markedly at 2 h and increases by 12 h, indicating ABA responsiveness.
Validation MethodDAP-seq identification of ZjSMR1 promoter peaks bound by ZjVND7 in diploid and autotetraploid genomes; yeast one-hybrid confirming direct binding of ZjVND7 to a 474 bp ZjSMR1 promoter fragment; subcellular localization of ZjSMR1-eGFP in tobacco leaves; qRT-PCR measuring ZjSMR1 expression in diploid and autotetraploid plants under drought and in diploid plants after ABA treatment.
Breeding Application ValueZjSMR1, as a directly ZjVND7-regulated cell-cycle control gene responsive to drought and ABA and with stronger promoter binding in autotetraploids, is a potential target to fine-tune the balance between cell division and endoreduplication in xylem parenchyma, thereby modulating vessel number and drought tolerance in sour jujube breeding, especially in autotetraploid rootstock lines.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress (4% soil water content for 7 days) applied to diploid and autotetraploid sour jujube; ABA foliar spray (10 mg/L) on diploid sour jujube with sampling at 2 h and 12 h.

miR2105 (Oryza sativa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • miR2105 expression is repressed by ABA, drought, and salt, whereas its target OsbZIP86 is induced under these conditions. [60]
  • miR2105 directly cleaves OsbZIP86 mRNA at the complementary site, as shown by 5'-RLM-RACE. [60]
  • miR2105 overexpression decreases, and STTM2105-mediated knockdown increases, drought and salt tolerance, affects stomatal aperture, and oppositely alters ABA levels under drought. [60]
  • Changes in OsNCED3 transcription and ABA content in miR2105 transgenic lines demonstrate that miR2105 negatively regulates drought-induced ABA biosynthesis via OsbZIP86. [60]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as a negative regulator of drought- and salt-induced ABA biosynthesis and stress tolerance by directing cleavage of OsbZIP86 mRNA; overexpression reduces ABA accumulation, drought and salt tolerance, and stomatal closure under stress, while knockdown has opposite effects. [60]

Regulatory Mechanism

miR2105 cleaves OsbZIP86 mRNA at the complementary site, lowering OsbZIP86 transcript levels and thereby indirectly reducing OsNCED3 expression and ABA biosynthesis under drought. [60]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in an ABA biosynthesis and signaling module under drought and salt stress by negatively regulating the OsSAPK10–OsbZIP86–OsNCED3 pathway that controls drought-induced ABA accumulation and stomatal closure. [60]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainmicroRNA regulating target mRNA by sequence-specific cleavage.
Expression PatternIn ZH11 seedlings, miR2105 is repressed by ABA, drought (air exposure), and 150 mM NaCl treatments; its expression increases rapidly upon re-watering or salt recovery and shows an expression pattern opposite to OsbZIP86 across tissues and developmental stages.
Validation MethodGeneration of osa-miR2105 overexpression (miR2105-ox) and short tandem target mimic knockdown (STTM2105) transgenic rice in ZH11 background; RT-qPCR of miR2105 and OsbZIP86 under ABA, drought and salt; 5'-RLM-RACE mapping of the miR2105-guided cleavage site in OsbZIP86 mRNA; drought and salt tolerance assays at vegetative and reproductive stages; measurements of water loss, stomatal aperture, ABA content, and ABA sensitivity in seed germination and seedling growth.
Breeding Application ValueDownregulation of miR2105 (STTM2105) increases drought and salt tolerance, enhances drought‑induced ABA accumulation, and improves grain yield under stress without obvious penalty on growth and major agronomic traits under normal conditions, making miR2105 a promising target for breeding stress-tolerant rice by weakening its negative regulation on OsbZIP86–OsNCED3–mediated ABA biosynthesis.
Environment / TreatmentABA (0.5 μM) treatment, drought/water deficiency by air exposure or withdrawal of solution/irrigation, lacunar drought during reproductive phase, and 150 mM NaCl salt stress; exogenous ABA (2.5–10 μM) during seed germination.

OsbZIP86 (Oryza sativa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsbZIP86 is a drought-, salt-, and ABA-inducible nuclear bZIP transcription factor whose expression is inversely regulated by miR2105. [60]
  • Gain- and loss-of-function analyses show that OsbZIP86 positively regulates drought and salt tolerance, reduces water loss, promotes stomatal closure, and enhances drought-induced ABA accumulation, with negligible effects on growth under normal conditions. [60]
  • OsbZIP86 directly binds G-box motifs in the OsNCED3 promoter and activates its transcription, especially under drought and ABA treatments. [60]
  • OsbZIP86 physically interacts with and is phosphorylated by OsSAPK10, and this phosphorylation enhances its ability to activate OsNCED3 transcription, placing OsbZIP86 at the center of a miR2105–OsSAPK10–OsbZIP86–OsNCED3 module controlling ABA biosynthesis under drought. [60]

Core Phenotypic Effect

A nuclear bZIP transcription factor that positively regulates drought- and salt-induced ABA biosynthesis and stress tolerance by directly binding to and activating the OsNCED3 promoter; overexpression enhances drought and salt tolerance and stomatal closure, whereas RNAi or CRISPR knockout reduces these traits under stress, with little effect under normal growth. [60]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsbZIP86 directly binds G-box motifs in the OsNCED3 promoter and functions as a transcriptional activator; its transcriptional activity is enhanced by phosphorylation at Ser149/152/155 by the SnRK2 kinase OsSAPK10, which increases OsNCED3 promoter activity under drought and ABA. [60]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts in an ABA biosynthesis and signaling pathway under drought and salt, functioning downstream of miR2105 and OsSAPK10 and upstream of OsNCED3 to regulate drought-induced ABA production and stomatal closure. [60]

Biosynthetic: ABA biosynthesis (indirectly promotes 9-cis-epoxycarotenoid dioxygenase OsNCED3 transcription in the ABA biosynthetic pathway). [60]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainBasic region-leucine zipper (bZIP) transcription factor; contains predicted serine phosphorylation sites in its regulatory region.
Expression PatternOsbZIP86 expression is induced by ABA, drought, and salt treatments in ZH11 seedlings and cannot be upregulated by these stresses in miR2105-ox lines; it is mainly expressed in stem, leaf sheath, young leaf, and seed with lower expression in panicle; promoter–GUS staining confirms high expression in young leaf, leaf sheath, stem, and seed. Its temporal and spatial expression pattern is opposite to that of miR2105.
Validation MethodGeneration of Ubi:OsbZIP86-GFP overexpression (bZIP86-ox), OsbZIP86-RNAi, and CRISPR/Cas9 knockout (crbzip86) rice in ZH11 background; drought and salt stress assays at vegetative and reproductive stages; measurements of water loss, stomatal aperture, grain yield traits, ABA content, and ABA sensitivity; subcellular localization of OsbZIP86-GFP in rice protoplasts, N. benthamiana leaves, and roots; EMSA and ChIP-qPCR to show direct binding to G-box motifs in the OsNCED3 promoter; dual-LUC assays to show transcriptional activation of OsNCED3; LCI, pull-down, BiFC, Co-IP, and phos-tag SDS-PAGE to demonstrate interaction with and phosphorylation by OsSAPK10; mutagenesis of predicted phosphorylation sites to test effects on OsNCED3 promoter activation.
Breeding Application ValueConstitutive overexpression of OsbZIP86 (bZIP86-ox) enhances drought and salt tolerance, decreases water loss, promotes stomatal closure, and increases drought-induced ABA accumulation without evident negative effects on growth and major agronomic traits under normal conditions, suggesting OsbZIP86 as a breeding target to improve stress tolerance by upregulating OsNCED3-dependent ABA biosynthesis only under stress.
Environment / TreatmentABA (0.5 or 50 μM), drought/water deficiency by air exposure or withdrawal of solution/irrigation, lacunar drought from flowering to maturity, and 150 mM NaCl salt stress.

OsSAPK10 (Oryza sativa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsSAPK10 was identified among SAPK family members as a strong interactor of OsbZIP86 that markedly enhances OsNCED3 promoter activity when co-expressed with OsbZIP86. [60]
  • OsSAPK10 physically interacts with OsbZIP86 in vitro and in vivo, as shown by pull-down, BiFC, Co-IP, and LCI assays. [60]
  • OsSAPK10 phosphorylates OsbZIP86 in vitro in an ATP-dependent manner, causing a phos-tag mobility shift that is reversed by alkaline phosphatase. [60]
  • Autophosphorylation of OsSAPK10 at Ser177 and the presence of Ser149/152/155 in OsbZIP86 are important for maximal activation of OsNCED3pro:LUC, indicating that OsSAPK10-mediated phosphorylation activates OsbZIP86 to promote OsNCED3 transcription. [60]

Core Phenotypic Effect

A SnRK2 family stress/ABA-activated protein kinase that interacts with and phosphorylates OsbZIP86, thereby enhancing OsbZIP86-dependent activation of OsNCED3 transcription and promoting ABA biosynthesis under drought/ABA. [60]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsSAPK10 interacts with OsbZIP86 and phosphorylates it on Ser149/152/155, producing a mobility shift in phos-tag SDS-PAGE that is abolished by alkaline phosphatase; phosphorylation enhances OsbZIP86’s transcriptional activation of the OsNCED3 promoter. [60]

Pathways

Regulatory: Component of ABA signaling under drought and salt stress; functions upstream of OsbZIP86 in the miR2105–OsSAPK10–OsbZIP86–OsNCED3 module that controls drought-induced ABA biosynthesis. [60]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSnRK2 family serine/threonine protein kinase (stress/ABA-activated protein kinase, SAPK).
Validation MethodLCI assay in N. benthamiana leaves to identify interaction between OsbZIP86 and OsSAPKs; GST pull-down to show direct interaction between MBP-OsSAPK10 and GST-OsbZIP86 in vitro; BiFC in rice protoplasts and Co-IP in N. benthamiana to confirm OsbZIP86–OsSAPK10 interaction in vivo; in vitro kinase assay with phos-tag SDS-PAGE demonstrating OsSAPK10-dependent phosphorylation of GST-OsbZIP86; dual-LUC reporter assays showing that co-expression of OsSAPK10 with OsbZIP86 strongly enhances OsNCED3pro:LUC activity, and that OsSAPK10S177A and OsbZIP86S149A/S152A/S155A mutations reduce this activation.
Breeding Application ValueAs a stress/ABA-activated SnRK2 kinase that phosphorylates and activates OsbZIP86 to enhance OsNCED3 expression, OsSAPK10 is part of a module promoting drought-induced ABA biosynthesis; although direct breeding evaluation was not performed here, modulation of OsSAPK10 activity together with OsbZIP86 could be used to fine-tune ABA signaling and drought tolerance in rice.
Environment / TreatmentAlthough expression and activation of OsSAPK10 under drought, ABA, and NaCl were documented previously, this study assayed OsSAPK10 function mainly under conditions mimicking ABA/drought signaling in transient systems (dual-LUC in N. benthamiana).

OsNCED3 (Oryza sativa L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsNCED3, a key ABA biosynthetic gene, contains multiple G-box motifs in its promoter. [60]
  • OsbZIP86 directly binds the P1 G-box region of the OsNCED3 promoter in vitro (EMSA) and in vivo (ChIP-qPCR), with stronger enrichment under drought and ABA. [60]
  • OsbZIP86 overexpression or miR2105 knockdown cause strong upregulation of OsNCED3 under drought, whereas OsbZIP86 knockdown/knockout or miR2105 overexpression reduce OsNCED3 expression. [60]
  • Dual-LUC assays show that OsbZIP86 activates the OsNCED3 promoter and that this activation is further enhanced by OsSAPK10, linking OsNCED3 expression to the miR2105–OsSAPK10–OsbZIP86 regulatory module. [60]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Key ABA biosynthetic gene whose expression is directly activated by OsbZIP86 binding to its promoter; OsNCED3 transcript levels are strongly upregulated in OsbZIP86-ox and STTM2105 and downregulated in miR2105-ox, crbzip86, and bZIP86-RNAi under drought, correlating with ABA content and drought tolerance of these lines. [60]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsbZIP86 binds specifically to a G-box-containing P1 region located 387 bp upstream of the OsNCED3 ATG and activates OsNCED3 transcription; OsbZIP86 occupancy on the OsNCED3 promoter is enhanced under drought and ABA, and co-expression with OsSAPK10 further increases OsNCED3 promoter-driven LUC activity. [60]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions as the main ABA biosynthetic target gene in the miR2105–OsSAPK10–OsbZIP86–OsNCED3 module that mediates drought-induced ABA accumulation and stomatal closure in rice. [60]

Biosynthetic: ABA biosynthesis: 9-cis-epoxycarotenoid dioxygenase (NCED) step that cleaves violaxanthin and neoxanthin to form xanthoxin, the rate-limiting step in de novo ABA biosynthesis. [60]

More Details
Protein Family / Domain9-cis-epoxycarotenoid dioxygenase (NCED) enzyme family.
Expression PatternUnder normal conditions, OsNCED3 shows only slight upregulation in OsbZIP86-ox and STTM2105; under drought, OsNCED3 transcripts are substantially increased in OsbZIP86-ox and STTM2105 and decreased in miR2105-ox, crbzip86, and bZIP86-RNAi compared with ZH11.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR analysis of OsNCED3 expression in OsbZIP86 and miR2105 transgenic lines under normal and drought conditions; EMSA using G-box-containing probes from the OsNCED3 promoter and GST-OsbZIP86 to show specific binding; ChIP-qPCR with OsbZIP86-6HA-GFP lines to demonstrate in vivo enrichment of OsbZIP86 on G-box-containing OsNCED3 promoter regions, enhanced by drought and ABA; dual-LUC assays showing that OsbZIP86 activates OsNCED3pro:LUC and that this activation is further enhanced by OsSAPK10.
Breeding Application ValueOsNCED3 encodes a key ABA biosynthetic enzyme and is strongly induced by drought via OsbZIP86; however, previous work cited here indicates that direct overexpression of ABA biosynthetic genes such as OsNCED3 can improve stress tolerance but negatively affect leaf traits and yield, suggesting that for breeding it is preferable to modulate OsNCED3 through upstream regulators (miR2105 and OsbZIP86) to enhance drought tolerance without growth penalties.
Environment / TreatmentDrought (water deficiency), ABA treatment, and salt (NaCl), which modulate OsbZIP86 activity and thereby OsNCED3 expression; OsNCED3 expression was assayed mainly under drought and ABA.

ZmPCK2 (Zea mays) Plant

Key Findings

  • Maize ZmPCK2 encodes a phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase whose expression in maize leaves is strongly induced by PEG-simulated water-deficit and ABA treatments. [61]
  • Overexpression of ZmPCK2 in rice (indica 9311) increases water-deficit tolerance, as indicated by higher biomass, chlorophyll content, F_v/F_m, and relative water content and lower water loss under PEG-induced stress. [61]
  • ZmPCK2 overexpression promotes stomatal closure, decreases stomatal conductance and leaf malate levels, thereby enhancing water-saving capacity. [61]
  • Under water-deficit conditions, ZmPCK2 overexpression up-regulates ABA biosynthesis genes (OsNCED4, OsNCED5) and ABA-responsive genes (OsRAB16A, OsRAB16D), increases ABA-induced stomatal closure and ABA sensitivity during germination. [61]
  • ZmPCK2 overexpression enhances seed germination under mannitol-induced osmotic stress, accompanied by reduced malate and increased total free amino acids in seeds. [61]
  • ZmPCK2 overexpression reduces ROS (O2−, H2O2) accumulation and MDA content and increases SOD, POD and CAT activities under water-deficit stress, conferring improved ROS-scavenging capacity and reduced oxidative damage. [61]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of ZmPCK2 in rice enhances tolerance to water-deficit stress. [61]

Regulatory Mechanism

ZmPCK2 overexpression enhances PCK enzymatic activity, lowering malate levels associated with stomatal closure and improved water retention; under water-deficit stress it increases transcript levels of ABA biosynthesis genes OsNCED4 and OsNCED5 and ABA-responsive genes OsRAB16A and OsRAB16D, leading to higher ABA responsiveness and ABA-induced stomatal closure; it also elevates activities of antioxidant enzymes SOD, POD and CAT, thereby reducing ROS (O2−, H2O2) and lipid peroxidation (MDA). [61]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA biosynthesis and signaling pathway (via up-regulation of OsNCED4, OsNCED5, OsRAB16A, OsRAB16D), stomatal closure pathway via malate metabolism, and antioxidant/ROS-scavenging pathways. [61]

Biosynthetic: Gluconeogenesis and malate metabolism (PCK-catalyzed decarboxylation of oxaloacetate to phosphoenolpyruvate affecting malate/OAA/PEP balance). [61]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainPhosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (PCK) enzyme family.
Expression PatternIn maize, ZmPCK2 is strongly induced in leaves by PEG6000 (water deficit) and ABA treatments. In ZmPCK2-overexpressing rice, the transgene is expressed in multiple tissues with highest expression in leaves at the productive stage.
Validation MethodFunctional validation via transgenic overexpression of ZmPCK2 in rice followed by physiological, biochemical and gene-expression analyses under PEG-induced water deficit, ABA treatment and mannitol-induced osmotic stress.
Breeding Application ValueZmPCK2 is a promising candidate gene for engineering water-saving and water-deficit-tolerant rice and possibly other crops; its overexpression improves drought-related traits, including stomatal water-saving capacity, seed germination under water-deficit conditions and ROS-scavenging, indicating potential utility in breeding or biotechnological development of drought-resilient cultivars.
Environment / TreatmentWater-deficit stress simulated by 20% PEG6000 in nutrient solution; osmotic stress induced by 200 mM mannitol during seed germination; ABA treatments (50 µM for stomatal assays; 5 µM during germination).

GmPP2A-B''71 (Glycine max (L.) Merr.) Plant

Key Findings

  • GmPP2A-B''71 is a soybean PP2A-B'' regulatory subunit gene whose transcript is strongly induced by drought and salt; it shows high basal expression in roots and leaves. [62]
  • GmPP2A-B''71 protein localizes to the nucleus and cytoplasm (GFP fusion in Arabidopsis protoplasts). [62]
  • Overexpression of GmPP2A-B''71 enhances drought and salt tolerance in transgenic Arabidopsis (Col-0) by increasing root length, fresh weight, survival rate, and reducing cell membrane damage (lower relative electrolyte leakage) under PEG and NaCl stress, with no penalty under normal conditions. [62]
  • In soybean hairy root composite plants (cv. Williams 82), overexpression of GmPP2A-B''71 (OE) improves drought and salt tolerance, whereas RNAi knockdown increases sensitivity; OE lines show lower ROS accumulation (NBT staining), higher proline, chlorophyll, POD and CAT activities, and lower MDA than EV and RNAi lines under stress. [62]
  • GmPP2A-B''71-OE soybean plants exhibit significantly higher expression of ROS-scavenging genes (GmCAT1, GmCAT2, GmPOD) and stress-related genes (GmLEA15, GmERF115), while these genes are down-regulated in GmPP2A-B''71-RNAi plants, indicating that GmPP2A-B''71 positively regulates the ROS scavenging and stress-response pathways to enhance drought and salt tolerance. [62]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of GmPP2A-B''71 enhances drought and salt tolerance in soybean and Arabidopsis, while RNAi knockdown in soybean increases sensitivity, largely through improved ROS scavenging and antioxidant capacity. [62]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a PP2A regulatory B'' subunit localized in nucleus and cytoplasm that positively regulates expression of ROS-scavenging enzymes (CAT, POD) and stress-related genes (LEA, ERF), leading to enhanced antioxidant enzyme activities, higher proline and chlorophyll, and reduced lipid peroxidation (MDA) under drought and salt stress. [62]

Pathways

Regulatory: ROS signaling and scavenging pathway involved in abiotic stress (drought and salt) tolerance; likely connected to broader stress/ABA-responsive transcriptional networks. [62]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainPP2A regulatory B'' subunit (PP2A-B''); member of PP2A-B'' family reported to contain EF-hand Ca2+-binding motifs.
Expression Pattern- Among 26 stress-responsive GmPP2A-B'' genes, GmPP2A-B''71 shows relatively high expression in soybean roots and leaves. - Strongly induced by drought and salt in soybean (cv. Williams 82); transcript levels peak at 12 h of drought (250 mM mannitol) and 10 h of salt (200 mM NaCl) treatments. - qPCR confirms strong induction under both drought and salt stresses compared with other nine tested GmPP2A-B'' genes.
Validation MethodFunctional validation via overexpression and RNAi knockdown in soybean hairy root composite plants, overexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana, combined with subcellular localization (GFP fusion), physiological/biochemical assays (ROS, proline, MDA, chlorophyll, CAT, POD), and qPCR of ROS-scavenging and stress-related genes under drought and salt stress.
Breeding Application ValueGmPP2A-B''71 is a strong candidate gene for improving soybean tolerance to drought and salt stress by enhancing ROS scavenging capacity and antioxidant defenses. Its overexpression confers higher survival, better growth, and reduced oxidative damage under stress without observable growth penalties under normal conditions, suggesting potential utility in transgenic or molecular breeding strategies to increase yield stability in saline and drought-prone environments.
Environment / Treatment- Drought stress simulated by 250 mM mannitol for short-term expression assays in soybean and by PEG6000 (4% and 6%) and water withholding for 10–14 days in Arabidopsis and soybean composite plants. - Salt stress applied as 200–250 mM NaCl in soybean (expression and tolerance assays) and 100–125 mM NaCl in Arabidopsis. - All plants grown under controlled greenhouse/ growth-chamber conditions.

SlGlk16 (Solanum lycopersicum) Plant

Key Findings

  • SlGlk16 was identified as a drought-specific differentially expressed G2-like transcription factor based on RNA-seq data of tomato under abiotic stresses. [63]
  • qRT-PCR confirmed that SlGlk16 expression is responsive to cold, drought, salt and multiple hormone treatments (ABA, GA, IAA, JA, SA), with characteristic early induction and later decline under stresses. [63]
  • Virus-induced silencing of SlGlk16 in tomato (cv. Money Maker) led to earlier wilting and more severe drought symptoms under PEG6000 treatment compared with wild-type and TRV2 controls. [63]
  • Under drought, SlGlk16-silenced plants showed lower SOD and POD activities, higher MDA content, and reduced proline levels, indicating compromised antioxidant and osmotic stress responses. [63]
  • These results demonstrate that SlGlk16 functions as a positive regulator of drought tolerance in tomato and likely acts by modulating ROS-scavenging enzyme activities and osmolyte accumulation. [63]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Silencing SlGlk16 reduces drought tolerance in tomato seedlings, causing earlier wilting and curling under PEG-induced drought, accompanied by decreased SOD and POD activities, reduced proline accumulation, and increased MDA content, indicating that SlGlk16 acts as a positive regulator of drought stress tolerance. [63]

Regulatory Mechanism

Positive regulator of drought stress responses that promotes antioxidant defense and osmotic adjustment by enhancing SOD and POD activities and proline accumulation and limiting MDA accumulation under drought, thereby maintaining cell membrane stability. [63]

Pathways

Regulatory: Drought stress response pathway involving regulation of reactive oxygen species scavenging and osmolyte (proline) accumulation in tomato leaves. [63]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainG2-like (GLK) transcription factor of the GARP superfamily.
Expression PatternDefined as a drought-specific differentially expressed G2-like gene from RNA-seq; qRT-PCR showed that SlGlk16 is responsive to multiple abiotic stresses (cold, drought, salt) and hormones (ABA, GA, IAA, JA, SA), with expression generally upregulated early and then downregulated under abiotic stresses and GA, upregulated under JA and IAA, and mostly downregulated under drought and ABA.
Validation MethodVirus-induced gene silencing (TRV2-SlGlk16) in tomato cultivar Money Maker followed by PEG6000-induced drought treatment, phenotypic observation, and measurement of SOD, POD activities and MDA and proline contents.
Breeding Application ValueAs a G2-like transcription factor that positively regulates drought tolerance, SlGlk16 is a promising target to breed or engineer tomato varieties with enhanced drought resistance, for example by selecting alleles/variants with higher activity or by overexpression or genome editing to improve antioxidant capacity and osmotic adjustment under water deficit.
Environment / TreatmentPEG6000-induced drought stress (15% PEG6000 in Hoagland's solution); additional treatments used for expression profiling included cold (4 °C), salt (200 mM NaCl), and hormone sprays with ABA, GA, IAA, JA and SA.

XERICO (XER) (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • XER is expressed in the epidermis of young Arabidopsis seedlings, including all stomatal lineage cells, and its transcript levels are induced by osmotic stress (mannitol). [64]
  • Loss-of-function xer-1 and xer-2 mutants show reduced ABA sensitivity in seedling assays, increased stomatal density and stomatal index, and impaired ABA- and mannitol-induced stomatal closure. [64]
  • xer mutants are more susceptible to drought, exhibiting higher percentages of wilted rosette tissue after water deprivation and faster water loss and higher transpiration/stomatal conductance than wild type. [64]
  • Genetic analyses show that xer-2 is epistatic to cbf4-2 in ABA, osmotic and drought responses and stomatal development, indicating that XER acts downstream of CBF4 to promote ABA-dependent stress tolerance. [64]

Core Phenotypic Effect

XER inhibits stomatal development and promotes ABA sensitivity and drought/osmotic stress tolerance; loss-of-function xer mutants have higher stomatal density and stomatal index, reduced ABA sensitivity during seedling growth, impaired ABA- and mannitol-induced stomatal closure, and increased susceptibility to drought with greater wilted tissue and water loss. [64]

Regulatory Mechanism

XER functions as a RING E3 ubiquitin ligase that modulates ABA levels, and its transcription is negatively regulated by the transcription factor CBF4, which binds the DRE element in the XER 5'-UTR and represses promoter activity and endogenous expression, especially under osmotic stress. [64]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts in the ABA-dependent osmotic and drought stress response pathway to promote ABA accumulation, stomatal closure and reduced stomatal density, contributing to water-deficit tolerance; functions downstream of CBF4 in this regulatory pathway. [64]

Biosynthetic: ABA metabolism and ABA signaling pathway involved in osmotic and drought stress responses. [64]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainStress-responsive RING E3 ubiquitin ligase.
Expression PatternXER is expressed in young seedlings, with XERpro:YFP activity in cotyledons and hypocotyls of 3- and 5-day-old seedlings, including pavement cells, meristemoids and mature guard cells in the cotyledon epidermis; XER transcripts are induced in 5-day-old seedlings by 300 mM mannitol, with early upregulation followed by downregulation as CBF4 accumulates.
Breeding Application ValueXER promotes ABA accumulation, stomatal closure and drought/osmotic stress tolerance, and restricts stomatal development. Enhancing XER activity or expression in crops could reduce stomatal density and promote ABA-induced stomatal closure, thereby decreasing transpiration and improving drought and osmotic stress tolerance and water-use efficiency, although excessive ABA accumulation and growth costs would need to be balanced.
Environment / TreatmentExogenous ABA (1 μM for seedling growth assays), mannitol (300 mM for osmotic stress), and soil water deprivation for 10–12 days followed by rewatering (drought stress).

CBF4/DREB1D (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • CBF4 is weakly expressed under normal conditions but is strongly induced by ABA, mannitol and dehydration in Arabidopsis seedlings and adult plants, with ABA-inducible expression in cotyledon and hypocotyl epidermis including stomatal lineage cells. [64]
  • CBF4 localizes to the nucleus and directly binds the DRE/CRT element in the XER 5'-UTR, associating with the XER promoter in Y1H and DAP-seq and repressing XER promoter-driven luciferase and endogenous XER transcripts; repression is abolished when the DRE is mutated. [64]
  • cbf4 loss-of-function mutants are hypersensitive to ABA during seedling growth, more tolerant to drought with reduced wilted tissue, lower water loss, and lower transpiration and stomatal conductance than wild type. [64]
  • cbf4 mutants have reduced stomatal index and density, and their stomata close more in response to ABA and mannitol; in xer-2 cbf4-2 double mutants, xer-2 is epistatic, demonstrating that CBF4 acts upstream of XER as a transcriptional repressor that dampens ABA and stress responses. [64]

Core Phenotypic Effect

CBF4 is a negative regulator of ABA and drought/osmotic stress responses and promotes stomatal development and stomatal opening; cbf4 null mutants are hypersensitive to ABA during seedling growth, more tolerant to drought with reduced wilting and water loss, have reduced stomatal index and density, and exhibit hypersensitive ABA- and mannitol-induced stomatal closure. [64]

Regulatory Mechanism

CBF4 is induced by ABA and osmotic stress, localizes to the nucleus, directly binds the DRE/CRT element in the XER 5'-UTR, and represses XER promoter activity and endogenous XER expression. This repression is lost when the core DRE nucleotides are mutated, and inducible expression of CBF4 blocks XER upregulation under mannitol, indicating direct transcriptional repression of XER by CBF4. [64]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in ABA-dependent osmotic and drought stress signaling as a negative regulator that attenuates stress responses. CBF4 is upregulated by ABA and osmotic stress and acts as a transcriptional repressor of XER via the DRE element in its 5'-UTR, thereby damping XER-mediated ABA accumulation, stomatal closure and drought tolerance; genetic analysis places CBF4 upstream of XER in this pathway. [64]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCBF/DREB1 transcription factor of the AP2/ERF family, containing an AP2 DNA-binding domain and a partial EAR (LxL) repression motif.
Expression PatternCBF4 is expressed at very low levels under normal conditions but is transcriptionally upregulated by ABA and osmotic stress (300 mM mannitol) in 5-day-old seedlings and by dehydration in seedlings and adult plants. CBF4pro:YFP shows ABA-induced expression in the cotyledon and hypocotyl epidermis, including stomatal lineage cells.
Breeding Application ValueCBF4 functions as a stress-induced transcriptional repressor that dampens ABA and osmotic/drought stress responses by repressing XER and likely other positive regulators of ABA biosynthesis and signaling. Loss-of-function of CBF4 increases ABA sensitivity and drought tolerance but reduces stomatal index. Modulating CBF4 activity or expression, for example by reducing its function in a controlled manner, could enhance drought tolerance and water-use efficiency via stronger ABA-mediated responses, although its role in buffering stress responses to sustain growth means that breeding applications must carefully balance enhanced tolerance with potential impacts on growth and stomatal development.
Environment / TreatmentABA treatment (50–100 μM for expression and 1 μM for seedling sensitivity assays), mannitol (300 mM for osmotic stress), dehydration, and soil water deprivation (drought) followed by rewatering.

OsDjC46 (Oryza sativa L. sub. japonica) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsDjC46 was identified as a ZFP36-interacting J-protein (DnaJ/Hsp40) in rice by yeast two-hybrid screening and confirmed to interact with ZFP36 in vivo and in vitro (Y2H, BiFC, Co-IP, GST pull-down). 2. OsDjC46 expression in rice leaves is rapidly induced by ABA, H2O2 and Ca2+, and is upregulated by drought and salt stress (20% PEG6000, 150 mM NaCl); ABA-induced expression depends on ABA-triggered H2O2 and Ca2+ signaling. 3. OsDjC46 is localized in the nucleus of rice protoplasts, consistent with its interaction with the nuclear transcription factor ZFP36. 4. Overexpression of OsDjC46 enhances, whereas CRISPR/Cas9 knockout reduces, drought and salt tolerance in rice, as evidenced by altered SOD and CAT activities, MDA content, electrolyte leakage, relative water content, proline accumulation, survival rate and growth parameters under PEG and NaCl treatments. 5. These results indicate that OsDjC46 positively regulates rice drought and salt tolerance by participating in ABA-mediated antioxidant defense via interaction with ZFP36. [65]

Core Phenotypic Effect

OsDjC46 positively regulates rice tolerance to drought and salt stress by enhancing ABA-mediated antioxidant defense, leading to higher SOD and CAT activities, improved osmotic adjustment and reduced cell membrane damage under PEG and NaCl treatments. [65]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsDjC46 is a nuclear J-protein (DnaJ/Hsp40) that interacts physically with the zinc finger transcription factor ZFP36 and is induced by ABA, H2O2 and Ca2+ signals; it modulates antioxidant defense by affecting SOD and CAT activities and proline accumulation, thereby limiting ROS-induced membrane damage under drought and salt stress, although the precise biochemical mechanism remains unresolved. [65]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in ABA signaling and ROS- and Ca2+-mediated stress-response pathways, participating in ABA-induced antioxidant defense under drought and salt stress. [65]

Biosynthetic: ABA-mediated antioxidant defense and ROS-scavenging pathway under drought and salt stress. [65]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainJ-protein (DnaJ) of the Hsp40 family, classified as a type C J-protein with a C-terminal J-domain.
Expression PatternOsDjC46 expression in rice leaves is rapidly and transiently induced by H2O2 (first peak at ~10 min, second peak at ~60 min) and by ABA (two peaks, at ~15 min and 60 min), and is upregulated within 4–5 h under drought (20% PEG6000) and salt (150 mM NaCl) stress. OsDjC46 expression is also induced by CaCl2, with a maximum around 180 min. ABA-induced OsDjC46 expression is abolished when plants are pretreated with H2O2 scavengers/inhibitors (DMTU, CAT, DPI) or Ca2+-related blockers and antagonists (EGTA, TFP, W7, KN-93), indicating dependence on ABA-triggered ROS and Ca2+ signaling.
Validation MethodGeneration and analysis of OsDjC46 overexpression and CRISPR/Cas9 knockout rice lines under drought (20% PEG6000) and salt (150 mM NaCl) stress, including survival and growth phenotypes plus SOD and CAT activities, MDA, electrolyte leakage, relative water content and proline content; supported by interaction validation with ZFP36 via Y2H, BiFC, Co-IP and GST pull-down, and subcellular localization of OsDjC46-YFP in rice protoplast nuclei.
Breeding Application ValueOsDjC46 is a nuclear-localized J-protein whose overexpression enhances, and knockout reduces, tolerance of rice seedlings to drought and salt stress by strengthening ABA-mediated antioxidant defense (higher SOD and CAT activities, higher proline and relative water content, lower MDA and electrolyte leakage, and better growth under PEG and NaCl). OsDjC46 is therefore a promising target gene for breeding or engineering rice with improved drought and salinity tolerance through transgenic approaches or marker-assisted selection focused on ABA–ROS signaling and antioxidant capacity.
Environment / TreatmentAbscisic acid (100 μmol·L^-1 ABA); hydrogen peroxide (10 mmol·L^-1 H2O2); CaCl2 (10 mM); drought stress simulated by 20% PEG6000; salt stress simulated by 150 mM NaCl; H2O2 scavengers and inhibitor (DMTU, CAT, DPI); Ca2+-related blockers and antagonists (EGTA, TFP, W7, W5 control, KN-93, KN-92 control).

MsSPL08 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • MsSPL08 is an SPL transcription factor in alfalfa that carries a conserved SBP domain and contains a predicted miR156 target site in its 3′-UTR. [66]
  • MsSPL08 is broadly expressed in aboveground tissues (leaves, stems, flowers) but is low/undetectable in roots and nodules. [66]
  • MsSPL08 expression is significantly downregulated in multifoliate (pinnate pentafoliate) alfalfa compared with trifoliate alfalfa, associating reduced MsSPL08 transcript levels with increased leaflet number. [66]
  • MsSPL08 is strongly induced by PEG-mediated drought and NaCl salt treatments, indicating participation in abiotic stress responses. [66]
  • The close paralog MtSPL4 in Medicago truncatula shows that loss-of-function causes increased leaflet number, increased branching, reduced plant height, and delayed flowering, supporting a role of the miR156/MsSPL08 module in regulating compound leaf development and plant architecture in legumes. [66]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Lower MsSPL08 expression is associated with multifoliate (pinnate pentafoliate) leaf phenotype in alfalfa, and MsSPL08 transcripts are induced under drought and salt stress, linking this gene to compound leaf development and abiotic stress response. [66]

Regulatory Mechanism

MsSPL08 encodes an SPL (SBP-domain) transcription factor predicted to be post-transcriptionally repressed by miR156 through a complementary site in its 3′-UTR; miR156/MsSPL08 together form a regulatory module implicated in controlling leaflet number and stress-responsive gene expression. [66]

Pathways

Regulatory: miR156–MsSPL transcriptional module in developmental regulation of compound leaf (leaflet number) and abiotic stress responses (drought and salt) in alfalfa. [66]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSPL (SQUAMOSA PROMOTER BINDING PROTEIN-LIKE) transcription factor family member containing a conserved SBP DNA-binding domain with two Zn-finger-like motifs (C2HC and C3H) and a nuclear localization signal.
Expression PatternMsSPL08 is widely expressed in aboveground tissues (leaves, stems, flowers) but not detected in roots and nodules; its expression is significantly lower in multifoliate alfalfa than in trifoliate alfalfa; MsSPL08 is upregulated in roots under 20% PEG and 300 mM NaCl treatments, with strong induction after 1–4 days.
Validation MethodAssociation of MsSPL08 with multifoliate vs. trifoliate leaf phenotype by RNA-Seq and expression comparison; qRT-PCR validation of MsSPL08 stress-responsive expression under PEG and NaCl; functional inference supported by phenotypic analysis of its highly conserved paralog MtSPL4 in Medicago truncatula Tnt1 insertion mutants.
Breeding Application ValueMsSPL08, as part of the miR156/MsSPL module, represents a promising target for breeding alfalfa with increased leaflet number and improved leaf/stem ratio, which can enhance forage protein yield and digestibility; its strong induction by drought and salt suggests that manipulating this module could also contribute to developing multifoliate, stress-tolerant alfalfa cultivars.
Environment / Treatment20% PEG-6000 (osmotic/drought stress) and 300 mM NaCl (salt stress) applied to 30-day-old alfalfa seedlings for 1, 2, and 4 days.

AcTS (Areca catechu) Plant

Key Findings

  • AcTS was identified from the Areca catechu genome as a trigonelline synthase gene (AC10G024220), encoding a 373-aa N-methyltransferase with conserved SAM-binding motifs and clustering phylogenetically with Coffea trigonelline synthases. [67]
  • Drought strongly induces AcTS expression in areca seedlings, with ~80-fold and ~6-fold transcript increases in leaves and roots at 2 days after treatment, correlating with marked accumulation of trigonelline in leaves but not roots. [67]
  • Transgenic Arabidopsis lines overexpressing AcTS (OxAcTS14 and OxAcTS17) accumulate higher endogenous trigonelline and exhibit enhanced drought tolerance, showing only slight wilting after prolonged drought and rapid recovery after rehydration compared with wild-type Col-0. [67]
  • AcTS overexpression and exogenous trigonelline both promote Arabidopsis root growth under mannitol-induced osmotic stress, supporting a role for AcTS-mediated trigonelline production in improving drought and osmotic stress resilience. [67]

Core Phenotypic Effect

AcTS encodes a trigonelline synthase whose overexpression elevates trigonelline accumulation and markedly improves drought tolerance and root growth under osmotic stress in transgenic Arabidopsis; in areca seedlings AcTS is strongly drought-inducible and associated with increased trigonelline levels in leaves. [67]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a trigonelline synthase (N-methyltransferase) catalyzing the methylation of nicotinic acid using S-adenosyl-L-methionine to produce trigonelline, thereby increasing cellular levels of this compatible osmolyte under drought. [67]

Pathways

Regulatory: Participates in drought stress response and osmotic adjustment through trigonelline-mediated protection, contributing to improved drought resilience and root growth under osmotic stress. [67]

Biosynthetic: Trigonelline biosynthesis: nicotinic acid N-methylation to trigonelline catalyzed by trigonelline synthase (N-methyltransferase). [67]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainN-methyltransferase (trigonelline synthase) containing conserved S-adenosyl-L-methionine (SAM) binding motifs.
Expression PatternUnder drought, AcTS transcript levels are strongly induced in areca leaves and roots: at 2 days after treatment, expression increases to about 80-fold in leaves and 6-fold in roots relative to controls; leaf expression remains significantly higher than controls at 4–8 days before dropping to control levels by day 10, while root expression is elevated at 4–6 days and significantly downregulated in the treatment group from day 8.
Validation MethodFunctional identification by transgenic overexpression of AcTS in Arabidopsis Col-0 (OxAcTS14 and OxAcTS17), followed by trigonelline quantification and drought/mannitol stress assays; expression analysis of AcTS in areca leaves and roots under drought by qRT-PCR.
Breeding Application ValueAcTS is a drought-inducible trigonelline synthase whose overexpression increases trigonelline levels and drought tolerance, providing a candidate gene to enhance drought resilience of areca seedlings and potentially other crops via manipulation of trigonelline-mediated osmotic adjustment.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by withholding water from areca seedlings for 10 consecutive days; osmotic stress induced by 150 mM mannitol in Arabidopsis growth medium; exogenous application of 1 mg/L trigonelline; drought treatment of soil-grown Arabidopsis for 14–16 days followed by rehydration.

TaTIP4;1 (Triticum aestivum) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaTIP4;1 expression in wheat is strongly induced by PEG6000, NaCl, ABA and H2O2 in a tissue- and treatment-dependent manner, with high basal expression in roots, stems and leaves. [68]
  • Overexpression of TaTIP4;1 in Arabidopsis and rice does not alter growth under normal conditions but significantly improves drought, salt and osmotic stress tolerance, as shown by higher survival, seed germination rates, root elongation, and plant height, and reduced leaf water loss. [68]
  • Under salinity, TaTIP4;1 overexpression markedly suppresses Na+ accumulation and lowers Na+/K+ ratios in Arabidopsis and rice, contributing to better ion homeostasis. [68]
  • TaTIP4;1 overexpression alleviates oxidative damage by reducing H2O2 and MDA accumulation and by enhancing CAT (and in rice, SOD) activities, and modulates proline levels in both Arabidopsis and rice under drought and salt/osmotic stress. [68]
  • In TaTIP4;1-overexpressing Arabidopsis and rice, multiple drought-responsive genes (e.g., AtDREB1B, AtRD29A/B, OsDREB2, OsNAC1, OsPIP2;1, OsRAB16C, OsPOX1) and salt-responsive genes (e.g., AtSOS2, AtNHX1, AtRD29A/B, AtPKS5, AtCAT1, OsPIP2;1, OsP5CS, OsCATB) show stronger stress-induced transcription than in WT, indicating a positive role of TaTIP4;1 in activating stress-response gene expression. [68]
  • In yeast, expression of TaTIP4;1 increases tolerance to NaCl and sorbitol, whereas mutation of individual serines S83, S201 or S207 to alanine reduces this tolerance, suggesting phosphorylation-dependent regulation of TaTIP4;1 activity. [68]
  • Overall, TaTIP4;1 functions as an essential positive regulator of seed germination and seedling growth under drought, salt and osmotic stress by affecting water relations, ROS and ion homeostasis, proline accumulation and the expression of numerous stress-responsive genes, making it a strong candidate gene for engineering crop tolerance to water deficit and salinity. [68]

Core Phenotypic Effect

TaTIP4;1 acts as a positive regulator of plant tolerance to drought, salt and osmotic stress: its overexpression in Arabidopsis and rice enhances seed germination and seedling growth under these stresses, increases survival and water content while reducing leaf water loss, Na+ accumulation, Na+/K+ ratio, ROS and lipid peroxidation, and promoting antioxidant enzyme activity and proline accumulation; in yeast, TaTIP4;1 expression also enhances tolerance to salt and osmotic stress. [68]

Regulatory Mechanism

TaTIP4;1 is a tonoplast aquaporin whose activity is modulated by phosphorylation of cytosolic serine residues: in yeast, mutation of serines S83, S201 or S207 to alanine reduces the TaTIP4;1-mediated enhancement of salt and sorbitol tolerance, indicating phosphorylation-dependent regulation. In planta, TaTIP4;1 overexpression alters cellular water relations, represses Na+ accumulation and Na+/K+ ratio, reduces ROS and membrane lipid peroxidation, enhances CAT (and partly SOD) activity, and modifies proline accumulation, which together help maintain water and redox homeostasis and ion balance under drought, salt and osmotic stress. TaTIP4;1 overexpression also positively affects the transcript levels of many drought and salt responsive genes under stress. [68]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in ABA- and stress-responsive regulatory networks: TaTIP4;1 expression in wheat is induced by ABA, PEG and NaCl, and its overexpression in Arabidopsis and rice enhances expression of multiple drought and salt responsive genes involved in ABA signaling, ion transport, water transport and ROS detoxification under dehydration and salinity. [68]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainTonoplast intrinsic protein (TIP) aquaporin, six-transmembrane membrane protein
Expression PatternIn wheat, TaTIP4;1 transcripts are strongly induced by 10% PEG6000 (drought/osmotic) in leaves (6–24 h) and roots (6–9 h), by 200 mM NaCl in leaves (all time points) and roots (3–24 h), by 100 μM ABA in roots (6–9 h), and by 10 mM H2O2 in leaves (3–12 h) and roots (6–9 h). Tissue expression analysis shows strong expression in roots, stems and leaves. In Arabidopsis and rice transgenic lines, TaTIP4;1 is highly expressed in selected overexpression lines relative to controls.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of TaTIP4;1 markedly enhances tolerance to drought, salt and osmotic stress in Arabidopsis and rice at seed germination and seedling stages, while not affecting growth under normal conditions. TaTIP4;1 improves plant water status, reduces Na+ accumulation and Na+/K+ ratio, alleviates ROS and lipid peroxidation damage, and promotes proline accumulation and the expression of multiple stress-responsive genes. These features indicate TaTIP4;1 is a promising genetic engineering target to improve crop performance (including cereals) under water deficit and salinity, by enhancing seed germination, root growth and early seedling vigor.
Environment / TreatmentPEG6000-induced water deficit/osmotic stress; NaCl-induced salinity; mannitol-induced osmotic stress; ABA and H2O2 treatments in wheat seedlings; sorbitol and NaCl stress in yeast.

PtaERF194 (Populus tremula × Populus alba) Plant

Key Findings

  • PtaERF194 encodes a nuclear AP2/ERF transcription factor with a conserved AP2 domain and is a stress-related transcriptional activator in poplar hybrid 717. [69]
  • Constitutive overexpression of PtaERF194 in poplar 717 suppresses growth (reduced plant height, internode length, leaf area, and stem vessel lumen size, increased leaf thickness and chlorophyll content), while RNAi knockdown produces plants largely similar to wild type, indicating functional redundancy. [69]
  • Overexpression of PtaERF194 enhances drought tolerance: OX plants maintain leaf turgor longer during water withholding, show higher leaf and apex water potential, smaller water loss from detached leaves, reduced stomatal conductance and transpiration but higher instantaneous water-use efficiency and CO2-use efficiency, and tend to exhibit lower stem percent loss of conductivity under drought. [69]
  • RNA-seq and co-expression network analyses reveal that PtaERF194 is co-expressed with 12 differentially expressed genes involved in ABA signaling and antioxidant/ROS-scavenging processes, including dehydrin and antioxidant enzymes (CCS, RBOH, SOD1, SOD2), supporting a role for PtaERF194 in coordinating ABA- and ROS-related gene networks to improve drought tolerance in poplar. [69]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of PtaERF194 suppresses vegetative growth (shorter plants, reduced internode length and leaf area, smaller stem vessel lumens) but enhances drought tolerance by increasing water-use efficiency, decreasing stomatal conductance and transpiration, reducing leaf water loss, maintaining higher leaf and shoot water potential under drought, and slightly lowering stem percent loss of conductivity; RNAi lines show morphology and physiology largely similar to wild type, indicating partial functional redundancy with other ERF transcription factors. [69]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a nuclear AP2/ERF transcriptional activator whose overexpression alters transcript levels of drought- and antioxidant-related genes; co-expression analysis shows positive correlations with dehydrin and multiple antioxidant defense genes (CCS, RBOH, SOD1, SOD2), indicating activation of ABA-related and ROS-scavenging pathways that support improved drought tolerance, alongside developmental effects on xylem vessel anatomy and leaf traits that reduce water loss. [69]

Pathways

Regulatory: Associated with ABA-dependent drought signaling and ROS-scavenging pathways, based on co-expression with dehydrin (ABA signaling) and antioxidant defense genes (CCS, RBOH, SOD1, SOD2) under conditions where PtaERF194 is overexpressed. [69]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAP2/ERF transcription factor with a conserved AP2 DNA-binding domain spanning amino acids 2–56.
Validation MethodFunctional characterization using transgenic poplar 717 lines overexpressing or RNAi-silencing PtaERF194 driven by the CaMV35S promoter; phenotypic, anatomical and physiological assays under well-watered and drought (water-withholding) conditions; RT-qPCR confirmation of transgene expression; subcellular localization by GFP fusion in Arabidopsis leaf protoplasts; and RNA-seq-based identification of co-expressed, drought- and antioxidant-related genes.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of PtaERF194 in poplar hybrid 717 enhances drought tolerance by increasing water-use efficiency, maintaining higher leaf and apex water potential, and reducing water loss and xylem vulnerability, making PtaERF194 a promising target for molecular breeding of drought-tolerant poplar for afforestation and production on water-limited sites, although its growth-suppressing effect means expression level or deployment strategy must be carefully managed.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed on 3-month-old soil-grown plants by withholding watering for up to 16 days (D+8, D+16) compared with continuously well-watered controls (W+8, W+16), following growth of transgenic and wild-type poplar 717 in controlled greenhouse conditions.

GhPIP2;7 (Gossypium hirsutum) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Silencing GhPIP2;7 in cotton markedly decreases salt tolerance, causing stronger leaf wilting and browning, reduced plant height, lower chlorophyll content, and impaired antioxidant enzyme activities under high salinity. [70]

Regulatory Mechanism

Virus-induced silencing of GhPIP2;7 reduces SOD and POD activities and increases MDA accumulation under salt stress, indicating compromised ROS-scavenging capacity and aggravated membrane lipid peroxidation. [70]

Pathways

Regulatory: Positive regulator in the salt stress response pathway, likely contributing to maintenance of water transport and antioxidant defense under salinity. [70]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAquaporin, plasma membrane intrinsic protein (PIP) subfamily
Expression PatternGhPIP2;7 is significantly induced by 150 mM NaCl and shows peak expression at 3 h after salt treatment, then decreases towards 48 h.
Validation MethodVirus-induced gene silencing (VIGS) of GhPIP2;7 in G. hirsutum followed by salt stress (400 mM NaCl) and physiological/biochemical measurements (chlorophyll, MDA, SOD, POD).
Breeding Application ValueGhPIP2;7 is a promising gene for improving salt tolerance in upland cotton; manipulation or selection of favorable GhPIP2;7 alleles/transcripts could enhance plant survival and maintain photosynthetic capacity under high salinity, providing a genetic resource for breeding salt-tolerant cotton varieties.
Environment / TreatmentSalt stress treatments: primarily 150 mM NaCl for expression profiling and 400 mM NaCl irrigation or leaf-disk incubation for functional assays.

GhTIP2;1 (Gossypium hirsutum) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

GhTIP2;1 positively regulates drought/osmotic stress tolerance: silencing in cotton increases sensitivity to PEG-induced osmotic stress with reduced root and leaf relative water content, whereas overexpression in Arabidopsis enhances drought (PEG) tolerance with better root growth and lower oxidative damage under stress. [70]

Regulatory Mechanism

Overexpression of GhTIP2;1 reduces accumulation of H2O2 and MDA and increases proline content under salt and osmotic stress, suggesting improved ROS detoxification and osmolyte accumulation; associated with higher expression of AtP5CS (proline biosynthesis), AtNHX (Na+/H+ antiporter), and AtLEA (late embryogenesis abundant protein) under stress. [70]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in osmotic/drought stress response and associated oxidative stress and ion homeostasis pathways; GhTIP2;1 overexpression enhances osmotic tolerance via increased proline accumulation and improved Na+ efflux, accompanied by induction of stress-responsive genes (AtP5CS, AtNHX, AtLEA). [70]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAquaporin, tonoplast intrinsic protein (TIP) subfamily
Expression PatternIn cotton, GhTIP2;1 is rapidly induced at early stages after drought/osmotic stress, with transcript levels peaking around 2 h after PEG treatment. In Arabidopsis ProGhTIP2;1:GUS lines, promoter activity is strong in cotyledons, rosette leaves, and roots under control conditions, but GUS staining decreases in leaves after 10–20% PEG6000 or 200 mM NaCl, while expression remains high in roots.
Validation Method(1) Virus-induced gene silencing (TRV:GhTIP2;1) in cotton followed by 13% PEG6000 treatment and measurement of chlorophyll and relative water content. (2) Stable overexpression of GhTIP2;1-eGFP in Arabidopsis (Col-0) with stress assays under 15% PEG6000 and 150 mM NaCl, including root growth, proline, MDA, H2O2, and stress-related gene expression.
Breeding Application ValueGhTIP2;1 is a strong candidate gene for improving drought and osmotic stress tolerance in cotton. Its positive effect on water status, ROS attenuation, and proline accumulation suggests that manipulating GhTIP2;1 (via transgenic approaches or future allele-based selection) could enhance cotton performance in water-deficit and saline environments, providing a valuable resource for breeding stress-resilient cultivars.
Environment / TreatmentOsmotic/drought stress induced by 13–15% PEG6000 in cotton and Arabidopsis; salt stress induced by 150–200 mM NaCl in expression and OE assays.

GhTIP1;1 (Gossypium hirsutum) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Silencing GhTIP1;1 in cotton reduces drought/osmotic tolerance, leading to wilting and yellowing of whole plants and reduced root and leaf relative water content under PEG-induced stress. [70]

Pathways

Regulatory: Participates in drought/osmotic stress response pathway, likely contributing to maintenance of cellular water status via tonoplast water transport. [70]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAquaporin, tonoplast intrinsic protein (TIP) subfamily
Expression PatternGhTIP1;1 is upregulated at early stages after drought/PEG treatment, with expression peaking around 2 h after osmotic stress.
Validation MethodVirus-induced gene silencing (TRV:GhTIP1;1) in cotton followed by 13% PEG6000 treatment and measurement of chlorophyll content and root and leaf relative water content.
Breeding Application ValueGhTIP1;1 represents an additional candidate gene for enhancing drought/osmotic stress tolerance in cotton; modulating its expression could improve maintenance of tissue water status under water-deficit conditions.
Environment / TreatmentOsmotic/drought stress induced by 13% PEG6000 in hydroponic culture for VIGS plants.

MdMTA (Malus domestica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MdMTA is an MTA-type m6A RNA methyltransferase in apple that forms a functional complex with MdMTB and catalyzes methyl group transfer to adenine in RNA probes containing the UGUAA/U motif. [71]
  • MdMTA is localized in the nucleus and its expression is induced by drought and PEG-8000 treatment. [71]
  • RNAi knockdown of MdMTA reduces global m6A levels in total RNA, causes widespread hypomethylation of mRNAs (especially in CDS and 3'UTR regions) and alters the transcriptome without a uniform direction of expression change. [71]
  • MdMTA RNAi plants exhibit developmental defects (shorter plants, weaker roots) and lower lignin content and hydraulic conductivity, whereas MdMTA OE plants show increased lignin accumulation under drought. [71]
