  • Under drought, MdMTA positively regulates drought tolerance: RNAi plants show stronger wilting, higher ion leakage, reduced photosynthetic parameters and lower survival, while OE plants display the opposite phenotypes. [71]
  • MdMTA-mediated m6A modification enhances the mRNA stability and, for selected targets, translation efficiency of genes involved in lignin biosynthesis and ROS scavenging, including MdLEA, MdLAC4, Md4CL3, MdORM2, MdMYC4, MdMPV17, MdCAT1, MdCAT2, MdPOD63 and MdPOD66. [71]
  • Through these effects on target genes, MdMTA promotes lignin deposition, reduces H_2O_2 and MDA accumulation, and increases CAT and POD enzyme activities under drought, conferring enhanced drought tolerance in apple. [71]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Core m6A RNA methyltransferase that installs m6A on mRNAs and positively regulates drought tolerance by promoting lignin deposition and ROS scavenging; MdMTA knockdown reduces global m6A levels, impairs plant growth, lignification, hydraulic conductivity and antioxidant enzyme activities, and decreases drought survival, whereas overexpression increases m6A levels and drought tolerance. [71]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as the catalytic m6A writer within an MdMTA–MdMTB complex to install m6A on target mRNAs, mainly in CDS and 3'UTR and for some targets in the 5'UTR. Under drought, MdMTA-induced m6A modification generally enhances mRNA stability and for specific targets (e.g. Md4CL3 via 5'UTR m6A, MdLEA and MdMYC4 via CDS m6A, MdPOD63 via 5'UTR m6A) also increases translation efficiency, thereby raising protein output of drought- and ROS-related genes. [71]

Pathways

Regulatory: Central component of the m6A RNA modification pathway in apple that, under drought stress, increases m6A levels on transcripts involved in lignin biosynthesis (e.g. MdLAC4, Md4CL3) and ROS scavenging (e.g. MdLEA, MdORM2, MdMYC4, MdMPV17, MdCAT1, MdCAT2, MdPOD63, MdPOD66), thereby enhancing their expression and contributing to lignin deposition, antioxidant enzyme activity, reduced H_2O_2 and MDA accumulation, and improved drought tolerance. [71]

Biosynthetic: m6A mRNA methylation (RNA epigenetic modification pathway) [71]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainMTA-type m6A RNA methyltransferase, orthologous to METTL3 in animals and closely related to Pyrus bretschneideri MTA.
Expression PatternMdMTA transcript levels are induced by drought and PEG-8000 treatments, showing time-dependent upregulation under these stress conditions.
Validation MethodFunctional analysis in transgenic apple GL-3 plants with MdMTA RNAi and overexpression; global m6A profiling by m6A-seq and LC-MS/MS; dot blot; drought and PEG stress assays; physiological measurements (survival, ion leakage, photosynthesis, lignin content, H_2O_2, MDA, CAT/POD activity); m6A-IP-qPCR, RNA-seq, mRNA stability assays with actinomycin D, and polysome-based translation efficiency assays; in vitro methylation assay together with MdMTB; Y2H and split-luciferase interaction assays; subcellular localization with GFP fusion.
Breeding Application ValueMdMTA is a positive regulator of drought tolerance in apple; increasing MdMTA activity elevates global m6A levels, enhances lignin deposition and ROS scavenging, and improves survival, photosynthetic performance and membrane integrity under drought. MdMTA therefore represents a promising target gene for breeding or engineering apple cultivars with enhanced drought resistance via improved secondary cell wall formation and antioxidant capacity.
Environment / TreatmentSoil drought stress (20–26 d withholding water followed by rewatering), PEG-8000-induced osmotic stress in hydroponic culture, and actinomycin D treatment to block transcription during drought for mRNA stability assays.

MdLEA (Malus domestica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MdLEA is among the hypomethylated and downregulated genes in MdMTA RNAi plants under drought and is upregulated in MdMTA OE plants. [71]
  • MdMTA-mediated m6A levels on MdLEA mRNA decrease in MdMTA RNAi and increase in OE plants under drought, as shown by m6A-IP-qPCR. [71]
  • Under drought, MdLEA mRNA is less stable in MdMTA RNAi plants than in GL-3, whereas its stability is maintained in the presence of MdMTA activity. [71]
  • The translation efficiency of MdLEA mRNA is also reduced in MdMTA RNAi plants compared with GL-3 under drought, indicating that MdMTA-mediated m6A enhances both mRNA stability and translation of MdLEA during drought stress. [71]

Core Phenotypic Effect

MdLEA mRNA is hypomethylated and downregulated in MdMTA RNAi plants and upregulated in MdMTA OE plants under drought; MdMTA-mediated m6A modification increases MdLEA mRNA stability and translation efficiency under drought, contributing to higher LEA transcript and protein levels during stress. [71]

Regulatory Mechanism

MdMTA-mediated m6A methylation on MdLEA transcripts (mainly in CDS/3'UTR regions) under drought slows MdLEA mRNA decay and increases its translation efficiency, leading to higher MdLEA transcript and protein abundance in MdMTA OE plants and lower levels in MdMTA RNAi plants. [71]

Pathways

Regulatory: Part of the MdMTA-dependent m6A regulatory pathway under drought; MdLEA acts as a stress-responsive LEA gene whose expression is enhanced by MdMTA-mediated m6A, contributing to cellular protection during drought and oxidative stress. [71]

More Details
Expression PatternUnder drought, MdLEA expression is reduced in MdMTA RNAi plants and increased in MdMTA OE plants compared with GL-3; under normal conditions, only MdLEA shows a significant expression difference between genotypes among the tested targets.
Validation MethodIdentified as a hypomethylated DEG in MdMTA RNAi vs GL-3 under drought by integrated m6A-seq and RNA-seq; expression confirmed by qRT-PCR; m6A enrichment validated by m6A-IP-qPCR; mRNA decay rate measured after PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D treatment; translation efficiency assessed via polysome-associated vs total RNA under drought.
Breeding Application ValueAs a drought-associated LEA gene whose m6A level, mRNA stability and translation efficiency are positively controlled by MdMTA under drought, MdLEA is a candidate target to enhance apple drought and osmotic stress tolerance by stabilizing stress-protective LEA transcripts, either via direct manipulation or through optimizing MdMTA-mediated m6A regulation.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress (20 d soil drought) and PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D treatment for mRNA decay assays.

MdLAC4 (Malus domestica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MdLAC4 is a hypomethylated, downregulated lignin-related transcript in MdMTA RNAi plants under drought and is upregulated in MdMTA OE plants. [71]
  • m6A-IP-qPCR shows reduced m6A enrichment on MdLAC4 mRNA in MdMTA RNAi and increased enrichment in OE plants under drought. [71]
  • MdLAC4 mRNA is less stable in MdMTA RNAi plants than in GL-3 under drought, indicating that MdMTA-mediated m6A promotes MdLAC4 transcript stability. [71]
  • Increased MdLAC4 expression is consistent with higher lignin content observed in MdMTA OE plants under drought, linking MdLAC4 to MdMTA-dependent lignin deposition. [71]

Core Phenotypic Effect

MdLAC4 is hypomethylated and downregulated in MdMTA RNAi plants and upregulated in MdMTA OE plants under drought; MdMTA-mediated m6A methylation increases MdLAC4 mRNA stability in response to drought, supporting lignin deposition. [71]

Regulatory Mechanism

MdMTA-mediated m6A on MdLAC4 mRNA under drought increases its stability, leading to higher transcript accumulation in MdMTA OE plants and reduced levels in MdMTA RNAi plants, thereby supporting lignin biosynthesis. [71]

Pathways

Regulatory: Component of the MdMTA-controlled lignin biosynthetic pathway; m6A-dependent upregulation of MdLAC4 under drought contributes to increased lignin deposition and improved drought-associated hydraulic properties. [71]

Biosynthetic: Lignin biosynthesis within the phenylpropanoid pathway. [71]

More Details
Expression PatternMdLAC4 transcript levels are lower in MdMTA RNAi plants and higher in MdMTA OE plants than in GL-3 under drought stress, while showing little difference under normal conditions.
Validation MethodIdentified as a hypomethylated DEG from m6A-seq and RNA-seq in MdMTA RNAi vs GL-3 under drought; MdLAC4 expression verified by qRT-PCR; m6A enrichment on MdLAC4 mRNA quantified by m6A-IP-qPCR; mRNA stability under PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D treatment measured in MdMTA RNAi and GL-3 plants.
Breeding Application ValueBecause MdLAC4 is a lignin-associated gene whose m6A level and mRNA stability are positively regulated by MdMTA under drought, increasing MdLAC4 expression or optimizing its m6A regulation could enhance lignin deposition and improve hydraulic conductivity and drought tolerance in apple trees.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress (20 d) and PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D treatment for mRNA stability assays.

Md4CL3 (Malus domestica) Plant

Key Findings

  • Md4CL3 is a hypomethylated, downregulated lignin biosynthetic gene in MdMTA RNAi plants and is upregulated in MdMTA OE plants under drought. [71]
  • m6A-IP-qPCR confirms that Md4CL3 mRNA has reduced m6A levels in MdMTA RNAi plants and increased levels in OE plants under drought. [71]
  • mRNA stability assays show that Md4CL3 transcript decay is similar between MdMTA RNAi and GL-3 plants under drought, but translation efficiency is significantly reduced in MdMTA RNAi plants, consistent with m6A in its 5'UTR affecting translation. [71]
  • These findings indicate that MdMTA enhances Md4CL3 protein production mainly by promoting translation, contributing to lignin deposition under drought. [71]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Md4CL3 transcripts are hypomethylated and downregulated in MdMTA RNAi plants and upregulated in MdMTA OE plants under drought; MdMTA-mediated m6A in the 5'UTR promotes Md4CL3 translation efficiency under drought without markedly altering mRNA decay rate, supporting lignin biosynthesis. [71]

Regulatory Mechanism

MdMTA installs m6A in the 5'UTR of Md4CL3 mRNA under drought, and reduced m6A in MdMTA RNAi plants leads to decreased translation efficiency, while mRNA stability is largely unchanged, indicating that 5'UTR m6A mainly enhances translation of Md4CL3. [71]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts in the MdMTA-dependent lignin biosynthesis pathway; MdMTA-mediated 5'UTR m6A on Md4CL3 promotes its translation under drought, supporting increased lignin deposition and improved drought-associated water transport. [71]

Biosynthetic: Phenylpropanoid and lignin biosynthesis (4-coumarate:CoA ligase step). [71]

More Details
Expression PatternMd4CL3 transcript levels are lower in MdMTA RNAi plants and higher in MdMTA OE plants than in GL-3 under drought, with minimal differences under normal conditions.
Validation MethodIdentified as a hypomethylated, differentially expressed lignin biosynthetic gene via m6A-seq/RNA-seq in MdMTA RNAi vs GL-3 under drought; validated by qRT-PCR and m6A-IP-qPCR; mRNA stability assessed by actinomycin D chase; translation efficiency determined by polysome association analysis, with Md4CL3 highlighted as having m6A in its 5'UTR that affects translation.
Breeding Application ValueMd4CL3, as a lignin biosynthetic gene with MdMTA-dependent m6A in its 5'UTR that enhances translation under drought, is a potential target to increase lignin deposition and improve drought-related water transport traits in apple.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress (20 d) and PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D treatment for mRNA stability; drought conditions for translation efficiency assays.

MdORM2 (Malus domestica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MdORM2 is a hypomethylated, downregulated H_2O_2-related gene in MdMTA RNAi plants under drought and is upregulated in MdMTA OE plants. [71]
  • m6A-IP-qPCR shows reduced MdORM2 m6A enrichment in MdMTA RNAi and increased enrichment in OE plants under drought. [71]
  • Under drought, MdORM2 mRNA decays faster in MdMTA RNAi than in GL-3 plants, indicating that MdMTA-mediated m6A promotes its mRNA stability. [71]
  • Regulation of MdORM2 aligns with changes in ROS-related traits (H_2O_2 and MDA levels, antioxidant enzyme activity) observed between MdMTA genotypes. [71]

Core Phenotypic Effect

MdORM2 transcripts are hypomethylated and downregulated in MdMTA RNAi plants and upregulated in MdMTA OE plants under drought; MdMTA-mediated m6A increases MdORM2 mRNA stability during drought, supporting ROS-related protective functions. [71]

Regulatory Mechanism

MdMTA-mediated m6A modification on MdORM2 mRNA under drought stabilizes the transcript, resulting in higher MdORM2 expression in MdMTA OE plants and lower expression in MdMTA RNAi plants. [71]

Pathways

Regulatory: Part of the MdMTA-dependent ROS regulatory network; m6A-dependent upregulation of MdORM2 under drought likely contributes to reduced H_2O_2 accumulation and improved oxidative stress management. [71]

Biosynthetic: Pathways related to H_2O_2 and ROS metabolism. [71]

More Details
Expression PatternUnder drought, MdORM2 expression is lower in MdMTA RNAi plants and higher in MdMTA OE plants than in GL-3, with little difference under normal conditions.
Validation MethodIdentified among hypomethylated DEGs related to H_2O_2 pathways from integrated m6A-seq and RNA-seq; validated by qRT-PCR and m6A-IP-qPCR; mRNA stability examined with PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D treatment in MdMTA RNAi vs GL-3 plants.
Breeding Application ValueMdORM2 is an H_2O_2-related gene whose m6A level and mRNA stability are positively controlled by MdMTA under drought; enhancing MdORM2 expression or its m6A regulation could contribute to improved ROS management and drought tolerance in apple.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress and PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D treatment for mRNA stability assays.

MdMYC4 (Malus domestica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MdMYC4 is a hypomethylated, downregulated transcript in MdMTA RNAi plants and is upregulated in MdMTA OE plants under drought. [71]
  • m6A-IP-qPCR shows that MdMYC4 m6A levels are reduced in MdMTA RNAi and increased in OE plants under drought. [71]
  • MdMYC4 mRNA is less stable and has reduced translation efficiency in MdMTA RNAi plants compared to GL-3 under drought, consistent with MdMTA-mediated CDS m6A promoting both stability and translation. [71]
  • Regulation of MdMYC4 is linked to changes in ROS-related traits and is consistent with its known role in anthocyanin accumulation in other species. [71]

Core Phenotypic Effect

MdMYC4 is hypomethylated and downregulated in MdMTA RNAi plants and upregulated in MdMTA OE plants under drought; MdMTA-mediated m6A modification increases MdMYC4 mRNA stability and translation efficiency during drought, supporting anthocyanin accumulation and ROS scavenging. [71]

Regulatory Mechanism

MdMTA-mediated m6A in the CDS region of MdMYC4 enhances both mRNA stability and translation under drought; in MdMTA RNAi plants, decreased m6A leads to faster MdMYC4 mRNA decay and lower translation efficiency. [71]

Pathways

Regulatory: Part of the MdMTA-controlled ROS and antioxidant regulation pathway; MdMTA-induced MdMYC4 upregulation likely promotes anthocyanin accumulation and ROS scavenging during drought. [71]

Biosynthetic: Anthocyanin biosynthesis and ROS scavenging pathways. [71]

More Details
Expression PatternUnder drought, MdMYC4 expression is lower in MdMTA RNAi plants and higher in MdMTA OE plants compared with GL-3, with minimal differences under normal conditions.
Validation MethodIdentified as a hypomethylated, differentially expressed gene from m6A-seq/RNA-seq; validated by qRT-PCR and m6A-IP-qPCR; mRNA stability measured with PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D; translation efficiency measured via polysome association, with MdMYC4 indicated as a CDS-methylated transcript whose translation efficiency is reduced in MdMTA RNAi plants.
Breeding Application ValueMdMYC4, regulated at the m6A, stability and translation levels by MdMTA under drought, may serve as a target to enhance anthocyanin-based antioxidant capacity and drought tolerance in apple.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress (20 d) and PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D for stability assays; drought for translation efficiency assays.

MdMPV17 (Malus domestica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MdMPV17 is a hypomethylated, downregulated ROS-related gene in MdMTA RNAi plants and is upregulated in MdMTA OE plants under drought. [71]
  • m6A-IP-qPCR reveals that MdMPV17 m6A levels decrease in MdMTA RNAi and increase in OE plants during drought. [71]
  • MdMPV17 mRNA is less stable in MdMTA RNAi plants, indicating that MdMTA-mediated m6A promotes its transcript stability under drought. [71]
  • The regulation of MdMPV17 coincides with changes in ROS-related phenotypes between MdMTA genotypes, suggesting a role in drought-associated ROS metabolism. [71]

Core Phenotypic Effect

MdMPV17 is hypomethylated and downregulated in MdMTA RNAi plants and upregulated in MdMTA OE plants under drought; MdMTA-mediated m6A modification increases MdMPV17 mRNA stability in drought conditions, supporting peroxisomal ROS metabolism. [71]

Regulatory Mechanism

MdMTA-mediated m6A on MdMPV17 mRNA under drought stabilizes the transcript; reduced m6A in MdMTA RNAi plants leads to faster decay and lower MdMPV17 mRNA levels. [71]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts in the MdMTA-dependent ROS scavenging network; increased MdMPV17 expression contributes to peroxisomal ROS metabolism, aiding in H_2O_2 detoxification and drought tolerance. [71]

Biosynthetic: Peroxisomal ROS metabolism and reactive oxygen detoxification pathways. [71]

More Details
Expression PatternUnder drought, MdMPV17 expression is reduced in MdMTA RNAi plants and elevated in MdMTA OE plants compared with GL-3.
Validation MethodIdentified as a hypomethylated, differentially expressed gene related to ROS metabolism in MdMTA RNAi vs GL-3 under drought by m6A-seq/RNA-seq; validated via qRT-PCR and m6A-IP-qPCR; mRNA stability tested using PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D in MdMTA RNAi and GL-3 plants.
Breeding Application ValueMdMPV17, whose m6A level and mRNA stability are positively regulated by MdMTA under drought, could be exploited, together with MdMTA, to improve peroxisome-associated ROS metabolism and drought tolerance in apple.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress (20 d) and PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D for stability assays.

MdCAT1 (Malus domestica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MdCAT1 expression is reduced in MdMTA RNAi plants and increased in MdMTA OE plants under drought, paralleling changes in catalase activity. [71]
  • m6A-IP-qPCR confirms that MdCAT1 mRNA carries MdMTA-dependent m6A modifications under drought (lower in RNAi, higher in OE), whereas m6A levels are similar among genotypes under control conditions. [71]
  • MdCAT1 mRNA stability is lower in MdMTA RNAi plants than in GL-3 under drought, while translation efficiency is not significantly affected. [71]
  • These regulatory effects align with higher H_2O_2 and MDA levels and lower CAT activity in MdMTA RNAi plants, implicating MdCAT1 in MdMTA-mediated ROS scavenging during drought. [71]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Under drought, MdCAT1 expression is downregulated in MdMTA RNAi plants and upregulated in MdMTA OE plants; MdMTA-mediated m6A modification increases MdCAT1 mRNA stability (with translation efficiency largely unchanged), contributing to higher catalase activity and lower H_2O_2 levels. [71]

Regulatory Mechanism

MdMTA-mediated m6A modification on MdCAT1 mRNA under drought enhances mRNA stability, leading to higher MdCAT1 transcript abundance in MdMTA OE plants and lower levels in MdMTA RNAi plants; translation efficiency is not significantly altered. [71]

Pathways

Regulatory: Part of the MdMTA-controlled antioxidant defense pathway; upregulation of MdCAT1 via m6A-dependent mRNA stabilization contributes to increased catalase activity, reduced H_2O_2 accumulation and improved drought tolerance. [71]

Biosynthetic: Antioxidant defense pathway, catalase-mediated H_2O_2 detoxification. [71]

More Details
Expression PatternMdCAT1 transcript levels are lower in MdMTA RNAi plants and higher in MdMTA OE plants than in GL-3 under drought, while showing no significant differences among genotypes under normal conditions.
Validation MethodMdCAT1 was selected based on altered expression in RNA-seq of MdMTA RNAi vs GL-3 under drought; expression validated by qRT-PCR; m6A modification confirmed by m6A-IP-qPCR using primers designed from previous m6A-seq datasets; mRNA stability measured by actinomycin D chase under drought; translation efficiency assessed via polysome profiling.
Breeding Application ValueMdCAT1 encodes a catalase involved in H_2O_2 scavenging whose m6A level, mRNA stability and expression are positively controlled by MdMTA under drought; enhancing MdCAT1 expression or its m6A regulation could increase catalase activity, reduce oxidative damage and improve drought tolerance in apple.
Environment / TreatmentDrought treatment for 20 d; PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D treatment for mRNA stability; drought for translation efficiency assays.

MdCAT2 (Malus domestica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MdCAT2 expression is downregulated in MdMTA RNAi and upregulated in MdMTA OE plants under drought. [71]
  • m6A-IP-qPCR shows that MdCAT2 transcripts carry MdMTA-dependent m6A marks under drought, with lower levels in RNAi and higher in OE plants, and no significant genotype differences under control conditions. [71]
  • MdCAT2 mRNA stability is reduced in MdMTA RNAi plants under drought, while translation efficiency remains similar to GL-3. [71]
  • These effects support a role for MdCAT2 in MdMTA-mediated enhancement of catalase activity and ROS detoxification during drought. [71]

Core Phenotypic Effect

MdCAT2 expression is reduced in MdMTA RNAi plants and increased in MdMTA OE plants under drought; MdMTA-mediated m6A increases MdCAT2 mRNA stability (without markedly altering translation efficiency), contributing to overall catalase activity and ROS scavenging. [71]

Regulatory Mechanism

MdMTA-mediated m6A on MdCAT2 mRNA boosts its stability under drought; translation efficiency is not significantly impacted, indicating that m6A mainly affects transcript longevity. [71]

Pathways

Regulatory: Within the MdMTA-dependent antioxidant defense pathway, MdCAT2 upregulation via m6A-dependent mRNA stabilization contributes to increased catalase activity and reduced oxidative damage under drought. [71]

Biosynthetic: Antioxidant defense pathway, catalase-mediated H_2O_2 detoxification. [71]

More Details
Expression PatternMdCAT2 transcript levels are lower in MdMTA RNAi plants and higher in MdMTA OE plants than in GL-3 under drought, with no significant differences under normal conditions.
Validation MethodIdentified as a differentially expressed catalase gene in RNA-seq of MdMTA RNAi vs GL-3 under drought; expression validated via qRT-PCR; m6A levels determined by m6A-IP-qPCR informed by prior m6A-seq; mRNA stability measured by actinomycin D chase; translation efficiency measured by polysome profiling.
Breeding Application ValueMdCAT2, like MdCAT1, is a catalase gene whose drought-induced expression and mRNA stability depend on MdMTA-mediated m6A; it is therefore a potential target for improving catalase capacity and drought tolerance in apple through modulation of m6A regulation or gene expression.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress (20 d) and PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D treatment for stability assays; drought for translation efficiency assays.

MdPOD63 (Malus domestica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MdPOD63 expression is reduced in MdMTA RNAi and increased in MdMTA OE plants under drought, paralleling changes in POD activity. [71]
  • m6A-IP-qPCR demonstrates MdMTA-dependent m6A enrichment on MdPOD63 mRNA under drought (lower in RNAi, higher in OE) but not under control conditions. [71]
  • MdPOD63 mRNA stability is lower in MdMTA RNAi plants, and its translation efficiency is significantly reduced compared with GL-3 under drought, consistent with functional m6A in the 5'UTR. [71]
  • These data support a role for MdPOD63 as an MdMTA-regulated peroxidase contributing to ROS detoxification and drought tolerance. [71]

Core Phenotypic Effect

MdPOD63 expression is reduced in MdMTA RNAi plants and increased in MdMTA OE plants under drought; MdMTA-mediated m6A modification (including in the 5'UTR) increases both MdPOD63 mRNA stability and translation efficiency during drought, supporting higher POD activity and improved ROS scavenging. [71]

Regulatory Mechanism

MdMTA-mediated m6A on MdPOD63 mRNA, including in the 5'UTR, enhances both mRNA stability and translation efficiency under drought; loss of MdMTA reduces m6A, accelerates mRNA decay and decreases translation. [71]

Pathways

Regulatory: Within the MdMTA-dependent antioxidant defense network, MdPOD63 upregulation via m6A-dependent increases in mRNA stability and translation contributes to elevated peroxidase activity, lower H_2O_2 and MDA levels and enhanced drought tolerance. [71]

Biosynthetic: Antioxidant defense pathway, peroxidase-mediated H_2O_2 scavenging. [71]

More Details
Expression PatternMdPOD63 transcript levels are lower in MdMTA RNAi plants and higher in MdMTA OE plants than in GL-3 under drought, with similar expression under normal conditions.
Validation MethodIdentified as a peroxidase-encoding DEG in RNA-seq of MdMTA RNAi vs GL-3 under drought; expression validated by qRT-PCR; m6A levels measured by m6A-IP-qPCR using primers designed from previous m6A-seq data; mRNA stability assessed with actinomycin D chase under drought; translation efficiency quantified via polysome profiling, with MdPOD63 highlighted as having 5'UTR m6A affecting translation.
Breeding Application ValueMdPOD63 encodes a peroxidase whose m6A level, mRNA stability and 5'UTR-dependent translation efficiency are positively regulated by MdMTA under drought; manipulating MdPOD63 or its m6A regulation could increase POD activity, improve ROS detoxification and enhance drought tolerance in apple.
Environment / TreatmentDrought treatment for 20 d; PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D for stability assays; drought for translation efficiency assays.

MdPOD66 (Malus domestica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MdPOD66 expression is lowered in MdMTA RNAi and elevated in MdMTA OE plants under drought in parallel with changes in POD activity. [71]
  • m6A-IP-qPCR shows MdMTA-dependent m6A enrichment on MdPOD66 mRNA under drought, with no significant genotype differences under control conditions. [71]
  • MdPOD66 mRNA decays faster in MdMTA RNAi plants than in GL-3 under drought, whereas its translation efficiency remains similar between genotypes. [71]
  • These findings support MdPOD66 as an MdMTA-regulated peroxidase gene contributing to ROS detoxification and drought tolerance primarily through mRNA stabilization. [71]

Core Phenotypic Effect

MdPOD66 expression is reduced in MdMTA RNAi plants and increased in MdMTA OE plants under drought; MdMTA-mediated m6A increases MdPOD66 mRNA stability, while translation efficiency remains largely unchanged, contributing to total peroxidase activity. [71]

Regulatory Mechanism

MdMTA-mediated m6A on MdPOD66 mRNA under drought enhances transcript stability; translation efficiency is not detectably altered, suggesting that m6A mainly affects decay rather than translation for this gene. [71]

Pathways

Regulatory: Part of the MdMTA-controlled antioxidant defense network; MdPOD66 upregulation via m6A-enhanced mRNA stability contributes to total peroxidase activity and H_2O_2 scavenging under drought. [71]

Biosynthetic: Antioxidant defense pathway, peroxidase-mediated H_2O_2 scavenging. [71]

More Details
Expression PatternMdPOD66 transcript levels are lower in MdMTA RNAi plants and higher in MdMTA OE plants relative to GL-3 under drought; expression differences are not evident under normal conditions.
Validation MethodSelected as a peroxidase-encoding DEG from RNA-seq; expression validated by qRT-PCR; m6A modification confirmed by m6A-IP-qPCR based on previous m6A-seq data; mRNA stability analyzed with actinomycin D chase; translation efficiency measured by polysome profiling.
Breeding Application ValueMdPOD66 is a peroxidase whose m6A level and mRNA stability are positively regulated by MdMTA under drought; its manipulation, along with MdMTA, may enhance peroxidase activity, reduce oxidative damage and improve drought tolerance in apple.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress (20 d) and PEG-8000 plus actinomycin D for mRNA stability; drought for translation efficiency assays.

PpNUDX8 (Prunus persica) Plant

Key Findings

  • PpNUDX8 encodes a NUDIX hydrolase (Prupe.5G062300.1) with a typical NUDIX domain and its promoter contains cis-elements associated with ABA responsiveness, drought inducibility, gibberellin and methyl jasmonate responsiveness, and light responsiveness. [72]
  • PpNUDX8 transcript levels in peach seedlings are induced by 15% PEG6000-simulated drought and by ABA treatment, indicating responsiveness to drought and ABA signals. [72]
  • Overexpression of PpNUDX8 in Wanglin apple calli reduces callus growth, decreases fresh weight, and increases MDA content under 4% PEG6000 treatment, indicating reduced drought tolerance. [72]
  • Overexpression of PpNUDX8 in Nicotiana tabacum results in greater wilting, shorter primary roots, lower chlorophyll, reduced proline and soluble protein contents, and higher MDA content and relative conductivity under PEG6000-induced drought, demonstrating that PpNUDX8 negatively regulates drought tolerance. [72]
  • In transgenic tobacco, PpNUDX8 overexpression alters NAD and NADH levels and the NAD/NADH ratio and modifies expression of the NAD biosynthetic enzyme QS2, indicating that PpNUDX8 disrupts NAD/NADH homeostasis, especially under drought stress. [72]
  • PpNUDX8 overexpression reduces ABA sensitivity in tobacco, conferring higher seed germination and green cotyledon rates and longer primary roots under ABA, and decreases ABA content before and after drought treatment. [72]
  • PpNUDX8 overexpression suppresses ABA signaling and stress-responsive gene expression by lowering SnRK2.2 expression, increasing PP2C6 and PP2C53 expression, and reducing drought-induced expression of DREBA1, RAB18, RD29A, RD29B, and LEA5. [72]
  • PpNUDX8 overexpression decreases expression of the ABA biosynthesis gene NCED6 while increasing expression of the ABA catabolism gene CYP1, contributing to lower ABA levels in transgenic tobacco. [72]
  • PpNUDX8 physically interacts with PpSnRk1γ, as shown by yeast two-hybrid and pull-down assays, suggesting integration into SnRK1-related signaling under drought. [72]

Core Phenotypic Effect

PpNUDX8 acts as a negative regulator of drought stress tolerance and ABA signaling by disturbing NAD/NADH homeostasis, lowering ABA content, and suppressing ABA and stress-responsive gene expression, leading to reduced drought tolerance when overexpressed. [72]

Regulatory Mechanism

As a NUDIX hydrolase, PpNUDX8 perturbs NAD/NADH homeostasis, likely by affecting expression of the NAD biosynthetic enzyme QS2; it negatively regulates ABA signaling by decreasing SnRK2.2 expression and increasing PP2C6 and PP2C53 expression; it reduces ABA content by repressing NCED6 and enhancing CYP1 expression; and it physically interacts with the signaling component PpSnRk1γ. [72]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling pathway and drought stress response pathways, including both ABA-dependent and ABA-independent (DREBA1-mediated) stress-responsive pathways linked to NAD/NADH homeostasis. [72]

Biosynthetic: NAD/NADH homeostasis and ABA metabolism (affecting NAD biosynthesis via QS2 and ABA biosynthesis/degradation via NCED6 and CYP1) [72]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainNUDIX hydrolase containing a typical conserved NUDIX motif (GX5EX7REUXEEXGU).
Expression PatternPpNUDX8 transcript levels in peach seedlings are induced by 15% PEG6000-simulated drought and by ABA treatment.
Validation MethodHeterologous overexpression in Wanglin apple calli and Nicotiana tabacum (35S::PpNUDX8), PEG6000-simulated drought assays, ABA sensitivity tests, physiological and biochemical measurements, gene expression analysis by qRT-PCR, and protein–protein interaction verified by yeast two-hybrid and pull-down assays.
Breeding Application ValueBecause PpNUDX8 acts as a negative regulator of drought tolerance and ABA signaling, reducing or knocking out PpNUDX8 activity in peach or related horticultural crops may enhance drought resistance, improve osmotic adjustment capacity, and increase ABA accumulation under stress; PpNUDX8 provides a candidate target gene for genetic engineering-based drought improvement in peach.
Environment / TreatmentPEG6000-simulated drought (4% PEG6000 in MS medium and 15% PEG6000 solution) and ABA treatments (1 μM and 10 μM ABA in MS for germination and seedling growth, 50 μmol/L ABA spray on peach seedlings).

JMJ710 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Negative regulator of drought and PEG-induced osmotic stress tolerance in rice; overexpression causes drought hypersensitivity, whereas RNAi knockdown and CRISPR loss-of-function mutants exhibit enhanced drought and osmotic tolerance. [73]

Regulatory Mechanism

JMJ710 acts as an H3K36me2-specific demethylase; under normal conditions it binds the chromatin of MYB48-1 and ASR4 and demethylates H3K36me2, maintaining their low expression and limiting activation of drought-responsive genes; drought stress downregulates JMJ710 and reduces its occupancy at these loci, leading to increased H3K36me2 and higher target gene expression. [73]

Pathways

Regulatory: Epigenetic regulation of drought-responsive gene expression via H3K36me2 demethylation at target genes such as MYB48-1 and ASR4. [73]

Biosynthetic: Histone H3K36 methylation / chromatin modification pathway. [73]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainJmjC domain-containing histone demethylase of the JMJD6 group with an additional F-box domain.
Expression PatternUbiquitously expressed in multiple tissues (leaf, root, coleoptile, spikelet, stem, pistil, stamen) with higher transcript levels in roots, stems, and stamens; transcription is slightly reduced under drought or PEG6000-induced osmotic stress.
Validation MethodOverexpression, RNAi knockdown, and CRISPR/Cas9 knockout in rice combined with drought and PEG6000 osmotic stress assays; in vitro and in vivo histone demethylation assays; ChIP-qPCR to demonstrate direct binding to and H3K36me2 demethylation at target gene loci; genetic interaction analysis with myb48-1 and jmj710/myb48-1 double mutants.
Breeding Application ValueJMJ710 is a promising target for breeding or genome editing of drought- and osmotic-tolerant rice: its downregulation or knockout enhances stress tolerance without obvious growth penalties under normal conditions, and it acts as a central epigenetic repressor of key drought-responsive genes such as MYB48-1 and ASR4.
Environment / TreatmentSoil-drying drought stress and PEG6000-induced osmotic stress (20% PEG6000) applied to seedlings; dehydration conditions for water loss assays.

MYB48-1 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Positive regulator of drought and osmotic stress tolerance; CRISPR myb48-1 knockout plants are hypersensitive to drought and PEG-induced osmotic stress, and loss of MYB48-1 partially suppresses the drought-tolerant phenotype of jmj710 mutants, indicating that MYB48-1 acts genetically downstream of JMJ710. [73]

Regulatory Mechanism

MYB48-1 expression is directly repressed by JMJ710-mediated H3K36me2 demethylation at its gene body under normal conditions; drought reduces JMJ710 expression and occupancy at MYB48-1, causing increased H3K36me2 levels and strong transcriptional activation of MYB48-1. [73]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts in a JMJ710–MYB48-1 epigenetic module that controls activation of downstream drought-responsive genes under dehydration stress. [73]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainMYB transcription factor family (MYB TRANSCRIPTION FACTOR 48).
Expression PatternExpression is low under normal conditions and specifically upregulated by drought stress; transcript levels are reduced in JMJ710 overexpression plants and elevated in JMJ710-RNAi and jmj710 mutants under drought stress, but similar among genotypes under normal conditions.
Validation MethodCRISPR/Cas9 knockout of MYB48-1 and construction of jmj710/myb48-1 double mutants combined with drought and osmotic stress assays, water loss measurements, and ChIP-qPCR of JMJ710 binding and H3K36me2 status at MYB48-1 chromatin.
Breeding Application ValueMYB48-1 is a key positive regulator downstream of JMJ710; enhancing MYB48-1 activity or expression, or relieving its repression via manipulation of JMJ710, could improve drought tolerance in rice, as loss-of-function myb48-1 is highly drought-sensitive and partially suppresses the drought-tolerant phenotype of jmj710 mutants.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress and PEG6000-induced osmotic stress applied to seedlings.

ASR4 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Downstream drought-responsive gene whose transcription is negatively regulated by JMJ710 under drought stress; although its own loss- or gain-of-function phenotype is not tested here, ASR4 is directly targeted by JMJ710 and shows JMJ710-dependent drought-inducible expression. [73]

Regulatory Mechanism

Under normal conditions, JMJ710 binds ASR4 chromatin and demethylates H3K36me2, maintaining low ASR4 expression; under drought stress, JMJ710 enrichment at ASR4 decreases, H3K36me2 at ASR4 increases, and ASR4 transcription is upregulated. [73]

Pathways

Regulatory: Part of the JMJ710-centered epigenetic module controlling drought-responsive gene expression via modulation of H3K36me2 levels. [73]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAsr (abscisic acid-, stress-, and ripening-inducible) family protein.
Expression PatternASR4 is induced by drought stress; its expression is downregulated in JMJ710 overexpression lines and upregulated in JMJ710-RNAi and jmj710 mutants under drought, but similar among genotypes under normal conditions.
Validation MethodCombination of RNA-seq, RT-qPCR, JMJ710-HA ChIP-qPCR, and H3K36me2 ChIP-qPCR under normal and drought conditions to verify that ASR4 is a direct target of JMJ710 and that its transcription responds to JMJ710-dependent changes in H3K36me2.
Breeding Application ValueASR4 is a JMJ710-regulated drought-responsive gene and part of the transcriptional network activated when JMJ710 is downregulated by drought; it represents a potential downstream target to monitor or manipulate when engineering JMJ710-based improvements in drought tolerance.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress applied to rice seedlings.

AtNOG1-1 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • AtNOG1-1, a small nucleolar GTP-binding protein from Arabidopsis, when constitutively expressed in rice, confers robust drought tolerance at vegetative and reproductive stages. [75]
  • AtNOG1-1-OX rice plants maintain or increase shoot biomass and grain yield under drought without yield penalty under well-watered conditions. [75]
  • Overexpression of AtNOG1-1 increases cell sap osmolality, relative water content, and ABA levels while decreasing leaf water loss during drought. [75]
  • AtNOG1-1 promotes formation and tighter packing of silica bodies around stomata, providing a secondary barrier to transpirational water loss. [75]
  • Transcriptome analysis shows that AtNOG1-1 modulates expression of core sets of rice genes involved in ABA signaling, JA metabolism, stomatal regulation, osmotic potential, and stress protection. [75]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Constitutive overexpression of AtNOG1-1 in rice enhances drought tolerance at both vegetative and reproductive stages without yield penalty, increases shoot biomass and seed yield under drought, elevates cell sap osmolality, relative water content, and ABA levels in leaves, and reduces leaf water loss, partly via promoting silica body formation around stomata that restricts transpirational water loss. [75]

Regulatory Mechanism

Nuclear-localized small GTPase that, when overexpressed, alters transcript levels of multiple ABA- and JA-related, stomatal, ion transport, and stress-protection genes, and promotes formation and dense arrangement of silica bodies around stomata to reduce transpirational water loss. [75]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts in drought stress-response pathways including ABA signaling, jasmonic acid metabolism, stomatal regulation, osmotic potential maintenance, and regulation of silica body formation around stomata. [75]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSmall GTPase nucleolar GTP-binding protein 1 (NOG1).
Expression PatternAtNOG1-1 transcripts are strongly accumulated in AtNOG1-1-OX rice lines relative to empty-vector control, whereas endogenous OsNOG1 transcript levels are not significantly altered in these overexpressors.
Breeding Application ValueAtNOG1-1 is a promising transgene for engineering drought-tolerant rice and potentially other cereals, as its constitutive overexpression confers strong drought tolerance at both vegetative and reproductive stages, increases biomass and grain yield under drought, and does not penalize yield under well-watered conditions.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by withholding watering on potted rice plants at vegetative (45–56 days old) and reproductive (heading, 85 days old) stages until soil moisture dropped to 0%, followed by re-watering for recovery; mild cyclic drought during reproductive stage for yield analysis; comparison with continuously well-watered controls.

AtNOG1-2 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • AtNOG1-2, a small nucleolar GTP-binding protein from Arabidopsis, when constitutively expressed in rice, confers strong drought tolerance at vegetative and reproductive stages. [75]
  • AtNOG1-2-OX rice plants show increased shoot biomass and grain yield under drought, without yield penalties under well-watered conditions. [75]
  • Overexpression of AtNOG1-2 elevates cell sap osmolality, relative water content, and ABA levels and reduces leaf water loss during drought. [75]
  • AtNOG1-2 has a predominant role in promoting elongation and increased number of silica bodies surrounding stomata, reducing the area available for stomatal transpiration. [75]
  • Transcriptome analysis shows AtNOG1-2 regulates overlapping and distinct sets of rice genes compared with AtNOG1-1, including many involved in ABA and JA signaling, stomatal regulation, osmotic potential, and stress protection, indicating partial functional divergence between the paralogs. [75]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Constitutive overexpression of AtNOG1-2 in rice confers enhanced drought tolerance at vegetative and reproductive stages without yield penalty, increases shoot biomass and seed yield under drought, elevates cell sap osmolality, relative water content, and ABA levels in leaves, and reduces leaf water loss, with a particularly strong effect on silica body formation around stomata that limits transpirational water loss. [75]

Regulatory Mechanism

Nuclear-localized small GTPase that, when overexpressed, alters expression of multiple ABA/JA, stomatal, ion transport, and stress-response genes and strongly promotes formation and dense arrangement of silica bodies surrounding stomata to reduce transpirational water loss. [75]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in drought stress-response pathways including ABA signaling, jasmonic acid metabolism, stomatal regulation, osmotic potential maintenance, and silica body formation around stomata. [75]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSmall GTPase nucleolar GTP-binding protein 1 (NOG1).
Expression PatternAtNOG1-2 transcripts are strongly accumulated in AtNOG1-2-OX rice lines relative to empty-vector control, whereas endogenous OsNOG1 transcript levels are not significantly altered in these overexpressors.
Breeding Application ValueAtNOG1-2 is a strong candidate gene for transgenic improvement of drought tolerance in rice and other cereals because its overexpression markedly enhances drought tolerance, promotes protective silica body coverage around stomata, and increases biomass and grain yield under drought without compromising performance under well-watered conditions.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by withholding watering on potted rice plants at vegetative and reproductive stages until soil moisture reached 0%, followed by re-watering, as well as mild cyclic drought during reproductive stage for yield analysis, compared with continuously well-watered controls.

OsNOG1 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsNOG1, comprising two highly similar paralogs in rice, encodes a small nucleolar GTP-binding protein required for drought tolerance and normal growth. [75]
  • RNAi-mediated knockdown of OsNOG1 reduces transcript and protein levels and leads to rapid wilting, poor survival, and low recovery under drought at vegetative and reproductive stages. [75]
  • OsNOG1-RNAi lines have reduced shoot biomass and seed yield under both well-watered and drought conditions. [75]
  • Physiologically, OsNOG1 knockdown decreases cell sap osmolality, relative water content, and ABA levels while increasing leaf water loss. [75]
  • OsNOG1 is necessary for proper formation and tight arrangement of silica bodies around stomata; OsNOG1-RNAi plants have smaller, fewer, and more widely spaced silica bodies, increasing the area for stomatal transpiration. [75]
  • Transcriptome analysis shows that OsNOG1 positively regulates many drought-, ABA-, JA-, stomatal-, ion transport-, antioxidant-, and growth-related genes, and its loss leads to downregulation of core drought-induced genes and upregulation of stress-sensitive gene sets. [75]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Downregulation of OsNOG1 in rice leads to enhanced drought susceptibility at vegetative and reproductive stages, with plants wilting and dying faster than control under drought, poor recovery after re-watering, and significantly reduced shoot biomass and seed yield under both well-watered and drought conditions; OsNOG1-RNAi lines show lower cell sap osmolality, lower relative water content, and reduced ABA levels under drought, but higher leaf water loss and diminished silica body size, number, and density around stomata. [75]

Regulatory Mechanism

Endogenous OsNOG1 functions as a positive regulator of drought tolerance and growth: its knockdown decreases expression of many ABA- and JA-related, stomatal, antioxidant, and drought-responsive genes, reduces silica body formation and packing around stomata, and thereby increases transpirational water loss and drought susceptibility. [75]

Pathways

Regulatory: OsNOG1 acts in drought stress-response pathways, including ABA signaling, jasmonic acid metabolism, stomatal regulation, osmotic potential maintenance, silica body formation around stomata, oxidative stress protection, and general drought tolerance. [75]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSmall GTPase nucleolar GTP-binding protein 1 (NOG1).
Expression PatternRT-qPCR shows that OsNOG1 transcripts are reduced approximately two- to five-fold in OsNOG1-RNAi lines compared with control plants, while western blotting detects a marked decrease of the 77-kDa NOG1 protein band in OsNOG1-RNAi lines relative to control.
Breeding Application ValueOsNOG1 is essential for drought tolerance and normal growth in rice; its loss-of-function via RNAi causes severe drought susceptibility and yield loss under both well-watered and drought conditions. These results indicate that maintaining or enhancing functional OsNOG1 alleles is important for breeding or biotechnological strategies aiming to improve drought tolerance and sustain yield in rice, and OsNOG1-linked markers could be used to avoid deleterious alleles.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress induced by withholding water from potted rice plants at vegetative and reproductive stages until soil moisture reached 0%, followed by re-watering for recovery; mild cyclical drought imposed at reproductive stage for yield analysis; comparisons with continuously well-watered plants.

OsTZF7 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsTZF7 is a tandem CCCH zinc finger RNA-binding protein in rice whose expression is tissue-specific and is strongly induced by PEG-simulated drought, salt, and ABA, but repressed by cold. [76]
  • Overexpression of OsTZF7 (OE lines) enhances drought tolerance and reduces water loss, whereas RNAi knockdown plants are more sensitive to drought and lose water faster; OsTZF7-OE seedlings are hypersensitive and RNAi seedlings less sensitive to exogenous ABA. [76]
  • RNA-seq reveals that OsTZF7 modulates expression of numerous stress- and ABA-related genes, including ABA signaling transcription factor OsABI5, dehydrin, LEA, aquaporin, and peroxidase genes, and that DEGs are enriched in ABA and drought-response pathways, plant hormone signaling, MAPK signaling, and phenylpropanoid biosynthesis. [76]
  • OsTZF7 localizes to the nucleus and to PB/SG-like cytoplasmic foci, colocalizing with PB and SG markers; its C-terminal leucine-rich NES and CRM1 mediate nuclear export, demonstrating nucleocytoplasmic trafficking. [76]
  • Recombinant OsTZF7 binds AU-rich (ARE) and U-rich ARE-like motifs in vitro within the 3' UTRs of downregulated genes, and a U-rich consensus motif is enriched among these targets, supporting a role in mRNA turnover in PBs/SGs. [76]
  • OsTZF7 physically interacts with three PWWP domain proteins (OsPWWP1/2/3), linking OsTZF7 to chromatin- and histone-modification-related complexes and suggesting additional regulatory functions beyond RNA metabolism. [76]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Positive regulator of drought tolerance and ABA response in rice; overexpression enhances survival and reduces water loss under PEG-simulated and soil drought, whereas RNAi knockdown plants are more drought-sensitive with faster water loss and reduced ABA sensitivity [76]

Regulatory Mechanism

RNA-binding protein that binds AU-rich and U-rich motifs in the 3' UTRs of target mRNAs, likely promoting their turnover in PBs/SGs. Shuttles between nucleus and PBs/SGs via a CRM1-dependent C-terminal leucine-rich nuclear export signal. Interacts with PWWP chromatin-associated proteins, suggesting a possible link to histone modification–related regulatory complexes. [76]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA-dependent drought stress signaling and cytoplasmic mRNA processing body/stress granule pathway; associated with gene sets for CCR4-NOT complex, histone methyltransferase complex, RNA polyadenylation, and cytoplasmic mRNA processing bodies under PEG-induced drought. [76]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainTandem CCCH zinc finger (TZF) RNA-binding protein containing CCCH-type zinc finger motifs and a C-terminal leucine-rich nuclear export signal
Expression PatternHighly expressed in root, stem, callus, and inflorescence; strong promoter-driven GUS activity in young leaf, young root, mature root, stem, sheath, coleoptile, pistil, and anthers, with lower activity in mature leaf, lemma, and palea. mRNA levels are upregulated by PEG4000-simulated drought, salt (NaCl), and ABA treatments, and downregulated by cold (4°C) in shoots and roots.
Validation MethodTransgenic overexpression and RNAi knockdown in rice assessed under PEG-simulated and soil drought and ABA treatments; transcriptome profiling (RNA-seq) of OE and RNAi lines versus wild type; GFP-based subcellular localization and colocalization with PB/SG markers; NES deletion and Leptomycin B treatment to test CRM1-dependent nuclear export; REMSA to demonstrate binding to ARE and ARE-like motifs; yeast two-hybrid and BiFC assays to verify interaction with PWWP proteins.
Breeding Application ValueOsTZF7 overexpression significantly enhances drought tolerance and reduces water loss in rice at both seedling and vegetative stages while modulating ABA sensitivity, indicating that OsTZF7 is a promising genetic target for improving drought resilience in rice breeding through transgenic approaches or marker-assisted selection in systems where altered ABA responsiveness and stress-related gene expression are desirable.
Environment / TreatmentPEG4000 (20%) for simulated drought stress in hydroponic culture; soil drought imposed by withholding water in barrels; salt stress (200 mM NaCl in rice, 150 mM NaCl in tobacco); ABA treatments (100 μM ABA for induction of OsTZF7 expression, 2 μM ABA in 1/2 MS medium for seedling growth assays); cold stress at 4°C.

FtMYB3 (Fagopyrum tataricum) Plant

Key Findings

  • FtMYB3 encodes a subgroup 4 R2R3-MYB transcription factor with conserved R2/R3 domains, bHLH interaction motif and C-terminal C1/C2 repression motifs. [77]
  • FtMYB3 is predominantly expressed in roots and is weakly expressed in flowers and seeds; its expression is induced by SA, MeJA, drought (PEG6000), salt (NaCl) and low temperature. [77]
  • Ectopic overexpression of FtMYB3 in tobacco and Arabidopsis reduces anthocyanin and PA contents, leading to lighter floral and seed coat pigmentation, while increasing rutin levels in Arabidopsis. [77]
  • In Arabidopsis overexpressing FtMYB3, expression of late flavonoid biosynthetic genes DFR, ANS and BAN and the PA transporter gene TT13 is significantly downregulated, whereas early biosynthetic genes are largely unaffected and FLS can be upregulated. [77]
  • FtMYB3 physically interacts with bHLH and WD40 proteins (TT8, GL3, EGL3, TTG1) from Tartary buckwheat and Arabidopsis, consistent with a role in MBW complexes. [77]
  • Transient expression assays show that FtMYB3 represses the promoter activity of both AtDFR and FtDFR2, confirming direct transcriptional repression of DFR genes. [77]
  • Overall, FtMYB3 acts as an R2R3-MYB repressor that negatively regulates anthocyanin and PA biosynthesis in Tartary buckwheat and in heterologous systems. [77]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as a transcriptional repressor that decreases anthocyanin and proanthocyanidin accumulation, leading to reduced pigmentation in tobacco petals and Arabidopsis seedlings and seed coats. [77]

Regulatory Mechanism

SG4-type R2R3-MYB repressor that (1) forms complexes with bHLH and WD40 proteins (TT8/GL3/EGL3/TTG1) to inhibit transcriptional activation of late flavonoid biosynthetic and PA transport genes, and (2) directly represses promoter activity of DFR genes (AtDFR and FtDFR2). [77]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions within the MYB-bHLH-WD40 (MBW) regulatory network controlling anthocyanin and proanthocyanidin biosynthesis, acting downstream of SA and MeJA and abiotic stress signals. [77]

Biosynthetic: Flavonoid biosynthesis pathway, specifically the anthocyanin and proanthocyanidin branches (late biosynthetic steps involving DFR, ANS, BAN and PA transport via TT13) [77]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainR2R3-MYB transcription factor, subgroup 4 (SG4), with N-terminal R2 and R3 DNA-binding domains, an R3 bHLH-interaction motif and C-terminal C1/C2 repression motifs
Expression PatternHighly expressed in roots, moderate in stems and leaves, very low in flowers and seeds at flowering stage; transcription strongly induced in seedlings by SA, MeJA, PEG6000 (drought), NaCl (salt) and low temperature.
Validation MethodFunctional validation by stable overexpression in Arabidopsis and tobacco with metabolite quantification (anthocyanin, PA, rutin), qRT-PCR analysis of flavonoid structural genes (EBGs and LBGs), yeast two-hybrid interaction assays with bHLH/WD40 partners, and transient repression of AtDFR and FtDFR2 promoters driving GUS.
Breeding Application ValueFtMYB3 is a negative regulator of anthocyanin and PA accumulation and is proposed as a potential target gene for improving Tartary buckwheat quality by modulating flavonoid composition and pigment levels, for example via gene editing or downregulation to enhance anthocyanin/PA contents and associated antioxidant capacity.
Environment / TreatmentHormone treatments: salicylic acid (1 mmol/L SA) and methyl jasmonate (2 mmol/L MeJA); abiotic stresses: 30% PEG6000 (drought), 150 mmol/L NaCl (salt), low temperature; time course 0–15 h.

TaHsfA2e-5D (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaHsfA2e-5D is a wheat HsfA2-type transcription factor located on chromosome 5D, with a conserved DBD, HR-A/B domains, NLS, and a C-terminal AHA activation motif. [78]
  • It is constitutively expressed in various wheat tissues and is strongly induced by heat, drought (PEG6000), salt, cold, and multiple phytohormones, consistent with the presence of stress-related cis-elements in its promoter. [78]
  • TaHsfA2e-5D protein localizes to the nucleus and exhibits transcriptional activation activity in yeast, dependent on its C-terminal AHA motif. [78]
  • Ectopic expression of TaHsfA2e-5D in yeast significantly improves thermotolerance, and overexpression in Arabidopsis enhances tolerance to both heat and drought stresses and reduces water loss. [78]
  • In TaHsfA2e-5D-overexpressing Arabidopsis, several Hsp genes and other stress-related genes including GolS1 and APX2 show elevated transcript levels, indicating that TaHsfA2e-5D enhances stress tolerance at least partly by activating stress-responsive genes. [78]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Wheat heat shock factor that functions as a nuclear transcriptional activator and positively regulates plant responses to heat and drought stresses, improving thermotolerance in yeast and enhancing heat and drought tolerance in Arabidopsis when overexpressed. [78]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a nuclear transcriptional activator via its C-terminal AHA activation domain; GAL4-based yeast assays show that full-length TaHsfA2e-5D, but not a C-terminal 48-amino-acid deletion mutant lacking the AHA motif, activates reporter gene expression, indicating that the AHA region is required for transactivation. Overexpression enhances transcript levels of multiple Hsp and other stress-responsive genes, consistent with transcriptional activation of stress-response pathways. [78]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in the heat shock and broader abiotic stress-response network by up-regulating Hsp genes and other stress-related genes (e.g., APX2, GolS1), thereby enhancing cellular protection mechanisms against heat and drought stresses. [78]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainHeat shock transcription factor of class A2 (HsfA2-type), containing a conserved N-terminal DNA-binding domain (DBD) and HR-A/B oligomerization domain typical of Hsfs, as well as a nuclear localization signal (NLS) and a C-terminal AHA transcriptional activation motif.
Expression PatternConstitutively expressed in multiple wheat tissues at the booting stage, with highest transcript levels in roots, stamens, and pistils, intermediate levels in glume and arista, and lowest levels in leaf, young spike, and stem. Strongly induced in wheat seedlings by high temperature (40 °C), low temperature (4 °C), PEG6000-induced dehydration, NaCl, MeJA, and ABA, and weakly but detectably induced by H2O2 and SA.
Validation MethodHeterologous overexpression in yeast (pYES2-TaHsfA2e-5D) and Arabidopsis thaliana (pSuper1300-TaHsfA2e-5D) followed by heat and drought stress assays; subcellular localization of TaHsfA2e-5D-GFP in tobacco epidermal cells; GAL4-based transcriptional activation assay in yeast using full-length and C-terminally truncated TaHsfA2e-5D; RT-qPCR analysis of downstream stress-responsive genes in transgenic Arabidopsis.
Breeding Application ValueProvides a promising candidate gene for molecular breeding and genetic engineering of wheat and other crops to enhance tolerance to terminal heat and drought stresses by manipulating a stress-responsive heat shock transcription factor.
Environment / TreatmentHeat stress of 40 °C (wheat seedlings, yeast liquid culture), 45 °C for 2 h (Arabidopsis seedlings on plates), and 40/32 °C day/night for 7 days (Arabidopsis in soil); drought stress imposed by PEG6000 treatment in wheat seedlings and withholding water for 14 days in Arabidopsis; additional treatments for expression analysis included 4 °C cold, 20% PEG6000, 0.2 M NaCl, 0.1 mM MeJA, 0.2 mM ABA, 10 mM H2O2, and 0.1 mM SA.

GhGPDH5 (Gossypium hirsutum) Plant

Key Findings

  • GhGPDH5 is a member of the cotton GPDH family located on chromosome A06 and classified as an FAD-dependent GPDH with DAO_C and DAO domains. [79]
  • Transcriptome analyses show that GhGPDH5 is strongly induced by drought, heat, and salt stresses and is highly expressed during early and mid fiber and ovule development. [79]
  • TRV-based VIGS of GhGPDH5 in TM-1 seedlings leads to significantly reduced drought tolerance, with silenced plants exhibiting more severe wilting than controls. [79]
  • GhGPDH5-silenced plants show increased leaf cell death and ROS-associated lesions and fail to close stomata tightly under drought stress, indicating that GhGPDH5 positively regulates drought stress tolerance and stomatal closure in G. hirsutum. [79]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Silencing GhGPDH5 significantly reduces drought tolerance in upland cotton seedlings, causing more severe wilting, increased cell death and ROS accumulation in leaves, and impaired stomatal closure under drought stress. [79]

Regulatory Mechanism

Silencing GhGPDH5 leads to increased ROS accumulation and lesion formation in leaves under drought, as shown by intensified DAB staining, indicating a role in maintaining redox homeostasis during drought stress. [79]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts in the drought stress response pathway to enhance drought tolerance, likely by contributing to osmotic adjustment and ROS/redox regulation and promoting stomatal closure under water deficit. [79]

Biosynthetic: Glycerol-3-phosphate and glycerol metabolism linked to glycolysis, providing precursor for glycerol and triacylglycerol synthesis and contributing to cellular redox and energy metabolism as a glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase. [79]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainFAD-dependent glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (group IV GPDH) containing DAO_C (PF16901) and DAO (PF01266) domains.
Expression PatternIn G. hirsutum, GhGPDH5 expression is strongly induced by drought, heat, and salt stresses, generally increasing with treatment time and then decreasing after peaking around 12 h; it shows high transcript levels during fiber development from 3–20 DPA and elevated expression in ovules from 3–20 DPA.
Validation MethodVirus-induced gene silencing (TRV-based VIGS) of GhGPDH5 in TM-1 seedlings, followed by natural drought treatment, trypan blue and DAB leaf staining, stomatal aperture microscopy, and qPCR confirmation of GhGPDH5 knockdown.
Breeding Application ValueGhGPDH5 is a positive regulator of drought tolerance in upland cotton seedlings; enhancing its activity or selecting favorable alleles could improve drought resistance by promoting stomatal closure, limiting water loss, and maintaining ROS/redox balance, thereby contributing to yield stability under water-limited conditions.
Environment / TreatmentAbiotic stress treatments including cold, heat, drought, and salt (for transcriptome profiling), and natural drought stress imposed on VIGS plants by withholding water before and after TRV:GhGPDH5 inoculation.

OsbHLH057 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as a positive regulator of rice resistance to R. solani and Xoo and of drought tolerance; overexpression lines show shorter disease lesions, reduced pathogen biomass, enhanced antioxidant enzyme activities and lower H2O2 under drought, whereas knockout lines are more susceptible and accumulate more H2O2; altered expression does not detectably affect plant growth and development under normal conditions. [80]

Regulatory Mechanism

Nuclear bHLH transcription factor that binds the AATCA cis-element in target promoters and activates transcription; directly activates Os2H16 via the AATCA element in its promoter; overexpression increases expression of defense-related PR genes (OsPR1a, OsPR10) and drought-associated marker genes (OsOSE2, OsDip1) under stress, enhances SOD, POD and CAT activities, and reduces H2O2 accumulation during drought. [80]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in rice defense and drought-response pathways by activating AATCA-motif-containing genes, including Os2H16 and many defense- and hormone-related genes, thereby enhancing responses to pathogens and drought. [80]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainBasic helix–loop–helix (bHLH) transcription factor; contains a bHLH domain (residues 93–144) and a leucine zipper (ZIP) motif (residues 150–185); placed in bHLH subfamily IV by phylogenetic analysis.
Expression PatternOsbHLH057 transcript is rapidly induced by R. solani infection (peak at 8 h), by Xoo infection (rapid induction at 4 h, peak at 6 h), and by 15% (w/v) PEG8000 osmotic stress (strong induction at 24 h).
Validation MethodFunctional validation by generating OsbHLH057 overexpression and CRISPR/Cas9 knockout rice lines and assaying responses to R. solani, Xoo, and drought/PEG treatments; EMSA and ChIP-qPCR to demonstrate specific binding to the AATCA cis-element in the Os2H16 promoter; AATCA-driven GFP reporter assays coexpressed with OsbHLH057 to show transcriptional activation; yeast transcriptional activation assay and nuclear localization analysis to confirm its role as a transcription factor.
Breeding Application ValueOsbHLH057 is a promising target for engineering rice with enhanced resistance to sheath blight and bacterial blight and improved drought tolerance, as its overexpression strengthens defense and drought responses without detectable penalties on plant growth and yield-related traits under normal conditions; the OsbHLH057–AATCA module offers a pathogen- and drought-inducible regulatory system to drive defense genes such as Os2H16, potentially reducing resistance costs associated with constitutive expression.
Environment / TreatmentInoculation with Rhizoctonia solani and Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae; 15% (w/v) PEG8000 treatment to mimic drought; soil drought by withholding water for 21 days.

Os2H16 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Os2H16 is a multifaceted defense-related gene encoding a 61-amino-acid peptide; its expression is transcriptionally activated via the AATCA cis-element in its promoter bound by OsbHLH057, and increased Os2H16 expression in OsbHLH057 overexpression lines correlates with enhanced resistance to R. solani and Xoo and improved drought tolerance, while reduced Os2H16 expression in OsbHLH057 knockout lines correlates with increased susceptibility. [80]

Regulatory Mechanism

Its promoter contains two R. solani-inducible fragments: a −411 to −309 region with a GT-1 element and a −513 to −412 region harboring the AATCA cis-element. Deletion or mutation of the AATCA motif abolishes pathogen-inducible GFP expression, while tandem AATCA motifs confer strong R. solani- and Xoo-inducible expression when fused to a minimal promoter. OsbHLH057 binds specifically to the AATCA-containing region, as shown by EMSA and ChIP-qPCR, and activates Os2H16 transcription in vivo. [80]

Pathways

Regulatory: Os2H16 functions in rice defense and drought-response pathways as a downstream effector gene whose promoter integrates inputs from at least two cis-elements: the GT-1 element, bound by OsASR2 (from previous work), and the AATCA cis-element, bound by OsbHLH057, thereby contributing to resistance to R. solani, Xoo, and drought. [80]

More Details
Expression PatternPromoter analysis shows that Os2H16 carries two R. solani-inducible regions: a −411 to −309 fragment containing GT-1 and a −513 to −412 fragment containing the newly defined AATCA cis-element; the AATCA element is required and sufficient (when repeated and fused to a minimal promoter) for strong R. solani- and Xoo-inducible reporter expression in transgenic rice. In OsbHLH057 OE lines, Os2H16 transcript levels are significantly higher, and in OsbHLH057 KO lines significantly lower, than in WT plants after R. solani, Xoo, or PEG8000 treatment.
Validation MethodFunctional analysis of Os2H16 regulatory elements by promoter deletion and motif mutagenesis fused to GFP in transient N. benthamiana assays and stable transgenic rice, combined with R. solani and Xoo inoculation; duplication of the AATCA motif upstream of a minimal promoter to test sufficiency for pathogen inducibility; EMSA and ChIP-qPCR to show that OsbHLH057 binds the AATCA-containing region of the Os2H16 promoter and activates Os2H16 expression in overexpression lines while knockout of OsbHLH057 suppresses Os2H16 induction under stress.
Breeding Application ValueOs2H16, together with its AATCA-containing promoter, represents a useful module for engineering rice with enhanced resistance to sheath blight and bacterial blight and improved drought tolerance. The AATCA cis-element confers strong R. solani- and Xoo-inducible expression when fused to a minimal promoter, suggesting that Os2H16 or other defense genes driven by AATCA-based synthetic promoters could provide inducible resistance with reduced fitness costs compared with constitutive promoters.
Environment / TreatmentInoculation with Rhizoctonia solani and Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae; treatments with chitin, Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzicola, and heat-treated R. solani to test AATCA responsiveness; 15% (w/v) PEG8000 to mimic drought.

OsSAP (Oryza sativa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

OsSAP positively regulates drought tolerance in rice by enhancing ROS-scavenging capacity and stress-responsive gene expression; loss-of-function CRISPR/Cas9 GeD lines show reduced survival, shorter shoots and roots, lower chlorophyll content, fewer tillers, lower 1,000-grain weight, higher H2O2 and MDA accumulation, weaker antioxidant enzyme activities, and lower induction of drought-related transcription factors under drought, whereas OsSAP-T6 overexpression lines show enhanced antioxidant capacity, reduced ROS accumulation, and improved growth and yield under drought. [81]

Regulatory Mechanism

Under drought, OsSAP promotes the antioxidant defense by increasing activities of SOD, POD, and CAT, leading to reduced accumulation of H2O2 and O2− and lower MDA levels; it also enhances the transcription of multiple stress-responsive genes, which correlates with improved ROS scavenging, delayed leaf senescence, and maintenance of growth and yield under water deficit. [81]

Pathways

Regulatory: Drought stress response and ROS-scavenging/antioxidant defense pathway in rice: OsSAP acts upstream to enhance activities of antioxidant enzymes (SOD, POD, CAT) and to elevate expression of drought-responsive transcription factors (OsABI5, OsRAB16A, OsLEA3, OsLIP9, OsCATA, OsDREB2A, OsNAC5, OsNAC6), thereby improving ROS detoxification, limiting lipid peroxidation, and increasing drought tolerance. [81]

More Details
Expression PatternRT-PCR showed that OsSAP transcripts were absent in all analyzed OsSAP CRISPR/Cas9 genome-edited plants: OsSAP was not expressed in GeD0 plants, GeD1 plants, or GeD2 seeds, whereas Cas9 remained expressed at varying levels across generations.
Validation MethodCRISPR/Cas9-mediated targeted knockout of OsSAP (multiple homozygous GeD lines), use of OsSAP-T6 overexpression lines, drought stress treatment at 20% soil moisture, phenotyping of survival, shoot/root length, chlorophyll content, tiller number and 1,000-grain weight, biochemical assays for H2O2, MDA, proline, SOD, POD and CAT activities, DAB and NBT staining for ROS visualization, and RT/qRT-PCR analysis of OsSAP, Cas9, and drought-responsive transcription factors.
Breeding Application ValueOsSAP is a strong candidate gene for breeding drought-tolerant, high-yield rice. Overexpression of OsSAP improves drought resistance while maintaining normal agronomic performance under non-stress conditions and increasing key yield components under drought, making it suitable for cultivar development without yield penalties. CRISPR/Cas9-mediated editing can generate homozygous OsSAP alleles already in T0 plants, and the ear-to-row field strategy with low coefficients of variation in T1 plants and T2 seeds (<5%) demonstrates that OsSAP-targeted editing can be efficiently integrated into short breeding cycles for rapid development of drought-tolerant rice varieties.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by withholding water until soil moisture content reached 20% at the four-leaf stage, followed by 7 days of re-watering for recovery; comparisons also made under well-watered control conditions.

TtASR-4A (Triticum turgidum L. subsp. durum) Plant

Key Findings

  • TtASR-4A was identified in the Svevo genome as the homoeologous ASR gene on chromosome 4A corresponding to the previously isolated Mahmoudi TtASR1. [82]
  • TtASR-4A (together with TtASR-4B) is constitutively expressed in leaves and roots and strongly salt-responsive in roots, with up-regulation in salt-tolerant and repression in salt-susceptible genotypes. [82]
  • Under PEG-induced water deficit, TtASR-4A/4B expression shows mainly non-discriminatory repression in roots of both tolerant and susceptible genotypes. [82]
  • Root expression level at 72 h of salt stress is suggested as a functional parameter for distinguishing salt tolerance and for developing ASR-based markers in durum wheat. [82]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Higher root expression at 72 h under prolonged high salinity is associated with the salt-tolerant genotype, whereas repression is observed in the susceptible genotype; under PEG-induced water deficit, TtASR-4A/4B expression does not distinguish drought tolerance. [82]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainASR (abscisic acid-stress-ripening) protein containing the conserved ABA_WDS (abscisic acid-water-deficit stress) domain (PF02496)
Expression PatternConstitutively expressed in leaves and roots without stress. Under 200 mM NaCl, using primers amplifying both TtASR-4A and TtASR-4B, transcripts are a-specifically induced in leaves of both genotypes (≈3.1–3.8-fold at 72 h) but show contrasting regulation in roots: down-regulated in susceptible HmiraK11835S (≈0.6–0.8 of control at 72 h) and up-regulated in tolerant HmiraK11857T (≈3-fold at 72 h). Under 15% PEG, expression is largely unchanged at 72 h: in roots both genotypes are slightly repressed (≈0.8 vs 0.5 of control in susceptible vs tolerant), with only transient early changes; leaves show mainly non-significant variation at 72 h. Root expression is generally higher than in leaves.
Validation MethodTime-course qRT-PCR expression profiling under 200 mM NaCl and 15% PEG in contrasting salt/drought-tolerant and -susceptible genotypes
Breeding Application ValueRoot expression of TtASR-4A/4B at 72 h after 200 mM NaCl is proposed as a discriminating parameter between salt-tolerant and salt-susceptible durum wheat genotypes (≈3 vs 0.6-fold), supporting TtASR-4A as part of a robust candidate-gene set for abiotic stress resistance and as a target for functional marker development in durum wheat breeding.
Environment / Treatment200 mM NaCl (salt stress) and 15% PEG 6000 (osmotic/water-deficit stress) applied to 10-day-old seedlings; sampling at 0, 6, 24, 72 h post-treatment.

TtASR-4B (Triticum turgidum L. subsp. durum) Plant

Key Findings

  • TtASR-4B was identified as the 4B homoeolog of the durum wheat ASR gene family, highly similar (≈97.3%) to TtASR-4A and sharing a simple 2-exon/1-intron structure. [82]
  • Combined TtASR-4A/4B transcripts are differentially regulated by high salinity in roots of tolerant vs susceptible genotypes, with up-regulation in tolerant and repression in susceptible plants. [82]
  • Under PEG-induced water deficit, TtASR-4A/4B are weakly repressed in roots of both genotypes and do not clearly distinguish drought tolerance. [82]
  • The contrasting salt-induced root expression pattern supports inclusion of TtASR-4B in ASR-based markers for salt-tolerance selection in durum wheat. [82]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Like TtASR-4A, TtASR-4B shows salt-responsive expression in roots, with higher transcript accumulation in the salt-tolerant genotype and repression in the susceptible one, supporting a role in root-mediated adaptation to high salinity; PEG-induced drought response is weak and not clearly associated with drought tolerance. [82]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainASR (abscisic acid-stress-ripening) protein family; simple 2-exon/1-intron gene structure typical of ASR genes
Expression PatternConstitutively expressed in leaves and roots without stress. Using primers that co-amplify TtASR-4A and TtASR-4B, transcripts are a-specifically induced in leaves under 200 mM NaCl in both susceptible and tolerant genotypes (≈3.1–3.8-fold at 72 h). In roots under NaCl, expression is down-regulated in susceptible HmiraK11835S (≈0.6–0.8 of control) and up-regulated in tolerant HmiraK11857T (≈3-fold) at 72 h. Under 15% PEG, both homoeologs show mainly weak repression in roots (≈0.8 vs 0.5 of control at 72 h in susceptible vs tolerant) and little change in leaves at 72 h.
Validation MethodTime-course qRT-PCR expression profiling under 200 mM NaCl and 15% PEG in contrasting salt/drought-tolerant and -susceptible genotypes using a primer pair that co-amplifies TtASR-4A and TtASR-4B.
Breeding Application ValueCombined root expression of TtASR-4A/4B at 72 h after salt treatment discriminates salt-tolerant from salt-susceptible durum wheat genotypes, making TtASR-4B together with TtASR-4A a promising component of ASR-based functional markers for selection of salt-tolerant durum wheat lines.
Environment / Treatment200 mM NaCl (salt stress) and 15% PEG 6000 (osmotic/water-deficit stress) applied to 10-day-old seedlings; sampling at 0, 6, 24, 72 h post-treatment.

TaASR-4A (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaASR-4A was identified as a group-4A homoeologous ASR gene in common wheat with a distinct 3-exon/2-intron structure. [82]
  • TaASR-4A is constitutively expressed and shows higher basal expression in roots than leaves. [82]
  • Under both high salinity and PEG-induced drought, TaASR-4A is strongly up-regulated in roots of tolerant Ta001T but repressed in roots of susceptible Ta002S, while leaf responses are weak or non-discriminatory. [82]
  • Root expression levels at 72 h of stress provide a clear molecular parameter to differentiate tolerant vs susceptible genotypes, highlighting TaASR-4A as a strong candidate for functional marker development. [82]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Root expression of TaASR-4A at 72 h under both high salinity and water deficit is strongly induced in the tolerant genotype and repressed in the susceptible one, making TaASR-4A expression a clear molecular correlate of salt and drought tolerance in common wheat. [82]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainASR (abscisic acid-stress-ripening) protein family, characterized by the conserved ABA_WDS domain typical of ASR proteins
Expression PatternConstitutively expressed in leaves and roots without stress, with higher baseline expression in roots than leaves. Under 200 mM NaCl, leaf expression shows mainly a-speciﬁc variation, with final 72 h levels close to control in both Ta001T and Ta002S; root expression diverges, being up-regulated in tolerant Ta001T (≈2.6-fold at 72 h) and down-regulated in susceptible Ta002S (≈0.4-fold). Under 15% PEG, leaves of both genotypes show repression to similar levels (≈0.2–0.3 of control at 72 h), whereas roots again show contrasting regulation: strong up-regulation in tolerant Ta001T (≈5-fold at 72 h) and repression in susceptible Ta002S (≈0.5-fold). Root expression is consistently much higher than leaf expression, especially in the tolerant genotype.
