Validation MethodTaASR-4A-specific qRT-PCR (primer pair P1) time-course expression analysis under 200 mM NaCl and 15% PEG in tolerant and susceptible common wheat genotypes.
Breeding Application ValueRoot TaASR-4A expression at 72 h after NaCl or PEG treatment robustly distinguishes tolerant from susceptible common wheat genotypes, supporting TaASR-4A as a key candidate gene for abiotic stress resistance and as a functional marker target for selecting salt- and drought-tolerant wheat in breeding programs.
Environment / Treatment200 mM NaCl (salt stress) and 15% PEG 6000 (water-deficit stress), applied to 10-day-old seedlings; expression monitored at 0, 6, 24, 72 h.

TaASR-4B (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaASR-4B was identified as a 4B homoeolog of the ASR gene family in common wheat, sharing a conserved 2-exon/1-intron structure with TaASR-4D. [82]
  • Combined TaASR-4B/4D transcripts show higher basal expression in roots than leaves, particularly in tolerant genotypes. [82]
  • Under both high salinity and PEG-induced drought, TaASR-4B/4D are up-regulated in roots of tolerant Ta001T and repressed in roots of susceptible Ta002S, while leaves are generally repressed in both genotypes. [82]
  • The contrasting root expression responses at 72 h of stress make TaASR-4B/4D valuable candidates for functional markers associated with salt and drought tolerance in common wheat. [82]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Combined TaASR-4B/4D root expression at 72 h is strongly induced in the tolerant genotype and repressed in the susceptible one under both high salinity and water deficit, providing a clear molecular signature of stress tolerance in common wheat. [82]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainASR (abscisic acid-stress-ripening) protein family with conserved gene structure of 2 exons separated by 1 intron
Expression PatternConstitutively expressed in leaves and roots, with higher baseline expression in roots, especially in the tolerant genotype. Using primer pair P2 that co-amplifies TaASR-4B/4D, under 200 mM NaCl leaf expression shows little discriminative change, with final strong repression at 72 h in both genotypes (≈0.26 and 0.36 of control in susceptible and tolerant, respectively). In roots under NaCl, expression diverges sharply: up-regulated in tolerant Ta001T (≈1.8-fold at 72 h) and repressed in susceptible Ta002S (≈0.5-fold). Under 15% PEG, leaves of both genotypes are down-regulated at 72 h (≈0.3 vs 0.5 of control in susceptible vs tolerant). In roots under PEG, TaASR-4B/4D are strongly induced in the tolerant line (≈5-fold at 72 h) and repressed in the susceptible one (≈0.3-fold).
Validation MethodqRT-PCR expression profiling using primer pair P2 that co-amplifies TaASR-4B and TaASR-4D under 200 mM NaCl and 15% PEG in tolerant and susceptible common wheat genotypes.
Breeding Application ValueRoot expression of TaASR-4B/4D at 72 h under NaCl and PEG effectively separates tolerant from susceptible common wheat genotypes, supporting TaASR-4B as a component of ASR-based functional markers for selecting salt- and drought-tolerant lines in breeding programs.
Environment / Treatment200 mM NaCl and 15% PEG 6000 applied to 10-day-old seedlings; expression monitored at 0, 6, 24, 72 h.

TaASR-4D (Triticum aestivum L.) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaASR-4D was identified as a 4D homoeolog of the wheat ASR gene family with a conserved 2-exon/1-intron gene structure. [82]
  • In combination with TaASR-4B, TaASR-4D shows higher expression in roots than leaves and generally higher expression in hexaploid than tetraploid wheat. [82]
  • Combined TaASR-4B/4D transcripts are up-regulated in roots of tolerant Ta001T and repressed in roots of susceptible Ta002S under both high salinity and PEG-induced drought, while leaves are repressed in both genotypes. [82]
  • This contrasting root expression pattern supports inclusion of TaASR-4D, together with TaASR-4B, in ASR-based molecular tools for selecting stress-tolerant common wheat. [82]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Combined TaASR-4B/4D root expression is strongly induced in the tolerant genotype and repressed in the susceptible genotype under both salt and drought, indicating that TaASR-4D, together with TaASR-4B, contributes to enhanced root-level adaptation to abiotic stress in common wheat. [82]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainASR (abscisic acid-stress-ripening) protein family with conserved 2-exon/1-intron structure similar to TaASR-4B
Expression PatternConstitutively expressed in leaves and roots, with higher basal expression in roots and stronger overall expression in hexaploid than tetraploid wheat. In this study, TaASR-4D expression is monitored together with TaASR-4B: leaves show strong repression at 72 h under both NaCl and PEG in both genotypes, whereas roots exhibit up-regulation in tolerant Ta001T and repression in susceptible Ta002S under both stresses (≈1.8 vs 0.5-fold under NaCl; ≈5 vs 0.3-fold under PEG at 72 h).
Validation MethodqRT-PCR expression profiling with primer pair P2 that co-amplifies TaASR-4B and TaASR-4D under 200 mM NaCl and 15% PEG in tolerant and susceptible common wheat genotypes.
Breeding Application ValueBecause combined TaASR-4B/4D root expression at 72 h under salt and drought clearly differentiates tolerant from susceptible genotypes, TaASR-4D is part of a multi-copy ASR gene set proposed as robust candidates for abiotic stress resistance and as targets for functional marker development in common wheat breeding.
Environment / Treatment200 mM NaCl and 15% PEG 6000 applied to 10-day-old seedlings; sampling at 0, 6, 24, 72 h.

PhACO1 (Petunia hybrida) Plant

Key Findings

  • PhACO1 encodes an ACC oxidase involved in the final step of ethylene biosynthesis in Petunia hybrida cv. 'Mirage Rose'. 2. CRISPR/Cas9 editing of PhACO1 (phaco1 mutants) markedly reduces ACO1 transcript levels and ethylene production in leaves under both control and stress conditions. 3. phaco1 mutants are more sensitive than WT to salt (NaCl) and drought stress, showing reduced growth, lower RWC, altered stomatal behavior, and less favorable K+/Na+ homeostasis. 4. Reduced ethylene production in phaco1 mutants is associated with altered expression of antioxidant, proline-related, ABA biosynthesis/signaling, and ethylene receptor/signaling genes under stress, indicating that PhACO1-mediated ethylene production is important for transcriptional regulation of abiotic stress–responsive pathways. [83]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Loss-of-function of PhACO1 via CRISPR editing lowers ethylene production in leaves and decreases tolerance to salt and drought stress, resulting in reduced growth, lower RWC, more closed stomata, and less favorable K+/Na+ ratios compared with WT plants. [83]

Regulatory Mechanism

PhACO1 encodes ACC oxidase catalyzing the conversion of ACC to ethylene; CRISPR editing greatly reduces ACO1 expression and ethylene production, which in turn alters transcription of antioxidant (CAT, POD, Osmotin), ABA biosynthesis/signaling (NCED1, AAO31, PLDα), and ethylene receptor/signaling (ETR2, ERS1, EIN2, EIL1) genes during salt and drought stress. [83]

Pathways

Regulatory: Ethylene biosynthesis pathway and ethylene-mediated abiotic stress response, with cross-talk to ABA signaling and antioxidant/proline metabolism under salt and drought stress. [83]

Biosynthetic: Ethylene biosynthesis (ACC oxidase–catalyzed conversion of ACC to ethylene). [83]

More Details
Expression PatternIn WT leaves, ACO1 transcript levels are strongly induced by salt stress and by drought stress, whereas phaco1 mutants show barely detectable ACO1 expression before stress and only weak induction under drought; ACO1 is also stress-inducible in aco3 mutants. Under all conditions, ACO1 expression and ethylene production are markedly higher in WT than in phaco1 mutants.
Validation MethodFunctional validation by CRISPR/Cas9 editing of PhACO1 combined with phenotypic analysis under salt and drought stress, ethylene production assays, and qRT-PCR profiling of stress-related genes in WT and phaco1 mutants.
Breeding Application ValuePhACO1 is required to maintain ethylene levels that support salt and drought stress tolerance in petunia. Reducing PhACO1 activity to prolong flower longevity can compromise abiotic stress tolerance; therefore, breeding or gene-editing strategies aiming at ethylene reduction in petunia and other horticultural crops should balance postharvest quality improvements against potential losses in salt and drought tolerance.
Environment / TreatmentSalt stress imposed by irrigating potted plants with 50, 100, then 150 mM NaCl, and drought stress imposed by withholding water for 10 days in a greenhouse.

PhACO3 (Petunia hybrida) Plant

Key Findings

  • PhACO3 encodes an ACC oxidase involved in ethylene biosynthesis in Petunia hybrida cv. 'Mirage Rose'. 2. CRISPR/Cas9 editing of PhACO3 (phaco3 mutants) substantially reduces ACO3 transcript levels and ethylene production in leaves under control and stress conditions. 3. phaco3 mutants are more sensitive than WT to salt and drought stress, with reduced growth, lower RWC, altered stomatal behavior, and less favorable K+/Na+ homeostasis. 4. Reduced ethylene production in phaco3 mutants is accompanied by altered expression patterns of antioxidant, proline-related, ABA biosynthesis/signaling, and ethylene receptor/signaling genes during stress, indicating that PhACO3-mediated ethylene biosynthesis contributes to transcriptional regulation of abiotic stress responses. [83]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Loss-of-function of PhACO3 via CRISPR editing lowers ethylene production in leaves and decreases tolerance to salt and drought stress, leading to weaker growth, lower RWC, more closed stomata, and poorer K+/Na+ homeostasis compared with WT plants. [83]

Regulatory Mechanism

PhACO3 encodes ACC oxidase catalyzing the final step of ethylene biosynthesis; CRISPR disruption greatly reduces ACO3 expression and ethylene production, which modifies transcriptional responses of antioxidant, ABA biosynthesis/signaling, and ethylene signaling genes under salt and drought stress. [83]

Pathways

Regulatory: Ethylene biosynthesis pathway and ethylene-mediated abiotic stress response, integrated with ABA signaling and antioxidant/proline metabolism during salt and drought stress. [83]

Biosynthetic: Ethylene biosynthesis (ACC oxidase–catalyzed conversion of ACC to ethylene). [83]

More Details
Expression PatternIn WT leaves, ACO3 transcript levels are strongly induced by salt and drought stress, whereas phaco3 mutants show barely detectable ACO3 expression before stress and little or no significant induction under stress; ACO3 is also stress-inducible in aco1 mutants. Across conditions, ACO3 expression and ethylene production remain much higher in WT than in phaco3 mutants.
Validation MethodFunctional validation by CRISPR/Cas9 editing of PhACO3 combined with salt and drought stress phenotyping, ethylene production measurements, and qRT-PCR profiling of stress-related genes in WT and phaco3 mutants.
Breeding Application ValueFunctional PhACO3 is important for maintaining ethylene levels that support salt and drought stress tolerance in petunia. Strategies that reduce PhACO3 activity to prolong flower longevity may compromise plant performance under abiotic stress; breeding and gene-editing programs should therefore weigh improvements in ornamental quality against potential reductions in stress tolerance.
Environment / TreatmentSalt stress applied by stepwise irrigation with 50, 100, then 150 mM NaCl and drought stress imposed by withholding water for 10 days in a greenhouse.

ShGPCR1 (Saccharum spp. hybrids) Plant

Key Findings

  • ShGPCR1 encodes a conserved GPCR-like protein with nine transmembrane helices and characteristic regulatory motifs, closely related to rice COLD1 and other monocot GPCRs. [84]
  • ShGPCR1 transcripts are significantly induced by drought, salinity, cold, and ABA in sugarcane leaves and culms. [84]
  • ShGPCR1 is predominantly localized at the plasma membrane in sugarcane embryogenic cells. [84]
  • Constitutive overexpression of ShGPCR1 in sugarcane confers enhanced tolerance to drought, high salinity, and cold without detectable growth penalties under non-stress conditions. [84]
  • ShGPCR1-OE plants maintain higher relative water content, root biomass, and overall biomass under drought and exhibit reduced foliar damage and higher survival under salinity and cold. [84]
  • Overexpression of ShGPCR1 is associated with increased expression of multiple drought-, salinity-, and cold-responsive marker genes and the antioxidant CAT gene under stress. [84]
  • ShGPCR1 overexpression significantly enhances GTP-induced cytosolic Ca2+ elevation, while global ionomycin-induced Ca2+ levels remain unchanged, consistent with a role in GPCR-mediated Ca2+ signaling during abiotic stress responses. [84]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Constitutive overexpression of ShGPCR1 in sugarcane confers enhanced tolerance to multiple abiotic stresses (drought, salinity, cold), with delayed and reduced leaf wilting, chlorosis, and necrosis, improved survival, and maintenance of root growth, relative water content, and biomass, without detectable negative effects on growth under non-stress conditions. [84]

Regulatory Mechanism

Plasma membrane-localized GPCR-like receptor that, when overexpressed, enhances GTP-dependent increases in cytosolic Ca2+ without altering ionomycin-induced global Ca2+ levels, consistent with Ca2+ influx from the apoplast. ShGPCR1 carries multiple predicted N-myristoylation and protein kinase C/casein kinase II phosphorylation sites that are likely important for membrane anchoring and regulation. Overexpression is associated with elevated expression of the antioxidant gene CAT, implying enhanced ROS detoxification under stress. [84]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in a GPCR/G-protein-mediated Ca2+-signaling pathway involved in abiotic stress responses, where plasma membrane-localized ShGPCR1 enhances GTP-triggered Ca2+ influx and is associated with activation of multiple drought-, salinity-, and cold-responsive genes (including LEA, DYH, SCDR4, GOLS, ERF3, SOS1, ShNHX1, SsNAC23, CBF2, ScADH3, and CAT), contributing to adaptive responses to water deficit, high salinity, and low temperature. [84]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGPCR-like membrane protein with nine predicted transmembrane helices, conserved Ras GTPase-activating protein domain, ATP-/GTP-binding region, multiple N-myristoylation sites, and predicted protein kinase C and casein kinase II phosphorylation sites characteristic of plant GPCRs.
Expression PatternShGPCR1 transcript levels are significantly induced by abiotic stresses and ABA: under drought, 5.2-fold in culms and 2.4-fold in leaves; under salinity, 7.1-fold in culms and 2.2-fold in leaves; under cold, modest but significant induction (~1.2–1.37-fold) in leaves and culms; ShGPCR1 is also induced by exogenous ABA treatment.
Validation MethodFunctional validation by constitutive overexpression of a full-length ShGPCR1 CDS (EC2 allele) in transgenic sugarcane (CP72-1210 background) followed by: (1) molecular confirmation of transgene integration (PCR, Southern blot) and overexpression (RT-PCR/RT-qPCR); (2) phenotypic assays under controlled drought, salinity, and cold stress regimes in greenhouse/growth chambers; (3) measurement of stress marker gene expression by RT-PCR/RT-qPCR; (4) subcellular localization of ShGPCR1::mGFP by transient expression and confocal microscopy; and (5) cellular Ca2+ imaging with Fluo-4 AM to test GTP-induced Ca2+ responses.
Breeding Application ValueOverexpression of ShGPCR1 in sugarcane produces lines with significantly enhanced tolerance to drought, salinity, and cold stresses while maintaining normal growth and biomass under non-stress conditions. ShGPCR1-OE plants retain higher relative water content, root biomass, and total biomass during prolonged water deficit, and show reduced foliar damage and better survival under high salinity and chilling/freezing treatments. These traits indicate that ShGPCR1 is a promising target for engineering or breeding sugarcane cultivars adapted to marginal, water- and salt-limited environments, potentially improving yield stability and biomass production without evident growth penalties.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress imposed by withholding water for 40 days until soil moisture reached ~10–15%; salinity stress by weekly soil drenching with 200 mM NaCl for 2 weeks; cold/chilling stress by exposure to 4°C for up to 3 weeks followed by −5°C or −20°C for 4 h; ABA treatments (10–100 nM) on detached leaves; GTP and ionomycin treatments during Ca2+ imaging.

MKP1 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • Forward genetic screening in the Bu-5 accession identified the acquired osmotolerance-defective mutant aod13, which is hypersensitive to osmotic and osmo-shock stress but shows normal growth and ABA sensitivity under non-stress conditions. [85]
  • High-resolution mapping, whole-genome resequencing, and complementation demonstrated that AOD13 encodes MKP1 and that aod13 carries a 1-bp deletion causing a frameshift and truncation of the C-terminal MAPK-binding domain. [85]
  • Loss of MKP1 in aod13 results in elevated MPK3/6 activation under osmotic stress and reduced induction of several DREB/CBF regulon target genes, indicating that MKP1 negatively regulates MPK3/6 and influences osmotic-stress transcriptional responses. [85]
  • aod13 mutants are more tolerant to salt stress than Bu-5 WT and aod13_MKP1, with lower shoot Na+ accumulation, establishing MKP1 as a negative regulator of salt tolerance and a positive regulator of osmotic-stress tolerance. [85]
  • aod13 exhibits enhanced immune responses, including elevated PR1, PR5, PR1, PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4 expression under osmotic stress; in pad4 aod13, these defense responses remain strongly induced and osmotolerance is not restored, showing that MKP1 promotes osmotolerance by repressing PAD4-independent immunity activated downstream of MPK3/6. [85]

Core Phenotypic Effect

MKP1, encoded by AOD13, promotes acquired osmotolerance to osmotic stress in an ABA-independent manner and suppresses PAD4-independent immune activation by negatively regulating MPK3/6 activity; loss of MKP1 (aod13/mkp1) causes hypersensitivity to osmotic and osmo-shock stress but increases tolerance to salt stress by reducing Na+ accumulation in shoots. [85]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a dual-specificity phosphatase that negatively regulates MPK3/6 activation; loss of MKP1 leads to elevated MPK3/6 activity under osmotic stress, reduced induction of DREB/CBF regulon genes, and enhanced PAD4-independent activation of immune marker genes such as PR1, PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4. [85]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in MAPK (MPK3/6) signaling to integrate abiotic stress responses (DREB/CBF regulon-mediated osmotic stress tolerance) with immune signaling, suppressing PAD4-independent defense pathways under osmotic stress. [85]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDual-specificity MAP kinase phosphatase with a C-terminal MAPK-binding domain that is truncated by the frameshift mutation in aod13.
Validation MethodForward mutant isolation (aod13), high-resolution mapping and whole-genome resequencing, genomic complementation with native promoter:MKP1 (aod13_MKP1), phenotyping in acquired osmotolerance, osmo-shock, and salt-stress assays with chlorophyll and survival measurements, MAPK activity immunoblot to assess MPK3/6 activation, Na+ content measurements, and genetic interaction with CRISPR-generated pad4 aod13 double mutant combined with RT-qPCR of stress- and defense-related marker genes.
Breeding Application ValueMKP1 is a positive regulator of acquired osmotolerance but a negative regulator of salt tolerance in Arabidopsis. Loss-of-function (aod13/mkp1) enhances salt tolerance by reducing shoot Na+ accumulation but causes strong hypersensitivity to osmotic stress accompanied by autoimmune activation, whereas complementation with genomic MKP1 restores WT-like osmotolerance and salt sensitivity. MKP1 thus represents a potential breeding or engineering target to balance osmotic-stress tolerance, salinity tolerance, and immune activation, but manipulation would require careful management of the trade-off between improved salt tolerance/stronger immunity and reduced osmotolerance.
Environment / TreatmentOsmotic stress imposed by 750 mM sorbitol following 7 d acclimation at 100 mM NaCl; osmo-shock stress imposed by direct transfer to 600 mM sorbitol; salt stress imposed by 100–225 mM NaCl for Na+ accumulation and tolerance assays; flg22 peptide treatment used as a positive control for MPK3/6 activation.

PAD4 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • PAD4 was specifically knocked out in the aod13 (mkp1) background using a CRISPR/Cas9 system, generating pad4 aod13 plants carrying a 1-bp insertion in the second exon that causes a frameshift and premature stop codon. [85]
  • In contrast to previous findings in Col-0, where pad4 single mutants confer acquired osmotolerance, pad4 aod13 plants in the Bu-5 background do not acquire osmotolerance and remain osmosensitive like aod13. [85]
  • Under osmotic stress, PR1 and PAD3, as well as FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4, are strongly induced in pad4 aod13, although PR1 expression is somewhat lower than in aod13, demonstrating that defense activation in the MKP1-deficient background is largely PAD4-independent. [85]
  • These results show that MKP1 promotes osmotolerance by suppressing PAD4-independent immunity and that PAD4 is not a major determinant of osmotic-stress sensitivity in the aod13 background lacking functional ACQOS. [85]

Core Phenotypic Effect

In the Bu-5 background carrying mkp1 (aod13), loss of PAD4 (pad4 aod13) does not restore acquired osmotolerance and plants remain hypersensitive to osmotic stress; immune marker genes PR1 and PAD3, as well as FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4, are still strongly induced under osmotic stress, though PR1 induction is somewhat reduced compared with aod13. Thus, the enhanced defense activation and osmosensitive phenotype of aod13 are largely PAD4-independent. [85]

Regulatory Mechanism

CRISPR/Cas9-induced frameshift in PAD4 disrupts its coding sequence; however, under osmotic stress in the mkp1 (aod13) background, PAD4 is not required for the strong activation of PR1, PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4 or for the osmosensitive phenotype, indicating that the dominant immune activation pathway in aod13 is PAD4-independent. [85]

Pathways

Regulatory: Previously known EDS1–PAD4-dependent autoimmunity is contrasted with a PAD4-independent immune pathway; in this study, osmotic stress in mkp1 (aod13) plants activates defense responses predominantly through a PAD4-independent pathway, even when PAD4 function is eliminated. [85]

More Details
Validation MethodCRISPR/Cas9-mediated knockout of PAD4 in the aod13 background to generate pad4 aod13 lines, confirmation of a 1-bp insertion causing frameshift and premature stop codon by sequencing, phenotyping pad4 aod13 in the acquired osmotolerance assay alongside Bu-5 WT and aod13, and RT-qPCR analysis of PR1, PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4 expression under normal and osmotic-stress conditions.
Breeding Application ValueIn the Bu-5 background lacking functional ACQOS, CRISPR/Cas9-mediated knockout of PAD4 in the mkp1 mutant (pad4 aod13) does not restore acquired osmotolerance and PAD4-independent immune responses remain strongly activated under osmotic stress. These data indicate that targeting PAD4 alone is unlikely to improve osmotolerance when MKP1 function is compromised, highlighting MKP1 and downstream MAPK signaling rather than PAD4 as primary breeding or engineering targets for modulating osmotolerance in such genetic backgrounds.
Environment / TreatmentOsmotic stress imposed by 100 mM NaCl pre-treatment for 7 d followed by 750 mM sorbitol exposure in the acquired osmotolerance assay, under which defense gene induction and osmotolerance phenotypes of Bu-5 WT, aod13, and pad4 aod13 were compared.

DREB3b (Glycine max) Plant

Key Findings

  • Natural coding variation in DREB3b (DREB3b^39Del vs DREB3b^Ref) is significantly associated with differences in salt tolerance index among soybean accessions. [86]
  • The DREB3b^39Del allele, originating from wild soybean, is associated with higher salt tolerance than the DREB3b^Ref allele in the 424-accession panel. [86]
  • Overexpression of DREB3b^39Del in transgenic soybean hairy roots enhances root growth under salt stress more than overexpression of DREB3b^Ref, demonstrating a stronger positive effect on salt tolerance. [86]
  • Both DREB3b^Ref and DREB3b^39Del up-regulate positive regulators of salt tolerance (SIN1, WRKY27, WRKY54) and down-regulate the negative regulator NAC29, with DREB3b^39Del producing larger expression changes under NaCl treatment. [86]
  • DREB3b^Ref shows clear signatures of artificial selection during domestication, becoming nearly fixed in cultivated soybean, while DREB3b^39Del remains enriched in wild soybean. [86]

Core Phenotypic Effect

DREB3b functions as a positive regulator of salt tolerance in soybean, with the wild-derived DREB3b^39Del allele enhancing salt tolerance more strongly than the domesticated DREB3b^Ref allele; overexpression of DREB3b^39Del in soybean hairy roots significantly improves root growth under salt stress and more strongly induces positive regulators and represses a negative regulator of salt tolerance. [86]

Regulatory Mechanism

As a DREB/AP2/ERF transcription factor, DREB3b modulates transcription of downstream salt-tolerance regulators, with the DREB3b^39Del allele showing stronger activation of positive regulators (SIN1, WRKY27, WRKY54) and stronger repression of the negative regulator NAC29 compared with DREB3b^Ref under NaCl stress, thereby enhancing salt tolerance. [86]

Pathways

Regulatory: Salt stress–responsive transcriptional regulatory pathway in which DREB3b, a DREB/AP2/ERF transcription factor, up-regulates positive regulators of salt tolerance (SIN1, WRKY27, WRKY54) and down-regulates the negative regulator NAC29, thereby enhancing salt tolerance; the DREB3b^39Del allele has a stronger regulatory impact on these targets under NaCl treatment than DREB3b^Ref. [86]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDREB subfamily of the APETALA2/ethylene-responsive factor (AP2/ERF) transcription factor superfamily.
Expression PatternDREB3b transcript levels are up-regulated under 200 mM NaCl treatment in W82 seedlings, consistent between RNA-seq (W82-Water vs W82-NaCl) and qRT-PCR validation.
Validation MethodOverexpression of DREB3b^Ref and DREB3b^39Del coding sequences in transgenic soybean hairy roots via Agrobacterium rhizogenes–mediated transformation, followed by phenotypic analysis of root growth under 0 and 100 mM NaCl and qRT-PCR measurement of downstream salt-tolerance genes; combined with natural-variation association analysis of DREB3b haplotypes and salt tolerance index in 424 soybean accessions.
Breeding Application ValueDREB3b^39Del is a favorable natural allele from wild soybean that enhances salt tolerance and can be introgressed into cultivated soybean varieties. The functional InDel marker (100 bp for DREB3b^39Del, 106 bp for DREB3b^Ref) enables rapid genotyping and marker-assisted selection for the salt-tolerance allele in breeding programs. Understanding that DREB3b^Ref underwent strong artificial selection and is linked to flowering time, plant height, and seed size helps breeders design strategies to introduce DREB3b^39Del while maintaining desirable agronomic traits.
Environment / TreatmentSalt stress imposed as 200 mM NaCl for 15 days on soybean seedlings for RNA-seq, expression, and salt tolerance index measurements, and 100 mM NaCl treatment for soybean hairy root growth and downstream gene expression assays.

DREB3a (Glycine max) Plant

Key Findings

  • DREB3a (Glyma.01G147600) is an up-regulated DREB transcription factor under salt stress in W82 seedlings. [86]
  • Natural coding variation in DREB3a, notably the DREB3a^167Ins allele (15-bp insertion adding 5 amino acids), is significantly associated with higher salt tolerance index compared with the DREB3a^Ref allele in a 424-accession panel. [86]
  • Haplotype analysis indicates that DREB3a haplotypes derive from an ancestral legume-like Hap. 2, but population genetic analyses do not show strong selective sweep around the DREB3a locus during domestication. [86]
  • DREB3a is thus identified as an excellent candidate gene responding to salt stress and contributing to natural variation in salt tolerance, although no transgenic functional test was performed in this study. [86]

Core Phenotypic Effect

DREB3a contributes to natural variation in salt tolerance in soybean, with the DREB3a^167Ins coding allele associated with higher salt tolerance index than the DREB3a^Ref allele, and DREB3a transcripts being induced by salt stress. [86]

Regulatory Mechanism

DREB3a is a salt-induced DREB/AP2/ERF transcription factor likely acting at the transcriptional level to influence salt tolerance, as inferred from its induction under NaCl and association of its coding variant DREB3a^167Ins with increased salt tolerance index, although direct downstream targets were not examined. [86]

Pathways

Regulatory: Salt stress–responsive DREB/AP2/ERF transcription factor pathway; DREB3a is induced by salt stress and is implicated as a transcriptional regulator contributing to salt tolerance, although downstream targets were not characterized in this study. [86]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDREB subfamily of the APETALA2/ethylene-responsive factor (AP2/ERF) transcription factor superfamily.
Expression PatternDREB3a expression is up-regulated under 200 mM NaCl in W82 seedlings, as shown by qRT-PCR, consistent with RNA-seq data indicating induction by salt stress.
Validation MethodNatural-variation haplotype and association analysis linking DREB3a coding variants, especially DREB3a^167Ins, to salt tolerance index in a 424-accession panel, combined with qRT-PCR validation of DREB3a induction under 200 mM NaCl.
Breeding Application ValueBecause the DREB3a^167Ins allele is associated with higher salt tolerance index and DREB3a is salt inducible, this locus represents a potential target for marker development and selection of salt-tolerant soybean lines, complementing DREB3b-based strategies once further functional validation is available.
Environment / Treatment200 mM NaCl treatment for 15 days applied to W82 seedlings for RNA-seq and qRT-PCR to assess DREB3a expression under salt stress, and NaCl treatment used for phenotypic evaluation of salt tolerance index in the 424-accession panel.

OsPUB33 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsPUB33 expression is induced by M. grisea infection and by SA, JA, and ACC, and is also rapidly upregulated by drought stress, but not by heat or salt. [87]
  • BMV-mediated silencing of OsPUB33 enhances resistance to M. oryzae, yielding smaller lesions and less fungal growth than in control plants. [87]
  • After blast infection, OsPUB33-silenced plants accumulate less ROS and H2O2, have reduced SOD but elevated CAT activity, and show increased expression of SA- and JA/ET-responsive defense genes and OsWRKY45. [87]
  • Under drought, OsPUB33-silenced plants show increased tolerance, with higher survival, altered water loss, and significantly increased proline and sugar contents and drought-responsive gene expression compared with controls. [87]
  • These data demonstrate that OsPUB33 acts as a negative regulator of both rice blast resistance and drought tolerance, likely via modulation of ROS homeostasis and stress-responsive transcriptional networks. [87]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Silencing OsPUB33 increases resistance to Magnaporthe grisea/M. oryzae and enhances tolerance to drought stress. After blast inoculation, OsPUB33-silenced plants show smaller lesions, less fungal growth, lower ROS and H2O2 accumulation, decreased SOD but increased CAT activity, and upregulated defense-related genes. Under drought, OsPUB33-silenced plants exhibit higher survival, altered water loss, increased proline and sugar contents, and elevated expression of drought-responsive genes, indicating OsPUB33 is a negative regulator of blast resistance and drought tolerance. [87]

Regulatory Mechanism

Under blast infection, OsPUB33 silencing reduces ROS and H2O2 accumulation by shifting SOD and CAT activities and enhances expression of SA- and JA/ET-responsive defense genes. Under drought, OsPUB33 silencing increases proline and sugar accumulation and upregulates drought-responsive genes, indicating that OsPUB33 modulates ROS homeostasis and stress-responsive gene expression to negatively regulate blast and drought tolerance. [87]

Pathways

Regulatory: Involved in biotic stress pathways linked to SA and JA/ET signaling during blast infection and in drought-response pathways associated with proline and sugar accumulation and drought-responsive genes; likely functions in an ABA-independent drought pathway. [87]

More Details
Expression PatternOsPUB33 expression is strongly induced by M. grisea infection and by SA, JA, and ACC treatments, and its transcript level is also rapidly induced by drought stress; its expression is not significantly altered by heat or salt at the tested time points and is not reported as ABA-responsive.
Validation MethodBMV-based virus-induced gene silencing of OsPUB33 in IR64 followed by M. oryzae inoculation for disease assays and withholding water for drought stress assays, combined with physiological measurements (ROS, H2O2, SOD, CAT, proline, sugar, survival, water loss) and qRT-PCR of defense- and drought-responsive genes.
Breeding Application ValueOsPUB33 negatively regulates both resistance to rice blast and tolerance to drought stress. Its silencing enhances blast resistance and drought tolerance, suggesting that reduced OsPUB33 activity, through mutation or targeted downregulation, could be valuable for breeding rice cultivars with improved disease resistance and drought resilience.
Environment / TreatmentInoculation with Magnaporthe grisea/M. oryzae; treatments with SA, JA, and ACC; drought stress imposed by withholding watering of potted plants.

OsATL69 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsATL69 expression is rapidly and strongly induced by drought stress and is also upregulated by ABA treatment. [87]
  • BMV-mediated silencing of OsATL69 decreases drought tolerance, with lower survival rates than controls after a drought and recovery regime. [87]
  • Under drought stress, OsATL69-silenced plants show significantly reduced proline and sugar contents and lower expression of drought-responsive genes compared with controls. [87]
  • These results indicate that OsATL69 positively regulates drought tolerance, likely through ABA-associated pathways that enhance osmoprotectant accumulation and drought-responsive gene expression. [87]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Silencing OsATL69 by BMV-based VIGS decreases tolerance to drought stress, leading to lower survival rate, reduced proline and sugar contents, and downregulated expression of drought-responsive genes compared with controls, indicating that OsATL69 is a positive regulator of drought tolerance. [87]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsATL69 contributes to drought tolerance by promoting accumulation of osmoprotectants (proline and soluble sugars) and maintaining expression of drought-responsive genes; silencing the gene reduces these responses and compromises drought tolerance. [87]

Pathways

Regulatory: Associated with ABA-dependent drought-response pathways, as its expression is induced by both drought stress and ABA, and it regulates drought-responsive gene expression. [87]

More Details
Expression PatternOsATL69 expression is strongly induced by drought stress within 2 h of treatment and is significantly upregulated by ABA; its expression shows no major changes under cold, heat, or salt stresses at the tested time points.
Validation MethodBMV-based virus-induced gene silencing of OsATL69 in IR64 followed by drought treatment by withholding water, with measurement of survival rate, water loss, proline and sugar contents, and qRT-PCR of drought-responsive genes.
Breeding Application ValueOsATL69 positively regulates drought tolerance; silencing it reduces survival, compatible solute accumulation, and drought-responsive gene expression. Maintaining or enhancing OsATL69 activity could be beneficial for breeding drought-tolerant rice cultivars, particularly in environments with frequent water deficit.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress applied by withholding watering of 4-week-old potted plants; ABA treatment in hydroponically grown seedlings.

OsATL32 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsATL32 expression is strongly induced by cold stress and is also stimulated by ABA treatment. [87]
  • BMV-mediated silencing of OsATL32 markedly decreases cold tolerance, with survival dropping to about 20% compared with over 80% in control plants after a 4°C treatment and recovery. [87]
  • OsATL32-silenced seedlings exhibit higher MDA content and electrolyte leakage and lower chlorophyll content under cold stress, indicating increased membrane and photosynthetic damage. [87]
  • Expression of multiple cold-responsive genes is significantly reduced in OsATL32-silenced plants compared with controls under cold conditions. [87]
  • These results demonstrate that OsATL32 positively regulates cold tolerance in rice, likely via ABA-linked pathways that sustain cold-responsive gene expression and limit cellular damage. [87]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Silencing OsATL32 via BMV-based VIGS reduces tolerance to cold stress, resulting in much lower survival, higher MDA content and electrolyte leakage, reduced chlorophyll content, and downregulated expression of cold-responsive genes compared with controls, indicating that OsATL32 is a positive regulator of cold tolerance. [87]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsATL32 promotes cold tolerance by limiting membrane damage and maintaining photosynthetic capacity and cold-responsive gene expression; silencing the gene increases lipid peroxidation (MDA), electrolyte leakage, and chlorophyll loss and reduces expression of cold-induced genes. [87]

Pathways

Regulatory: Associated with ABA-dependent cold-response pathways, as OsATL32 is induced by both cold and ABA and regulates cold-responsive gene expression. [87]

More Details
Expression PatternOsATL32 expression is strongly induced by cold stress, with a marked increase 12 h after treatment, and is also significantly upregulated by ABA; it is not reported as responsive to drought, heat, or salt at the tested time points.
Validation MethodBMV-based virus-induced gene silencing of OsATL32 in IR64 followed by cold treatment at 4°C, then recovery, with assessment of survival rate, MDA content, electrolyte leakage, chlorophyll content, and qRT-PCR of cold-responsive genes.
Breeding Application ValueOsATL32 positively regulates cold tolerance in rice. Maintaining or enhancing OsATL32 activity could be used to improve seedling survival and reduce cellular damage under low temperature, making it a candidate gene for breeding cold-tolerant rice cultivars grown in cooler environments.
Environment / TreatmentCold stress treatment at 4°C for 2 days followed by recovery; ABA treatment in hydroponic seedlings.

ZTL (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • Loss-of-function ztl mutants have reduced ABA-induced stomatal closure, higher stomatal conductance and increased water loss compared with WT, without changes in stomatal density. [88]
  • Overexpression of ZTL (p35S::HA::ZTL) in the ztl-3 background rescues the elevated water loss phenotype, confirming ZTL’s role in stomatal regulation. [88]
  • ZTL is required to sustain ABA-induced expression of key ABA-signalling (e.g. OST1, ABI5, ABF4) and ABA-responsive (RD29A, RAB18) genes. [88]
  • ZTL physically interacts with OST1 but does not promote OST1 degradation, and ZTL acts synergistically with OST1 and antagonistically with PRR5 to integrate circadian clock outputs with ABA signalling to control stomatal aperture. [88]

Core Phenotypic Effect

ZTL positively regulates ABA-induced stomatal closure and water conservation; loss-of-function ztl mutants show reduced ABA sensitivity of stomata, increased stomatal conductance and leaf water loss, and this phenotype is rescued by ZTL overexpression; ZTL is required to sustain ABA-induced expression of key ABA-signalling genes and acts synergistically with OST1. [88]

Regulatory Mechanism

ZTL is a blue light photoreceptor and F-box protein that functions in an E3 ubiquitin ligase complex; it post-translationally regulates clock proteins such as PRR5 and TOC1 by promoting their proteasomal degradation and physically interacts with OST1 without affecting OST1 protein stability; ZTL is also required at the transcriptional level to sustain ABA-induced expression of several ABA-signalling and ABA-responsive genes. [88]

Pathways

Regulatory: Circadian clock signalling pathway and ABA signalling pathway controlling guard-cell ion transport and stomatal closure. [88]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainF-box clock protein and blue light photoreceptor with an N-terminal flavin-binding LOV (Light, Oxygen or Voltage) domain and Kelch repeats, acting in an E3-ligase complex.
Expression PatternZTL protein abundance shows a daily rhythm with high levels in the late afternoon/evening (around ZT 8–9), and ZTL function is analysed in leaves/guard cells at this time when ABA sensitivity is high; ZTL is required to sustain ABA-induced transcription of several ABA-signalling genes (e.g. OST1, ABI5, ABF4, RD29A, RAB18).
Validation MethodReverse-genetic analysis of loss-of-function ztl alleles (ztl-1, ztl-3, ztl-21), double and triple mutants with ost1-3 and prr5-1; physiological assays of stomatal aperture, stomatal conductance and detached-leaf water loss; radicle emergence assays; RT-qPCR of ABA-signalling and ABA-responsive genes; complementation of ztl-3 with p35S::HA::ZTL; co-immunoprecipitation of ZTL with OST1 in Arabidopsis protoplasts; protein stability assays in protoplasts.
Breeding Application ValueBecause ZTL is required for efficient ABA-induced stomatal closure, limits water loss without altering stomatal density, and its role is conserved in Populus, modulating ZTL activity offers a potential strategy to improve water use efficiency and drought tolerance by aligning stomatal behaviour with the circadian clock in crops and trees.
Environment / TreatmentExogenous ABA application (10 μM) for stomatal assays and gene expression; detached-leaf desiccation as a proxy for drought; growth under controlled long-day light:dark and temperature cycles; CO2-free aeration for stomatal aperture measurements.

OST1 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • ost1-3 mutants have elevated stomatal conductance and detached-leaf water loss compared with WT, confirming OST1 as a key positive regulator of ABA-induced stomatal closure. [88]
  • The stomatal and water loss phenotypes of ztl-3 resemble those of ost1-3, and the ztl-3 ost1-3 double mutant shows an even stronger water loss phenotype, indicating synergistic action of OST1 and ZTL. [88]
  • OST1 physically interacts with both ZTL and PRR5 in plant cells, integrating ABA kinase signalling with circadian clock components. [88]
  • OST1 protein stability is not detectably affected by ZTL or proteasome inhibition in protoplasts, suggesting that ZTL regulates ABA signalling primarily via mechanisms other than OST1 degradation. [88]

Core Phenotypic Effect

OST1 acts as a central positive regulator of ABA-induced stomatal closure; ost1-3 mutants show reduced stomatal closure in response to ABA, elevated stomatal conductance, and higher detached-leaf water loss compared with WT; in combination with ztl-3, loss of OST1 produces an even stronger water loss phenotype, indicating that OST1 and ZTL act synergistically to control stomatal aperture. [88]

Regulatory Mechanism

Serine/threonine SnRK2 protein kinase (SnRK2.6/SRK2E) that is released from PP2C inhibition upon ABA perception and autophosphorylation; phosphorylates targets to inhibit K+ influx and activate S-type anion channels to drive guard-cell depolarisation and stomatal closure; interacts physically with ZTL and PRR5, but its protein stability is not altered by ZTL in protoplast assays. [88]

Pathways

Regulatory: Core ABA signal transduction pathway controlling guard-cell ion channels and stomatal closure. [88]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSnRK2 family serine/threonine protein kinase (SnRK2.6/SRK2E).
Expression PatternOST1 transcript levels are induced by ABA treatment in WT Arabidopsis, but this ABA-induced expression is significantly reduced in the ztl-3 background.
Validation MethodUse of the strong loss-of-function ost1-3 T-DNA mutant; physiological measurements of stomatal conductance and detached-leaf water loss in ost1-3 and in double/triple mutants with ztl-3 and prr5-1; comparison of ABA-induced stomatal closure phenotypes; co-immunoprecipitation of OST1 with ZTL and PRR5 in Arabidopsis protoplasts; protein stability assays of OST1 with or without ZTL and proteasome inhibition.
Breeding Application ValueGiven OST1’s central role in ABA-induced stomatal closure and its synergistic interaction with the clock protein ZTL, manipulating OST1 activity or its upstream clock regulators has potential to enhance drought tolerance and water use efficiency in crops by strengthening ABA-responsive stomatal closure while maintaining coordination with daily environmental cycles.
Environment / TreatmentExogenous ABA (10 μM) for stomatal assays and gene expression; desiccation of detached leaves to mimic drought; controlled long-day light and temperature regimes.

PRR5 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • PRR5 loss-of-function alleles alter stomatal ABA responses: prr5-11 stomata close more readily in response to ABA than WT, whereas prr5-1 stomata are more open than WT without ABA. [88]
  • PRR5 physically interacts with OST1 in plant cells. [88]
  • In water loss assays, prr5-1 single mutants resemble WT, but removing PRR5 in a ztl-3 ost1-3 background partially suppresses the severe water loss phenotype of the double mutant. [88]
  • Genetic and biochemical evidence indicates that PRR5 acts downstream of ZTL but upstream of OST1 as an inhibitor of ABA-signalling, integrating circadian clock outputs into stomatal regulation. [88]

Core Phenotypic Effect

PRR5 modulates ABA-induced stomatal closure and water loss; prr5-11 mutants show enhanced ABA-induced stomatal closure compared with WT, while prr5-1 mutants have more open stomata than WT in the absence of ABA; loss of PRR5 partially suppresses the extreme water loss phenotype of the ztl-3 ost1-3 double mutant, indicating that PRR5 inhibits ABA-signalling downstream of ZTL but upstream of OST1. [88]

Regulatory Mechanism

PRR5 is a pseudo-response regulator clock protein whose abundance is controlled post-translationally by ZTL; in this study PRR5 physically interacts with OST1, and genetic data indicate that PRR5 inhibits ABA-induced stomatal closure, acting downstream of ZTL but upstream of OST1. [88]

Pathways

Regulatory: Circadian clock signalling pathway intersecting with ABA signalling that controls stomatal movements. [88]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainPSEUDO-RESPONSE REGULATOR (PRR) family circadian clock protein.
Expression PatternPRR5 is an evening-expressed circadian clock protein; PRR proteins as a family are expressed sequentially from dawn to dusk.
Validation MethodAnalysis of prr5-11 and prr5-1 loss-of-function mutants in stomatal aperture assays under ABA treatment with timing adjusted for circadian period; detached-leaf water loss assays in prr5-1 single mutants and in double/triple mutants with ztl-3 and ost1-3; co-immunoprecipitation demonstrating physical interaction between PRR5 and OST1 in protoplasts.
Breeding Application ValueBecause PRR5 modulates ABA-induced stomatal closure and interacts with OST1, altering PRR5 activity provides a means to fine-tune the balance between water loss and stomatal closure in coordination with the circadian clock, potentially allowing breeders to adjust drought responses and growth–stress trade-offs in crops.
Environment / TreatmentExogenous ABA treatment with timing adjusted to circadian period; desiccation of detached leaves; controlled long-day light and temperature regimes.

PttZTL1,2 (Populus tremula × Populus tremuloides) Plant

Key Findings

  • PttZTL1,2 expression is rhythmic in WT Populus leaves and is significantly reduced in PttZTL1,2 RNAi lines. [88]
  • Downregulation of PttZTL1,2 lengthens the free-running circadian period in Populus leaves while maintaining strong rhythmicity. [88]
  • PttZTL1,2 RNAi lines show reduced ABA-induced stomatal closure, higher stomatal conductance and increased detached-leaf water loss compared with WT, largely without major changes in stomatal density. [88]
  • These phenotypes mirror those of Arabidopsis ztl mutants, demonstrating that ZTL-dependent regulation of circadian clock function and stomatal ABA responses is conserved between Arabidopsis and Populus. [88]

Core Phenotypic Effect

RNAi-mediated downregulation of PttZTL1 and PttZTL2 lengthens the free-running circadian period in Populus leaves and reduces ABA-induced stomatal closure, leading to increased stomatal conductance and higher detached-leaf water loss, largely without changes in stomatal density; these phenotypes mirror Arabidopsis ztl mutants, indicating conserved ZTL function in Populus. [88]

Regulatory Mechanism

PttZTL1 and PttZTL2 act as Populus ZTL homologues required to maintain normal circadian period and to promote ABA-induced stomatal closure; reduced PttZTL1,2 expression slows the circadian clock and decreases stomatal responsiveness to ABA, increasing stomatal conductance and water loss. [88]

Pathways

Regulatory: Circadian clock pathway and ABA signalling pathway controlling stomatal movements and water loss in Populus. [88]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainZTL homologues (F-box blue light photoreceptor with LOV domain) in Populus.
Expression PatternPttZTL expression is rhythmic in WT Populus leaves and is downregulated to approximately 40% of WT levels at ZT 8–9 in PttZTL1,2 RNAi lines 5 and 7.
Validation MethodGeneration of transgenic Populus tremula × P. tremuloides cv. T89 PttZTL1,2 RNAi lines; RT-qPCR to confirm downregulation of PttZTL1 and PttZTL2 transcripts; delayed fluorescence imaging to measure circadian period; stomatal aperture assays under ABA treatment; stomatal conductance measurements; detached-leaf water loss assays; stomatal density measurements.
Breeding Application ValueBecause reduced PttZTL1,2 expression lengthens circadian period and increases stomatal conductance and water loss in Populus, while ZTL function is conserved with Arabidopsis, manipulating ZTL activity in trees could be exploited to adjust stomatal behaviour, improve water use efficiency and enhance drought tolerance in forestry breeding programs.
Environment / TreatmentGrowth under long-day photoperiods (LD 18:6 then 15:9) at 18–20°C; exogenous ABA treatment in stomatal assays; detached-leaf desiccation to mimic drought.

GH3.1 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Loss of GH3.1 together with the other group II GH3s in the gh3oct octuple knockout reduces the plant’s capacity to inactivate IAA (no detectable IAA-Asp, reduced IAA-Glu, oxIAA and oxIAA-glc), leading to high-auxin developmental phenotypes and conferring increased tolerance to salinity and water-deficit stress without major penalties in primary root growth [89]

Regulatory Mechanism

Group II GH3-mediated conjugation of IAA to amino acids (especially IAA-Asp and IAA-Glu) provides a major IAA inactivation route; in gh3oct, loss of this pathway abolishes IAA-Asp formation and diminishes IAA-Glu and downstream oxIAA and oxIAA-glc, indicating that a substantial fraction of oxIAA is produced via GH3-dependent conjugates; altered ABA and SA levels under salinity in gh3oct suggest indirect modulation of stress hormone homeostasis [89]

Pathways

Regulatory: Auxin (IAA) inactivation pathway via IAA–amino acid conjugation; contributes to hormone-regulated responses to salinity and water-deficit stress, in part through crosstalk with ABA and SA signaling [89]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGroup II GRETCHEN HAGEN 3 (GH3) family acyl acid amido synthetase
Expression PatternIn seedling roots, GH3 group II transcripts (including GH3.1) are upregulated 6 h after transfer to 75 or 150 mM NaCl; GH3.4 and GH3.9 transcripts are not detected in roots under these conditions
Validation MethodT-DNA/transposon insertion knockout (gh3oct octuple mutant including GH3.1); RT-qPCR confirmation of loss of full-length GH3 transcripts; whole-genome resequencing to verify GH3 insertions; phenotypic assays under control and stress conditions; targeted hormone quantification linking loss of GH3 activity to changes in IAA and its metabolites, ABA and SA
Breeding Application ValueResults show that complete inactivation of group II GH3s, including GH3.1, enhances tolerance to salinity and water-deficit stress while maintaining overall viability and a robust, highly branched root system. This supports using GH3.1 and its paralogues as gene-editing targets, for example via stress-inducible or tissue-specific knockdown of group II GH3 activity, to engineer crops with improved salt and drought tolerance and modified root architecture without severely compromising growth.
Environment / TreatmentSalinity stress imposed by 50–150 mM NaCl; osmotic stress via sorbitol and mannitol; reduced water potential via PEG8000 perfusion of MS plates; soil water-deficit by withholding irrigation; co-treatment with YUCCA-pathway IAA biosynthesis inhibitors yucasin (20 μM) and PPBo (1 μM) to reduce endogenous IAA; standard MS growth conditions as control

GH3.2 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Elimination of GH3.2 together with the other group II GH3s in gh3oct diminishes IAA inactivation capacity (loss of IAA-Asp, reduced IAA-Glu and oxIAA/oxIAA-glc) and leads to high-auxin phenotypes and enhanced tolerance to salinity and water deficit; in gh3.2 single mutants, IAA concentrations are increased in shoots but decreased in roots, and oxIAA is reduced in shoots, indicating a contribution of GH3.2 to auxin metabolism even when other GH3s are present [89]

Regulatory Mechanism

GH3.2 participates redundantly in the GH3-mediated IAA-amide conjugation pathway, which modulates IAA, IAA-Asp, IAA-Glu and oxIAA levels; combined loss in gh3oct alters auxin and ABA/SA homeostasis associated with stress tolerance [89]

Pathways

Regulatory: Auxin inactivation pathway via IAA–amino acid conjugation, contributing to salinity and water-deficit stress responses and hormone homeostasis [89]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGroup II GRETCHEN HAGEN 3 (GH3) acyl acid amido synthetase
Expression PatternGroup II GH3 genes expressed in roots, including GH3.2, are transcriptionally induced 6 h after transfer to 75 or 150 mM NaCl; GH3.4 and GH3.9 showed no detectable expression in roots under these conditions
Validation MethodUse of gh3oct octuple knockout (including GH3.2) plus gh3.2 single mutant; RT-qPCR of GH3 transcripts; hormone profiling (IAA and metabolites) in single and octuple mutants; phenotypic assays under salinity and drought stress
Breeding Application ValueBecause GH3.2 contributes redundantly to a GH3 network whose complete inactivation improves salinity and drought tolerance, GH3.2 is part of a promising multi-gene target set for gene-editing strategies aiming to attenuate auxin conjugation specifically under water-stress conditions to enhance stress resilience in crops.
Environment / TreatmentNaCl salinity (0, 50, 75, 150 mM); osmotic stress by osmolytes; PEG8000-induced reduced water potential; soil drought; co-treatment with yucasin and PPBo to modulate endogenous IAA

GH3.3 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Loss of GH3.3 as part of the gh3oct octuple knockout contributes to the complete absence of IAA-Asp, reduced IAA-Glu and oxIAA/oxIAA-glc, and the resulting high-auxin developmental phenotypes and enhanced tolerance to salinity and water deficit; in gh3.3 single mutants, IAA-Asp is slightly decreased in shoots, indicating a measurable but redundant role in forming IAA-Asp conjugates in vivo [89]

Regulatory Mechanism

GH3.3 participates in the GH3-dependent IAA-amide conjugation route; its combined loss with other group II GH3s shifts auxin catabolism away from amide conjugates and is associated with altered oxIAA, ABA and SA levels and changes in salt and drought tolerance [89]

Pathways

Regulatory: Auxin inactivation via IAA–amino acid conjugation, affecting developmental and salinity/drought responses [89]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGroup II GRETCHEN HAGEN 3 (GH3) acyl acid amido synthetase
Expression PatternGroup II GH3 transcripts in roots, including GH3.3, are induced by NaCl within 6 h, consistent with salt responsiveness of GH3-mediated auxin conjugation
Validation MethodUse of gh3oct knockout including GH3.3 plus gh3.3 single mutant metabolite profiling; RT-qPCR; targeted auxin metabolite quantification; developmental and stress tolerance assays
Breeding Application ValueBecause elimination of the full set of group II GH3s including GH3.3 improves water-stress tolerance, GH3.3 forms part of a GH3 gene module that can be collectively targeted by genome editing or inducible suppression to enhance salinity and drought tolerance in crops.
Environment / TreatmentNaCl stress (up to 150 mM), PEG8000-induced water deficit, soil drought, and osmolyte treatments, under which gh3oct plants (lacking GH3.3) show increased tolerance

GH3.4 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Inactivation of GH3.4 together with the other group II GH3s in gh3oct contributes to the loss of IAA-Asp and reduced IAA-Glu and oxIAA/oxIAA-glc, leading to elevated effective auxin signaling, high-auxin developmental phenotypes and improved tolerance to salinity and water deficit; GH3.4 itself is not detectably expressed in roots under the NaCl conditions tested, suggesting tissue- or condition-specific contribution within the redundant GH3 network [89]

Regulatory Mechanism

GH3.4 forms part of the GH3-mediated IAA-amide conjugation machinery whose absence in gh3oct shifts auxin catabolism away from amide conjugates toward other routes, accompanied by altered ABA and SA levels and enhanced salt and drought tolerance [89]

Pathways

Regulatory: Auxin inactivation pathway via GH3-mediated IAA–amino acid conjugation, functioning redundantly with other GH3s in development and stress responses [89]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGroup II GRETCHEN HAGEN 3 (GH3) acyl acid amido synthetase
Expression PatternIn 10-d-old seedling roots exposed to 0, 75 or 150 mM NaCl for 6 h, GH3.4 transcript was not detected, whereas other group II GH3s were upregulated
Validation MethodUse of gh3oct knockout including GH3.4; RT-qPCR showing no GH3.4 expression in roots under NaCl; hormone quantification and phenotypic assays to link combined GH3.4 loss to auxin metabolism and stress tolerance
Breeding Application ValueAs part of the redundant GH3 module, GH3.4 does not need to be individually targeted, but its inclusion in multi-gene knockout or silencing strategies that remove group II GH3 activity can contribute to improved salt and drought tolerance and altered root architecture in crops.
Environment / TreatmentNaCl stress, PEG8000-induced water deficit and soil drought conditions under which gh3oct plants (lacking GH3.4) outperform Col-0; NaCl up to 150 mM is also used to test GH3.4 root expression, where it remains undetectable

GH3.5 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

In gh3oct, loss of GH3.5 together with other group II GH3s enhances salt and water-deficit tolerance through reduced IAA inactivation and altered hormone profiles; uniquely, gh3.5 single mutants show salt-tolerant root growth, but this tolerance is not suppressed by decreasing endogenous IAA with YUCCA inhibitors, implicating GH3.5 in IAA-independent, possibly SA/JA-related, pathways of salinity tolerance [89]

Regulatory Mechanism

GH3.5 participates in GH3-mediated IAA conjugation but also modulates responses to salinity via IAA-independent mechanisms, as indicated by persistence of salt tolerance in gh3.5 when IAA biosynthesis is inhibited, suggesting regulation through other hormone pathways (e.g. SA or JA) consistent with prior reports cited in the paper [89]

Pathways

Regulatory: Auxin inactivation and broader hormone conjugation pathway (GH3.5 affects auxin and, from previous studies cited here, also JA and SA), impacting salt stress responses [89]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGroup II GRETCHEN HAGEN 3 (GH3) acyl acid amido synthetase
Expression PatternGH3 group II genes expressed in roots, including GH3.5, are induced by NaCl in transcript analyses, consistent with salt-responsiveness of GH3.5
Validation MethodPhenotypic comparison of gh3.5 single mutant and gh3oct under NaCl and PEG stress; co-treatment of gh3.5 with IAA biosynthesis inhibitors to test IAA dependence; hormone profiling and developmental phenotyping in the gh3oct background
Breeding Application ValueGH3.5 is a particularly attractive target because its loss alone can improve salt tolerance of root growth without the full suite of high-auxin phenotypes seen in gh3oct. Combining GH3.5 manipulation with broader group II GH3 editing could be used to fine-tune crop salt and drought tolerance via both auxin-dependent and auxin-independent hormone pathways.
Environment / TreatmentNaCl treatments (for gh3.5 single mutant salt assays and for gh3oct), PEG8000-mediated reduced water potential (where gh3.5 does not differ from Col-0), soil drought, and manipulations of endogenous IAA by yucasin and PPBo to test IAA-dependence of salt tolerance

GH3.6 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Combined loss of GH3.6 with other group II GH3s in gh3oct abolishes IAA-Asp production, reduces IAA-Glu and oxIAA/oxIAA-glc, and results in high-auxin developmental phenotypes and increased tolerance to salinity and water deficit; gh3.6 single mutants show reduced IAA-Asp in roots, indicating a specific but redundant role in IAA-Asp formation [89]

Regulatory Mechanism

GH3.6 participates in GH3-mediated IAA-conjugation; its loss within gh3oct shifts auxin catabolism away from amide conjugates and is associated with altered ABA and SA accumulation and increased salt and drought tolerance [89]

Pathways

Regulatory: Auxin inactivation via GH3-mediated IAA–amino acid conjugation contributing to salinity and drought stress responses [89]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGroup II GRETCHEN HAGEN 3 (GH3) acyl acid amido synthetase
Expression PatternAs part of the root-expressed group II GH3 set, GH3.6 transcript is induced by NaCl in seedling roots, based on RT-qPCR analysis of group II GH3s
Validation MethodUse of gh3oct (including GH3.6 lesion) plus gh3.6 single mutant for IAA metabolite profiling; RT-qPCR; hormone quantification and stress phenotyping to connect GH3.6-containing GH3 network with auxin and stress responses
Breeding Application ValueGH3.6 is part of the GH3 network whose full inactivation improves salinity and drought tolerance, suggesting that multiplex editing strategies targeting GH3.6 together with other group II GH3s could be used to enhance abiotic stress resilience in crops.
Environment / TreatmentNaCl stress regimes, PEG8000-induced water-deficit and soil drought under which gh3oct (lacking GH3.6) outperforms Col-0

GH3.9 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Although GH3.9 is not detectably expressed in roots under NaCl treatment, its disruption along with other group II GH3s in gh3oct contributes to loss of IAA-Asp, reduced IAA-Glu and oxIAA/oxIAA-glc and the resulting high-auxin phenotypes and improved salinity and water-deficit tolerance; marginal GH3.9 expression in reproductive tissues suggests a minor residual role in those organs in the mutant [89]

Regulatory Mechanism

GH3.9 participates in GH3-mediated auxin conjugation but appears to have limited root expression under NaCl in this study; its genetic loss alongside other GH3s in gh3oct contributes to the overall shift in auxin catabolism and hormone balance that enhances stress tolerance [89]

Pathways

Regulatory: Auxin inactivation pathway via GH3-mediated IAA conjugation, acting redundantly with other group II GH3s in development and stress responses [89]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGroup II GRETCHEN HAGEN 3 (GH3) acyl acid amido synthetase
Expression PatternIn NaCl-treated roots, GH3.9 transcript was not detected by RT-qPCR at 0, 75 or 150 mM NaCl, unlike several other group II GH3s; marginal GH3.9 expression remained in reproductive tissues of gh3oct plants
Validation MethodRT-qPCR confirming lack of GH3.9 expression in roots under NaCl; detection of marginal GH3.9 expression in reproductive tissues of gh3oct; hormone and phenotypic assays in gh3oct to relate combined group II GH3 loss to auxin metabolism and stress tolerance
Breeding Application ValueGH3.9 is part of the group II GH3 gene set whose collective inactivation enhances salt and drought tolerance, making it one component of a multi-gene editing strategy to reduce auxin conjugation during water stress.
Environment / TreatmentNaCl treatment for both expression profiling and stress assays; PEG8000-induced water deficit and soil drought conditions for assessing gh3oct performance

GH3.17 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Loss of GH3.17 together with the other group II GH3s in gh3oct contributes to the abolition of IAA-Asp, reduction in IAA-Glu and oxIAA/oxIAA-glc, and the resulting high-auxin phenotypes and enhanced tolerance to salinity and water deficit; the persistence of detectable IAA-Glu in gh3oct indicates that auxin–Glu conjugation can still occur without GH3.17 and other group II GH3s, revealing additional GH3 or GH3-like enzymes acting in this pathway [89]

Regulatory Mechanism

GH3.17 participates in GH3-mediated IAA-amide formation, especially IAA–Glu based on prior work; its loss along with other GH3s in gh3oct shifts auxin metabolism and reduces oxIAA and oxIAA-glc levels, supporting a model in which a substantial fraction of oxIAA arises from GH3-dependent conjugates [89]

Pathways

Regulatory: Auxin inactivation via IAA–amino acid conjugation, influencing auxin homeostasis and stress responses; GH3.17 is highlighted in the discussion as a key contributor to IAA-Glu formation in prior work, and this study shows that IAA-Glu is still detectable in gh3oct, implying additional GH3-like activities beyond GH3.17 [89]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGroup II GRETCHEN HAGEN 3 (GH3) acyl acid amido synthetase
Expression PatternGroup II GH3 transcripts in roots, including GH3.17, are induced by NaCl according to RT-qPCR, supporting salt-responsive regulation of GH3.17
Validation MethodUse of gh3oct octuple knockout (including GH3.17 lesion); hormone profiling demonstrating altered IAA, IAA-Glu, oxIAA and oxIAA-glc; developmental and stress phenotyping to connect combined GH3.17 loss to auxin metabolism and stress tolerance
Breeding Application ValueBecause GH3.17 is part of a GH3 module whose complete removal enhances tolerance to salinity and water deficit, but is not solely responsible for IAA–Glu formation, it should be considered within multiplex editing strategies targeting several GH3 genes to modulate auxin conjugation and stress tolerance in crops.
Environment / TreatmentNaCl salinity, PEG8000-based water-deficit and soil drought conditions under which gh3oct (lacking GH3.17) performs better than Col-0

Hv14-3-3A (Hordeum vulgare) Plant

Key Findings

  • Hv14-3-3A is one of six barley 14-3-3 genes, located on chromosome 4H and carrying conserved 14-3-3 motifs and multiple ABA- and stress-responsive cis-elements in its promoter. [90]
  • Hv14-3-3A shows broad expression across barley tissues and is strongly drought inducible in leaves of the drought-tolerant genotype XZ141 but downregulated in the drought-sensitive genotype XZ54. [90]
  • BSMV-VIGS silencing of Hv14-3-3A in barley seedlings (XZ141) greatly reduces Hv14-3-3A transcript levels and results in increased drought sensitivity, characterized by enhanced drought damage, increased stomatal density, elevated VPD, reduced net CO2 assimilation and stomatal conductance, and failure to increase iWUE under drought. [90]
  • Hv14-3-3A physically interacts with key drought- and stomatal-related proteins HvOST1.1, HvOST1.5, HvSLAC1 and HSP90-1/2, indicating that it is a core component of the OST1–SLAC1 and HSP90-associated signaling modules for rapid stomatal responses to drought in barley. [90]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Silencing Hv14-3-3A decreases drought tolerance by increasing stomatal density and vapor pressure deficit and reducing net CO2 assimilation, stomatal conductance and intrinsic water use efficiency in barley leaves under drought. [90]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a 14-3-3 scaffold protein that binds OST1 protein kinases, the anion channel SLAC1 and HSP90-1/2, suggesting modulation of OST1–SLAC1 signaling complexes and HSP90-associated chaperone functions in guard cells during drought. [90]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in the ABA-dependent OST1–SLAC1 stomatal regulation pathway and associated drought-stress signaling network in barley guard cells. [90]

More Details
Protein Family / Domain14-3-3 protein (general regulatory factor) containing the canonical PF00244 14-3-3 domain with conserved internal binding space, phosphorylation site, annexin-like, PKC pseudosubstrate and nuclear export signal motifs.
Expression PatternHv14-3-3A is predominantly expressed across examined barley tissues including inflorescences and roots, and shows high transcript levels compared with other Hv14-3-3s. Under drought, Hv14-3-3A transcripts are strongly induced in leaves of the drought-tolerant genotype XZ141 but reduced in the drought-sensitive genotype XZ54.
Validation MethodBSMV-mediated virus-induced gene silencing (BSMV-VIGS) of Hv14-3-3A in barley seedlings combined with qRT-PCR validation of knockdown, drought stress treatment, stomatal density and gas exchange measurements; protein–protein interaction assays (firefly luciferase complementation in Nicotiana benthamiana and yeast two-hybrid) with HvOST1s, HvSLAC1 and HvHSP90s.
Breeding Application ValuePositive regulator of drought tolerance and water-use efficiency in barley seedlings; manipulating Hv14-3-3A expression or its interacting stomatal signaling partners (OST1–SLAC1, HSP90-1/2) could be used to breed barley lines with improved drought tolerance via optimized stomatal density and maintenance of photosynthesis under water deficit.
Environment / TreatmentSoil-drying drought stress applied for 3–10 days to potted barley seedlings after the two- to three-leaf stage; comparison to well-watered control conditions.

GmaSOD20.1 (Glycine max) Plant

Key Findings

  • Identified as a member of the soybean SOD gene family through genome-wide analysis. [92]
  • Root expression is markedly induced by oxidative stress (H2O2), with higher transcript levels at 3–12 h compared with the untreated control. [92]
  • Shows continuous upregulation under salinity (250 mM NaCl), reaching maximal expression at 12 h after treatment. [92]
  • The stress-inducible expression pattern supports its role as part of the ROS-scavenging response to abiotic stress in cultivated soybean. [92]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Displays robust, time-dependent transcriptional induction in roots under oxidative and salt stress, indicating a stress-responsive SOD component of the antioxidant defense in cultivated soybean [92]

Pathways

Biosynthetic: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) detoxification / antioxidant defense (dismutation of O2− to H2O2 and O2) [92]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSuperoxide dismutase (SOD) family, antioxidant enzyme
Expression PatternIn roots, strongly upregulated by H2O2 and NaCl; expression increased at 3, 6 and 12 h after oxidative and salt treatments compared with 0 h control and remained continuously higher under NaCl, with maximum level at 12 h
Validation MethodqRT-PCR expression analysis of roots under H2O2 and NaCl stress treatments
Breeding Application ValueStress-inducible antioxidant gene in cultivated soybean; a candidate target for marker-assisted selection or transgenic approaches aiming to enhance tolerance to salinity and oxidative stress by reinforcing ROS-scavenging capacity in roots
Environment / TreatmentOxidative stress (10 mM H2O2), salinity stress (250 mM NaCl) applied to soybean seedlings in Hoagland solution; roots sampled at 0, 3, 6, 12 h

GsoSOD11.1 (Glycine soja) Plant

Key Findings

  • Identified as a member of the wild soybean SOD gene family in the genome-wide survey. [92]
  • Root expression is clearly induced by oxidative stress (H2O2), with higher transcript levels at 3–12 h relative to the untreated control. [92]
  • Under salinity stress (250 mM NaCl), shows continuous upregulation throughout the 0–12 h time course, with maximum expression at 12 h. [92]
  • The observed stress-responsive expression indicates that GsoSOD11.1 participates in the ROS-scavenging response of wild soybean to oxidative and salt stress. [92]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as a stress-responsive SOD gene in wild soybean, with robust transcriptional induction in roots under oxidative and salinity stress conditions [92]

Pathways

Biosynthetic: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) detoxification / antioxidant defense (dismutation of O2− to H2O2 and O2) [92]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSuperoxide dismutase (SOD) family, antioxidant enzyme
Expression PatternIn wild soybean roots, strongly upregulated by H2O2 and NaCl; expression is higher at 3, 6 and 12 h of oxidative and salt treatment than at 0 h and shows continuous induction under NaCl with peak expression at 12 h
Validation MethodqRT-PCR expression analysis of wild soybean roots under H2O2 and NaCl stress treatments
Breeding Application ValueA stress-inducible antioxidant gene from wild soybean that could be exploited as a genetic resource for improving salt and oxidative stress tolerance in cultivated soybean through introgression or transgenic approaches
Environment / TreatmentOxidative stress (10 mM H2O2) and salinity (250 mM NaCl) imposed on hydroponically grown wild soybean seedlings; roots sampled over a 0–12 h time course

GsoSOD20.1 (Glycine soja) Plant

Key Findings

  • Identified as part of the Glycine soja SOD gene family through genome-wide analysis. [92]
  • Exhibits continuous transcriptional upregulation in roots exposed to PEG 6000, indicating responsiveness to osmotic/drought stress. [92]
  • Expression remains higher than the 0 h control over the examined time points under PEG treatment. [92]
  • The drought-inducible expression pattern suggests GsoSOD20.1 contributes to the antioxidant response of wild soybean under water-deficit stress. [92]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Functions as a PEG-inducible SOD gene in wild soybean roots, exhibiting sustained transcriptional activation under simulated drought stress [92]

Pathways

Biosynthetic: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) detoxification / antioxidant defense (dismutation of O2− to H2O2 and O2) [92]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSuperoxide dismutase (SOD) family, antioxidant enzyme
Expression PatternIn wild soybean roots, shows continuous upregulation under PEG-induced drought stress relative to 0 h control, with sustained higher expression across the measured time points
Validation MethodqRT-PCR analysis of root expression under PEG 6000-induced drought stress
Breeding Application ValueA drought-inducible antioxidant gene from wild soybean that can serve as a promising resource for breeding or engineering soybean genotypes with improved tolerance to water-deficit conditions
Environment / TreatmentOsmotic/drought stress induced by 15% PEG 6000 in half-strength Hoagland solution, with roots sampled over a 0–12 h time course

GsoGPX17.1 (Glycine soja) Plant

Key Findings

  • Identified among Glycine soja GPX family members in a genome-wide survey. [92]
  • Displays continuous transcriptional upregulation in roots at 3–9 h of H2O2 treatment relative to 0 h, indicating strong oxidative stress responsiveness. [92]
  • Under PEG 6000-induced osmotic stress, expression at 12 h is higher than at 0 h, showing induction by drought-like conditions. [92]
  • The combined oxidative and osmotic stress responsiveness supports its role in the ROS-scavenging response of wild soybean under multiple abiotic stresses. [92]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Behaves as a strongly stress-inducible GPX gene in wild soybean roots, with sustained transcriptional activation under oxidative and osmotic stress conditions [92]

Pathways

Biosynthetic: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) detoxification via reduction of H2O2 and organic hydroperoxides using thiol electron donors [92]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGlutathione peroxidase (GPX) family, non-heme peroxidase
Expression PatternUnder H2O2 treatment, shows continuous upregulation at 3, 6 and 9 h relative to 0 h control; under PEG treatment, expression at 12 h is higher than at 0 h, indicating induction by drought/osmotic stress
Validation MethodqRT-PCR time-course analysis of root expression under H2O2 and PEG 6000 treatments
Breeding Application ValueA dual oxidative- and drought-inducible GPX gene from wild soybean, representing a promising candidate for enhancing multi-stress tolerance in soybean breeding or genetic engineering aimed at reinforcing ROS-scavenging capacity
Environment / TreatmentOxidative stress (10 mM H2O2) and PEG 6000-induced osmotic stress applied to wild soybean seedlings; roots monitored over 0–12 h

GsoGPX10.1 (Glycine soja) Plant

Key Findings

  • Identified as a GPX family member in Glycine soja via genome-wide analysis. [92]
  • Root expression is continuously elevated following 250 mM NaCl treatment relative to 0 h, with the highest level at 12 h. [92]
  • The NaCl-inducible expression indicates involvement in the antioxidant response of wild soybean to salinity stress. [92]
  • Provides a candidate GPX gene for improving salt stress tolerance in soybean through exploitation of wild germplasm. [92]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as a NaCl-inducible GPX gene in wild soybean roots, showing sustained transcriptional activation under salinity stress [92]

Pathways

Biosynthetic: ROS detoxification pathway, reducing H2O2 and hydroperoxides using thioredoxin or related electron donors [92]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGlutathione peroxidase (GPX) family, non-heme peroxidase
Expression PatternIn wild soybean roots, expression is continuously upregulated by NaCl treatment compared with 0 h control, with maximum transcript accumulation at 12 h
Validation MethodqRT-PCR analysis of root expression under NaCl treatment
Breeding Application ValueA salinity-inducible GPX gene from wild soybean that may be utilized as a donor locus or engineering target to improve salt tolerance in cultivated soybean by enhancing ROS-scavenging under high salinity
Environment / TreatmentSalinity stress imposed by 250 mM NaCl in nutrient solution; root tissues analyzed over a 0–12 h time course
