GsoGPX1.1 (Glycine soja) Plant

Key Findings

  • Identified as a GPX family member in Glycine soja through genome-wide analysis. [92]
  • Displays increased root expression under PEG 6000 treatment, with higher transcript abundance at 12 h relative to 0 h. [92]
  • The PEG-inducible pattern indicates involvement in the antioxidant response of wild soybean to osmotic/drought stress. [92]
  • Represents a drought-responsive GPX candidate from wild germplasm for improving stress tolerance in soybean. [92]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Functions as a PEG-inducible GPX gene in wild soybean roots, showing enhanced transcription under simulated drought conditions [92]

Pathways

Biosynthetic: ROS detoxification, reducing H2O2 and lipid hydroperoxides via thiol-based peroxidase activity [92]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGlutathione peroxidase (GPX) family, non-heme peroxidase
Expression PatternUnder PEG treatment, expression in roots is higher at 12 h than at 0 h, indicating induction by osmotic/drought stress
Validation MethodqRT-PCR expression profiling of roots under PEG 6000-induced osmotic stress
Breeding Application ValueA PEG-inducible GPX gene from wild soybean that can be considered in breeding or engineering strategies aimed at improving drought tolerance by bolstering ROS detoxification in roots
Environment / TreatmentOsmotic/drought stress applied as 15% PEG 6000 in half-strength Hoagland solution; time-course sampling of roots (0–12 h)

GmaGPX17.1 (Glycine max) Plant

Key Findings

  • Identified as a member of the Glycine max GPX gene family through genome-wide analysis. [92]
  • Root expression is continuously elevated under 250 mM NaCl compared with 0 h, peaking at 12 h after treatment. [92]
  • The NaCl-inducible expression pattern supports a role in the antioxidant response of cultivated soybean to salinity. [92]
  • Represents a GPX candidate for manipulating salt stress responses in soybean improvement programs. [92]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as a NaCl-inducible GPX gene in cultivated soybean roots, with sustained transcriptional activation under salt stress [92]

Pathways

Biosynthetic: ROS detoxification pathway, catalyzing reduction of H2O2 and other hydroperoxides [92]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGlutathione peroxidase (GPX) family, non-heme peroxidase
Expression PatternIn roots, shows continuous upregulation under NaCl treatment relative to 0 h, with maximal transcript accumulation at 12 h
Validation MethodqRT-PCR analysis of root expression under NaCl treatment
Breeding Application ValueA salt-inducible GPX gene in cultivated soybean that provides a candidate locus for breeding or engineering improved salinity tolerance by reinforcing ROS-scavenging in roots
Environment / TreatmentSalinity stress imposed by 250 mM NaCl; root tissues sampled from treated Glycine max seedlings over 0–12 h

GmaGPX10.1 (Glycine max) Plant

Key Findings

  • Identified as a member of the GPX gene family in Glycine max. [92]
  • Exhibits continuous upregulation in roots under PEG 6000 treatment compared with 0 h, indicating responsiveness to osmotic/drought stress. [92]
  • The PEG-inducible expression suggests involvement in the antioxidant response of cultivated soybean under water-deficit conditions. [92]
  • Serves as a candidate GPX gene for manipulating drought stress responses in soybean breeding and biotechnology. [92]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Functions as a PEG-inducible GPX gene in cultivated soybean roots, exhibiting sustained transcriptional activation under osmotic/drought stress [92]

Pathways

Biosynthetic: ROS detoxification via reduction of H2O2 and hydroperoxides [92]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGlutathione peroxidase (GPX) family, non-heme peroxidase
Expression PatternIn roots, shows continuous increase in expression under PEG treatment relative to 0 h control
Validation MethodqRT-PCR analysis of root expression under PEG 6000 treatment
Breeding Application ValueA drought/osmotic stress-inducible GPX gene in cultivated soybean, providing a candidate for improving drought tolerance through enhanced ROS-scavenging capacity in root tissues
Environment / TreatmentOsmotic/drought stress induced by 15% PEG 6000; roots sampled between 0 and 12 h after treatment

SlBAG9 (Solanum lycopersicum) Plant

Key Findings

  • SlBAG9 is a tomato BAG family protein containing a BAG domain and N-terminal IQ calmodulin-binding motif, identified and cloned based on the SL4.0/ITAG4.0 genome. [93]
  • SlBAG9 shows organ- and stress-specific expression, with high transcript levels in red fruits and responsiveness to multiple abiotic stresses and ABA. [93]
  • Heterologous overexpression of SlBAG9 in Arabidopsis significantly reduces seed germination and seedling growth under mannitol, NaCl, and ABA, indicating that SlBAG9 negatively regulates osmotic stress and ABA tolerance. [93]
  • SlBAG9-overexpressing plants exhibit attenuated induction of ABA/stress-responsive genes (ABI3, RD29A, DREB2A, P5CS1) and ROS-scavenging genes (FSD1, CAT1), lower SOD and CAT activities, higher H2O2 and MDA levels, and aggravated oxidative damage under stress, linking SlBAG9 to ABA signaling and antioxidant defense regulation. [93]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Heterologous overexpression of tomato SlBAG9 in Arabidopsis negatively regulates osmotic stress tolerance, increasing sensitivity to drought- and salt-induced osmotic stress and to ABA during seed germination and seedling growth. [93]

Regulatory Mechanism

Under mannitol, NaCl, and ABA treatments, SlBAG9 overexpression attenuates transcriptional upregulation of ABA-responsive genes (ABI3, RD29A) and stress-responsive genes (DREB2A, P5CS1) as well as ROS-scavenging genes (FSD1, CAT1), resulting in lower SOD and CAT activities, excessive H2O2 accumulation, enhanced lipid peroxidation (MDA), and thus increased oxidative damage and stress sensitivity. [93]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts in ABA-mediated drought and salt stress signaling and ROS scavenging pathways, where SlBAG9 overexpression dampens induction of ABA/stress-responsive genes and ROS-detoxifying genes, leading to reduced antioxidant defense under osmotic and ABA stress. [93]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainBcl-2-associated athanogene (BAG) family protein containing a C-terminal BAG domain and an N-terminal IQ calmodulin-binding motif unique to plant BAGs.
Expression PatternIn tomato, SlBAG9 shows highest transcript levels in red fruits and is induced by high temperature; its expression generally decreases under drought and H2O2 treatment and is downregulated by H2O2 while responsive to various abiotic stresses and ABA.
Validation MethodHeterologous overexpression of SlBAG9 in Arabidopsis Col-0 (stable homozygous lines 2-12 and 4-9), phenotypic assays of seed germination and seedling growth under mannitol, NaCl, and ABA; qRT-PCR of stress/ABA-responsive and ROS-scavenging genes; biochemical assays of H2O2 and MDA contents and SOD and CAT enzyme activities under stress treatments.
Breeding Application ValueSlBAG9 functions as a negative regulator of drought-, salt-, and ABA-induced osmotic stress tolerance; reducing its expression or activity in tomato could enhance tolerance by promoting expression of stress/ABA-responsive and ROS-scavenging genes and strengthening antioxidant defenses. SlBAG9 and its expression patterns under stress may serve as molecular markers or targets in tomato resistance breeding programs aimed at improving abiotic stress and oxidative stress tolerance.
Environment / TreatmentOsmotic stress simulated by mannitol (200 and 300 mM), salt stress by NaCl (175 and 200 mM), ABA treatment (1.0 and 1.5 μM) during Arabidopsis seed germination and seedling growth; tomato seedlings treated with PEG6000 drought (20%), 100 mM NaCl, high temperature (42 °C), cold (4 °C), 50 μM cadmium, 100 μM ABA, and 10 mM H2O2.

HhNAC54 (Hibiscus hamabo) Plant

Key Findings

  • HhNAC54 is a member of the NAC transcription factor family identified from the Hibiscus hamabo genome. [94]
  • Transcriptome analysis and qRT-PCR show that HhNAC54 is strongly induced by drought (PEG), salt (NaCl), ABA, and MeJA treatments, indicating a role in multiple abiotic stress responses. [94]
  • Overexpression of HhNAC54 in Arabidopsis thaliana (Col-0) results in transgenic lines whose root growth is less inhibited by high salinity than wild type on NaCl-containing medium, demonstrating that HhNAC54 positively contributes to salt tolerance. [94]
  • HhNAC54 is thus a key stress-responsive NAC gene from H. hamabo and a promising candidate for dissecting abiotic stress response mechanisms and improving salt tolerance in plants. [94]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of HhNAC54 enhances salt tolerance, as shown by significantly less root growth inhibition and longer roots of transgenic Arabidopsis compared with wild type under high NaCl conditions. [94]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainNAC transcription factor family with conserved N-terminal NAC domain
Expression PatternStrongly upregulated under drought (PEG), salt (NaCl), ABA, and MeJA treatments in H. hamabo, with qRT-PCR expression trends consistent with RNA-seq data, indicating induction at multiple time points during stress responses.
Validation MethodFunctional validation by overexpressing HhNAC54 in Arabidopsis thaliana Col-0 (homozygous T3 lines OE1–OE3) followed by salt tolerance assays on NaCl-supplemented 1/2 MS medium and comparison of root lengths between transgenic and wild-type plants.
Breeding Application ValueHhNAC54 is a stress-inducible NAC transcription factor from the salt- and drought-tolerant semi-mangrove tree Hibiscus hamabo that enhances salt tolerance when overexpressed. It provides an excellent gene resource for molecular breeding and genetic engineering of salt-tolerant woody and semi-mangrove plants and can be used as a candidate marker or transgene to improve salt tolerance in other crops.

MiCOL16A (Mangifera indica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MiCOL16A encodes a group II CONSTANS-like BBX protein with one B-box and one CCT domain and is closely related to AtCOL6/AtCOL16. [95]
  • In mango, MiCOL16A is highly expressed in leaves, especially on nonflowering branches and during the late floral induction period, and shows a robust circadian rhythm with peaks after dusk under different photoperiods. [95]
  • MiCOL16A protein localizes to the nucleus and exhibits transcriptional activation activity in yeast dependent on its MR domain. [95]
  • Overexpression of MiCOL16A in Arabidopsis delays flowering under both LD and SD conditions, associated with reduced expression of AtFT and AtSOC1. [95]
  • MiCOL16A overexpression enhances salt and drought tolerance in Arabidopsis, with longer primary roots and higher survival rates under NaCl and drought treatments, accompanied by increased expression of stress-responsive genes AtNHX1, AtRD20, AtSOS1 and AtCOR15A. [95]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression delays flowering and enhances tolerance to salt and drought stress in Arabidopsis, indicating a negative role in flowering promotion and a positive role in abiotic stress responses for mango. [95]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a nuclear transcriptional activator via its MR domain in yeast; overexpression represses expression of flowering integrator genes AtFT and AtSOC1 and increases expression of stress-responsive genes AtNHX1, AtRD20 and AtSOS1 under salt stress and AtCOR15A, AtRD20 and AtNHX1 under drought stress in Arabidopsis. [95]

Pathways

Regulatory: Photoperiodic flowering regulatory pathway and abiotic stress (salt and drought) response pathway. [95]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCONSTANS-like (COL16) BBX family transcription factor with one B-box zinc finger domain and one CCT domain.
Expression PatternIn mango, MiCOL16A is expressed in all tested tissues, with highest expression in leaves and lowest in buds and flowers; expression in leaves of nonflowering branches is higher than in flowering branches and peaks during the late floral induction period before decreasing. MiCOL16A shows circadian oscillation with expression increasing after dusk, peaking about 6 h after dusk and then declining under LD, MD and SD photoperiods.
Validation Method35S::MiCOL16A overexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana Col-0 under LD and SD conditions with flowering time and plant height measurements; salt and drought stress assays (NaCl, mannitol, water withholding) assessing root length and survival; qRT-PCR analysis of AtFT, AtSOC1 and stress-responsive genes; subcellular localization of MiCOL16A-GFP in onion epidermal cells; yeast transcriptional activation assay of BD-MiCOL16A and MR-deletion construct.
Breeding Application ValueCandidate gene for manipulating flowering time and improving salt and drought tolerance in mango breeding and cultivation, as its overexpression delays flowering and enhances abiotic stress tolerance in transgenic plants.
Environment / TreatmentPhotoperiod conditions (LD 16 h light/8 h dark, MD 12 h light/12 h dark, SD 8 h light/16 h dark); salt stress using NaCl (100–200 mM in agar media, 300 mM in soil drench); osmotic/drought stress using mannitol (300–500 mM) and water withholding.

MiCOL16B (Mangifera indica) Plant

Key Findings

  • MiCOL16B encodes a group II CONSTANS-like BBX protein with one B-box and one CCT domain and is closely related to AtCOL6/AtCOL16. [95]
  • In mango, MiCOL16B is highly expressed in leaves, particularly on flowering branches and during the early floral induction period, with low expression in buds and flowers and no clear circadian rhythm. [95]
  • MiCOL16B protein localizes to the nucleus and exhibits transcriptional activation activity in yeast dependent on its MR domain. [95]
  • Overexpression of MiCOL16B in Arabidopsis delays flowering under both LD and SD conditions, associated with reduced expression of AtFT and AtSOC1. [95]
  • MiCOL16B overexpression enhances salt and drought tolerance in Arabidopsis, with longer primary roots and higher survival rates under NaCl and drought treatments, accompanied by increased expression of stress-responsive genes AtNHX1, AtSOS1, AtRD20 and AtCOR15A. [95]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression delays flowering and improves tolerance to salt and drought stress in Arabidopsis, indicating MiCOL16B negatively regulates flowering and positively contributes to abiotic stress responses in mango. [95]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a nuclear transcriptional activator via its MR domain in yeast; overexpression represses expression of flowering integrator genes AtFT and AtSOC1 and increases expression of stress-responsive genes AtNHX1, AtSOS1 (salt) and AtCOR15A, AtRD20 and AtNHX1 (drought) in Arabidopsis. [95]

Pathways

Regulatory: Photoperiodic flowering regulatory pathway and abiotic stress (salt and drought) response pathway. [95]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainCONSTANS-like (COL16) BBX family transcription factor with one B-box zinc finger domain and one CCT domain.
Expression PatternIn mango, MiCOL16B is expressed in all tested tissues, with highest levels in leaves and lowest in buds and flowers; expression in leaves of flowering branches is higher than in nonflowering branches and peaks during the early floral induction period before decreasing. MiCOL16B is expressed throughout the day but its transcript levels fluctuate without a clear diurnal rhythm under LD, MD and SD conditions.
Validation Method35S::MiCOL16B overexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana Col-0 under LD and SD conditions with flowering time and plant height measurements; salt and drought stress assays (NaCl, mannitol, water withholding) assessing root length and survival; qRT-PCR analysis of AtFT, AtSOC1 and stress-responsive genes; subcellular localization of MiCOL16B-GFP in onion epidermal cells; yeast transcriptional activation assay of BD-MiCOL16B and MR-deletion construct.
Breeding Application ValueUseful target for adjusting flowering time and increasing salt and drought tolerance in mango breeding programs, since its overexpression delays flowering and confers enhanced abiotic stress tolerance in transgenic plants.
Environment / TreatmentPhotoperiod conditions (LD 16 h light/8 h dark, MD 12 h light/12 h dark, SD 8 h light/16 h dark); salt stress using NaCl (100–200 mM in agar media, 300 mM in soil drench); osmotic/drought stress using mannitol (300–500 mM) and water withholding.

TaERF-6-3A (Triticum aestivum) Plant

Key Findings

  • TaERF-6-3A is an AP2/ERF (ERF subfamily) transcription factor from wheat identified as drought-responsive in RNA-seq and qRT-PCR analyses. [96]
  • TaERF-6-3A expression in wheat is induced by drought, salt, and cold stresses, and its promoter contains multiple ABA- and stress-related cis-elements. [96]
  • TaERF-6-3A is a nuclear protein with transcriptional activation activity, mediated by its C-terminal region, and physically interacts with AtCIPK22 in yeast. [96]
  • Overexpression of TaERF-6-3A in Arabidopsis thaliana increases sensitivity to drought and salt, reducing survival and increasing water loss relative to wild type. [96]
  • Under drought and salt, TaERF-6-3A overexpression lines show higher MDA content, reduced proline accumulation, and lower activities of antioxidant enzymes POD, CAT, and SOD. [96]
  • The expression of stress-responsive genes RD29A and P5CS1 and antioxidant-related genes POD1, CAT1, and Cu/Zn SOD is significantly downregulated in TaERF-6-3A overexpression lines under drought and salt stress. [96]
  • The data support that TaERF-6-3A acts as a negative regulator of drought and salt stress tolerance, likely by repressing stress- and antioxidant-related gene expression and weakening antioxidant defenses. [96]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Negative regulator of drought and salt stress responses; overexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana increases sensitivity to drought and high salinity. [96]

Regulatory Mechanism

TaERF-6-3A is a nuclear AP2/ERF transcription factor with a C-terminal transcriptional activation domain in yeast, and its overexpression in Arabidopsis negatively regulates drought and salt tolerance by repressing the expression of stress-responsive (RD29A, P5CS1) and antioxidant-related (POD1, CAT1, Cu/Zn SOD) genes, leading to reduced antioxidant enzyme activities and altered osmotic adjustment. [96]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainAP2/ERF transcription factor, ERF subfamily, containing a single AP2/ERF DNA-binding domain
Expression PatternIn wheat JW1, TaERF-6-3A is induced by drought, salt, and cold stress. Under drought (20% PEG6000), expression rapidly increases, peaking at 1 h, then declines to a minimum at 12 h and recovers towards initial levels by 24 h. Under salt (200 mM NaCl), expression slightly decreases at 1 h, then increases to a peak at 6 h and declines thereafter. Under cold (4 °C), expression is unchanged at 1 h, then sharply increases to a peak at 6 h and gradually returns to basal level by 24 h. It is also upregulated by drought in RNA-seq and qRT-PCR analyses.
Breeding Application ValueTaERF-6-3A functions as a negative regulator of drought and salt tolerance, so reducing or knocking out its activity in wheat could enhance stress resistance by increasing antioxidant capacity and proline accumulation. TaERF-6-3A thus represents a candidate target gene for improving drought and salinity tolerance in wheat through transgenic breeding or gene-editing approaches, and the transcriptomic identification strategy illustrated here provides a route to discover additional negative regulators for breeding stress-resilient cultivars.
Environment / TreatmentIn wheat JW1: drought stress induced by 20% PEG6000, salt stress by 200 mM NaCl, and cold stress at 4 °C (samples at 0, 1, 6, 12, 24 h). In Arabidopsis: drought imposed by withholding water for 10–12 days followed by re-watering for survival analysis, and salt stress by irrigation with 300 mM NaCl solution for 15 days.

MsTIP4 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • MsTIP4 is an aquaporin (MIP family) gene located on chromosome 1 of Medicago sativa. [97]
  • MsTIP4 is strongly induced by 150 mM NaCl, with >3-fold transcript increase at 48 h compared with 0 h. [97]
  • MsTIP4 shows preferential expression in roots and relatively high expression in stems and flowers, indicating tissue-specific roles in water transport. [97]
  • Its salt-responsive and root-preferential expression suggest MsTIP4 as a candidate gene for improving salt tolerance and water homeostasis in alfalfa. [97]
More Details
Protein Family / DomainAquaporin; major intrinsic protein (MIP) family.
Expression PatternUnder 150 mM NaCl, MsTIP4 transcript decreases slightly at 4–8 h, then increases, reaching >3-fold induction at 48 h; spatially, MsTIP4 is highly expressed in roots and also relatively highly expressed in stems and flowers.
Breeding Application ValueSalt-inducible aquaporin preferentially expressed in roots and also in stems and flowers; proposed as a candidate gene to improve alfalfa salt stress resistance and water homeostasis via gene editing or genetic breeding.
Environment / TreatmentSalt stress imposed by 150 mM NaCl for 0, 4, 8, 12, 24 and 48 h under hydroponic conditions.

MsTIP10 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • MsTIP10 encodes a predicted tonoplast/mitochondrion-localized aquaporin on chromosome 4 of M. sativa. [97]
  • MsTIP10 is induced 2–2.5-fold by 150 mM NaCl at 12–24 h. [97]
  • MsTIP10 is preferentially expressed in roots, suggesting a role in root water transport under salt stress. [97]
  • Its salt-inducible root expression makes MsTIP10 a candidate gene for improving salt tolerance in alfalfa. [97]
More Details
Protein Family / DomainAquaporin; major intrinsic protein (MIP) family.
Expression PatternUnder 150 mM NaCl, MsTIP10 transcript shows 2–2.5-fold induction at 12–24 h; spatially, MsTIP10 is highly expressed in roots.
Breeding Application ValueNaCl-inducible tonoplast/mitochondrion-localized aquaporin preferentially expressed in roots; considered a candidate gene for enhancing salt stress adaptation and cellular water balance in alfalfa.
Environment / Treatment150 mM NaCl treatment for 0, 4, 8, 12, 24 and 48 h.

MsTIP12 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • MsTIP12 encodes a predicted tonoplast aquaporin located on chromosome 5 of M. sativa. [97]
  • MsTIP12 is strongly induced by 150 mM NaCl, with >4-fold induction at 4 h and >10-fold at 12 h. [97]
  • MsTIP12 is preferentially expressed in roots, consistent with a role in root water regulation under salt stress. [97]
  • Its strong salt-inducible root expression supports MsTIP12 as a key candidate for improving salt tolerance in alfalfa. [97]
More Details
Protein Family / DomainAquaporin; major intrinsic protein (MIP) family.
Expression PatternUnder 150 mM NaCl, MsTIP12 transcript increases >4-fold at 4 h, then decreases and peaks again at 12 h with >10-fold induction; MsTIP12 is highly expressed in roots.
Breeding Application ValueStrongly NaCl-inducible tonoplast aquaporin highly expressed in roots; promising candidate for engineering enhanced salt tolerance and water management in alfalfa.
Environment / Treatment150 mM NaCl treatment for up to 48 h.

MsNIP2 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • MsNIP2 encodes a nodulin 26-like intrinsic protein aquaporin located on chromosome 2 of M. sativa and is predicted to be plasma membrane localized. [97]
  • MsNIP2 is strongly induced by 150 mM NaCl, with transcript levels increasing from 4 to 48 h and peaking at 48 h. [97]
  • MsNIP2 is highly expressed in roots and relatively highly expressed in stems and flowers, indicating a role in root and shoot water homeostasis under salt stress. [97]
  • Its salt-responsive and root-preferential expression profile makes MsNIP2 a key candidate for breeding salt-tolerant alfalfa. [97]
More Details
Protein Family / DomainNodulin 26-like intrinsic protein (NIP) aquaporin; major intrinsic protein (MIP) family.
Expression PatternUnder 150 mM NaCl, MsNIP2 transcript increases continuously from 4 to 48 h, reaching the highest level at 48 h; tissue profiling shows high expression in roots and relatively high expression in stems and flowers.
Breeding Application ValueSalt-responsive nodulin 26-like intrinsic protein aquaporin, highly expressed in roots and predicted to be plasma membrane localized; considered a promising target for improving root water homeostasis and salt tolerance in alfalfa.
Environment / Treatment150 mM NaCl treatment for up to 48 h under hydroponic conditions.

MsNIP9 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • MsNIP9 encodes a NIP-type aquaporin on chromosome 5 of M. sativa and is predicted to localize to the plasma membrane. [97]
  • MsNIP9 is strongly induced by 150 mM NaCl, with >6-fold higher transcript levels at 48 h than at 0 h. [97]
  • MsNIP9 is highly expressed in roots and relatively highly expressed in stems and flowers. [97]
  • Its strong NaCl inducibility and root-preferential expression make MsNIP9 a strong candidate gene for improving salt tolerance in alfalfa. [97]
More Details
Protein Family / DomainNodulin 26-like intrinsic protein (NIP) aquaporin; major intrinsic protein (MIP) family.
Expression PatternUnder 150 mM NaCl, MsNIP9 transcript decreases slightly at 4 h, then increases and remains relatively stable from 8 to 48 h, reaching >6-fold induction at 48 h; spatially, MsNIP9 is highly expressed in roots and also relatively high in stems and flowers.
Breeding Application ValueNaCl-inducible plasma membrane NIP aquaporin with high root expression; potential target for enhancing salt stress tolerance and regulation of water and small-molecule transport in alfalfa.
Environment / Treatment150 mM NaCl treatment for 0–48 h.

MsPIP1 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • MsPIP1 (MsPIP2;2) encodes a plasma membrane PIP aquaporin located on chromosome 1 of M. sativa. [97]
  • MsPIP1 transcript is downregulated by 15% PEG treatment from 2 to 32 h. [97]
  • MsPIP1 is preferentially expressed in leaves, indicating a role in leaf water relations. [97]
  • Its PEG-responsive and leaf-preferential expression, together with previous overexpression data in Arabidopsis, support MsPIP1 as a candidate gene for improving drought tolerance in alfalfa. [97]
More Details
Protein Family / DomainPlasma membrane intrinsic protein (PIP) aquaporin; major intrinsic protein (MIP) family.
Expression PatternUnder 15% PEG, MsPIP1 transcript decreases from 2 to 32 h; in spatial profiling, MsPIP1 is preferentially expressed in leaves.
Breeding Application ValueDrought/PEG-responsive plasma membrane PIP aquaporin preferentially expressed in leaves; serves as a candidate gene for modulating leaf water status and drought tolerance in alfalfa, and corresponds to the previously characterized MsPIP2;2.
Environment / TreatmentOsmotic/drought stress simulated by 15% PEG treatment for 0, 2, 8 and 32 h.

MsPIP3 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • MsPIP3 encodes a PIP-type aquaporin on chromosome 3 of M. sativa. [97]
  • MsPIP3 transcript is downregulated at 2 and 8 h and slightly upregulated at 32 h under 15% PEG treatment. [97]
  • MsPIP3 shows stem-preferential expression in spatial profiling. [97]
  • Its PEG-responsive and stem-preferential expression suggests a role in stem water transport under drought and makes it a candidate gene for drought adaptation breeding. [97]
More Details
Protein Family / DomainPlasma membrane intrinsic protein (PIP) aquaporin; major intrinsic protein (MIP) family.
Expression PatternUnder 15% PEG, MsPIP3 transcript decreases at 2 and 8 h, then increases slightly at 32 h; tissue profiling indicates that MsPIP3 is preferentially expressed in stems.
Breeding Application ValueDrought/PEG-responsive plasma membrane PIP aquaporin preferentially expressed in stems; candidate gene for modifying stem hydraulic properties and drought adaptation in alfalfa.
Environment / Treatment15% PEG treatment for 0, 2, 8 and 32 h.

MsPIP4 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • MsPIP4 encodes a PIP-type aquaporin on chromosome 4 of M. sativa. [97]
  • MsPIP4 transcript is downregulated at 8 and 32 h under 15% PEG treatment. [97]
  • MsPIP4 shows leaf-preferential expression. [97]
  • Its PEG-responsive and leaf-preferential expression suggests MsPIP4 as a candidate gene for improving drought tolerance through modulation of leaf water status. [97]
More Details
Protein Family / DomainPlasma membrane intrinsic protein (PIP) aquaporin; major intrinsic protein (MIP) family.
Expression PatternUnder 15% PEG, MsPIP4 transcript decreases at 8 and 32 h; tissue profiling shows MsPIP4 is preferentially expressed in leaves.
Breeding Application ValueDrought/PEG-responsive plasma membrane PIP aquaporin with leaf-preferential expression; candidate gene for improving leaf water-use efficiency and drought tolerance in alfalfa.
Environment / Treatment15% PEG treatment for 0, 2, 8 and 32 h.

MsNIP1 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • MsNIP1 encodes a NIP-type aquaporin located on chromosome 1 of M. sativa. [97]
  • MsNIP1 transcript is significantly induced (~2–2.5-fold) by 15% PEG at 2, 8 and 32 h. [97]
  • MsNIP1 is most highly expressed in roots. [97]
  • Its PEG-inducible and root-preferential expression suggests MsNIP1 as a candidate gene for improving drought tolerance through enhanced root water uptake. [97]
More Details
Protein Family / DomainNodulin 26-like intrinsic protein (NIP) aquaporin; major intrinsic protein (MIP) family.
Expression PatternUnder 15% PEG, MsNIP1 transcript is significantly increased by ~2–2.5-fold at 2, 8 and 32 h; tissue profiling shows MsNIP1 has the highest expression in roots.
Breeding Application ValueDrought/PEG-inducible root-preferential NIP aquaporin; candidate gene for enhancing root water uptake and maintaining water balance under drought in alfalfa.
Environment / Treatment15% PEG treatment for 0, 2, 8 and 32 h.

MsNIP6 (Medicago sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • MsNIP6 encodes a NIP-type aquaporin located on chromosome 4 of M. sativa. [97]
  • MsNIP6 transcript is induced under 15% PEG treatment, with elevated levels at 2, 8 and 32 h. [97]
  • MsNIP6 shows highest expression in leaves in spatial profiling. [97]
  • Its PEG-responsive and leaf-preferential expression makes MsNIP6 a candidate gene for improving drought tolerance through modulation of leaf water and solute transport. [97]
More Details
Protein Family / DomainNodulin 26-like intrinsic protein (NIP) aquaporin; major intrinsic protein (MIP) family.
Expression PatternUnder 15% PEG, MsNIP6 transcript shows a pattern similar to MsPIP3, with changes including induction at 2 and 8 h and higher levels at 32 h; spatially, MsNIP6 shows highest expression in leaves.
Breeding Application ValueDrought/PEG-responsive NIP aquaporin with highest expression in leaves; potential candidate for modulating leaf water and solute transport under drought in alfalfa.
Environment / Treatment15% PEG treatment for 0, 2, 8 and 32 h.

PtDi19-2 (Populus trichocarpa) Plant

Key Findings

  • PtDi19-2 is a Di19 family C2H2-type zinc-finger transcription factor from poplar containing conserved zf-Di19 and Di19_C domains and is localized to the nucleus. [98]
  • PtDi19-2 expression in poplar is strongly induced by drought, PEG, NaCl, ABA, and cold, indicating involvement in multiple abiotic stress responses. [98]
  • PtDi19-2 acts as a transcriptional activator with its activation domain located in the C-terminal region and can interact with PtDi19-7 in yeast two-hybrid assays. [98]
  • Overexpression of PtDi19-2 in Arabidopsis increases ABA sensitivity at germination, enhances tolerance to mannitol-induced osmotic stress and soil drought, and improves physiological drought-resistance traits (higher water content, proline, POD activity; lower electrolyte leakage, MDA, and H2O2). [98]
  • PtDi19-2 overexpression promotes ABA-induced stomatal closure and upregulates ABA/drought-responsive genes (AtABF3, AtDREB2A, AtERD1, AtRD29A), supporting a role as a positive regulator in ABA-dependent drought-response pathways. [98]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of PtDi19-2 in Arabidopsis increases sensitivity to ABA and enhances tolerance to drought/osmotic stress by improving survival, water status, osmotic adjustment, antioxidant activity, and limiting membrane damage and ROS accumulation. [98]

Regulatory Mechanism

Functions as a Cys2/His2-type Di19 transcription factor with transcriptional activation activity dependent on its C-terminal region; acts as a nuclear transcriptional activator that positively regulates ABA-mediated stomatal closure and drought-responsive gene expression, thereby enhancing cellular osmotic adjustment and antioxidant defenses under drought. [98]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA-dependent drought stress signaling pathway, including promotion of ABA-induced stomatal closure and upregulation of ABA/drought-responsive genes such as AtABF3, AtDREB2A, AtERD1, and AtRD29A in Arabidopsis. [98]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDi19 (drought-induced 19) protein containing two typical Cys2/His2 zinc-finger domains, with conserved zf-Di19 and Di19_C domains.
Expression PatternTranscriptome and qRT-PCR analyses show PtDi19-2 is strongly induced in poplar, particularly leaves, by prolonged drought; its expression also responds markedly to PEG (20% PEG-6000; ~10-fold induction at 6–12 h), NaCl (200 mM; ~4-fold induction), ABA (100 μM), and cold (4 °C) treatments.
Validation MethodTransgenic overexpression in Arabidopsis followed by ABA, mannitol, and soil-drought treatments; physiological and biochemical assays (relative water content, electrolyte leakage, proline, MDA, POD activity, H2O2 staining); subcellular localization via 35S::PtDi19-2::GFP in tobacco; yeast GAL4-based transcriptional activation assay; yeast two-hybrid interaction assay with PtDi19-7; qRT-PCR analysis of ABA/drought marker genes in overexpression lines.
Breeding Application ValuePtDi19-2 is a drought- and ABA-inducible Di19 transcription factor from poplar whose overexpression in Arabidopsis markedly enhances drought tolerance and ABA sensitivity by improving water retention, osmotic adjustment, antioxidant capacity, and ABA-mediated stomatal closure. These features make PtDi19-2 a promising candidate gene for molecular breeding of drought-tolerant woody plants and potentially other crops through transgenic approaches that strengthen ABA-dependent drought-response pathways.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress (soil drying in pots; prolonged drought in transcriptome data), osmotic stress via 20% PEG-6000 and mannitol (300–400 mM), salt stress (200 mM NaCl), exogenous ABA (0.7–100 μM), and cold stress (4 °C).

PtDi19-7 (Populus trichocarpa) Plant

Key Findings

  • PtDi19-7 is a Di19 family C2H2-type zinc-finger transcription factor from poplar that is among the most strongly induced PtDi19 members by drought, PEG, salt, cold, and ABA, particularly in leaves. [98]
  • PtDi19-7 localizes to both the nucleus and cell membrane and exhibits transcriptional activation activity in yeast. [98]
  • The N-terminal region of PtDi19-2 interacts with PtDi19-7 in yeast two-hybrid assays, indicating that PtDi19-7 can function in a Di19 protein complex. [98]
  • Overexpression of PtDi19-7 in Arabidopsis increases ABA sensitivity at germination, enhances tolerance to mannitol-induced osmotic stress and soil drought, and improves physiological drought-resistance traits (higher water content, proline, POD; lower electrolyte leakage, MDA, and H2O2). [98]
  • Overexpression of PtDi19-7 in hybrid poplar 84K enhances drought tolerance, with higher leaf water and chlorophyll content, better membrane stability, increased proline and POD activity, and longer, heavier roots under drought. [98]
  • PtDi19-7 overexpression promotes ABA-induced stomatal closure in Arabidopsis and ABA-induced lateral bud dormancy in 84K poplar. [98]
  • PtDi19-7-overexpressing Arabidopsis shows upregulation of ABA/drought-responsive genes (AtABF3, AtDREB2A, AtERD1, AtRD29A) under drought, supporting a role as a positive regulator in ABA-dependent drought-response pathways. [98]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Overexpression of PtDi19-7 enhances drought tolerance and ABA sensitivity in Arabidopsis and hybrid poplar 84K, leading to improved survival and growth under drought, better water retention and membrane integrity, higher osmoprotectant and antioxidant levels, deeper and heavier roots in poplar, and ABA-induced lateral bud dormancy. [98]

Regulatory Mechanism

Functions as a Di19 family transcription factor with transcriptional activation activity; localized to nucleus and membrane, suggesting roles in nuclear transcriptional regulation and potential membrane-associated signaling; positively regulates ABA-mediated stomatal closure, lateral bud dormancy, and expression of ABA/drought-responsive genes, thereby enhancing osmotic adjustment and antioxidant defenses and promoting deeper root growth under drought. [98]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA-dependent drought-response pathway controlling stomatal closure, lateral bud dormancy, and expression of ABA/drought-responsive genes such as AtABF3, AtDREB2A, AtERD1, and AtRD29A. [98]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainDi19 (drought-induced 19) protein belonging to the Cys2/His2-type zinc-finger transcription factors, containing conserved zf-Di19 and Di19_C domains.
Expression PatternRNA-seq and qRT-PCR show PtDi19-7 is one of the most strongly induced PtDi19 members in poplar leaves under prolonged drought, and is also highly responsive to salt, PEG (20% PEG-6000), ABA (100 μM), and cold (4 °C) treatments; under cold, its expression reaches more than 30-fold above the control at 12 h.
Validation MethodTransgenic overexpression in Arabidopsis and 84K poplar followed by ABA and drought/osmotic stress assays; detailed physiological and biochemical measurements (relative water content, chlorophyll, electrolyte leakage, MDA, proline, POD activity, root metrics); ABA-induced lateral bud dormancy assays in 84K; stomatal aperture assays in Arabidopsis under ABA; subcellular localization via 35S::PtDi19-7::GFP in tobacco; yeast GAL4-based transcriptional activation assays; yeast two-hybrid interaction assays with PtDi19-2-N; qRT-PCR of ABA/drought marker genes in Arabidopsis overexpression lines.
Breeding Application ValuePtDi19-7 is a strongly drought- and ABA-inducible Di19 transcription factor that, when overexpressed, substantially enhances drought tolerance and ABA sensitivity in both Arabidopsis and hybrid poplar 84K by improving water conservation, root system development, antioxidant capacity, and ABA-induced lateral bud dormancy and stomatal closure. These properties make PtDi19-7 a high-value candidate gene for molecular breeding of drought-tolerant poplar cultivars and other woody crops via manipulation of ABA-dependent signaling pathways.
Environment / TreatmentDrought stress (soil drying of Arabidopsis and 84K poplar); osmotic stress via mannitol (300–400 mM) and 20% PEG-6000; salt stress (200 mM NaCl); ABA treatments (0.7 μM for Arabidopsis germination, 1 μM for stomatal assays, 5–10 μM for 84K lateral bud dormancy, 100 μM ABA for poplar seedlings); cold stress at 4 °C.

OsNAC016 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • Loss-of-function osnac016 and OsNAC016^ko mutants exhibit BR-deficient architecture (erect leaves, dwarfism, shortened roots) and enhanced drought tolerance and ABA sensitivity, whereas OsNAC016-overexpressing plants show enlarged leaf inclination, enhanced BR responsiveness, and reduced drought tolerance and ABA sensitivity. [99]
  • OsNAC016 promotes BR signaling by positively regulating BR biosynthetic genes, especially D2, which it activates by binding BRRE motifs in the D2 promoter; RNA-seq revealed that OsNAC016-regulated genes substantially overlap with OsBZR1-regulated BR-responsive genes. [99]
  • OsNAC016 negatively regulates ABA-mediated drought responses by repressing ABA early-response genes, including direct binding to and repression of the OsPP2C68 promoter; osnac016 mutants display increased expression of ABA early-response genes and ROS-scavenging genes under drought. [99]
  • OsNAC016 physically interacts with and is phosphorylated by GSK2 in the BR pathway and SAPK8 in the ABA pathway; phosphorylation at conserved sites accelerates OsNAC016 degradation and is associated with increased ubiquitination. [99]
  • The E3 ubiquitin ligase OsPUB43 interacts with OsNAC016, ubiquitinates it, and promotes its degradation via the 26S proteasome, contributing together with autophagy to OsNAC016 turnover. [99]
  • ABA and BR antagonistically regulate OsNAC016: BL and bikinin treatments inhibit GSK2, increasing OsNAC016 protein accumulation, whereas ABA enhances SAPK8-mediated phosphorylation and ubiquitination of OsNAC016 and accelerates its degradation, enabling OsNAC016 to balance BR-driven growth with ABA-dependent drought tolerance. [99]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Loss-of-function osnac016 and OsNAC016^ko mutants display BR-deficient plant architecture with erect leaves, severe dwarfism, shortened internodes, and reduced root growth but enhanced drought tolerance and ABA sensitivity; OsNAC016 overexpression causes enlarged leaf inclination, stronger BR responses, reduced drought tolerance, and decreased ABA sensitivity. [99]

Regulatory Mechanism

OsNAC016 functions as a transcription factor that binds BRRE motifs in the D2 promoter and ABRE-containing regions in the OsPP2C68 promoter to regulate their transcription. It is phosphorylated by GSK2 at GSK3 consensus motifs and by SAPK8 at Ser-244 and Thr-283, and these phosphorylations promote its ubiquitination and degradation. OsPUB43 physically associates with OsNAC016 and ubiquitinates it, targeting the protein to the 26S proteasome (and autophagy) for turnover. BL and bikinin inhibit GSK2 activity and thereby stabilize OsNAC016, whereas ABA activates SAPK8, enhancing OsNAC016 phosphorylation and degradation. [99]

Pathways

Regulatory: Brassinosteroid (BR) signaling pathway promoting plant architecture; abscisic acid (ABA) signaling and drought-response pathway, where OsNAC016 mediates antagonism between BR-mediated growth and ABA-mediated drought tolerance. [99]

Biosynthetic: Regulates brassinosteroid biosynthesis indirectly by activating expression of the BR biosynthetic gene D2; participates in ABA signaling rather than ABA biosynthesis. [99]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainNAC transcription factor containing N-terminal NAC-binding subdomains (A–E) and a C-terminal transactivation domain with a PEST sequence.
Expression PatternOsNAC016 is preferentially expressed in roots with lower expression in other organs and is repressed by BL in roots while being induced by ABA in roots.
Validation MethodT-DNA insertion and CRISPR/Cas9 knockout mutants; 35S:OsNAC016 overexpression lines; BR (BL) lamina joint inclination assays; ABA sensitivity assays for primary root growth, stomatal closure, seed germination, and root hair elongation; drought survival assays; subcellular localization; domain mapping and transactivation assays in yeast; Y2H, BiFC, split luciferase complementation, pull-down and semi-endogenous Co-IP with GSK2, SAPK8, and OsPUB43; in vitro and in vivo kinase assays; cell-free protein degradation assays; in vivo ubiquitination assays; RNA-seq; ChIP-qPCR and dual-LUC transactivation assays for D2 and OsPP2C68 promoters.
Breeding Application ValueOsNAC016 is a central regulator that promotes BR-mediated plant growth (leaf inclination, plant height, root growth) while repressing ABA-mediated drought responses. Reducing OsNAC016 activity yields erect leaves, dwarfism, and enhanced drought tolerance and ABA sensitivity, whereas overexpression enhances BR responsiveness but compromises drought tolerance. Therefore, tuning OsNAC016 expression or its phosphorylation/ubiquitination (via GSK2, SAPK8, and OsPUB43) provides a strategy to breed rice ideotypes with optimized architecture and controlled trade-offs between growth and drought tolerance.
Environment / TreatmentBrassinolide (BL) treatment; ABA treatment; drought (water withholding and air-drying); bikinin (GSK3-like kinase inhibitor); proteasome inhibitor MG132; autophagy inhibitor E64d.

GSK2 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • GSK2 physically interacts with OsNAC016 as shown by Y2H, BiFC, split luciferase, pull-down, and semi-endogenous Co-IP assays. [99]
  • GSK2 directly phosphorylates OsNAC016 in vitro and in E. coli, and phosphorylation is reduced in OsNAC016 S/T-to-A mutants. [99]
  • A phosphomimic OsNAC016^S/T-D variant is degraded faster than native OsNAC016 in cell-free degradation assays, indicating that GSK2-mediated phosphorylation promotes OsNAC016 destabilization. [99]
  • BL and bikinin treatments, which inhibit GSK2 activity, increase OsNAC016-GFP accumulation in planta, linking GSK2 activity to OsNAC016 stability in BR signaling. [99]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as a negative regulator of BR signaling that phosphorylates OsNAC016, leading to reduced stability of OsNAC016 and thereby modulating BR-mediated plant growth and ABA–BR crosstalk. [99]

Regulatory Mechanism

Directly phosphorylates OsNAC016, and phosphorylation accelerates OsNAC016 degradation via the ubiquitin–proteasome and autophagy pathways; BL and bikinin inhibit GSK2, stabilizing OsNAC016. [99]

Pathways

Regulatory: Brassinosteroid signaling pathway, acting as a key repressor that modulates OsNAC016 stability and downstream BR-responsive gene expression. [99]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGSK3/SHAGGY-like protein kinase, a core negative regulator in the BR signaling pathway.
Validation MethodY2H interaction assays with OsNAC016; BiFC and split luciferase complementation in planta; semi-endogenous Co-IP with OsNAC016-GFP; in vitro pull-down assays; in vitro kinase assays using GST-GSK2 and His-OsNAC016; kinase assays in E. coli co-expressing GST-GSK2 and His-OsNAC016 or phospho-mutant OsNAC016; cell-free degradation assays comparing native and GSK2-phosphomimic His-OsNAC016.
Breeding Application ValueGSK2 is a central negative regulator of BR signaling and directly phosphorylates OsNAC016, promoting its degradation. Manipulating GSK2 activity or its interaction with OsNAC016 provides a potential route to fine-tune BR signaling strength and OsNAC016 levels for controlling plant architecture and growth–drought trade-offs in rice.
Environment / TreatmentBL and bikinin treatments, which inhibit GSK2 activity and thereby increase OsNAC016 accumulation.

SAPK8 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • SAPK8 physically interacts with OsNAC016 as confirmed by Y2H, BiFC, Co-IP, and pull-down assays. [99]
  • SAPK8 directly phosphorylates OsNAC016 in E. coli-based kinase assays; phosphorylation is strongly reduced when Ser-244 and Thr-283 of OsNAC016 are mutated to Ala. [99]
  • Mutation of SAPK8 Ser187 to Ala abolishes its ability to phosphorylate OsNAC016, indicating that SAPK8 autophosphorylation at this site is required for kinase activity toward OsNAC016. [99]
  • ABA treatment in cell-free assays accelerates OsNAC016 degradation, and phosphomimic OsNAC016^S244D T283D is more unstable than native OsNAC016, showing that SAPK8-mediated phosphorylation negatively regulates OsNAC016 stability in ABA signaling. [99]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Functions as an ABA-activated SnRK2 kinase that phosphorylates OsNAC016, leading to reduced OsNAC016 stability and contributing to activation and timely termination of ABA/drought responses. [99]

Regulatory Mechanism

ABA-activated SAPK8 phosphorylates OsNAC016 at specific SnRK2 consensus sites, and this phosphorylation accelerates OsNAC016 ubiquitination and degradation, thus modulating the duration and strength of ABA/drought responses. [99]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling and drought-response pathway, functioning downstream of ABA perception and upstream of OsNAC016 degradation. [99]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSnRK2 family protein kinase (OSMOTIC STRESS/ABA-ACTIVATED PROTEIN KINASE).
Validation MethodY2H interactions between SAPK8 and OsNAC016; BiFC in rice protoplasts and N. benthamiana; Co-IP; in vitro pull-down; kinase assays in E. coli co-expressing GST-SAPK8 and His-OsNAC016 or OsNAC016 phosphosite mutants; kinase assays with SAPK8^S187A; cell-free degradation assays of His-OsNAC016 with and without ABA and with phosphomimic His-OsNAC016^S244D T283D; phos-tag and phosphorylation-state analyses in OsNAC016-GFP plants under ABA and inhibitor treatments.
Breeding Application ValueSAPK8 phosphorylates OsNAC016 and promotes its degradation upon ABA treatment, thereby relieving OsNAC016-mediated repression of drought-responsive genes. Modulating SAPK8 activity toward OsNAC016 could help enhance or fine-tune ABA-dependent drought tolerance while balancing growth in rice.
Environment / TreatmentABA treatment, which activates SAPK8 and promotes OsNAC016 phosphorylation and degradation.

OsPUB43 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • Among several tested PUB E3 ligases, OsPUB43 shows strong interaction with OsNAC016 in Y2H and is confirmed to interact with OsNAC016 by BiFC, pull-down, Co-IP, and split luciferase assays. [99]
  • OsPUB43 interacts preferentially with the N-terminal (1–200 aa) region of OsNAC016, the domain that exhibits the fastest degradation rate in cell-free assays. [99]
  • Co-expression of OsPUB43-GFP with OsNAC016-3×FLAG in N. benthamiana enhances OsNAC016 poly-ubiquitination and accelerates OsNAC016-3×FLAG degradation compared to GFP control. [99]
  • These results demonstrate that OsPUB43 acts as an E3 ubiquitin ligase targeting OsNAC016 for degradation, contributing to the control of OsNAC016 levels in rice. [99]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as a U-box E3 ubiquitin ligase that physically interacts with OsNAC016, promotes its ubiquitination, and accelerates its degradation via the 26S proteasome pathway, thereby negatively regulating OsNAC016 stability. [99]

Regulatory Mechanism

Recognizes OsNAC016 (mainly via its N-terminal domain), catalyzes its poly-ubiquitination, and targets OsNAC016 for degradation via the 26S proteasome pathway, contributing to phosphorylation-dependent turnover of OsNAC016 under ABA/drought conditions. [99]

Pathways

Regulatory: Ubiquitin/26S proteasome system acting downstream of ABA- and BR-regulated phosphorylation events to control OsNAC016 abundance, thereby affecting BR and ABA signaling balance. [99]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainPlant U-box (PUB) type E3 ubiquitin ligase with ARM repeats.
Expression PatternOsPUB43 shows relatively high expression in roots, consistent with faster OsNAC016 degradation in root extracts.
Validation MethodY2H screening with OsNAC016; BiFC in N. benthamiana; in vitro pull-down; semi-endogenous Co-IP with OsNAC016-GFP plants; split luciferase complementation; domain mapping pull-down assays; cell-free degradation assays with OsNAC016 domains; in vivo ubiquitination assays in N. benthamiana co-expressing OsPUB43-GFP and OsNAC016-3×FLAG; in vitro degradation assays showing OsPUB43-GFP accelerates OsNAC016-3×FLAG turnover.
Breeding Application ValueOsPUB43 ubiquitinates and destabilizes OsNAC016, thereby modulating the balance between BR-mediated growth and ABA-mediated drought responses. Altering OsPUB43 activity could provide a handle to adjust OsNAC016 protein abundance and fine-tune rice growth and drought tolerance.
Environment / TreatmentABA indirectly promotes OsNAC016 ubiquitination by increasing its phosphorylation, and MG132 treatment stabilizes ubiquitinated OsNAC016-GFP, indicating 26S proteasome involvement.

OsPP2C68 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsPP2C68 is an ABA early-response gene whose expression is induced by drought and ABA, with higher induction in osnac016 mutants than in wild-type. [99]
  • ABA treatment promotes OsPP2C68 expression, particularly in osnac016, consistent with OsNAC016 acting as a negative regulator. [99]
  • ChIP-qPCR in OsNAC016-GFP plants shows enrichment of OsNAC016 at ABRE-containing regions of the OsPP2C68 promoter, indicating direct binding and repression by OsNAC016. [99]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts as an ABA early-response gene whose transcription is activated by drought and ABA and negatively regulated by OsNAC016, contributing to ABA signaling and drought response. [99]

Regulatory Mechanism

Transcription is induced by drought and ABA and is directly repressed by OsNAC016 through binding to ABRE-containing promoter regions; ABA promotes its expression more strongly in the absence of OsNAC016. [99]

Pathways

Regulatory: ABA signaling and drought-response pathway as an ABA early-response phosphatase gene regulated by OsNAC016. [99]

More Details
Expression PatternOsPP2C68 expression is strongly induced by drought and ABA; its transcript levels are higher in osnac016 mutant than in wild-type after drought or ABA treatment and are promoted by ABA especially in osnac016.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR analysis of OsPP2C68 transcript levels in leaves of wild-type and osnac016 plants under air-drought and ABA treatment; ChIP-qPCR analysis in OsNAC016-GFP plants detecting binding of OsNAC016 to ABRE-containing fragments of the OsPP2C68 promoter.
Breeding Application ValueOsPP2C68 is an ABA early-response gene whose expression is induced by drought and ABA and is repressed by OsNAC016. Manipulating the OsNAC016–OsPP2C68 regulatory module may contribute to enhancing drought tolerance and optimizing ABA sensitivity in rice.
Environment / TreatmentAir-drought treatment (30 min) and ABA treatment, which induce OsPP2C68 expression, particularly in osnac016 mutants.

Gh_A08G0694 (Gossypium hirsutum) Plant

Key Findings

  • A genome-wide survey identified 78 LEA3 genes in cotton, and Gh_A08G0694 (GhLEA3) on chromosome A08 was the most highly induced gene in leaves and roots under drought and salt stress. [100]
  • Gh_A08G0694 protein is localized to chloroplasts and belongs to the group 3 LEA family containing the LEA3 Pfam domain PF03242. [100]
  • VIGS knockdown of Gh_A08G0694 in cotton causes heightened sensitivity to drought and salinity, with reduced biomass, chlorophyll content, relative leaf water content, and antioxidant activity, and increased ion leakage, excised leaf water loss, H2O2 and MDA accumulation; expression of stress-related genes GhP5CS, GhMYB and GhGT-2 is reduced under stress. [100]
  • Overexpression of Gh_A08G0694 in Arabidopsis enhances drought and salt tolerance, increasing survival after rewatering, improving water status, lowering ion leakage and oxidant levels, increasing antioxidant enzyme activities, and promoting higher germination rates and longer roots under NaCl and mannitol; stress-responsive genes AB15, P5CS and RD22 are upregulated. [100]
  • Yeast two-hybrid assays show that Gh_A08G0694 directly interacts with GhVDAC1 and GhGAPA/gapA, and silencing Gh_A08G0694 downregulates VDAC1 and GAPA expression, indicating that Gh_A08G0694 positively regulates these organelle-associated stress-related genes to enhance osmotic stress tolerance. [100]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Positive regulator of drought and salt stress tolerance: knockdown in cotton increases sensitivity to osmotic and salt stress, while overexpression (in Arabidopsis) enhances survival, water retention, membrane stability, antioxidant capacity and root growth under drought and salinity. [100]

Regulatory Mechanism

Acts as a protective LEA3 protein localized in chloroplasts and interacting with mitochondrial outer membrane protein VDAC1 and chloroplastic GAPA, contributing to stabilization of organelle function, maintenance of ROS balance and modulation of downstream stress-responsive gene expression during drought and salt stress. [100]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in ABA- and osmotic-stress-responsive pathways that involve calcium signaling via VDAC1 and metabolic energy supply via GAPA, integrating these processes to enhance cotton tolerance to drought and salinity. [100]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainGroup 3 late embryogenesis abundant (LEA3) protein containing the LEA3 Pfam conserved domain PF03242.
Expression PatternGh_A08G0694 (GhLEA3) is strongly induced in cotton roots and leaves from 3 h to 48 h after exposure to drought (PEG-6000) and salt (NaCl) stress, with much higher expression in stressed tissues than under normal conditions.
Validation MethodVirus-induced gene silencing (VIGS) knockdown in cotton; transgenic overexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana; physiological and biochemical stress assays; RT-qPCR of stress-responsive genes; yeast two-hybrid interaction assays; subcellular localization of eGFP-Gh_A08G0694.
Breeding Application ValueProvides a strong candidate gene for breeding or engineering cotton with improved drought and salinity tolerance; overexpression of Gh_A08G0694 is proposed to enhance osmotic stress tolerance, stabilize organelle function and energy supply, and thereby improve yield stability under variable, stress-prone environments.
Environment / TreatmentDrought simulated with 17% PEG-6000 and by withholding water; salinity imposed with 250 mmol·L^-1 NaCl in hydroponic solution and soil; additional osmotic and salt stress applied to Arabidopsis seeds and seedlings using mannitol (100–300 mmol·L^-1) and NaCl (75–150 mmol·L^-1).

Flavonoid / Phenylpropanoid / Secondary Metabolism (2 genes)

PtrMYB074 (Populus trichocarpa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Acts, together with PtrWRKY19, as an essential positive regulator of PtrbHLH186 expression in stem-differentiating xylem, thereby contributing to a regulatory pathway that enhances lignification, alters vessel and fibre development and confers drought-tolerance phenotypes via downstream PtrbHLH186. [50]

Regulatory Mechanism

PtrMYB074 forms a heterodimer with PtrWRKY19; it does not directly bind the W-box-containing PtrbHLH186 promoter fragment in EMSA, but is recruited to the PtrbHLH186 promoter via PtrWRKY19. The PtrMYB074–PtrWRKY19 dimer is required for effective transcriptional activation of PtrbHLH186. [50]

Pathways

Regulatory: Component of a PtrMYB074–PtrWRKY19–PtrbHLH186 transcriptional regulatory module controlling secondary xylem development and wood formation; PtrWRKY19 binds the W-box in the PtrbHLH186 promoter and recruits PtrMYB074, and the heterodimer is required to activate PtrbHLH186 transcription. [50]

Biosynthetic: Regulation of lignin and secondary cell wall biosynthesis through control of downstream transcription factor PtrbHLH186 and its monolignol pathway targets. [50]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainR2R3-MYB transcription factor (woody dicot-specific MYB TF).
Expression PatternFunctions within stem-differentiating xylem (SDX) of P. trichocarpa as part of a wood-formation transcriptional regulatory network.
Validation MethodProtein–protein interaction assays (large-scale yeast two-hybrid with PtrMYB074N as bait; BiFC in SDX protoplasts); ChIP-seq and FLAG ChIP-qPCR in PtrMYB074-FLAG overexpression lines; EMSA with PtrMYB074 and PtrWRKY19; CRISPR-Cas9 knockout mutants (ptrmyb074) combined with ChIP-qPCR and RT-qPCR of PtrbHLH186; overexpression in SDX protoplasts.
Breeding Application ValueAs a woody dicot-specific regulator at the top of a PtrMYB074–PtrWRKY19–PtrbHLH186 module, modulation of PtrMYB074 activity offers a way to tune the activation of PtrbHLH186 and thereby adjust lignin amount and composition, vessel number and size, and associated drought-tolerance traits in Populus wood, enabling breeding or engineering strategies for optimized growth vs. wood property trade-offs.

PtrWRKY19 (Populus trichocarpa) Plant

Core Phenotypic Effect

Serves as the DNA-binding component that targets PtrbHLH186 via a W-box in its promoter and, together with PtrMYB074, is essential for activation of PtrbHLH186 expression in xylem, thereby enabling downstream increases in lignification, altered vessel development and enhanced drought tolerance mediated by PtrbHLH186. [50]

Regulatory Mechanism

WRKY transcription factor that directly recognizes and binds a W-box (TTGACT) motif in the PtrbHLH186 promoter, as shown by ChIP-qPCR and EMSA. Dimerization with PtrMYB074 leads to formation of a protein–DNA complex with two shifted bands in EMSA and is required for functional transactivation of PtrbHLH186; binding of PtrWRKY19 alone to the promoter is insufficient for activation. [50]

Pathways

Regulatory: Acts with PtrMYB074 in a PtrMYB074–PtrWRKY19–PtrbHLH186 module; directly binds W-box in PtrbHLH186 promoter and recruits PtrMYB074, forming a heterodimer required for transcriptional activation of PtrbHLH186 in secondary xylem. [50]

Biosynthetic: Regulation of lignin and secondary cell wall biosynthesis by directly binding the PtrbHLH186 promoter and, together with PtrMYB074, activating PtrbHLH186, which controls monolignol pathway genes. [50]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainWRKY transcription factor; binds W-box (TTGACT) motif in target promoters.
Expression PatternMost highly expressed and xylem-specific PtrMYB074 interactor among 54 identified TF partners, based on RNA-seq of xylem, phloem, leaf and shoot tissues.
Validation MethodYeast two-hybrid and BiFC with PtrMYB074 for interaction; generation of PtrWRKY19-FLAG overexpression lines; ChIP-seq and FLAG ChIP-qPCR in SDX; EMSA to test binding to W-box and mutated W-box; CRISPR-Cas9 knockout mutants (ptrwrky19) combined with ChIP-qPCR using anti-PtrMYB074 antibody and RT-qPCR of PtrbHLH186; transient overexpression in SDX protoplasts.
Breeding Application ValuePtrWRKY19 provides a DNA-binding entry point for controlling the PtrMYB074–PtrWRKY19–PtrbHLH186 axis. Manipulating PtrWRKY19 expression or activity, alone or together with PtrMYB074, could be used to fine-tune PtrbHLH186 activation, allowing modification of lignin content/composition and xylem anatomy to improve wood properties and potentially drought resilience in Populus breeding or engineering programs.

Yield / Growth / Development (2 genes)

MtSPL4 (Medicago truncatula) Plant

Key Findings

  • MtSPL4 is the Medicago truncatula paralog of alfalfa MsSPL08, sharing about 93% amino-acid identity and similar gene structure. [66]
  • A Tnt1 retrotransposon-tagged mtspl mutant line (NF10482-4, R108 background) carrying a disruption in MtSPL4 shows strong alterations in plant architecture. [66]
  • Loss of MtSPL4 function leads to multifoliate compound leaves with variable leaflet numbers (1–7 leaflets), increased lateral branches, reduced plant height, and delayed flowering. [66]
  • Mutant seedlings display three cotyledons and trifoliate first true leaves compared with two cotyledons and a simple true leaf in wild type, and adult leaves lose normal serrations at the leaf margin, demonstrating that MtSPL4 is required for normal compound leaf patterning and shoot development in M. truncatula. [66]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Loss-of-function of MtSPL4 in Medicago truncatula causes multifoliate leaves with increased and variable leaflet number, enhanced lateral branching, reduced plant height, and delayed flowering, indicating that MtSPL4 negatively regulates leaflet number and branching while promoting stem elongation and timely flowering. [66]

Regulatory Mechanism

MtSPL4 encodes an SPL (SBP-domain) transcription factor that acts as a key regulator of compound leaf morphogenesis, shoot branching, stem elongation, and flowering time; disruption of MtSPL4 alters these developmental programs. [66]

Pathways

Regulatory: Developmental regulation pathway controlling compound leaf formation, shoot branching, and flowering time in Medicago truncatula via SPL transcription factor MtSPL4. [66]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainSPL (SQUAMOSA PROMOTER BINDING PROTEIN-LIKE) transcription factor containing a conserved SBP DNA-binding domain with two Zn-finger-like motifs and a nuclear localization signal, highly similar in sequence and structure to alfalfa MsSPL08.
Expression PatternMtSPL4 is expressed mainly in aboveground tissues (leaves, stems, flowers), with expression profiles similar to MsSPL08, supporting a role in shoot and leaf development.
Validation MethodTnt1 retrotransposon-tagged loss-of-function mutant analysis of MtSPL4 (mtspl line NF10482-4) in Medicago truncatula, with detailed comparison of leaf, shoot, and flowering phenotypes between mutant and wild-type plants.
Breeding Application ValueFunctional characterization of MtSPL4 demonstrates that SPL genes can be used to modulate leaflet number, branching, plant height, and flowering time in legumes. This provides a mechanistic basis and model for targeting SPL homologs such as MsSPL08 to breed multifoliate, compact, high-leaf biomass forage legumes like alfalfa.

D2 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • D2, a BR biosynthetic gene, shows altered expression in osnac016 and OsNAC016-overexpressing plants, indicating regulation by OsNAC016. [99]
  • BL and ABA treatments repress D2 expression, with genotype-dependent differences consistent with OsNAC016-mediated regulation. [99]
  • OsNAC016 significantly activates a D2 promoter–LUC reporter in transient dual-LUC assays in N. benthamiana. [99]
  • ChIP-qPCR in OsNAC016-GFP plants shows specific enrichment of OsNAC016 at BRRE-containing regions of the D2 promoter, demonstrating direct binding and transcriptional activation in vivo. [99]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Encodes a BR biosynthetic enzyme; its expression is positively regulated by OsNAC016 and contributes to BR-mediated plant architecture. [99]

Regulatory Mechanism

Transcriptionally activated by OsNAC016, which binds BRRE motifs in the promoter; expression is under negative feedback from BL and is repressed by ABA. [99]

Pathways

Regulatory: Brassinosteroid biosynthesis and signaling pathway contributing to BR-mediated plant growth and architecture. [99]

Biosynthetic: Brassinosteroid biosynthesis. [99]

More Details
Expression PatternD2 expression in roots is downregulated by BL treatment in all genotypes but remains higher in osnac016 and lower in OsNAC016-overexpressing plants relative to wild-type; ABA represses D2 expression, especially strongly in osnac016.
Validation MethodRT-qPCR analysis of D2 transcript levels in wild-type, osnac016, and OsNAC016 overexpression plants with or without BL and ABA treatment; transient dual-LUC assays with the D2 promoter fused to LUC co-expressed with OsNAC016; ChIP-qPCR assays detecting OsNAC016-GFP enrichment at BRRE-containing regions of the D2 promoter.
Breeding Application ValueAs a BR biosynthetic gene directly activated by OsNAC016, D2 contributes to BR level control and plant architecture. Understanding OsNAC016–D2 regulation offers a way to adjust BR biosynthesis to optimize leaf inclination and plant height while considering impacts on drought tolerance.
Environment / TreatmentBL treatment (10 µM) and ABA treatment, which both repress D2 transcription; BL also modulates D2 through OsNAC016-mediated feedback.

Biotic Stress / Disease Resistance (2 genes)

OsPUB39 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsPUB39 expression is strongly induced by Magnaporthe grisea infection and by SA, JA, and ACC treatments in rice. [87]
  • BMV-mediated silencing of OsPUB39 in IR64 seedlings causes larger lesions and more fungal growth after M. oryzae inoculation, indicating decreased blast resistance. [87]
  • After pathogen challenge, OsPUB39-silenced plants accumulate more ROS and H2O2 and exhibit increased SOD activity but decreased CAT activity compared with controls. [87]
  • Expression of SA- and JA/ET-responsive defense genes (OsLOX1, OsPR3, OsNH1, OsPR1a) and OsWRKY45 is downregulated in OsPUB39-silenced plants after infection. [87]
  • These data support OsPUB39 as a positive regulator of rice blast resistance, likely acting through modulation of ROS homeostasis and SA/JA/ET-dependent defense gene expression. [87]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Silencing OsPUB39 by BMV-based VIGS decreases resistance to Magnaporthe grisea/M. oryzae, leading to larger lesions, greater fungal growth, higher ROS and H2O2 accumulation, altered SOD and CAT activities, and reduced expression of multiple defense-related genes, indicating that OsPUB39 positively regulates blast resistance via ROS homeostasis and defense-gene activation. [87]

Regulatory Mechanism

Silencing OsPUB39 enhances pathogen-induced ROS accumulation (higher H2O2), increases SOD activity and reduces CAT activity, and downregulates SA- and JA/ET-responsive defense genes, indicating regulation of ROS-scavenging capacity and defense gene activation during blast infection. [87]

Pathways

Regulatory: Involved in biotic stress defense pathways linked to SA and JA/ET signaling during rice blast infection. [87]

More Details
Expression PatternOsPUB39 transcript levels are strongly induced by inoculation with M. grisea in incompatible interactions and by treatment with the signaling hormones SA, JA, and ACC; its expression is not reported as responsive to the tested abiotic stresses.
Validation MethodBMV-based virus-induced gene silencing of OsPUB39 in rice cultivar IR64 followed by M. oryzae inoculation, ROS (DAB staining, H2O2) and antioxidant enzyme assays, and qRT-PCR analysis of defense-related gene expression.
Breeding Application ValueOsPUB39 is a positive regulator of resistance to the rice blast fungus Magnaporthe grisea/M. oryzae. Enhancing OsPUB39 function or expression could be used to increase basal blast resistance in rice breeding, potentially reducing lesion size and pathogen growth without major penalties reported in this study.
Environment / TreatmentInoculation with Magnaporthe grisea/M. oryzae; treatments with SA, JA, and ACC to test hormone responsiveness.

OsPUB34 (Oryza sativa) Plant

Key Findings

  • OsPUB34 expression is strongly induced by M. grisea infection and by SA, JA, and ACC treatments. [87]
  • BMV-mediated silencing of OsPUB34 increases resistance to M. oryzae, with smaller lesions and reduced fungal biomass relative to controls. [87]
  • After infection, OsPUB34-silenced seedlings accumulate less ROS and H2O2 and show decreased SOD activity but increased CAT activity. [87]
  • SA- and JA/ET-responsive defense genes (OsLOX1, OsPR3, OsNH1, OsPR1a) and OsWRKY45 are upregulated in OsPUB34-silenced plants compared with controls. [87]
  • These results indicate that OsPUB34 acts as a negative regulator of rice blast resistance, likely by modulating ROS-scavenging enzymes and SA/JA/ET-dependent defense gene expression. [87]

Core Phenotypic Effect

Silencing OsPUB34 by BMV-based VIGS increases resistance to Magnaporthe grisea/M. oryzae, resulting in smaller lesions, less fungal growth, reduced pathogen-induced ROS and H2O2 accumulation, decreased SOD activity, increased CAT activity, and elevated expression of several defense-related genes, indicating that OsPUB34 negatively regulates blast resistance. [87]

Regulatory Mechanism

Silencing OsPUB34 reduces pathogen-induced ROS and H2O2 accumulation by lowering SOD activity and enhancing CAT activity, and upregulates SA- and JA/ET-responsive defense-related genes, indicating that OsPUB34 constrains ROS-scavenging enzyme balance and defense gene activation during blast infection. [87]

Pathways

Regulatory: Functions in negative regulation of biotic stress responses associated with SA and JA/ET signaling during rice blast infection. [87]

More Details
Expression PatternOsPUB34 transcript levels are strongly induced by inoculation with M. grisea in incompatible interactions and are also induced by SA, JA, and ACC treatments; expression under the tested abiotic stresses is not reported as significantly changed.
Validation MethodBMV-based virus-induced gene silencing of OsPUB34 in IR64 followed by M. oryzae inoculation, ROS and H2O2 measurements, antioxidant enzyme (SOD, CAT) assays, and qRT-PCR of defense-related genes.
Breeding Application ValueOsPUB34 acts as a negative regulator of resistance to the rice blast fungus. Partial loss of OsPUB34 function via silencing leads to smaller lesions and reduced fungal growth, suggesting that reduced OsPUB34 activity could be exploited in breeding or engineering to enhance rice blast resistance.
Environment / TreatmentInoculation with Magnaporthe grisea/M. oryzae; treatments with SA, JA, and ACC to assess hormone responsiveness.

Other (1 genes)

AtALMT9 (Arabidopsis thaliana) Plant

Key Findings

  • AtALMT9 is a vacuolar anion channel that mediates Cl- influx into the vacuole during stomatal opening. [91]
  • Cytosolic application of GABA at 100 μM, 2 mM, or 10 mM does not inhibit AtALMT9-mediated Cl- currents and does not cause current rundown over at least 20 minutes. [91]
  • GABA applied to the vacuolar lumen (2 mM GABA_lum) likewise has no detectable effect on AtALMT9-mediated Cl- currents. [91]
  • GABA does not affect the activation of AtALMT9 by 1 mM cytosolic malate2-, indicating that AtALMT9 is not directly regulated by GABA from either side of the vacuolar membrane. [91]

Core Phenotypic Effect

AtALMT9 functions as a vacuolar anion channel mediating Cl- influx into the vacuole, and its ion transport activity and malate-dependent activation are not directly affected by GABA applied from either the cytosolic or vacuolar side. [91]

Regulatory Mechanism

GABA, applied from either the cytosolic or vacuolar side, does not directly regulate AtALMT9 channel activity or its activation by cytosolic malate2-. [91]

Pathways

Regulatory: Vacuolar Cl- transport associated with stomatal opening; AtALMT9 activity within this pathway is not directly regulated by GABA signaling. [91]

More Details
Protein Family / DomainALMT (aluminum-activated malate transporter) family vacuolar anion channel.
Validation MethodCytosolic-side-out patch-clamp electrophysiology on isolated mesophyll vacuoles from Nicotiana benthamiana leaves transiently expressing AtALMT9-GFP, testing Cl- currents in response to GABA and cytosolic malate.
Breeding Application ValueClarifies that GABA does not directly modulate the vacuolar anion channel AtALMT9, indicating that breeding or engineering strategies aiming to alter GABA-dependent stomatal behavior should target other regulators or ion transporters rather than AtALMT9 itself.
Environment / TreatmentApplication of GABA at 100 μM, 2 mM, or 10 mM to the cytosolic side (GABA_cyt) in the presence of 100 mM cytosolic Cl- and 0 or 1 mM cytosolic malate2-; application of 2 mM GABA to the vacuolar (pipette) solution (GABA_lum).

References
